By GREG MEACHEM of The Advocate RICHMOND, B.C. - If Red Deer Rustlers find Richmond Sockeyes to be in a surly mood in tonight's opening game of their Western Canada Centennial Cup semifinal series, they shouldn't be surprised. After all, the Sockeyes' general manager-coach is none other than Orland Kurtenbach, who earned a reputation as a hard-nosed, no-nonsense player during an NHL career that featured stops in New York (Rangers), Toronto and Vancouver. "Yes, I like a physical game, but I also like to see a lot of puck movement," he offered Monday during practice in preparation for tonight's 8:30 p.m. MDT contest. "What I don't like is a lot of goony stuff. There's nothing wrong with good, tough hockey as long as it doesn't take away from the flow of the game." With the Sockeyes, Kurtebach appears to have the best of both worlds. While admitting that his team is "fairly aggressive overall," he quickly pointed out that the B.C. champs are
Richmond Sockeyes, who will finish first in the Coastal Division, rocked the Falcons twice last week.

(CP) - Jason Phillips scored three goals to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to the Centennial Cup junior "A" hockey championship Saturday night with a 5-2 win over the Humboldt Broncos. But the Sockeyes had to fall back on an outstanding performance in goal by Frank Romeo, who stood up to 46 Humboldt shots while his teammates managed only 23 at the Broncos net. Romeo series. was named most valuable player in the -team, roundrobin British Columbia's Sockeyes skated to period leads of 2-0 and 2-1 before Paul Rutherford scored the winning goal in the third. Dave Tomlinson also scored for Richmond, which took seven of the game's 12 minor penalties. Curtis Chamberlin and Kevin Luke scored for the Saskatchewan champion Broncos.

An all-Spokane officiating crew will work the exhibition game between the U.S. and Canadian Olympic teams Nov. 28 in Tacoma. Dennis LaRue, the lone American selected to referee in the 1988 Olympics, will be the referee in the Tacoma Dome. His linesmen will be Kerry Blair and Cory Bowles. All three formerly played Junior B hockey in Spokane, and LaRue played one year of senior amateur. LaRue and Blair also will work the U.S.- Canada game Nov. 27 in Los Angeles. Mark Rudolph, officiating program director of the Amateur Hockey Association of the U.S., said LaRue may receive other exhibition assignments. Don Cherry, who played pro hockey for the 1962-63 Spokane Comets, has been inducted into the Rochester (N.Y.) Americans Hall of Fame. Cherry played and coached for Rochester in the pro American Hockey League after leaving Spokane. He later coached in the National Hockey League. Joe Crozier, another former Comet defenseman, NHL coach and Rochester coach, was one of the original inductees in the Americans' Hall of Fame last year. Dan Holden, the only local player ever to play for the Spokane Chiefs in the Western Hockey League, is playing for A Acadia University in Wolfville, Nova Scotia. Holden played at the University of Calgary in 1984-85, then joined the Chiefs the following season. Former Chief defenseman Rick Herbert, who holds the WHL record of 329 games played, has joined the Richmond Sockeyes of the Tier Two British Columbia Junior Hockey League. Marc Pezzin, who coached Herbert in Spokane and Brandon, said Herbert was not interested in playing a sixth season in the WHL after being released by the Chicago Blackhawks. Herbert plans to play college hockey in Canada next year, Pezzin said.

RICHMOND, B.C. (CP) - Bill Hardy, Dave Tomlinson and Matt Hervey scored two goals apiece Tuesday night to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to an 11-4 Centennial Cup playoff victory over the Red Deer Rustlers. The victory opened a best-of-seven Western semifinal between the B.C. and Alberta Tier II junior hockey champions. Second game in the series will be played here tonight before the remainder of the series shifts to Red Deer. Jim Dunn, Tony Bobbitt, Jason Phillips, Mike McCormick, and Dean Rutlege also scored for the Sockeyes who held period leads of 4-2 and 6-3 and had a huge 60-34 edge in shots on goal. Kevin Melrose, Brian Puhalsky, Greg Pulliam and Rick Anderson scored for the Rustlers who got three goals on the power play. Richmond also scored three powerplay goals as they took 91 minutes in penalties while the visitors took 101

Notes, quotes and leftovers from the wonderful world of sports... ...Nanaimo Midget Clippers' coach Bill Bestwick can't say enough about the support his team gave his six-year-old daughter Brooke following open heart surgery last week. "The kids gave her a big stuffed St. Bernard and as soon she found out it was from the team, she called it Clipper," said Bill. "She's doing fine. She just has to easy for a while. Bill and wife Bonnie have known for six months Brooke required the surgery, but it came down to a case of waiting for the hospital called. "It's a nice relief that it's over," said Bill. "She was pretty strong through it all. She's actually quite proud of herself. She says, 'You wanna see my zipper'. Brooke will of course get additional support from, her brother Brett. .. Richmond Sockeyes' rugged captain Clarke looked like a madman when he dropped his gloves and challenged Clipper smurf Jack Piechutta in Friday's BCJHL game. What brought that act on Rob? "He konked me on the head," smiled Clarke. "I just got riled up.' . HUMBOLDT, Sask. Jason Phillips scored three goals to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to an easy 7-3 win over the Dartmouth Fuel Kids in the Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship yesterday. It was the second loss in two days for Dartmouth. The Nova Scotians opened the week-long, round-robin tournament Saturday with a 7-2 defeat at the hands of the host Humboldt Broncos. Bryon Moller, Steve Jaques, Dave Tomlinson and Matt Hervey also scored for the Sockeyes, who piled up 44 shots against Dartmouth's 29. Richmond zipped to a 3-1 lead in the first period and built the margin to 6-2 in the second. Jerry Scott had two goals and Brian King had one for the Fuel Kids, who had the bulk of the fans behind them. The Sockeyes took 11 of 17 minors and a major, and both teams had a misconduct. Humboldt was to play its second game later Sunday against the Pembroke Lumber Kings from Ontario. Richmond plays Pembroke in the only game today.

By DAN SCHNICK Herald-Tribune staff Ritchie Webb hopes someday to be in Ken Head's shoes. North Stars coach has what Fort St. John coach-general manager Webb considers a luxury with 11 veterans. **It would be nice to have as many returnees as Grande Prairie.' " says Webb as he prepares his 0-10 club for their third meeting of the Peace Cariboo Junior Hockey League season with the Stars. Webb has been busy negotiating with managers from the Richmond Sockeyes, Victoria Cougars and Abbotsford Falcons for players. So far he has traded forward Blaine Moore for defenceman Steve McCuaig from the B.C. Junior Falcons. **We need someone of quality and experience," says Head as he attempts to put an end to the worst start in Huskies history. **I think we're playing well as a team but are lacking that experience and leadership that pulls a team together.' Nevertheless, Head knows their is a battle to be won when the club storms the Fort tomorrow night. **(Webb's) always working," said Head whose club is off to an 8-3 start. "He doesn't sit idle, he has cuts to be made. "They're getting better they're ready to win. They only lost 5-4 to the (Dawson Creek) Kodiaks the other night. A

Staff Writer To longtime Clipper fans, it may be kind of funny to see Bill Hardy dressed in black and red instead of orange and white. The ex-Clipper now plays for Richmond Sockeyes, who will be at Civic Arena to face the Clippers tonight in game three of the B.C. Junior Hockey League Coastal Division best of seven semi-final series. Richmond holds a 2-0 lead consecutive wins on home ice Tuesday and Wednesday. Game three is slated for 8 p.m., and a crowd of over 1,300 is expected. Hardy, who wore Clipper colors for four years, after coming up through the Nanaimo minor hockey system, decided to play for a different team this season in hopes of securing a college scholarship. Last season, Hardy had his best season as a junior, scoring 39 goals and 72 assists, but on a team that finished below .500 and third in the standings. This season, the six-feet, 180- pounder saw his point totals drop, with 19 goals and 33 assists.Hardy, although unsuccessful in his attempt to get a scholarship so far, still feels he made the right move. "It's probably true that I would have got more points and ice time in Nanaimo, but I don't regret the move," said Hardy. "'There's still the playoffs. That's why I left Nanaimo, so I could be on a winning team.' Hardy, a top sniper with Nanaimo last season, found himself in the middle of the pack with Richmond, behind such prolific pointgetters as Dave Tomlinson, Rob Clarke and Bryan Moller. JOB HAS CHANGED He has seen, his duties slightly changed, to where he now plays more of an all-around role for the Sockeyes. "My role is to be in the corners and grind,' said Hardy. *(Linemates) Brian Kozak and Jason Phillips are very talented with the puck. I try and get them the puck from the corner and then get open." EDMONTON (CP) The Edmonton Oilers kept shaking their heads at questions Wednesday, making sure it really was May 6 and they were in the Stanley Cup semifinal and not April 9 and round one of the playoffs. "This isn't shades of LA, it's a duplicate," said coach Glen feeling a little of the deja vu himself. "I hope the next four games are the same." Sather was alluding to Edmonton losing the first game of the playoffs 5-2 to Los Angeles, then beating the Kings four straight. Edmonton swept Winnipeg before losing to the Detroit Red Wings 3-1 in Game 1 of the semifinal Tuesday. "I heard a lot of these questions after the first LA game,' Sather told reporters during the off day of the Campbell Conference final. "It was the same situation, they beat us, the next game we came back and we played like we're capable of playing." Edmonton beat Los Angeles 13-3 in Game 2 but the Oilers were unanimous in their agreement they won't likely beat the Red Wings by that lopsided a score in the second game of the best-ofseven semifinal tonight. "This team is a lot better defensively than LA, we'll have a lot tougher time beating Detroit," said Kent Nilsson, the lone Oiler scorer Tuesday. Fuel Kids finish Broncos HUMBOLDT, Sask. (CP) - The Humboldt Broncos won a place in the final of the Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship tournament Wednesday night with a 6- 1 victory over the Richmond Sockeyes. The losers, with a -robin record of 2-1, will meet the Pembroke Lumber Kings in tonight's Perron * 4,4 The job that currently consumes almost every waking hour for Jean Perron has gnawed at many of his predecessors, leaving a trail of frayed nerves and queasy stomachs as it spit out the victims. There was Claude Ruel, whose nervous system was so shattered, he once abandoned his spot behind the bench before a game had ended. There was Al McNeil, who couldn't survive the tumult and the shouting, even though his results were outstanding. There was Bernard Geoffrion, a fun-loving, effervescent guy who became Exhibit A in a demonstration of the effects of on-the-job stress. There was Bob Berry, who was a moderate smoker when he took the job, but sent the stocks of tobacco companies soaring by the time he relinquished it. +

paragraphs.text: cool fans Graham Parsons was thankful, although he was still wondering what all the fuss was about. The Rustler general manager appealed to Red Deer fans Monday to stop throwing debris on the ice and at the Richmond Sockeyes players, after the Canadian Amateur Hockey Association warned that if the fans didn't behave, the series would either be cancelled or moved to a neutral site. As a result, the crowd was unusually quiet for the majority of the Rustlers' 7-5 overtime win Tuesday, and well-behaved throughout the entire contest. "I asked the fans to stop throwing things, they responded in a positive fashion and I'm extremely grateful," said Parsons. "It just shows that our fans have class. "But really, there never was as much trouble as Richmond made it out to be. Everything was blown out of proportion the last two games." Parsons wasn't impressed with the CAHA's get-tough approach. "They said we had three options - the series could be awarded to Richmond, it could be moved, or it could be cancelled. "Fifty per cent of the blame has to fall on Richmond, yet they're getting all the breaks (from the CAHA). "All the life has been taken out of the series." Parsons urged fans to bring signs and banners to tonight's game.

By GREG MEACHEM of The Advocate With close to 1,700 fans roaring at the top of their lungs, Richmond Sockeyes' forward Jason Phillips must have felt the heat. Certainly, Red Deer Rustler goaltender Ken Weiss did. Fortunately for Weiss and the Rustlers, Phillips' thermostat was turned up little higher. The Sockeyes' ace sniper came up empty in a one-on-one duel with Weiss late in a 10- minute overtime period Tuesday at the Arena. Rustlers held on to their one-goal cushion, added an empty net marker 14 seconds later and scored a 7-5 win, taking a 3-2 lead in the best-seven Western Centennial Cup semifinal series. The sixth game is tonight at 7:30 p.m. at the Arena. With 24 seconds left in the overtime stanza, Richmond was awarded a penalty shot after referee Cliff Stauble ruled a Red Deer player had intentionally dislodged the Rustler goal. While Phillips stood at center ice, sizing up the opportunity, Rustler winger Rich Anderson persuaded the crowd to turn up the volume. Phillips skated in on Weiss and shot low to the corner, but instead of finding the mesh, it was blocked by the goaltender's pad. "I thought he was going to try to deke me," said a relieved Weiss, who turned in his fourth straight glittering performance, stopping 46 shots. "But he kept holding the puck to the outside. My heart was pounding away, I went into a butterfly and it hit me. "I felt confident I could stop it, but it was a lot tougher for him than it was for me. . If he makes it, they're still alive. If he doesn't, he's the goat." The first two periods of the game had all the intensity of a taffy-pull. In the most lacklustre contest of the series to date, Rustlers led 3-2 after one period and 4-3 after two, before being outscored 2-1 in the third. Dixon Ward notched the winner just 59 seconds into overtime and the Rustlers moved to within one win of a date with Humboldt Broncos in the Western final, and an automatic berth in the Centennial Cup (Canadian championship) slated for next month in Humboldt. The best-of-seven Western final starts Monday in Humboldt. "We sort of moonwalked through the first two periods," said Rustler coach Larry Billows. "We had more life in the third, but maybe that's because the fans finally started to get excited." With •Big Brother' the Canadian Amateur Hockey Association monitoring the situation. the Red Deer crowd was less lively Tuesday. The CAHA warned that the series would be either cancelled or moved to a neutral location if the fans didn't stop harassing the Sockeyes and throwing debris into their bench. "That's the way hockey should be played (without fan interference)." noted Sockeye general manager-coach Orland Kurtenbach. •What bothered me the first two games here (Saturday and Sunday) was not just the fans, but the refereeing. "Tonight, the refereeing was excellent, he had a good feel for the game. As a result, the teams were

ATLANTIC CITY, N..J./ The Canadian Press Michael Olajide is ready to take on Thomas Hearns for the International Boxing Federation middleweight crown, but the trainer of his latest victim says the Canadian got lucky. "Michael Olajide got a Mothers Day gift," said Angelo Dundee after his fighter, Troy Darrell, lost to Olajide yesterday in a unanimous, 10-round decision. Olajide, of Vancouver, 23-0 and ranked No. 2 by the IBF, admitted the fight wasn't his best, and Dundee was quick to agree. "I'm amazed my guy lost," said Dundee. "Olajide definitely got licked." Judge Al De Vito scored it 96-92 for Olajide, while Rocky Castellani's scorecard was 97-94 and William Kostrub's read 95-94. The victory means Olajide will be negotiating today at IBF headquarters in Newark with Hearns for a battle for the vacant middleweight title. Olajide will fight either Hearns, the No. 1 contender, or the next leading available contender. Olajide, who has moved to New York City to train, floored Darrell twice in the opening round with quick flurries to the head but couldn't finish the job. Darrell, from New Rochelle, N.Y., bolstered by surviving the round, then waged an inspired inside fight. Richmond Sockeyes win Centennial Cup HUMBOLDT, Sask./ /The Canadian Press Jason Phillips scored three goals to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to the Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship Saturday night with a 5-2 win over the Humboldt Broncos. But the Sockeyes had to fall back on an outstanding performance in goal by Frank Romeo, who stood up to 46 Humboldt shots while his teammates managed only 23 at the Broncos net. Romeo was named most valuable player in the four-team, roundrobin series.

By ELLIOTT PAP Sun Sports Reporter HUMBOLDT, Sask. - It was a bedraggled, dishevelled •but euphoric group of Richmond Sockeyes who pulled out of this prairie town Sunday, their mission accomplished. Stowed among their gear and luggage, in an unassuming crate marked 'fragile', stood the symbol of their achievement. The Sockwere bringing home the Centennial Cup. Coach Orland Kurtenbach's troops stunned the cocky Humboldt Broncos 5-2 Saturday night to win the tier two junior hockey championship of Canada. The triumph capped? a remarkable post-season for the Sockeyes, who won 27 of 34 playoff games and five different titles: B.C. Junior League, Mowat Cup, AlbertaB.C., Abbott Cup (western Canada) and the Centennial Cup. "It's just an A-1 feeling," beamed Kurtenbach, a winner in his first crack at coaching junior. "I don't care if it's the Stanley Cup, Memorial Cup, Adams Cup (Central League) or Centennial Cup, The it's total elation." Sockeyes once again led by the goal-scoring of Jason Phillips and the goaltending of Frank Romeo, both of whom carted off major individual awards. Phillips concluded a fantastic week with a hat trick Saturday and was selected the tournament's

by Russ Ullyot The Chilliwack Progress The British Columbia. Junior Hockey League begins its 1987-88 season this weekend, with the experts watching to see if Richmond Sockeyes can repeat their powerhouse | performance of last spring. After winning the Coastal Division, the Sockeyes went on the Centennial Cup, emblamatic of Tier Two Junior A hockey supremacy in Canada. The Sockeyes didn't have the best regular season record in the BCJHL last year = that distinction belonged to the Kelowna Packers, who lost only five of of 52 games all season. The BCJHL has added a 12th team for 1 the '87-88 season. Back in the fold after a one-year leave are the Salmon Arm Tigers, who will be part of the Interior Division, which includes Kelowna Packers, Vernon Lakers, Penticton Knights, Merritt Centennials and Summerland Buckaroos. The Coast Division features Richmond Sockeyes, Delta Flyers, Nanaimo Clippers, Juan De Fuca Whalers, Abbotsford Falcons and Chilliwack Eagles. (Here is a preview of the Falcon's Coastal Division competition.) Richmond Sockeyes Last year's record: 38-14-0 - first place. The Sockeyes have just four players - none of whom play defence - returning from last year's championship roster, but coach Orland Kurtenbach knows his players and the organization does one of the best jobs of recruiting in all of hockey. Last year's club also had only four returning players, and two those were traded away by Christmas. The only player who has been with the club for more than one season is 20-year-old goalie Glenn Pencer, who is entering his fifth season with the Sockeyes. Last year, he posted a 4.20 goals-against average, while sharing the netminding with Frank Romeo. The other returning players include right winger Rob Clarke, 20. Clarke was third in the Coast Division scoring with 65 goals and 35 assists last year. Centre Jason Tallo, 17, and right winger Tony Bobbit, 19, round out the four returnees.

Something's rotten at the Red Deer Arena and it's not dead fish! The stink is coming from a small group of people who think that by paying their $6 or $4 admission, they can do anything short of pulling a shotgun. Those kind of people can't even be called fans. It's them who are throwing coins, empty aluminum cans, golfballs, and other garbage, most of which has been directed at the B.C. champion Richmond Sockeyes. The clowns throwing debris could force the Canadian Amateur Hockey Association to cancel the series or move it elsewhere. The culprits are not all adults or teenagers either. Many of them are youngsters who have been seen tossing aluminum cans onto the ice after every Red Deer goal. Beach balls aren't dangerous. Dead fish aren't safe either, but even National Hockey League crowds toss chickens and the odd octopus onto the ice. Coins on the ice can cause a player to trip and become seriously injured. Flying aluminum cans, eggs and golfballs can blind an eye or cause other injuries. Tossing cans at players involved in a scuffle could hit anyone, including linesmen or the referee. An errant toss could even fall short and hit someone in the crowd. Six dollars doesn't entitle anyone to throw objects onto the playing surface or at the other team. Six dollars allows you to holler for your team, clap and even get involved in a wave or two. Exercise your lungs and boo. Jeer the referee if you want. For a lousy $6, I certainly wouldn't want to live with the fact a tossed coin, can or golfball hurt someone. There are those who would argue they're only getting even for the treatment endured by Red Deer when they played last week in B.C. Richmond has already paid that price with a $500 fine for lack of security. Eggs and tomatoes flew at Red Deer players and there's even a report one Richmond fan was seen with rocks. Two wrongs don't make a right and could result in a serious injury. The behavior of the two teams doesn't help. I've watched junior hockey for about a dozen years now and Richmond is perhaps the most undisciplined team I've seen. As one Western Hockey League scout said Saturday when you have a junior club loaded with 20-year-olds you can go one of two ways. The older players will either march over the opposition with talent and experience or they will lose their composure and run amuck. Richmond has 10 players on its roster aged 20. Richmond, at least in the last two games, has attempted to intimidate Red Deer and it hasn't, and won't, work. The only thing its done is offer Red Deer a chance to work its powerplay. The two games here produced a ghastly 655 minutes in penalties, 360 to Richmond. Granted, a large portion of the time 300 minutes - is the obligatory 10-minute game misconduct for fighting. However, there's still another 355 minutes of fighting,

By GREG MEACHEM of The Advocate His numbers told the story Ken Weiss was the Alberta Junior Hockey League's best goaltender this season. He's now in the process of proving statistics don't lie. The 21-year-old native of Englewood, Colorado, backstopped Sherwood Park Crusaders to the North Division pennant, led them past Fort Saskatchewan Traders in a semifinal round, but couldn't prevent St. Albert Saints from winning a tough, seven-game North final. His season didn't end there, however. South Division winner Red Deer Rustlers ousted the Saints in six games in the AJHL championship series, named Weiss as their special affiliate, and headed west to take on B.C. champion Richmond Sockeyes. Rustler regular Glen Furgason opened the series, was the unfortunate victim of an 11-4 bombing, and Weiss has been in the Red Deer net ever since. With the Crusaders' puckstopper minding the store, the Rustlers fell 6-4 in Richmond, but have since rebounded to deadlock the best-of-seven series at 2-2 with 6-3 and 7-1 wins in Red Deer Saturday and Sunday. "I was a little worried that there might be a rift between myself and the e rest of the team," Weiss admitted Monday, prior to a light skate in preparation: for tonight's fifth game at 7:30 p.m. at the Arena. "After all, Glen has been here all season and he's taken them this far. But the guys have been great, if there's any problem

By GREG MEACHEM of The Advocate RICHMOND, B.C. Richmond Sockeyes are named after one of British Columbia's most famous natural resources - the Sockeye salmon, a spirited, but hardly voracious breed of fish. In Tuesday's opening game of the Western Canada Centennial Cup semifinal at the Richmond Arena, they looked more like sharks. Or barracudas. Or piranhas. Or all three. Sockeyes were hungry, it was feeding time, and the Red Deer Rustlers just happened to be on the menu. Rustlers meekly allowed themselves to be devoured 11-4. The second game of the bestof-seven series will be played here tonight at 8:30 p.m. MDT, with the remainder to be played in Red Deer. The ridiculously easy win, witnessed by about 1,250 fans, was the Sockeys' 16th without a loss in post-season play. And, at no time Tuesday were they in serious trouble of marring their impressive record. "I was very pleased with the effort of our team tonight," said Richmond general managercoach Orland Kurtenbach. "Hard work is our key to success, and we haven't been outworked too often in the playoffs." Certainly, they weren't outhustled by the Rustlers, who never fully recovered from starry Sockeye forward Dave Tomlinson's opening goal just 21 seconds into the contest. "Our defence was awful, I've never see Furgie (goaltender Glen Furgason) look so bad, and less than half of our forwards came to play," snapped Rustler coach Larry Billows. "Richmond worked hard, beat us to the puck, and generally just played the game the way it's supposed to be played. Meanwhile, we didn't have any intensity or desire at all." Richmond, faster, bigger and more talented than any team the Rustlers have faced this season, fired 60 shots at Furgason, who couldn't be blamed for the vast majority of the goals. Red Deer, in comparison, directed 34 at Sockeyes netminder Frank Romeo, who wasn't required to make more than a half dozen quality saves. Just over a minute after Tomlinson opened the scoring. standout defenceman Matt Hervey doubled the count with a blast from the point. But the Rustlers, on the strength of power play goals from Kevan Melrose and Greg Pulliam, were able to get out of the period with a 4-2 deficit. And, the narrowed the gap to one on Rich Anderson's play marker at 26 seconds of the middle frame. But Richmond rebounded with two unanswered goals be-

RICHMOND, B.C. (CP) Bill Hardy, Dave Tomlinson and Matt Hervey scored two goals apiece Tuesday night to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to an 11-4 Centennial Cup playoff victory over the Red Deer Rustlers. The victory opened a -seven Western semi-final between the B.C. and Alberta Tier II junior hockey champions. The second game in the series will be played here tonight before the remainder of the series shifts to Red Deer. Jim Dunn, Tony Bobbitt, Jason Phillips, Mike McCormick, and Dean Rutlege also scored for the Sockeyes who held period leads of 4- 2 and 6-3 and had a huge 60-34 edge in shots on goal. Kevin Melrose, Brian Puhalsky, Greg Pulliam and Rick Anderson scored for the Rustlers who got three goals on the power play.

Sun News Services RED DEER, Alta. - Red Deer Rustlers beat the Richmond Sockeyes 7-5 in overtime Tuesday in a Centennial Cup playoff hockey game that saw no repeat of the unruly fan behavior that marred Sunday's match. The victory enabled the Rustlers to take a 3-2 lead in the best-of-seven tier two junior western semi-final series. The sixth game goes here tonight. "There wasn't a bit of trouble," said Sockeye coach Orland Kurtenbach. "The management asked the fans to refrain from throwing anything on the ice and there was no problem. Dixon Ward's goal 59 seconds into the 10-minute overtime period was the winner. Geoff Gosselin added an insurance marker at 9:50 into an empty net just after Sockeye Jason Phillips missed a penalty shot. The Sockyes were led by Brian Kozak, who had two goals, while Paul Rutherford, Mike Claringbull and Phillips contributed singles. The Rustlers held period leads of 3-2 and 4-3, fell behind 5-4 in the third period but managed to pull even at 13:16 to force the overtime. Richard Anderson and Brian Puhalsky each scored twice for Red Deer and Greg Pulliam added a single.

Flyers 5, Islanders 1 PHILADELPHIA (AP) - The Philadelphia Flyers may have won the battle and lost the war. The Flyers erupted for three firstperiod goals Saturday night to beat the New York Islanders 5-1, winning their NHL Patrick Division final series in seven games and advancing to the Stanley Cup semifinals against the Montreal Canadiens. The Canadiens reached the Wales Conference final with a 5-3 victory Saturday over the Quebec Nordiques, also in a seventh game. When the Flyers take the ice here tonight for the first game of the bestof-seven series, the injury-riddled division champs might best be called survivors. Coach Mike Keenan's best scorer, Tim Kerr, is listed as day-to-day with a dislocated shoulder. And captain Dave Poulin has a broken rib and played Saturday night loaded with pain-killer, seeing no action after the first period. "He was in pain every minute he was out there," defenceman Mark Howe said of Poulin. "But we needed him. He gave all he could give." Aches And Pains J. J. Daigneault is nursing a tender ankle, Ilkka Sinisalo has sore stomach muscles, Ron Sutter is getting over a 2½-month layoff with a back injury, and Murray Craven is gone with a broken foot. In Saturday's deciding game, a fast start by the Flyers spelled a quick end for the Islanders' playoff hopes. Brian . Propp and Brad Marsh scored short-handed goals 44 seconds apart midway through the first period as the Flyers raced to a 3-0 lead. Dave Brown scored at 6:21 before Propp and Marsh both scored with Lindsay Carson in the penalty box. The two short-handed goals in one period matched a Stanley Cup record shared by 16 other teams. Humboldt Leads Centennial Cup HUMBOLDT, Sask. (CP) - Humboldt Broncos beat Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-2 Sunday night to take an early lead in the round-robin Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship. Humboldt also won the tournament opener, beating Dartmouth Fuel Kids 7-2 Saturday night. The Fuel Kids, crowd favorites in the early going, fell 7-3 to Richmond Sockeyes in the only other game Sunday. The fourteam tournament ends Saturday.

By JACK WILSON of The Advocate The Alberta-B.C. junior hockey championship could be cancelled or moved unless fans behave, the Canadian Amateur Hockey Association says. The association said in an announcement today that unruly behavior of fans at the Arena has cast a shadow over the series between the Red Deer Rustlers and the Richmond Sockeyes.

fight-filled championship at the Red Deer Arena. Sgt. Williams said the man was arrested because he persisted in breaching the p peace. He was arrested and released following the game which was won 7-1 by Red Deer to even the best-of-seven series at two games each. Sgt. Williams said three policemen were at the Arena. He said the policemen were not assigned to the game but patrolled the building as part of their normal patrol zone. Arena manager Albert Mason said his staff didn't call the police to help quell the secondperiod disturbance. The Rustlers have between 10-12 security members. The game was delayed 30 minutes by four on-ice battles, as well as a fracas behind the Richmond Sockeyes players bench that was instigated by a group of disorderly fans who threw debris at the Richmond players, including eggs. Red Deer fans have thrown debris after every Rustler goal, including eggs, tomatoes, and fish. One player on the Richmond bench was hit in the face by an egg. The series has been punctuated by fan disturbances. Last week in Richmond two Red Deer players recieved suspensions of one and three games each for a post-game altercation with fans. The Richmond club was fined $500 by the CAHA for failing to provide proper security at that game.

squad. The Rustlers embarrassed themselves, their coach and their league Tuesday night, barely showing up in an 11-4 loss to Orland Kurtenbach's Richmond Sockeyes. "That's the worst game I've ever seen us play," muttered Red Deer coach Larry Billows, who took over the club in February. "Our defence stunk and our goaltender (Glen Furgason) played probably his worst game. We weren't skating, we were floating. We didn't play with any aggression. On their last two goals, we just gave up." The only consolation for Red Deer is the fact this Centennial Cup western semi-final series is a best-of-seven, not total goals. Richmond now leads it 1-0 with game two tonight, 7:30, at Richmond Arena. "I thought they played tougher hockey out in Alberta," said Sockeye goaltender Frank Romeo. "I'm surprised we beat them this bad. I don't think they took us that seriously. But the next game will probably be completely different. It will probably be really close.' HUMBOLDT, Sask. (CP) - Tony Bobbitt's goal at 1:04 of a 10-minute overtime period gave the Richmond Sockeyes a 4-3 win over the Humboldt Broncos in a Centennial Cup junior hockey playoff game Wednesday night. The victory gave the Sockeyes a 2-1 lead in the best-of-seven Tier 2 western final series. The fourth game will be played in Richmond Saturday. Dave Tomlinson, Mike Claringbull and Rob Clarke scored the other goals for the Sockeyes, who were outshot 39-23 and trailed 1-0 after the first period and 2-1 after the second. BillMcDougall had two goals and Curtis Chamberlin one for the Saskatchewan champion Broncos. Richmond, the British Columbia champions, took 13 of 25 minor penalties and the game's only major. NBA awards franchises NEW YORK (AP) - Charlotte, N.C., Minneapolis, Orlando, Fla., and Miami were awarded expansion franchises on Wednesday by the National Basketball Association board of governors after a four-hour meeting., Charlotte and Miami will be admitted for the 1988-89 season. Minneapolis and Orlando enter the following year. Each expansion franchise will pay an entry fee of $32.5million US. Bids from Toronto and Orange County, Calif., were rejected. The Toronto bid was spearheaded by businessman David Fingold. The other principals were Bill Ballard, son of Toronto Maple Leafs owner Harold Ballard; Dusty Cohl; Michael Cohl; Robert Cohl and former NBA great Wilt Chamberlain. The Toronto group had planned to use Maple Leaf Gardens as the team's home base.

RICHMOND, B.C. (CP) - Barry Kress set up four goals as the Richmond Sockeyes outhustled the Delta Flyers to record a 7-2 British Columbia Junior Hockey League victory Wednesday night. Rob Clarke and Dwayne Wills each scored two goals for Richmond while goaltender Willie Popp stopped 35 shots. Chris Dixon made only 16 saves while allowing seven goals for the losers. The win gives the Sockeyes sole possession of second place in the Coastal Division, 11 points behind the first -place Flyers.

Quesnel Millionaires have gone back to life in the gutter. By dumping Grande Prairie North Stars 4-1 in the best-of-seven championship series, the Mills moved into the aristocracy of the Peace Cariboo Junior Hockey League. They were considered the favorites after eliminating the four-time PCJHL champion Prince George Spruce Kings in the semifinals and proved their worth by soundly defeating the Stars in the final. But, now, the Millionaires have to go against an even bigger fish - the Richmond Sockeyes. Tomorrow night, the Millionaires begin a best-ofthree series in Richmond to determine the B.C. representative in the Centennial Cup playoffs. The Sockeyes went undefeated en route to claiming the B.C. Junior Hockey League championship. No PCJHL club has ever won a single game in these playoffs. Coupled with the fact the Sockeyes went undefeated in the BCJHL playoffs, the Millionaires have reverted to their traditional role of underdogs. *We have absolutely nothing to lose and everything to gain whereas they have everything to lose,' says Tom Marsh, the coach who engineered Quesnel's first ever PCJHL title as a Junior A club. Marsh watched Richmond wrap up the BCJHL title in the fifth and deciding game of its best of nine championship series with Kelowna Packers. The 26- year-old bench boss thinks he has figured out a way to reel in the Sockeyes.

Paopao heads east REGINA (CP) - Saskatchewan Roughriders traded veteran quarterback • Joe 1 Paopao to the Ottawa Rough Riders Tuesday for second-year quarterback Tom Burgess. Paopao, 31, a 10-year pro. ranked seventh in the Canadian Football League last year. He completed 207 of 363 passes for 2,647 yards, 13 touchdowns and 24 interceptions. He appeared in 15 games. The Long Beach State product joined Saskatchewan from British Columbia Lions in 1984 as a free agent. Paopao holds two Saskatchewan passing records with 38 completions against Montreal on July 19. 1985. and most vaards passing in a game with 438 against Toronto on July 12. 1985. "I have a great deal of respect for Joe Paopao and I have also felt that he did an outstanding job." coach John Gregory said in a news release. Tom Burgess, 23, completed 95 of 199 passes for 1.199 yards, five touchdowns and 12 interceptions in his rookie season.

There's something very fishy going on in Red Deer. That's where Richmond' Sockeyes are playing off against the hometown Rustlers for the right to meet Humboldt Broncos to decide a berth in the Centennial Cup of Junior A hockey. Apparently the series has been a rough one. In the first two games last week in Richmond, a pair of Red Deer players were suspended for going into the crowd. With the series shifting to Red Deer for the remaining games, the hometown fans have decided to get a little revenge on the Sockeyes, and they have some rather unique ways of doing it. The most popular has been to throw salmon on to the ice. And we're not talking the canned variety either. Real whole salmon with a slice in the belly, SO that when it hits the ice, all the guts make a nice mess on the ice.

HUMBOLDT - When Bernie Lynch and his Humboldt Bronco coaching staff were assembling their roster for this season, they had one type of player in mind: a Duncan Ryhorchuk style-player. "Let's see, what are all the cliches - grinder, mucker, lunchpail type player?" said Lynch, the Broncos' coach, looking for the proper description of his scrappy forward. "Let me put it this way, he's a good, honest hockey player. If he comes by a pass, or comes by a goal, you know he came by it the hard way. He's the kind of guy that gives you callouses on your hands from patting him on the back.' With the Broncos hanging on to a 3-2 lead in the third period, Ryhorchuk tipped a point shot from Rob Rice past Pembroke netminder Grant Robb, giving Humboldt breathing space ahead of the hard-charging Pembroke team. With the 4-2 victory and a 7-2 win over Dartmouth Fuel Kids on Saturday, the Broncos assured themselves a spot in the playoff round, which begins Thursday. In Sunday's afternoon game, Dartmouth was beaten 7-3 by Richmond Sockeyes. Against Pembroke, the Broncos opened a 3-1 first-period lead on goals by Rice, Kevin Luke and Curtis Chamberlin, but struggled through the second period. The Lumber Kings dominated the play and pulled to within one on a power-play goal by Mike Eastwood, who scored both Pembroke goals. "We were just going through the motions in the second," said Ryhorchuk, who was selected Humboldt's player of the game. "You can't do that against these teams. We were able to play like that in our league, but all the teams here are champions. If you get a two-goal lead on them, they're not going to die."

There was a rude welcoming committee awaiting the Dartmouth Fuel Kids at the Centennial Cup tournament. After a trip which lasted close to 20 hours on Friday, Humboldt Broncos gave the Fuel Kids a 7-2 butt-kicking Saturday evening. Sunday afternoon, Dartmouth turned the other cheek, just to have it booted 7-3 by Richmond Sockeyes.

first aid practices, stressed Moro, who has an industrial first aid "A ticket" and practices conventional and sports first aid at the Exhibition Park race track in Vancouver. (He has also served as a trainer for the Vancouver Whitecaps, Nanaimo Clippers Junior A Tier 2 hockey team, this year's Centennial Cup Champion Richmond Sockeyes, last year's B.C. Winter Games, and the Mac's Super Series hockey tournament for the last three years; he was also the head trainer at UBC last year). Moro said it should simply be recognized that people with conventional first aid training have limitations when i it comes to sport-related injuries. He offered as an example a soccer tournament where both regular first aid attendents and sports aiders were on duty. During the course of a match, a player went down with a twisted ankle and was attended to by both "aiders." The conventional first aid attendent recommended splinting the leg and sending the player to emergency. But the sports aider iced the injury and taped the ankle for support and the player went back into the match a little later. Ice is a key ingredient to immediate and long-term treatment of most sports-related injuries as part of the "RICE" system. That is, rest, ice, compression and elevation. Rest simply means putting the injured part at rest while ice and elevation are used together for the same purpose. Icing the injured area constricts the flow of blood and, thus, decreases the injury bleeding into the area around it. And elevating the injury similarly slows blood flow. Richmond Sockeyes, champions of the B.C. Junior Hockey League, are now Western Canadian Tier Two Junior A champions. Wednesday night in Richmond, the Sockeyes defeated Humboldt Broncos 5-4 in the seventh and deciding game of the Abbott Cup. There was a controversial ending to the series as the Broncos failed to line up for the traditional series ending handshake - probably because, to the Broncos, the series isn't over. And that's | because the two teams will run into each other again as both were already assured berths in the four-team Centennial Cup tournament, the national Tier Two Junior A championship, which begins tonight (Saturday) in Humboldt. Richmond was given a berth in the Centennial Cup tournament because there were two spots reserved for Western teams, and Humboldt already had one as host team. Other teams in the tournament will be from Pembroke, Ont., and Dartmouth, Nova Scotia. Richmond took to the ice Tuesday holding a 3-2 lead in the series, but dropped a 4-3 overtime decision to force the seventh game. The first three games of the series were played in Humboldt, with Richmond winning two of them. Humboldt evened the series with a 2-1 victory Friday night, before Richmond scored a 5-3 win Sunday night to take the series lead. Richmond now has won playoff series this spring against Nanaimo Clippers, Delta Flyers, Kelowna Packers (for the BCJHL championship), Red Deer Rustlers (for the B.C. - Alberta championship) and Humboldt. Richmond and Humboldt are favored to meet in the Centennial Cup final next weekend. Today's opening game will pit the Broncos against the Fuel Kids from Dartmouth, and tomorrow (Sunday) the Sockeyes will take on the Lumber Kings from Pembroke. The teams continue play in a roundrobin through Wednesday, with the second and third place teams meeting in a semi-final with the win-

RED DEER, Alta. (CP) - Humboldt Broncos have advanced to , the Centennial Cup western finals but they might not have a team to play against. The Saskatchewan Junior Hockey League champion Broncos dumped Manitoba Junior Hockey League champion Selkirk Steelers 9-1 last night, sweeping that best-of-seven semi 4-0. However, the B.C.-Alberta series between Red Deer Rustlers and the Richmond Sockeyes could be cancelled if fans don't behave, the Canadian Amateur Hockey Association says. The association said in a statement yesterday that unruly behavior of fans in both cities could result in the series being cancelled or moved to another city.

coach Brian de Bruyn, who resigned Jan. 13, citing an inability to properly motivate his team. General manager Graham Parsons made it clear that his choice to fill the position would be either former Rustler bench boss Wynne Dempster, or Larry Billows, a veteran coach of three tier two leagues. Later in the month, the Red Deer Central Lions Speed Skating Club played host to the Western Canada Cup, Dempster withdrew from the Rustler coaching sweepstakes, and Donna Nowochin's earned a berth to the provincial women's curling championship. February barely started when Billows made his debut as Rustler coach in a 6-5 overtime loss to Sherwood Park. As the month progressed, Jim Richardson and Irene Wardill won the Red Deer Farmers and Farmerettes bonspiels; the Flying Fathers appeared at the Arena; Nowochin was eliminated from the provincials; and the Red Deer College Queens and Kings locked up the North Division titles in the ACAC volleyball conference. March provided one highlight after another. Kurt Browning of Caroline headed to Cincinatti to compete in the world figure skating championships, the RDC Kings and Queens captured the ACAC volleyball crowns and then grabbed bronze medals at the Canadian finals, and the Kings hockey club was ousted by NAIT in the ACAC final. In addition, Bev Watson of Red Deer became the world women's, para-skiing Chiefs champion, Deer Moose laid claim to their second consecutive major bantam provincial hockey title, and the Rustlers won gut-wrenching seven-games series with the .Calgary Spurs and heavily-favored Calgary Canucks to win the AJHL South title. Rustlers created headlines in April by downing St. Albert Saints 4-2 in the AJHL final, and then taking the eventual Canadian champion Richmond Sockeyes to seven games in the Western Centennial Cup semifinal. The contests at the Arena with the Sockeyes were particularly memorable, as Red Deer fans (?) pelted the ice (and the Richmond players) with debris in the first two games before settling down when the CAHA threatened to move the series. Rustlers led 3-2 at one point, but eventually succumbed to the bigger and more talented Richmond crew. Other highlights in April. Volleyball and basketball players Bob Bragg and Mary Lynn Lang were named the RDC male and female athletes of the year, the Moose Chiefs placed third at the Western Canada bantam finals, and Tom Eirikson of Longview was crowned allaround champ of the Red Deer Westerner Silver Buckle Rodeo. As April turned into May, Rimbey native Rod Palm continued his winning ways in the Advocate Road Race, in which saw Sandy Last of Calgary placed first in the women's division. A week later, with Red Deer Riggers preparing for the start of the Foothills Baseball League season, playing-manager Terry Hendley accepted the Advocate award as Red Deer's Athlete of the Year. Dr. Bob Carter was also honored, winning the Alberta Energy Company-AdvocateAlberta Sports Council coach of the year laurels. Red Deer's native were featured in June. Notes and quotes from around the B.C. Junior Hockey League... •Nanaimo Clippers certainly won't have m much of a home ice advantage when Vernon Lakers visit Civic Arena Friday night. The highly rated Lakers lead the B.C. Junior Hockey League with an 11-3 mark away from home and are riding an 11-game road winning streak. Laker general manager Mel Lis has a theory on why the Lakers are so tough on the road. "Most arenas are bigger than our own and since we skate so well, we do better in those rinks," said Lis. "Also the pressure of friends and relatives isn't with them on the road. think the guys are more together on our away games. They really don't talk enough hockey at home, whereas on the road we're closer to one another in that bus atmosphere so we tend to talk and think more about hockey while we're on the road." • A few weeks ago, Brian 'McFarlane had been traded to Kelowna Packers, only to discover he enjoyed life more as a Richmond Sockeye. from the game with 51 seconds left. But in the third stanza it was all Richmond. The Sockeyes held Delta to only seven shots in the final 20 minutes and got a goal each from Brian Kozak and Bill Hardy to jump into a 5-4 lead. But the Flyers weren't finished yet as Momotani squirted through the Sockeyes' defence and scored at 17:53 to send the game into overtime. At the 4:37 mark of the extra session, Paul Rutherford banged in a rebound and the Sockeyes were the champions again. Richmond head coach Orland Kurtenbach said he had a feeling heading into the game that it would be the final one of the series. "When you get up three games to nothing you sort of feel the fourth game is forthcoming," said Kurtenbach, adding that the score would have a lot higher if it wasn't for Delta's superb goaltending. "Chris had a helluva game," Kurtenbach said. "'He made just some fantastic saves.' Hopper said when any team wins four straight games, you know it wasn't a fluke. "They won because they were the better team and they were more physical," he said. But immediately following the game, Hopper was already looking forward to next season. "I've been putting together a list of players to go after and we're going to put together a very powerful hockey club.'

One has to pity poor Chris Dickson. Dickson, goaltender of Delta Flyers, was spectacular in stopping 49 shots, but his squad still dropped a 6-5 sudden-death overtime decision to the Richmond Sockeyes and was eliminated from the B.C. Junior Hockey League playoffs on Saturday at Sungod Arena. The Sockeyes took the best-ofseven Coastal Division final series in four straight games and will go on to meet Kelowna Packers, the Interior Division champs, for the league championship. "I didn't expect them to handle us as easily as they did," said a disgruntled Flyers' head coach Rick Hopper following the final game. "I would have liked to have been a lot more competitive against them.'

membership. Sometimes, in pursuit of truth, party lines get blurred. Ice thrills An early week bouquet is due the Red Deer Rustlers. The Rustlers are not the best team in the West • the Richmond Sockeyes and Humboldt Broncos will fight for that honor. But Red Deer's reborn Alberta Junior Hockey League team might be the best loved. They earned the community's support by overcoming seemingly insurmountable odds, by playing entertaining hockey, and by being more than a little brash. (The fans, mistakenly, thought throwing trash would show that they, too, were brash - but that's another story.) The Rustlers and their fans proved a couple of points: junior hockey can still draw here and a team can be operated successfully on a tight budget, without dragging down investors. They kept this town enthralled for a wild month of playoff hockey. And they may just have sealed their own fate, by showing once and for all that Red Deer could support a major junior hockey franchise if given the chance. Other voices Never underestimate the staying power of a bad idea, especially one that serves a political cause. Take "comparable worth" - or pay equity, as it has become known the belief that jobs can be objectively judged for their social value. The drive to establish pay equity by governmental action has slipped from the front pages lately, but its supporters have hardly abandoned their fight. The latest sign of the movement's vitality is a report by the National Committee on Pay Equity, whose board of directors includes such venerable organizations as the League of Women Voters, the American Library Association and the YWCA. Funded by the Ford Foundation, the report claims to be the first to confirm "what has long been suspected by pay equity advocates: that race, ethnicity and sex are all significant factors in setting pay." Actually, the report proves no such thing. It merely documents a well known fact: Women and minorities tend to be concentrated in certain jobs that tend to pay less than other jobs. The report's authors believe this pattern clinches the case for widespread discrimination. If such bias is so widespread, they suggest, what's the alternative to wholesale adjustment of wage scales? As imperfect as the present system may be in allocating income, it is far more responsive than the bureaucratic solution favored by the pay-equity forces. - An editorial from Rocky Mountain News, Denver.

Humboldt Broncos won a place in the final of the Centennial Cup Junior A hockey championship tournament Wednesday night with a 6-1 victory over the Richmond Sockeyes. The losers. with a round-robin record of 2-1, will meet the Pembroke Lumber Kings in tonight's semifinal. The final will be played Saturday night. Bill McDougall and Garnett Kazuik led Humboldt with two goals apiece, while Duncon Ryhorchuk and Curtis Chamberlin had single goals. The win gave Humboldt, from Saskatchewan. a 3-0 record in the round-robin portion of the tournament. Stan Czenczek scored the Richmond goal. The two teams were tied 1-1 after the first period but Humboldt jumped ahead to 4-1 after the second. The Broncos outshot the British Columbia champion Sockeyes 37-19 in a scrappy game that saw Humboldt take 15 of 27 minors. Both teams took two five-minute majors and two game misconducts. Humbolt also picked up two 10-minute misconducts.

The Broncos outshot the British Columbia champion Sockeyes 37-19 in a scrappy game that saw Humboldt take 15 of 27 minors. Both teams took two five-minute majors and two game misconducts. Humbolt also picked up two 10-minute misconducts. The Lumber Kings, from Ontario, finished with a 1-2 record in the round-robin section of the tournament. The fourth team involved, the Dartmouth Fuel Kids from Nova Scotia, lost all three of its games.

Rustlers honored Red Deer Rustlers, who came within one game of a berth in the Centennial Cup final, have been named the Carling O'Keefe-Radio 7 Central Alberta athletes of the month for April. Rustlers won the Alberta Junior Hockey League crown and took the Richmond Sockeyes to seven games in the Western Canada Centennial Cup semifinals. Bev Meyer, who won the Alberta five-pin bowling championship, and the Red Deer Moose Chiefs, who won the Alberta bantam hockey title Meanwhile, a pair of Rustler helpers trainer Terry McQueen, and promotions man Darryl Kuntz - were selected to share volunteer of the month honors.

The Ontario champions will meet the loser of today's final round-robin game between the Humboldt Broncos of Saskatchewan and the Richmond Sockeyes of British Columbia, who both have 2-0 records in the tournament. The semifinal game will be played Thursday. Bruce Coles led Pembroke to its first victory after two losses with two goals and three assists. Chris Clarke and Peter White each had two goals for the Lumber Kings, while Scott Mohns and Mike Eastwood had singles. Jasmin Breton, Steve Brown, Ron Petrie and Brian King scored for Dartmouth, the Nova Scotia representatives who lost Minor baseball North Star Sports rode the arm, and bat, of Terrill Trautman to a 9-4 win over McAuley's Electric in Red Deer senior 'A' Little League play Tuesday. Trautman and Todd Opatril each rapped a pair of singles for North Star Sports while Jerett Terlesky had two singles and Shane Opp a single and a double for McAuley's Electric. Ball hockey draw Bemoco and the Dairy Queen played to a 3-3 tie in Red Deer Ball Hockey League play Tuesday. Dan Bell, Dan Dobler and Scott Wnek shared the Bemoco scoring while Jeff Gosselin, Darren Tymchyshyn and Gordon Pruden replied for the Dairty Queen. TOP JUNIOR NAMED Cindy Laszlo was named overall champion of the weekend Archers Nook annual junior tournament. Laszlo topped the 10- 12 age class. joining Heidi Schmid (four-six). Eagles nip Sockeyes By The Canadian Press Neil Eisenhut scored twice to lead Langley Eagles to a 5-4 win over Richmond Sockeyes in B.C. Junior Hockey League action Friday night. In the other games, Kelowna Packers crushed Abbotsford Falcons 10-1, Penticton Knights downed Summerland Buckaroos 6.4 and Vernon Lakers thrashed 1 Merritt Warriors 13-4. In Langley, single goals were scored by Rick Graham, Wes Gentles and Verne Kinley. Tony Bobbitt scored twice for Richmond with singles from Bill Hardy and Mike Clairinbull. Jamie Stewart stopped 46 shots for Langley, while Richmond netminder Glen Pencer had 27 saves. In Vernon, the Lakers scored seven unanswered goals in t the third period en route to their 13-4 win over the Merritt Warriors. One line did most of the damage for the Lakers with a total of 17 points. Center Kevin Scott scored five goals and added three assists while right winger Doug Spooner scored four times and left winger Stacey Hartnell picked up five assists. Lakers' Campbell Blair, who is chasing a league record for career assists, set up three goals to give him 153 career assists - two less than the career mark held by Ian Kidd, formerly of Penticton Knights. -In Kelowna, four players scored two goals apiece to lead the Packers. Mike•Murinko, Pat Mangold, Greg Andrusak a and Bruce Bianchini scored two goals each, while singles came from Jay Luknowsky and Kevin Harter. Brad Heschuk led the Knights with two goals, including the winner. Penticton's singles came from Sean Hartnell, Jackson Penney, Jan' Zeidel and Dowell Frantz.

night, but it was rookie Todd Finner who touched. off a key 9-7 B.C. Junior Hockey League victory Delta Flyers. Finner bagged his 15th and 16th goals and contributed three assists before leaving the game after an entertaining scrap with Jeff Nottingham at the end of the second frame. The Clippers, 17-15-1, are now just three points behind third place Delta (19-14) in the Coastal Division. And while it wasn't classic playoff-style hockey, it was what Clipper coach Rich Dutka expected as both teams were little sloppy in opening the New Year. "It's what we need to get us rolling, said Dutka. "You get some your troops back after Christmas and they've been off so long, their timing's going to be off. "I thought we excecuted some well practiced breakouts and I thought we dominated them evenstrength. Their D-men didn't want too much to do muche puck. They didn't have too time to do much with the puck." The Clippers, who face first place Richmond Sockeyes tonight (8 o'clock, Civic Arena), are now 2-3 against Delta. "That wasn't one of Delta's better games, but always play. well against that club. We just have bad luck against them in their building." Nanaimo counted three goals on a powerplay Dutka spent 30 minutes perfecting in Friday morning's workout. "You have to get your powerplay working because there many penalties called in this league," he said. "We're leading the Coastal Division in powerplays so the guys are realizing that what we practice will work in game situations." Darren Biggs drew first star ratings ahead of Finner as he returned to the lineup by producing two goals and the same number of assists. Mike Knapton, back from suspension, Craig Frankford, Corky Parkes and Kent Cookman rounded out Nanaimo's scoring. Cookman's goal was rather amusing. Known better for his checking than his shooting, Cookman missed the emtpy Delta

Cup opener HUMBOLDT, Sask. (CP) - The Humboldt Broncos scored twice i in an overtime period to beat the Richmond Sockeyes 5-3 Monday night in the opening game of the -of -seven Centennial Cup Western Canada junior hockey final. Curtis Chamberlain had three goals, including the winner, for the Saskatchewan champion Broncos. Brad Bergen and Bill McDougall had the other Humboldt markers. The Sockeyes, the British Columbia champions, had goals from Bryon Moller, Matt Hervey and Jason Phillips. Richmond led 2-1 after the second period and the teams were tied at 3-3 after regulation time. Regardless of how the teams finish, both will take part in the national tier two championship which starts May 1 in Humbolt.

Sockeyes oust Lumber Kings HUMBOLDT, Sask. (CP) The Richmond Sockeyes whipped the Pembroke Lumber Kings 9-3 in a semifinal clash Thursday night to advance to the final game in the Centennial Cup Junior A hockey championship tournament. The Sockeyes, from British Columbia, will meet the Saskatchewan champion Humboldt Broncos in the sudden-death final Saturday night. The Lumber Kings, the Ontario champions who. had a 1-2 record in the round-robin, got goals from Dave Van Hoof, George Dupont and Chris Clarke.

By GREG MEACHEM of The Advocate There were tears, but there was no shame in the Red Deer Rustlers' dressing room Thursday night. The Rustlers had just dropped a onesided. 9-1 decision to the Richmond Sockeyes in the seventh and final game of the Western Canada Centennial Cup semifinal series.

Falcons continue winning ways For the first time in the 1987-88 B.C. Junior Hockey League season, Abbotsford Falcons will be going after a third straight victory when they play host to defending Centennial Cup champion Richmond Sockeyes tonight (Saturday) at 8 p.m. at MSA Arena. ABBOTS A a FALCONS Abbotsford, who won two of three games last week on a road swing through the Okanagan, looked impressive Tuesday night at MSA when they stopped Chilliwack Eagles 7-3 behind the two-goal one-assist performance of smooth-skating centreman Blaine Moore. The win, Abbotsford's third in their past four games, pulled the Falcons to within one point of the (continued on page 4)

By The Canadian Press The Richmond Sockeyes and Juan De Fuca. Whalers played to arr 8-8 overtime tie in a penalty-filled B.C. Junior Hockey League game Wednesday night in Richmond. The Sockeyes. appeared to have the game in hand until a string of penalties left them playing shorthanded for the last 14 minutes of the third period. The Whalers fired three power play goals to tie the game. Jason Talo got two goals for Richmond, while Mauro Padula, Mario Desjardins, Greg Hadden, Keith Fair, Rob Clarke and Kirk Phare added singles.

HUMBOLDT, Sask. - Jason Phillips scored three goals to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to the Centennial Cup Tier Two junior A hockey championship Saturday night with a 5-2 win over the Humboldt Broncos. But the Sockeyes had to fall back on an outstanding performance in goal by Frank Romeo, who stood up to 46 Humboldt shots while his teammates managed only 23 at the Broncos net. Romeo was named most valuable player in the four-team, roundrobin series. British Columbia's Sockeyes skated to period leads of 2-0 and 2-1 before i Paul Rutherford scored the winning goal in the third. Dave Tomlinson also scored for Richmond, which took seven of the game's 12 minor penalties. Curtis Chamberlin and Kevin Luke scored for the Saskatchewan champion Broncos. 000 SHERBROOKE Jody Gage notched two goals Saturday to lift the Rochester Americans to a 43 comeback win over the Sherbrooke Canadiens in the second game of the American Hockey League final. Rochester's win evened the series at one game each and ended Canadiens' goaltender Vincent Riendeau's winning streak at home at 23 games. The loss was the Canadiens' first at home since March 29. Rochester scored first at 5:51 of the opening period when Gage tipped a shot past a screened Riendeau.

Humboldt gets even RICHMOND, B.C. (CP) Mike Gaber scored at 3:31 of overtime Tuesday to give the Humboldt Broncos a 4-3 victory over the Richmond Sockeyes and new life in their Centennial Cup playoff series. The victory forced a seventh and deciding game in the Western Canada final here tonight. Bill McDougall led the Broncos with two goals and Curtis Chamberlin was the other marksman for Humboldt which trailed 1-0 at the end of the first period and was tied 2-2 entering the third period. Paul Rutherford led the Sockeyes with two goals while Dave Tomlinson added the other goal for Richmond which was outshot 46-45. Humboldt got spectacular netminding from Bruce Hoffort, who made 42 saves. Punch resigns Bruins' post NEW WESTMINSTER, B.C. (CP) - Ernie (Punch) McLean has resigned as head coach of the New Westminster Bruins of the Western Hockey League. McLean needs to spend more time with his mining interests in northern British Columbia, general manager John Olver said, adding McLean will remain with the club as a director. Olver said no decision has been made on a replacement. McLean took the Bruins to four consecutive Memorial Cup tournaments starting in 1975 and New Westminster won the Canadian title in 1977 and 1978. After selling his interest in the WHL team five years ago, McLean returned as coach in November, 1985. New Westminster finished fifth in the Western Division this season and missed the playoffs.

HUMBOLDT, Sask. (CP) Humboldt Broncos beat Pembroke Lumber Kings 42 Sunday night to take an early lead in the round-robin Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship. Humboldt also won the tournament opener, beating Dartmouth Fuel Kids 7- 2 Saturday night. The Fuel Kids, crowd favorites in the early going, fell 7-3 to Richmond Sockeyes in the only other game Sunday. The four -team tournament ends Saturday. The hometown Broncos built a 3-1 lead in the first period and hung on for the win after the Lumber Kings battled back to make it 3-2 in the second. Kevin Luke, Rob Rice, Duncon Ryborchuk and Curtis Chamberlin, with . the winner at 16:09 of the first period, scored for the Broncos. Mike Eastwood got both goals for the Ontario team. Humboldt outshot Pembroke 42-32 and took 14 of penalties. Richmond 7 Dartmouth3 In the earlier game, Jason Phillips scored three goals to lead Richmond Sockeyes to their easy win over Dartmouth. Bryon Moller, Steve Jaques, Dave Tomlinson and Matt Hervey also scored for the Sockeyes, who piled up 44 shots against Dartmouth's 29. Richmond zipped to a 3-1 lead in the first period and built the margin to 6-2 in the second. Jerry Scott had two goals and Brian King had one for the Nova Scotia team. The Sockeyes took 11 of 17 minors and a major, and both teams had a misconduct. !. Richmond plays Pembroke in the only game today. 7,

Fighting fans threaten series between juniors RED DEER, Alta. (CP) - The junior hockey series between the Red Deer Rustlers and the Richmond Sockeyes could be cancelled fans don't behave, the Canadian Amateur Hockey Association says. The CAHA said in a statement yesterday that unruly behavior of fans in both cities could result in the series being cancelled or moved to another city. One man was arrested during Sunday's game here, won 7-1 by the Rustlers. The best-of-seven between the Alberta and British Columbia junior champions is tied 2-2. Sunday's game was delayed 30 minutes as fights broke out on and off the ice. Last week in Richmond, two Red Deer players were suspended for becoming involved in a postgame altercation with fans and the Sockeyes were fined $500 by the CAHA for failing to provide proper security.

Hockey TORONTO Referee Kerry Fraser, who was involved in a controversial call in a National Hockey League playoff game between Montreal and Quebec, won't work any more games this season, says Jim Gregory, the NHL's executive director of hockey operations. Fraser was among two referees dropped as the NHL pared down to six for the rest of the playoffs. L NEW YORK - The NHL's board of governors voted yesterday t to form a committee to study public ownership of franchises. It. was the second loss in two days for Dartmouth. The Nova Scotians opened the week-long, roundrobin tournament Saturday with a 7-2 defeat at the hands of the host Humboldt Broncos. paragraphs.text: Hockey degraded On Sunday, April 12, I attended the Rustlers vs Richmond Sockeyes gäme and was disappointed in the fans, refereeing and coaching. First, the fans were throwing garbage on the ice, raw fish for example, which delayed the game. I thought Red Deer citizens had more respect for the other team. Second, the coaches appeared to have lost control of their players. In the second period there were about six to eight fights, which shows the unsportsmanship of both teams. On the other hand, in the sport of football, if a player is fighting he IS out of the game immediately. I suggest that a player who is involved fighting should be suspended. Third. the coaches must begin to implement more positive player attitudes to insure that life injuries do not occur. Injuries may ruin the futures of great lavers. The true sport of hockey is only being degraded by that style of play on Sunday. J.K. Miller Red Deer

In the case of the Red Deer Rustlers, the disappointment was all too real and a touch bitter after dropping the Alberta-B.C. junior hockey playoff series to the Richmond Sockeyes Thursday. Placed in proper context, however, Red Deer's season must be considered a winner. Not only for the team but for junior hockey here. Crowds were always around 1,700 or better in the final 11 home playoff games which allowed management to balance the ledger. On the ice the Rustlers showed junior hockey has a place here. Off the ice the vast majority of fans showed they want it. The less said about the clowns, who nearly had the series cancelled after game four, the better. The true fans, to a large extent, helped the club. Their support was applauded by management. Their support I'm sure, spurred the club on when the going was rough or a series hung in the balance. And how many of those do we remember so well? The Rustlers pulled two stunning upsets by knocking off the favored Calgary Canucks in seven games and the St. Albert Saints in six to win the Alberta championship.

The mind was willing, but the legs wouldn't cooperate. Red Deer Rustlers, playing their 27th post-season game in 47 days, ran out of gas Thursday at the Arena. They were no match for the Richmond Sockeyes, who claimed a convincing 9-1 victory in the seventh and deciding game of the western Centennial Cup tier two junior hockey semifinal series. "There were too many (playoff) games in too few nights. It was a gruelling schedule and it took its toll," Rustler coach Larry Billows said sadly. "'The guys wanted it, but there was no intensity tonight. They just had nothing left." The Sockeyes, bigger and more talented than the Alberta Junior Hockey League champion Rustlers, blew open a scoreless game with five unanswered second-period goals. From there, it was simply a matter of if, and when, Red Deer would ruin goaltender Jamie Stewart's shutout. Pete deGraaf notched the lone Red Deer goal at 13:14 of the third period, deflecting Greg Pulliam's point shot into the Richmond goal with the Rustlers on a power play. The single goal was small consolation for the Rustlers, who dropped the first two games in Richmond, rallied to win three in a row at the Arena, then lost the final two. "I thought we were the better club the last three games," said a relaxed Orland Kurtenbach, the Sockeyes' general managercoach. "Certainly we deserved to win the last two, and the (7-5 Red Deer win) overtime game (Tuesday) could have gone either way. "I'm proud of these guys. They worked extremely hard." Equally as proud of his charges was Billows, who moved behind the Red Deer bench Feb. 4, just one month before the playoffs started. "I'm very happy with the way these guys played for me," he stressed. "I've never coached a team with so much character. There's a lot of heart in this dressing room." Rustler captain Pulliam agreed. "I'm just proud to be a part of this team," said the 20-year-old defenceman, who played the best hockey of his career along the playoff trail. "This team never quit, we never saw the end coming. "It seemed that there was always a light at the end of the tunnel. Even in the third period tonight, the light was still there." But it flickered, and eventually was snuffed out by the British Columbia champion Sockeyes, who got three goals from Dave Tomlinson and singles from Rob Clarke, Mike Claringbull, Jason Phillips, Jim Gunn,

By MONTE STEWART Herald-Tribune staff QUESNEL - Tom Marsh paused momentarily, looked around the dressing room and, eyeing Millionaires' netminder Scooter Henson, asked rhetorically: "Hey, Scoot, it is a little different, eh?" The Quesnel Millionaires had just defeated Grande Prairie North Stars 4-2 in the decisive game of the Peace Cariboo Junior Hockey League championship series Saturday night. The victory before a packed house at the Civic Arena culminated a rags to riches story for the Millionaires, who only a year ago were the doormats of the PCJHL. The Millionaires clinched the best-of-seven PCJHL championship series in five games. Friday, they thrashed the North Stars 8-2, setting the stage for Saturday's finale. "These guys have worked so hard all year long," said Marsh, the 26-year-old coach who, in his first full season of coaching at any level, lifted a team that won only six games last year out of the gutter and into the caviar set. Quesnel will now face B.C. Junior Hockey League champion Richmond Sockeyes in first round Centennial Cup play. Although Joe Dragon knocked in Murray Toews' goalmouth pass with 5:02 left in the third period to narrow the North Stars' deficit to 4-2 in the fightfilled clincher. But, for all intents and purposes, the game was over before the end of the middle frame. The Millionaires broke open a thrifty first period with three unanswered second- period markers. Don Doucette, Marty Peters and Warren Wurzer staked Quesnel to a 4-1 lead that the North Stars, try as they may, could not overcome. "We didn't have what it took to win the whole cake," said Grande Prairie captain Toews, whose assist extended his consecutive points streak to 61 games, regular season and playoffs combined. "'They had 21 guys that wanted to win that Peace Cariboo title." The contest was marred by a brawl with 6:14 remaining in the second period. The North Stars -

games, if required, are slated for April 1 in St. Albert, April 3 in Red Deer and April 4 in St. Albert The AJHL winner will meet, in all likelihood, Richmond Sockeyes . Richmond won the BCJHL title Tuesday, sweeping Kelowna Packers in a best-of-nine series. . . Sockeyes are highly favored to defeat the Peace-Caribou winner . Quesnel leads Grande Prairie 2-0 in that final Humboldt and Lloydminster will tangle for the SJHL crown, while Winnipeg leads Selkirk 1-0 in the MJHL championship series.

Maybe we shouldn't have traded him. I opined in this space a few weeks back that Langley Eagles coach and g.m. Muzz MacPherson made a wise move when he traded scoring ace Bryan Moller to the Richmond Sockeyes. But trust a sports journalist to be proved wrong. Seems Bryan (not Byron as quoted in one Canadian Press wire story recently) has been contributing greatly to the Junior A Sockeyes climb to glory in amateur hockey ranks. On Monday, Moller scored a goal in Richmond's 5-3 losing cause to the Humboldt Broncos in the first game of the best-ofseven Centennial Cup final to decide the Western Canadian champion. Though Richmond lost, Moller has been consistently good and may help, the 'Eyes turnaround this first spell of bad luck in an otherwise sparkling season. Game two in the series took place yesterday night (Tuesday) but passed press deadline.

RICHMOND, B.C. (CP) - The Red Deer Rustlers paid a heavy price for penalties Wednesday night as the Richmond Sockeyes fired four powerplay goals in the third period for a 6-4 Centennial Cup playoff victory. However, the comeback victory that gave Richmond a 2-0 lead in the best-of-seven Western semifinal was spoiled by a post -game incident involving a thrown stick and a Red Deer player who stormed into the stands. The third and fourth games in the rough Tier II junior hockey series will be played Saturday and Sunday in Red Deer, Alta. An elderly woman was struck by a Red Deer stick thrown into the crowd. She received a cut and medical aid while the Rustlers' Brad Wilson took a match penalty. Teammate Rick Anderson, who climbed over the glass behind the Red Deer bench and nearly came to blows with a fan, received a gross misconduct. Red Deer coach Larry Billows, who attempted to intercede between Anderson and the fan, said spectators spat on his team and there was not enough security in the Richmond Arena. The Alberta coach had no real explanation for his team's collapse.

by Alan Watson It's getting to be that time for the Abbotsford Falcons. Thanks to the ¿Juan de Fuca Whalers, who enjoyed a four-win-one-loss week - one of those victories coming at the expense of the Falcons Abbotsford now find themselves looking quite a ways up to the the fourth and final playoff position in the B.C. Junior Hockey League's Coastal Division. As of Saturday, Nov. 29, the Falcons were only four points back of the Whalers. As of Sunday, Dec. 6, Abbotsford were 10 points behind the Whalers, who still have three games in hand. Falcons have lost a number of onegoal games this season, and were victimized again Friday night in Nanaimo, when the Whalers escaped with an 8-7 triumph in overtime. Saturday at MSA Arena, the Falcons controlled the final two periods against the second -place Richmond Sockeyes, received a sensational goaltending performance from rookie John Bukowski and took an exciting 3-2 win to keep their playoff hopes -alives. Abbotsford have 21 games left in the regular-season. Tuesday night at MSA Arena, the Whalers played a disciplined road game and skated away with a crucial 4-2 victory. Falcon coach Dave Cherniwchan was in a jovial mood following the big win over Richmond, the Falcons' first taste of victory against the defending Centennial Cup champions this season.
day night, keeping the Richmond Sockeyes literally on

After being burned in a couple of B.C. Junior Hockey League deals this season, Abbotsford Falcon general manager Gary McCaskill is moving slowly towards finalizing a deal with the Coastal Division-leading Delta Flyers. The transaction is part-way completed, yet may not be finalized, McCaskill said on Monday. "There is a tentative deal in the works that could see Damien Holland, become an Abbotsford Falcon," said McCaskill. Holland, one of the premier goaltenders in the league, was in Abbotsford Saturday before the Falcons took to the ice against Richmond Sockeyes, and was ready to play, but McCaskill and Falcon owner Byron Hitchens decided at the last minute to hold off for a while before signing him to a playing card. "I got cold feet on the deal and I really can't say I know why," said McCaskill. "'We decided at the last minute not to card him. It was just a gut feeling. "I just got burned on the Shawn Crowthers' deal, and we've lost other guys we've traded for (Paul Thompson, Brent Jones). I pick up a couple players, and bang, they're gone. I want anybody we deal for to come in and want to be an Abbotsford Falcon. I didn't get that feeling from L Damien Holland right away. But, he's coming to practice and we'll have to see." If the deal with Delta for Holland goes through, it could involve as many as three Flyers coming to Abbotsford in exchange for one Falcon, that one obviously having to be a front-line player.
