By ELLIOTT PAP San Sports Reporter HUMBOLDT, Sask. - Dressed in their black uniforms, Richmond Sockeyes look every bit the villains they are turning out to be in this year's Centennial Cup tournament. The fans in Humboldt cheer against them and the referees don't seem to appreciate their somewhat rugged style. So all coach Orland Kurtenbach's boys do is win, win, win. The 'Eyes polished off Ontario champion Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-1 Monday night to go 2-0 in the round-robin and clinch a semifinal berth in the national junior A hockey championships. Richmond has today off and will meet the host Humboldt Broncos, who are also 2-0, Wednesday. At stake will be a bye into Saturday's final, while the loser will meet either Pembroke or Atlantic champion Dartmouth in Thursday's semi-final. Tonight's match sees 0-2 Pembroke and 0-2 Dartmouth squaring off for the remaining playoff spot. The Sockeyes were too much for the Lumber Kings last night despite spending half the game in the penalty box. Referee Wes Smith of Saskatoon called all the borderline stuff - with Richmond committing most of it - and that left Kurtenbach in a state of near apoplexy. "All I'm asking for is a fair shake from the refereeing," said Kurten-

THE MAN who checked in at the registration desk at the downtown Vancouver hotel asked if he could get the Canadiens-Nordiques game on his television set. The clerk replied in the negative. "Well, then maybe I'll take in the RichmondHumboldt game. It should be a heck'uva series," he commented. He knew the teams would be returning to Humboldt to play for the Centennial Cup and hoped to take in at least one of the games. I happened to be on holidays at the time and the next day planned to be in Kelowna. At Arby's, I ran into big Gene McWillie. I didn't realize he was in Kelowna with the Canadian men's national softball team preparing for the Pan-American Games later this summer and the World Softball championship to be held in Saskatoon in 1988. So, as well as taking in the doubleheader that night, I also got a chance for a short interview with the team's manager, Dave Pearce of Regina.

team played in the national finals. In Humboldt, the Broncos have a good shot at winning the Centennial Cup. In Medicine Hat, the Blades came closer than they have in years at a shot at the Memorial Cup. The Hilltops once again played in the Prairie Junior Football Conference finals. Reigning Canadian five-pin champ Blair Pizzey of Saskatoon won the $25,000-CBC championship in Toronto. Blaine Sands of Tisdale won a gold medal in the Canadian senior boxing championships in New Brunswick. In less than two weeks, eight Saskatoon athletes will be competing in the Canadian women's gymnastics events in 1 Winnipeg. These athletes have put Saskatchewan on the map. And during the next three years, Saskatoon will be the place on the map that will be circled.

"That third goal put the crimpers on it," said Sockeye coach Orland Kurtenbach. "I told them before the third period that we've had a series of mountains to climb: some of them were smaller, but they've all been tough to climb. We had one last one to go in the third period and they haven't failed me all year. In the stress situations, they've come through like gangbusters." While Kurtenbach celebrated the championship in his first year with Richmond, Lynch, who could move on to major. junior hockey next season, was left to reflect on his record with the Broncos. "We're 156-29 in the last two years," said Lynch, "but couldn't win the game we wanted to win. It's bitter. "But we've started something and we've got something to build on here. We played hard to the end of the game, and the guys who are coming back will know what it takes to get to the Centennial Cup and they'll make sure the rookies coming in will learn what it takes. You build on that. It's bitter and disappointing, but the future certainly isn't bleak for this organization. If anything, it's extremely bright." Tournament tidbits: Saturday's final drew a standing-room-only crowd of around 2,400. • Richmond and Humboldt played nine times in three weeks, with the Sockeyes winning five times and each team scoring 30 goals. "If we played them again tomorrow, we'd win and the series would continue,' said Lynch.. . Richmond took seven of 12 minor penalties. . Pembroke . Lumber Kings, who lost to Richmond in the semi-final, will play host to next year's Cen-
Sockeyes win Centennial al Cup

HUMBOLDT - Frank Romeo looked like a walking trophy shop as he ambled into the champagne war going on in the Richmond Sockeyes' dressing g room following their 5-2 Centennial Cup-clinching win over Humboldt Broncos at the Uniplex on Saturday. With a plaque for MVP of the final game under one arm, a trophy as tournament MVP under the other, and the Centennial Cup firmly in his grasp, Romeo surveyed the scene with wide eyes, saying with a grin: "This is unbelievable. I'm the happiest guy in the world. Winning the Cup, being MVP, it's like Christmas all over again." If this was the holiday season, the Humboldt shooters would be calling the Sockeye Richmond 46-23, but stingy Romeo was netminder Scrooge. The, Broncos outshot giving away nothing. It was the third time Romeo was named player of the game during the tournament, winning the award in each of his starts. "I said to him when we were shaking hands, 'Frank, you know you really played over your head this tournament and that's what you've got to do to be great,' " said a disappointed Bernie Lynch, coach of the Broncos. "I told him he came up really big and played great in the tournament and that's the sign of a great hockey player. That's what you've got to do to be a champion." Richmond also received an -winning performance from Jason 1 Phillips, who was selected the tournament's most gentlemanly player. The speedy winger scored

Sockeyes gain final in Centennial Cup

HUMBOLDT, Sask. - The Richmond Sockeyes whipped the Pembroke Lumber Kings 9-3 Thursday to advance to Saturday's Centennial Cup Tier II Junior A hockey final against the Humboldt Broncos. Richmond scorers were Dean Rutledge, Steve Jaques, Matt Hervey, Brian Kozak, Paul Rutherford, Jason Phillips, Tony Bobbitt, Mike McCormick and Bill Hardy.

to attend the 60th anniversary fastball reunion here Aug. 28, 29 and 30. . .Rollie also informs that former Sault Ste. Marie hockey star Jim Farelli is doing a fine job of coaching the Pembroke Lumber Kings, who are on the trail of the Centennial Cup. .Jim is a nephew of former NHL referee Lou Farelli of North Bay.. Speaking of that fastball reunion, if Tony Poeta's sporting goods store looks more like a post office lately, it's because he's been in charge of mailing out invitations to the big get-together. .Replies are already starting to come in. . Former North Bay athlete Bus Wall phones from the Sault to correct me on a hockey forward line on which he played. . .His linemates were Adam (Perly) Finnigan and Rolly Benoit. did play with Don Richmond and Bill Ringler," says Bus, "but that was in bantam hockey.' . .The other line was a junior trio. .Wall also plans to attend the fastball reunion. .He also asked this writer to pass on regards to Dr. Stan Brown from Curly Keenan of the Sault. . .Keenan remembers when Stan played for the 1924 Allan Cup-wining Sault Greyhounds.. Sockeyes the men in black in Centennial Cup tourney

By ELLIOTT PAP Sun Sports Reporter HUMBOLDT, Sask. - Dressed in their black uniforms, Richmond Sockeyes look every bit the villains they are turning out to be in this year's Centennial Cup tournament. The fans in Humboldt cheer against them and the referees don't seem to appreciate their somewhat rugged style. So all coach Orland Kurtenbach's boys do is win, win, win. The 'Eyes polished off Ontario champion Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-1 Monday night to go 2-0 in the round-robin and clinch a semifinal berth in the national junior A hockey championships. Richmond has today o off and will meet the host Humboldt Broncos, who are also 2-0, Wednesday. At stake will be a bye into Saturday's final, while the loser will meet either Pembroke or Atlantic champion Dartmouth in Thursday's semi-final. Tonight's match sees 0-2 Pembroke and 0-2 Dartmouth squaring off for the remaining playoff spot. The Sockeyes were too much for the Lumber Kings last night despite spending half the game in the penalty box. Referee Wes Smith of Saskatoon called all the borderline stuff - with Richmond committing most of it and that left Kurten- bach in a state of near apoplexy. "All I'm asking for is a fair shake from the refereeing," said Kurten-

read sports sections of the major daily newspapers. At least one newspaper report suggested the story was the broken Can- in an attempt to demoralize adiens in their series against the Nordiques. EAST RUTHERFORD, N.J. (AP) Rumors that Providence College would be losing someone from its athletic department to the New York metropolitan area turned out to be on target - almost. However, the move wasn't the one everyone expected. Rick Pitino remained the Friars' head basketball coach. It was athletic director Lou Lamoriello who said goodbye Thursday to Providence and became president of the New Jersey Devils. No contract terms were disclosed. Lamoriello, 44 replaces Bob Butera, who resigned last Friday but remained with the NHL club as a consultant. •... OTTAWA (CP) - With four finalists for the tier two Centennial Cup junior hockey final decided, the governing Canadian Amateur Hockey Association announced Thursday the playoff schedule for the round-robin series in Humboldt, Sask. Starting Saturday, Atlantic champion Dartmouth, N.S., Fuel Kids open against the host Humboldt. Cup matchups announced The Canadian Amateur Hockey Association announced the playoff matchups for the tier two Centennial Cup junior hockey finals yesterday. The Atlantic-champion Dartmouth Fuel Kids open against the home club from Humboldt, Sask. tomorrow. The Richmond B. C. Sockeyes play Dartmouth Sunday while Humboldt meets the Lumber Kings from Pembroke, Ont. The roundrobin concludes with Pembroke against Richmond Monday, Dartmouth versus Pembroke Tuesday and Richmond meeting Humboldt Wednesday.

HUMBOLDT, Sask. - Pembroke Lumber Kings, on the strength of five power play goals, trounced Dartmouth Fuel Kids 8-4 Tuesday night to advance to the Centennial Cup playoff round. The Ontario champions finished the round-robin portion of the national Junior A hockey championships at 1-2 while Dartmouth was winless at 0-3. Pembroke will meet the loser of tonight's first-place showdown between Richmond Sockeyes and Humboldt Broncos in Thursday's semi-final. The RichmondHumboldt winner receives a bye into Saturday night's championship 1 final.

Senior Women's Championship Semi-finals

DARTMOUTH, N.S. (CP) - It's been a good year for Richard Weanus, president of the Dartmouth Fuel Kids. His junior A hockey team has made it into the national Centennial Cup championships, scheduled to start Saturday in Humboldt, Sask. And the Fuel Kids are only $10,000 to $15,000 in debt. "There's a lot of relief now," he said in an interview this week. "Last year, this time we were $30,000 in the red." Most people have no idea, Weanus said, of how much it costs to operate a junior A team in Canada. The Fuel Kids, who defeated Charlottetown Abbies last week to advance to the national championship, have operated in the past on annual budgets of $50,000 to $55,000. "Most of that money went to transportation, ice costs and equipment," Weanus said. "Those are the three big expenses." This year, the team's budget soared to $80,000. "Part of that increase was due to a jump in ice costs, travel costs and refereeing," Weanus said. "But it was also due to a recruitcost something we didn't have before this Christmas.' The team took its first step into recruiting when it went after John Spencer, a high-school student who played AAA midget hockey for the Valley Wildcats in Greenwood, N.S. The Fuel Kids offered Spencer $1,000 towards room and board. The straight A student is being considered for a scholarship to Princeton University in New Jer-

After that, four more players were given money for room and board. The team also found fulltime employment for Scott MacTaVishe and Chris Conrad and partjobs for seven other players. "We decided we needed a recruiting package to become competitive. We try to rely on the quality of the (Dartmouth) Sportsplex and the very good coaching staff. But sometimes, something more is needed." It costs the Fuel Kids about $2,000 a year to keep each player in equipment. The Fuel Kids' executive is hoping money from a summer hockey camp will pay off their outstanding debts. They're also hoping for some profit from the Centennial Cup. Four teams are competing for the Cup - the Fuel Kids, the Humboldt Broncos, the Pembroke, Ont., Lumber Kings and the Richmond, B.C., Sockeyes. Weanus the teams will be allowed to split any money left after series expenses are paid. The Fuel Kids could be entering the series without their star centre, Jerry Scott. Friday, May 8, 1987 Saskatoon, Saskatchewan Star-Phoenix Sports B13 It's Sockeyes and Broncos in Centennial Cup final

The two prize fighters of Junior A Tier II hockey will meet for the ninth time in less than three weeks, this time with the Centennial Cup championship on the line, at 7:30 p.m. on Saturday. Richmond Sockeyes earned the chance to settle their grudge series with Humboldt Broncos by defeating the Pembroke Lumber Kings 9-3 in the semi-final on Thursday at the Uniplex.

"If they had won, I wouldn't have wanted to shake hands with them either. We knew we had to play each other again in the Centennial Cup; it's not like we wanted to be buddies with them.' -Richmond's Steve Jaques

With a horde of college and professional scouts attending the Centennial Cup, several more players will likely be recruited this week.

"I don't blame them," said Jaques. "If they had won, I wouldn't have wanted to shake hands with them either. We knew we had to play each other again in the Centennial Cup; it's not like we wanted to be buddies with them.'

Centennial Cup victory for Richmond HUMBOLDT, Sask. (CP) Jason Phillips scored three goals to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to the Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship Saturday night with a 5-2 win over the Humboldt Broncos. But the Sockeyes had to fall back on an outstanding performance in goal by Frank Romeo, who stood up to 46 Humboldt shots while his teammates managed only 23 at the Broncos net. Romeo was named most valuable player in the four-team, series. British Columbia's Sockeyes robin skated to period leads of 2-0 and 2-1 before Paul Rutherford scored the winning goal in the third. Dave Tomlinson also scored for Richmond, which took seven of the game's 12 minor penalties. Curtis Chamberlin and Kevin Luke scored for the Saskatchewan champion Broncos.

Sockeyes sock it to Broncos with Centennial Cup victory

HUMBOLDT, Sask. (CP) Jason Phillips scored three goals to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to the Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship Saturday night with a 5-2 win over the Humboldt Broncos. But the Sockeyes had to fall back on an outstanding performance in goal by Frank Romeo, who stood up to 46 Humboldt shots while his teammates managed only 23 at the Broncos net.

The roundrobin 1 concludes with Pembroke against Richmond Monday, Dartmouth versus Pembroke Tuesday and Richmond meeting Humboldt Wednesday.

OTTAWA (CP) - With four finalists for the Centennial Cup junior hockey final decided, the governing Canadian Amateur Hockey Association announced Thursday the playoff schedule for the round-robin series in Humboldt. Starting Saturday at 7:30 p.m., Atlantic champion Dartmouth, N.S., Fuel Kids open against the host Humboldt Broncos. Belle Buttons present skit at lodge in JC JUNCTION CITY - The Eagles Lodge No. 830, Junction City, honored a group of Herington Centennial members Saturday evening. A Centennial Belles group, the Belle Buttons, put on a skit. They sang their theme song and rang their bells, singing the "Kansas Waltz," composed by Teen Donahue. Those participating in the skit were Rosie Girton, Georgia Robison, Karen Donahue, Mary Ann Brown, Bobbi Ann Johnson, Beulah Vanderslice, Clyde Shamburg and Ross Girton. Bob Roberts, dressed as Uncle Sam, distributed centennial flyers and Harry Robison distributed song sheets. Acting mayor, Vance Donahue, presented a Herington Centennial Cup to the president of the Eagles Lodge, Clint Gfeller, and invited Junction Citians to attend the Centennial celebration. Others attending from Herington were Estelle Baker, Verna Warneke, Mr. and Mrs. Glen Fudge. Out-of-town people with the Herington group were Mr. and Mrs. Bill (Sybil Schlesener) Rock, of Milford; Estelle Baker's family, Mr. and Mrs. C.J. Baker of Salina, Mr. and Mrs. Bob Thompson and Barbara Muller of Wichita; Mr. and Mrs. Earl Higgins of Abilene; Mr. and Mrs. Herman McNeil, Mr. and Mrs. George Trow, all of Salina, Carroll Gates and Clyde Shamburg, both of Glasco.

Sockeyes built for success By ELLIOTT PAP Sun Sports Reporter HUMBOLDT, Sask. - The timehonored theory that you can't build a winner overnight has taken a kick in the butt from the Richmond Sockeyes. The newly crowned western junior A hockey champions are a team for the present. Forget last year, forget next year. The future is now. Centennial Cup, here we come. A year ago, the 'Eyes captured the B.C. Junior Hockey League's Coastal Division title. But when training camp opened last fall, there were just two returning players - Jim Gunn and goalie Glenn Pencer. It wasn't so much a housecleaning as a demolition. The man behind it all is owner Bruce Taylor, a Burnaby roofing contractor. "Two years ago I was with the Burnaby Bluehawks, and we won the Coastal Division but lost the league final," says Taylor. "Last year we won the Coastal and we lost the league final. You learn a little something along the way. I think we've learned what it takes to win a Centennial Cup." The Sockeyes begin the final

"When the Humboldt Broncos were in Selkirk (playing the Centennial Cup quarter-finals against the Manitoba Junior Hockey League-champion Steelers) it was interesting to note they all wore face shields. I sat with an official from the SJHL (Saskatchewan Amateur Junior Hockey League) and asked him what the reaction was from their making that compulsory, and he said there was no reaction at all. He said they just wore them.' The Saskatchewan official told Allard that the rule had resulted in a 'substantial reduction' in dental accidents. The MAHA last year paid $105,000 in insurance premiums for some 27,000 members. "'That's a lot of money," said Allard. "Making (a face protection rule) compulsory would certainly result in a reduction in dental accidents, which is where most of the claims are filed against.'

HUMBOLDT - Question: If Humboldt Broncos and Richmond Sockeyes were willing to battle each other like life-long enemies throughout a seven-game series which meant nothing, what will they do in a single game to advance to the Centennial Cup final? Richmond and Humboldt will meet in the final game of the Centennial Cup round robin at the Uniplex on Wednesday, with the winner earning an automatic berth in the championship game Saturday night. The loser will face either Pembroke Lumber Kings or Dartmouth Fuel Kids in the semi-final on Thursday.

"Those other teams had to be champions to get here, but we know who we have to beat to win the Centennial Cup," said Richmond defenceman Steve Jaques, after the Sockeyes defeated Pembroke 4-1 on Monday to pull even with Humboldt at 2-0 in the Centennial Cup standings. "It'll be a big game on Wednesday.' The Sockeyes needed two thirdperiod goals from Dave Tomlinson to stop the Lumber Kings, who fell to 0-2 in the tournament. Pembroke will play Dartmouth, also 0-2 at 7:30 tonight. "We just hung in for two periods while we killed all those penalties," said Richmond coach Orland Kurtenbach, whose team

"Everybody has a job to do," explained Lynch. "The forwards are watching their forwards, the defenceman watch their defenceman and the goalies watch their goalies. We feel that instead of letting them sit in the stands with their girlfriends or parents, they should do the scouting. It's their tournament; why not let them show some initiative to help themselves win it." What the Broncos might have seen was almost a mirror image of themselves. Both teams have a handful of talented offensive players and a host of laborers. In the Centennial Cup tournament both teams have beaten Pembroke and Dartmouth with a combined goals-for-and-against of 11-4.

HUMBOLDT, Sask. - Named to the Centennial Cup hockey tournament all-star team, which was selected before semifinal play, were three Humboldt players, two from Richmond and one from Pembroke. Humboldt's Bill McDougall, who tied for the round-robin scoring lead with four goals and three assists in three games, was the choice at centre along with teammates Rob Rice, a burly defenceman who hails from Prince Edward Island, and right wing Duncan Ryhorchuk. Getting the nod from Richmond were left wing Jason Phillips, second in round-robin scoring with three goals and three, assists, and defenceman Matt Hervey. By FRANK LUBA Sports Reporter HUMBOLDT, Sask. - Since the Richmond Sockeyes and the Humboldt Broncos first began tangling, all the talk has been of how the two junior A hockey clubs are so evenly matched. Now, suddenly, they're both underdogs - against each other in the Centennial Cup Canadian championship. Confusing? You bet.

VIENNA (CP) - Saying Canadians never can get enough of good hockey, Al Eagleson, the organizer of the Canada Cup tournament, has released a schedule of 34 games to be played this summer among the best players in the world. Team Canada opens camp in Montreal on Aug. 3. There are 14 exhibition games in the Canada Cup schedule and 20 tournament games, if the final goes the maximum three games. Hamilton gets 10 games. The six-team round-robin tournament begins Aug. 28 and ends Sept. 6. The playoffs are scheduled to begin Sept. 8. "We think Hamilton will do well," Eagleson said at a news conference Thursday. "It's going to be a big test for the Hamilton market." Tickets will be costly. The National Hockey League Board of Governors will hold a special meeting Friday in Chicago to discuss the possibility of owner Peter Pocklington selling shares in the Edmonton Oilers to the public, the league announced Wedneday . .. In Red Deer, Alta., the Richmond Sockeyes scored five first-period goals and coasted to a 7-3 victory over the Red Deer Rustlers to square their western Centennial Cup semi-final at three wins each Wednesday. The Sockeyes, British Columbia Junior Hockey League champions, led 5-1 after the first period and d 5-3 after the second. The Rustlers took 18 of 31 minor penalties and two misconducts. The deciding game in the series will be played tonight in Red Deer with the winner meeting the Humboldt Broncos.

By DARRON HARGREAVES Sun Staff Writer When the Selkirk Steelers face off against the Humboldt Broncos tonight, they'll be facing a team similar in many ways to the South Winnipeg Blues, who pushed the Steelers to seven games in the Manitoba Junior Hockey League final. The Broncos, says coach and general manager Bernie Lynch, "are a fast-skating team, a good passing team, small on the forward lines, but big on the blueline." The Blues didn't have size anywhere, though they were certainly fast and often artistic. Steeler coach Frank Porco also notes the physical makeup of the Broncos is the opposite of his club, which has the big forwards and a smaller defence. Like the Blues' trio of Craig Oster, Jerry Chumola and Todd Bohonos, the Broncos have one big line that does most of the damage. Look for left winger Mike Gaber, centre Bill McDougall and right winger Dave Shyiak to cause the Steelers a few problems. The flashy centre from Toronto scored three times and added four assists. "It's my best game of the playoffs by far," said the 20-year-old McDougal. "But we can play better." Humboldt Broncos have a goal and coach Bernie Lynch isn't about to let them forget it. When the Broncos briefly lost sight of their quest for a Centennial Cup championship, Lynch - an individual who has never been afraid to experiment with a new idea - came up with his own method to pick up his sagging squad. Now standard equipment for every Bronco player includes a hospital identification bracelet, with the word GOAL boldly printed on it. "Everytime they pick their nose, blow their nose, take a drink, look at their watch - anything they do with their hand - they see this word: GOAL," explains Lynch. "It means goals, oneness, attitude and loyalty. "*Attitude means dedication, discipline, determination. Loyalty means loyalty to the team, loyalty to the cause. By ELLIOTT PAP With both Richmond Sockeyes and Humboldt Broncos assured spots in next week's Centennial Cup tournament, their current western final series could have been a formality. Instead it has become a delight. The Sockeyes and Broncos will finally settle the Abbott Cup junior A hockey championship tonight (7:30) when they meet in game seven of their best-of-seven at Richmond Arena. Humboldt forced the decider Tuesday, edging Richmond 4-3 in overtime as Mike Gaber deflected home Brad Bergen's point shot at 3:31 of the extra 10-minute period. There is little to choose between the two powerhouse teams. In the first six games of this series, three were decided in overtime, one by one goal and the other two by two goals. Each team has won twice on the road and once at home. Richmond has outscored Humboldt by one goal, 21-20. "We made two really bad mistakes and it cost us the game tonight," lamented Sockeye coach Orland Kurtenbach. "I was very upset with those two plays." The first one came at 8:28 of the third period when Richmond's Tony Bobbitt closed his hand on the puck and Humboldt promptly scored on the power play, tying the contest 3-3. The second came at 3:02 of overtime when Sockeye defenceman Steve Jaques helmet-butted Bronco star Bill McDougall in the mouth after McDougall had interfered with him. Jaques received a five-minute match penalty and likely a three-game suspension while McDougall got a minor. By FRANK LUBA Sports Reporter When two teams are as close as Richmond Sockeyes and Humboldt Broncos, mistakes are costly. Richmond made more mistakes last night and the result was a 4-3 overtime decision for Humboldt which evened the best-of-seven Abbott Cup Western Canadian junior A hockey championship series at 3-3.

Fast start for Broncos From L-P Wire Services Buoyed by familiar surroundings, the Humboldt Broncos won their first two games of the Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship. Following a 7-2 victory over the Dartmouth (N.S.) Fuel Kings Saturday night, the Saskatchewan Amateur Junior Hockey League's Broncos beat the Pembroke (Ont.) Lumber Kings 4-2 Sunday night in the round-robin t tournament. The Broncos built a 3-1 lead in the first period and hung on for the victory after the Lumber Kings battled back to make it 3-2 in the second stanza. Kevin Luke, Rob Rice. Duncan Ryhorchuk and Curtis Chamberlin, with the winner at 16:09 of the first period, scored for the Broncos. Mike Eastwood scored both goals for Pembroke, who was outshot 42- 32. On Saturday, Humboldt overcame an early 2-1 deficit to defeat Dartmouth. Dave Shyiak and Bill McDougall each scored twice for the Broncos, who fired 40 shots at Dartmouth netminder Troy Crosby. Al Novakowski, Mike Gaber and Mike Chighisola added singles for the Broncos. Steve Brown and Norman LeBlanc replied for Dartmouth, who tested Humboldt goaltender Trevor Lloyd 22 times. In Sunday's other game, the Richmond (B.C.) Sockeyes defeated Dartmouth 7-3, led by Jason Pl Phillips' three goals. Byron Moller, Steve Jaques, Dave Tomlinson and Matt Hervey also scored for the Sockeyes, who outshot Dartmouth 44-29. Jerry Scott had two goals and Brian King had one for Dartmouth, whose record dropped to 0-2. Richmond stands at 1-0, while Pembroke sports an 0-1 mark. Richmond and Pembroke play in today's only game. Volleyballers win silver Saskatchewan's women's team won a silver medal at the national junior volleyball championships in Saskatoon on Sunday. They lost to Ontario's entry from Scarborough 3- 0 (15-12, 15-13, 15-10) in the final. Chris Klassen of Saskatoon led Saskatchewan with 19 kills. Terri Wildeman of Leroy chipped in with 16 kills, and Janice Chase of Yellow Grass added nine kills and seven service aces. Sherbrooke defeated Montreal 3-0 to win the all-Quebec bronze-medal final. Saskatchewan's men won a bronze by defeating the University of Manitoba 3-0 (15-11, 15-7, 15-12). The University of Alberta defeated Limoilou (Que.) 3-1 (15-0, 12-15, 17- 15, 16-14) to win the gold. Middle hitter Chris Klassen of Saskatoon and setter Artis Tweddle of Harris were named to the women's all-star team. Power. hitter Brad Dusyk of Montmartre earned a spot on the men's all-star team.

By ELLIOTT PAP Sun Sports Reporter HUMBOLDT, Sask. - After two days and three games, the coaches of Centennial Cup co-favorites Richmond and Humboldt are begging to differ. The bone of contention centres around Ontario champion Pembroke Lumber Kings, and whether they too are legitimate threats for the national junior A hockey crown. "I'm impressed with Pembroke. They're a very disciplined team,' said Sockeye coach Orland Kurtenbach after watching the Kings drop a close 4-2 decision to Humboldt's Broncos Sunday night. "It looks like this is a three-team tournament now." Bernie Lynch, coach of the Broncos, couldn't agree less. "I saw nothing out there that will make me change my mind, countered Lynch. "I still think it will be us and Richmond in the final.' The only consensus reached on the tourney's opening weekend is that Atlantic champion Dartmouth Fuel Kids are the dregs of this event. The Kids were easy 7-3 losers to Richmond yesterday afternoon, the loss following a 7-2 trouncing they received Saturday from Humboldt. Tonight's game pits the 'Eyes against Pembroke with a semi-final berth assured Richmond if it wins. The Sockeyes are still in fine shape, however, with Dartmouth proving to be so hopeless. In the worst case scenario, Richmond could finish the roundat 1-2, as could the Fuel(less) Kids.

The best-of-seven Western Canadian playoffs for the Centennial Cup is tied 2-2. Dean Rutledge replied with Richmond lone goal in the third period.

There appears to be a healthy amount of mutual respect between the Richmond Sockeyes and the Humboldt Broncos. Richmond, the B.C. junior A hockey champions, held a 2-1 edge in the best-of-seven Abbott Cup Western Canadian championship with Saskatchewan's title-holding Humboldt as play resumed last night at Richmond Arena. Both squads are loaded with 20- -olds and their corresponding experience. But discipline and control are the key words as both teams advance to the Canadian championship Centennial Cup next weekend in Humboldt no matter what happens in this series. *Any team that wants to act like idiots is going to pay the price," said Humboldt coach Bernie Linch. He's expecting the Richmond portion of the series to be much like the Humboldt games: "lots of skating, lots of hard work." All that has been accompanied by close checking but play has not been as physical as the Sockeyes' Western semifinal with the rowdy Red Deer Rustlers.

HUMBOLDT (CP) - The Pembroke Lumber Kings clinched a spot in the semi-final game of the Cen- tennial Cup hockey championship tournament Tuesday night with an 8-4 victory over the Dartmouth Fuel Kids. The Ontario champions will meet the loser of today's final round-robin game between the Humboldt Broncos and the Richmond Sockeyes of British Columbia, who both have 2-0 records in the tournament. The semi-final game will be played Thursday night. Bruce Coles led Pembroke to its

HUMBOLDT (CP) - The Richmond Sockeyes whipped the Pembroke Lumber Kings 9-3 in a semifinal clash Thursday night to advance to the final game in the Centennial Cup junior a hockey championship tournament. The Sockeyes, from British Columbia, will meet the Saskatchewan champion Humboldt Broncos in the sudden-death final Saturday night. The game is rematch of the Western Canada championship in which the Sockeyes edged the Broncos 4-3 in the best-of-seven series. Richmond, which made it to the semi-final with a 2-1 record in the round-robin portion of the tournament, got past Pembroke on goals by Trevor Dickie, Steve Jaques, Matt Hervey, Brian Kozak, Paul Rutherford, Jason Phillips, Tony Bobbitt, Bryon Moller and Bill Hardy. The Lumber Kings, the Ontario champions, who had a 1-2 record in the round-robin, got goals from Dave Van Hoof, George Dupont and Chris Clarke. The Sockeyes, who outshot Pembroke 44-32 and took 11 of 17 minor penalties, led 1-0 after the first period and 3-0 after the second.

guided the Junior Nats in the two years before Templeton took over. This past season was Hitchcock's third at the helm of the Blazers. His WHL coaching record, including playoffs, is an amazing 188-62-9. Chambers has considerable coaching experience. A former head coach of the OHL's Toronto Marlboros, he has been top man with Canada's national under-18 team for the last two years and he has coached Italy's national senior team at several world championships. Jodoin, meanwhile, guided the Patriotes to their first-ever Canadian Interuniversity Athletic Union hockey championship March 22 in Edmonton. Besides Sauter, Wilson and Hitchcock, only one other WHL coach applied, that being Wayna Naka of the Victoria Cougars. Templeton was the sole OHL coach to apply while there were two entries from the Quebec Major Junior Hockey League - Real Paiement, head coach of the Granby Bisons, and Jacques Tremblay, who held the same post with the St. Jean Castors. One other applicant of note from this province was Humboldt Broncos head coach and general manager Bernie Lynch, who has guided his team to two consecutive Saskatchewan Amateur Junior Hockey League championships. The national junior team will hold its annual summer camp this August, likely in Ottawa. A final selection camp will be held in December.

'Eyes go up 3-2 in Tier 2 tussle The Canadian Press RICHMOND - Matt Hervey, a former member of the Victoria Cougars in major junior hockey, scored two goals to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to a 5-3 win over the Humboldt Broncos in a Centennial Cup hockey playoff game that finished late Sunday night. The victory gave the Sockeyes a 3-2 lead in the best-of-seven Western Canada Tier 2 junior final. The sixth game is scheduled in Richmond tonight.

RICHMOND, B.C. (CP) - Matt Hervey scored two goals Sunday night to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to a 5-3 win over the Humboldt Broncos in Centennial Cup hockey playoff action. The victory gave the Sockeyes a 3-9 lead in the best-of-seven Western Canada final. The sixth game is scheduled here Tuesday night. Humboldt led 2-0 after after the first period but Richmond tied ° game 2-2 after the middle frame. Brian Kozak, Dave Tomlinson and Dave Rutherford added goals for Richmond. Bill McDougall scored two goals for Humboldt while Garnet Kazuik completed the scoring for the Saskatchewan club. Goalie Frank Romeo made 30 saves for Richmond while Bruce Hoffort blocked 22 shots for Humboldt. The Broncos' tight-checking game stymied the Sockeyes for the first half of the game, the same tactic the visitors employed in a 2-1 victory Saturday night. McDougall Brad Bergen scored one goal apiece as the Broncos evened the series Saturday. Dean Rutledge replied with Richmond lone goal in the third period of the paced game. Hoffort stopped shots while Romeo made 30 saves in Richmond's net. In Medicine Hat, Alta., Dale Kusnher scored his second goal of the game on the power play at 13:44 of the third period to give the Tigers a 5-4 win over the Portland Winter Hawks in a Western Hockey League playoff game Sunday. The win gives the Tigers a 1-0° lead in the WHL final series. Both teams teams spent much of the game feeling each other out because it was the first game between east and west division teams this season. There was a time when Orland Kurtenbach found few things less appealing than earning a living as a hockey coach. Fired from his post as coach of the Vancouver Canucks after the 1978 season, Kurtenbach kissed hockey good-bye, choosing to settle into life as an insurance broker in British Columbia. Now the 50-year-old native of Cudworth is giving coaching another shot, after being lured out of retirement this year to guide the Richmond Sockeyes of the British Columbia Junior Hockey League. Success has come quickly for the Sockeyes, who lead the Humboldt Broncos 2-1 in the -of-seven Abbott Cup series, with the fourth game being held in Richmond tonight. wanted to see if I still enjoyed the game or not," said Kurtenbach, who had a career coaching record of 36-62-27 with Vancouver. "When I left the Canucks' coaching position, I had quite a sour taste in my mouth. I wasn't happy with the situation. But I have a nice bunch of kids here and I'm enjoying hockey again. "It's more of a relaxed situation now. I haven't put any pressure on myself. And I enjoy dealing with the kids, not only in terms of hockey - although it's satisfying to see them progress from the start of the year to now - but also helping them with the personal things.' That is the style of Kurtenbach, a soft-spoken gentleman whose big-league demeanor is a welcome addition to the ranks of junior hockey. Kurtenbach is the epitome of class behind the Sockeyes bench, and that characteristic has rubbed off on his players,

*One way or another, Richmond Sockeyes are going to the Centennial Cup. If they beat Humboldt Broncos in their best-of-seven western final, the B.C. junior A hockey champion Sockeyes take the western berth into the -team national championship. But the Broncos also happen to be hosts of the Cup and if they win the western final, the Sockeyes will round out the tournament entry. The western final goes tomorrow and Tuesday in Humboldt. If there's a split in the games, a third game will be played Wednesday with Thursday and Friday off for travel days and the best-of-seven resuming Saturday at Richmond Arena. If either team sweeps the first two games, the travel days are Wednesday and Thursday and play opens Friday at Richmond Arena. Richmond advanced to the final Thursday in Red Deer when Dave Tomlinson scored three goals to pace the Sockeyes to a 9-1 thrashing of the Alberta champion Rustlers, allowing the West Coast squad to win the best-of-seven western semifinal 4-3. The teams were tied 0-0 after the first period, but Richmond exploded for five goals in the second. Rob Clarke, Mike Claringbull, Jason Phillips, Jim Gunn, Mike McCormick and Tony Bobbitt also tallied for the Sockeyes. Red Deer's Pete DeGraaf spoiled Jamie Stewart's shut-out bid late in the third period.

Tony Bobbitt scored for the B.C. Junior Hockey League champions after 1:04 of extra play and the Sockeyes made the goal stand up for the remainder of the 10-minute overtime period for a 4-3 victory over Humboldt Broncos in a Centennial Cup Western Canadian final series game. The victory gave the Sockeyes a 2-1 lead in the -of-seven battle for the Abbott Cup. The series now shifts to Richmond Arena for the remainder c of the games, beginning Saturday at 7:30 p.m. "We kind of stole one tonight," said Sockeyes' coach Orland Kurtenbach. Richmond Sockeyes are going to the Centennial Cup. The B.C. Junior Hockey League champions qualified for the national tier two finals by blasting Red Deer Rustlers 9-1 in Thursday's seventh game of their rowdy western semi-final. Richmond now moves on to meet Saskatchewan's Humboldt Broncos for the Abbott Cup, also known as the western Canada title. In most years, the winner of the Abbott Cup would be the sole western representative in the Centennial Cup tournament. However, since Humboldt is host team for the 1987 tourney, , the 'Eyes are in regardless of how they fare against the Saskatchewanchamps. Sockeye coach Orland Kurtenbach doesn't feel this will take any, edge off the best-of-seven series that begins Monday in Humboldt.

Phillips has had a wonderful week here, darting and dashing to four goals and five assists in Richmond's four games. He is the tourney's leading scorer and was named to the all-star team at the awards banquet Friday night. "I've had an okay tournament," acknowledged Phillips on the eve of the national junior A hockey final between the Sockeyes and host Humboldt Broncos. HUMBOLDT The smooth road that the Humboldt Broncos have been following to the Centennial Cup has finally revealed its first bumps. The Broncos were out-worked, out-hit and out-scored Tuesday at the Uniplex, as the Richmond Sockeyes evened their best-of-seven Abbott Cup series at one win apiece. With the teams splitting the first two games, Game Three will be played tonight in Humboldt at 7:30. The loss was Humboldt's first since being upset by Melville Millionaires in the first game of the SAJHL playoffs. After 17 consecutive wins, the loss to the Sockeyes jolted Humboldt coach Bernie Lynch from the party wagon the Broncos have been riding through the playoffs. "To be embarrassed like this in our building and in front of our fans is uncalled for and unnecessary," said Lynch. "Maybe they've forgotten what it's like to be hungry and what it's like to have to work. Somewhere between the parking lot and the arena, they vanished. "Part of the problem is that y we played /rec hockey' against Selkirk, waltzed through Lloydminster, waltzed through Yorkton and waltzed through Melville after the first game. Now they're going to have to earn that respect back." First order of business for the Broncos will be finding a way to stop Richmond's Jason Phillips, who has been a scoring threat on almost every shift. Phillips, who scored once in Monday's opener, scored three goals and added an assist on Tuesday. "I give all the credit to Brian Kozak," said Phillips. "'He set me up all over the ice. Sockeyes tumble before Broncos Canadian Press HUMBOLDT, Sask. - Humboldt Broncos won a place in the final of the Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship tournament last night with a 6-1 victory over Richmond Sockeyes. The losing Sockeyes, with a round-robin record of 2-1, will meet Pembroke Lumber Kings in tonight's semifinal. The final will be played Saturday night. In their previous two games in the round-robin, the Broncos and Sockeyes had identical goals for (11) and against (4) records. Bill McDougall and Garnett Kazuik led Humboldt with two goals apiece, while Duncan Ryhorchuk and Curtis Chamberlin had singles. The win gave Humboldt, from Saskatchewan, a 3-0 record in the round-robin portion of the tournament. Stan Czenczek scored the Richmond goal. The two teams were tied 1-1 after the first period but Humboldt moved ahead 4-1 in the second. The Broncos outshot the B.C. champion Sockeyes 37-19 in a scrappy game that saw Humboldt take 15 of 27 minors. Both teams took two five-minute majors and two game misconducts. Humboldt also picked up two 10- minute misconducts. The Lumber Kings, from Ontario, finished 1-2 in the round-robin section of the tournament.

HUMBOLDT Curtis Chamberlin may have had other things on his mind when he arrived at the Humboldt Uniplex, but a couple of . periods and a scare from the Richmond Sockeyes were enough to snap him back to hockey. Chamberlin scored three times, including twice in the 10-minute overtime period, to lead Humboldt Broncos to a 5-3 win over the Sockeyes in the first game of the best-of-seven Abbott Cup series which opened' in Humboldt on Monday. "I came to the rink today, and it was like I was thinking about something else," said Chamberlin, following the Broncos 17th consecutive playoff win. "We've had a week off (since defeating Selkirk Steelers) and you get used to doing other things. I wasn't geared up to come to the rink at all. Finally I had to say to myself, 'Come on, Curtis, you've got to get going, you've got to start working.' That's what I'm known for: working hard. "I'm not noted as a goal scorer. That's the way our line is; when we put the puck in the net it's a bonus.' Chamberlin converted a two-onone pass from Joey Johannson, firing it high over sliding Sockeye goalie Frank Romeo, to give the Broncos their first lead of the game with 1:39 remaining in the overtime period. Starting tomorrow, Atlantic champion Dartmouth, N.S., Fuel Kids open against the host Humboldt Broncos. Then on Sunday, the Richmond Sockeyes play Dartmouth, and Humboldt meets the Pembroke, Ont., Lumber Kings. The round-robin concludes with Pembroke against Richmond on Monday, Dartmouth playing Pembroke on Tuesday and Richmond meeting Humboldt on Wednesday. HUMBOLDT Jason Phillips scored three goals to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to the Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship Saturday night with a 5-2 win over the Humboldt Broncos. But the Sockeyes had to fall back on an outstanding performance in goal by Frank Romeo, who stood up to 46 Humboldt shots while his teammates managed only 23 at the Broncos net. "This is unbelievable," said Romeo, the tournament and final game MVP. "I'm the happiest guy in the world. Winning the cup, being MVP. it's like Christmas all over again." British Columbia's Sockeyes skated to period leads of 2-0 and 2-1 before Paul Rutherford scored the winning goal in the third. Dave Tomlinson also scored for Richmond, which took seven of the game's 12 minor penalties before a standing-room crowd of 2,400. "I think we were really afraid of making a mistake early in the game and wanted everything to go right," said Broncos general manager and coach Bernie Lynch. "It should have been the other way, we should have come out and taken it right to them. That's what Richmond did, they came right at us. They did a super job and they're deserving champions."

The Saskatchewan Television Network will be broadcasting the final game of the Centennial Cup hockey championship live from the Humboldt Uniplex on May 9 at 7:30 p.m. The Centennial Cup is a national tournament involving the top Tier Two junior teams in Canada. A Sask Tel fibre optics network will transmit the signal to CKTV in Regina, CFQC in Saskatoon, CICC in Yorkton and CIPA in Prince Albert. Richmond eyes hockey title Richmond Sockeyes will attempt to wrap up their sixth playoff series and first Abbott Cup title this weekend on the familiar ice of Richmond Arena. The B.C. Junior League champions entertain Saskatchewan's Humboldt Broncos tonight and Sunday (7:30) in games four and five of the best-of-seven Centennial Cup western junior A final. Richmond leads it 2-1. Coach Orland Kurtenbach's road warriors have not been home since April 8 - eight games ago - when they took a 2-0 lead over Red Deer Rustlers in the Alberta-B.C. final. And their stay at home will be short-lived. On Friday, they'll return to Humboldt to compete in the four-team Centennial Cup national championships.

By ELLIOTT PAP With both Richmond Sockeyes and Humboldt Broncos assured spots in next week's Centennial Cup tournament, their current western final series could have been a formality. Instead it is has become a delight. The Sockeyes and Broncos will finally settle the Abbott Cup junior A hockey championship tonight (7:30) when they meet in game seven of their -seven at Richmond Arena. Humboldt forced the decider Tuesday, edging Richmond 4-3 in overtime as Mike Gaber deflected home Brad Bergen's point shot at 3:31 of the extra 10-minute period. There is little to choose between the two powerhouse teams. In the first six games of this series, three were decided in overtime, one by one goal and the other two by two goals. Each team has won twice on the road and once at home. Richmond has outscored Humboldt by one goal, 21-20. "We made two really bad mistakes and it cost us the game tonight," lamented Sockeye coach Orland Kurtenbach. "I was very upset with those two plays." The first one came at 8:28 of the third period when Richmond's Tony Bobbitt closed his hand on the puck and Humboldt promptly scored on the power play, tying the contest 3-3. The second came at 3:02 of overtime when Sockeye defenceman Steve Jaques helmet-butted Bronco star Bill McDougall in the mouth after McDougall had interferred with him. Jaques received a five-minute match penalty - and likely a three-game suspension - while McDougall got a minor. Gaber's winner came with the teams playing 4-on-4 but the subIAN SMITH Dickie sequent Humboldt power play put Richmond on the ropes. netminder Paul Rutherford, with two, and Dave Tomlinson scored for overtime. the 'Eyes.

Leading the Sockeyes, of course, is former Vancouver Canuck captain and coach Orland Kurtenbach. Taylor doesn't hesitate to lavish praise on the man he chose to run his team. "We wouldn't have made it this far without Kurt," says Taylor emphatically. "He is very disciplined and he commands the boys' respect. He showed his class in Red Deer when the fans there were throwing eggs and fish at our bench and he remained very calm. All his reactions were done behind the scenes to the right people." With Kurtenbach doing the coaching and Taylor scouring for talent, the Sockeyes rebuilt around a core of junior hockey veterans. Changes were numerous and often. Only nine players remain from training camp. And there is but one Richmond product, seldom-used rookie Jason Talo, on the roster. The players hail from all over the hockey map. By ELLIOTT PAP Sun Sports Reporter HUMBOLDT, Sask. - The showdown that has been so widely predicted will indeed come to pass: Richmond Sockeyes and Humboldt Broncos are going to meet for the Centennial Cup Saturday night. The Sockeyes advanced to the biggest game in tier two junior hockey by waxing Pembroke Lumber Kings 9-3 in Thursday's semi-final. Although the score sounds impressive, the 'Eyes were not particularly sharp last night, a fact that didn't escape the ever -vigilant eyes of coach Orland Kurtenbach.

Although the Humboldt Broncos and Richmond Sockeyes planning to play for the Abbott Cup, perhaps the Abbott-and-Costello Cup would be a more suitable prize. With both teams already guaranteed a spot in the Centennial Cup tournament next month in Humboldt, the best-of-seven Western final, which begins at 7:30 tonight in Humboldt, has all the significance of a Bud-and-Lou flick. That doesn't mean, however, that the Broncos are planning to turn in their slapshots for slapstick. "People figure that because we're automatically in the Centennial Cup, we're going to slack off," said Humboldt captain Curtis Chamberlin. "But' we put a lot of pressure on ourselves. We want to go in the front door, more for ourselves and our pride than anything else." That's why Bronco coaches Bernie Lynch and Donn Clark went on a scouting mission last week, travelling to Red Deer to scout the Sockeyes in their series against the Alberta-champion Rustlers immediately after the Broncos swept the Selkirk Steelers in the Anavet Cup. "They play simple hockey, they're big and they work hard," Clark reported. "They're like Sel-* kirk, only they're like a better Steelers team. They dump and chase and play good aggressive hockey."

Richmond ties series HUMBOLDT (CP) - Jason Phillips scored three goals as the Richmond Sockeyes beat the Humboldt Broncos 5-3 in a Centennial Cup junior hockey playoff game Tuesday night. The victory tied the best-of-seven tier-two western final series 1-1. The third game will be played tonight in Humboldt. Stan Czenczek and Brian Kozak scored the other goals for the Sockeyes, the champions of British Columbia. The Broncos, who had a 45- 31 edge in shots on goal, got goals from Dave Shyiak, Kevin Luke and Bill McDougall.

Sockeyes lose first in overtime Canadian Press HUMBOLDT, Sask. - The Humboldt Broncos scored twice in an overtime period to beat the Richmond Sockeyes 5-3 Monday night t in the opening game of the best-of-seven Centennial Cup Western Canada junior hockey final. Curtis Chamberlain had three goals, including the winner, for the Saskatchewan champion Broncos. Brad Bergen and Bill McDougall had the other Humboldt markers. The Sockeyes - British Columbia champions - had goals from Bryan Moller, Matt Hervey and Jason Phillips. Richmond led 2-1 after the second period and the teams were tied at 3-3 after regulation time.

'Eyes go up 3-2 in Tier 2 tussle The Canadian Press RICHMOND Matt Hervey, a former member of the Victoria Cougars in major junior hockey, scored two goals to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to a 5-3 win over the Humboldt Broncos in a Centennial Cup hockey playoff game that finished late Sunday night. The victory gave the Sockeyes a 3-2 lead in the best-of-seven Western Canada Tier 2 junior final. The sixth game is scheduled in Richmond tonight.

By ELLIOTT PAP Sun Sports Reporter HUMBOLDT, Sask. - After two days and three games, the coaches of Centennial Cup co-favorites Richmond and Humboldt are begging to differ. The bone of contention centres around Ontario champion Pembroke Lumber Kings, and whether they too are legitimate threats for the national junior A hockey crown. "I'm impressed with Pembroke. They're a very disciplined team," said Sockeye coach Orland Kurtenbach after watching the Kings drop a close 4-2 decision to Humboldt's Broncos Sunday night. "It looks like this is a three-team tournament now.' Bernie Lynch, coach of the Broncos, couldn't agree less. "I saw nothing out there that will make me change my mind," countered Lynch. "I still think it will be us and Richmond in the final." The only consensus reached on the tourney's opening weekend is that Atlantic champion Dartmouth Fuel Kids are the dregs of this event. Kids were easy 7-3 losers to Richmond yesterday afternoon, the loss following a 7-2 trouncing they received Saturday from Humboldt. Tonight's game pits the 'Eyes against Pembroke with a semi-final berth assured Richmond if it wins. The Sockeyes are still in fine shape, however, with Dartmouth proving to be so hopeless. In the worst case scenario, Richmond could finish the round-robin at 1-2, as could the Fuel(less) Kids.

RICHMOND - Dave Tomlinson scored with 2:37 remaining in regulation time Wednesday night to snap a 4-4 tie and give the Richmond Sockeyes a 5-4 victory over the Humboldt Broncos and a Centennial Cup tournament berth.

If they didn't realize it before, the Selkirk Steelers will now recognize that they have to find a way to stop Humboldt Bronco centre Bill McDougall. McDougall, who set SAJHL records for goals and points this season, scored three goals and assisted on the other four as Humboldt downed Selkirk 7-4 in the opening game of their best-of-seven Centennial Cup Western semifinal in Selkirk on Thursday. Mike Gaber, with two, Duncan Ryhorchuk and Kevin Luke also scored for the Broncos, while Kevin Lajeunesse, Jeff Beaudin, Kevin Mayo and Jim Bartley scored for the Steelers. The teams were tied 2-2 after one period and Humboldt led 5-4 after two. Broncos coach Bernie Lynch was not satisfied with his team's performance, despite the win. "We showed the effects of our eight-day layoff," said Lynch. "They should never have scored four goals; we gift three of them. We were really rusty in the first and second, but we played a solid third period and I'm happy with that. "But the key to the game was that they played 20 playoff games and we've played 12. They were really sharp with the puck and passing. "I didn't really feel that we were in control tonight. We'll try to be more dominant (tonight)." Game two of the series will be played tonight in Selkirk. The following three games will be played in Humboldt, beginning Sunday at 7:30 p.m.

By Ken Juba of the Star-Phoenix Humboldt 9 Selkirk 3 HUMBOLDT Next stop for Dave Shyiak is Northern Michigan University. But for the rest of this season -as the Humboldt Broncos take their run at a Centennial Cup championship - the 20-yearold native of Brandon can be found planted squarely in front of the opposition's net, stirring up trouble and slashing at rebounds. Shyiak steamrolled his way to three goals to lead the Broncos to a 9-3 win over Selkirk Steelers at the Uniplex on Sunday, giving Humboldt a 3-0 lead in the best-ofseven western semi "He's a very intense player," Broncos coach Bernie Lynch said of the NCAAbound Shyiak. "If all the players in Canada had half his heart, we'd never lose an international game. He'll play all game if you want him to." Shyiak, who scored 30 power-play goals during the regular season, continued to be the trigger man for Humboldt's extrastrength unit, scoring twice while the Broncos held the man advantage against Selkirk. The second-year Bronco opened the scoring on a power play, 6:05 into the game, banged in a rebound for his second power-play marker in the second period, and completed his hat trick at 5:27 of the third, converting a pass from Bill McDougall, before earning a game misconduct for a third-period fight. Humboldt and Selkirk each picked up three fighting majors and three game misconducts. The Broncos, who outshot Selkirk 49-17, took 14 of 26 minor penalties. The Broncos can wrap up the series tonight in Humboldt. Game time is 7:30 at the Uniplex. "Too many good players injured, too many tough series to get here, and not enough left," is how Steelers coach Frank Porco assessed his team's performance against Humboldt to date.

HUMBOLDT - The green, cardboard step chart in the dressing room of the Humboldt Broncos says it all. Game-by-game the Broncos have been charting their playoff success, marking each win on their climb to the prize at the top of the ladder: the Centennial Cup. They moved one level higher on Monday, pounding Selkirk Steelers 9-1 to sweep the Centennial Cup Western se in four games and win the Anavet Cup given to the winner of the Saskatchewan-Manitoba championship series. But a quick glance at the step chart shows that the Broncos have seven more wins t to reach their goal. "I know this isn't too much," said defenceman Al Novakowski, who won a Centennial Cup championship as a member of the Penticton Knights last year. "It's something, but there's something more down the road. Lots of kids here have never won anything never a midget title, never a bantam title. For them, winning the league title was great and winning this was great. And it just keeps getting sweeter." This victory was, however, especially sweet for Humboldt coach Bernie Lynch and the Bronco veterans, who remembered the bitter seven-game semi-final series ' which they lost to Winnipeg South Blues last year. When the Broncos skated a victory lap around the Uniplex, hoisting the SAJHL championship trophy over their heads (Winnipeg failed to turn in the Anavet Cup in time for it to be transported to Humboldt), Lynch said the memory of last year's defeat was erased. tastes a lot better than most people think it does," said Lynch. "There was a lot of bitterness over last t year's series. But this is just like burying the hatchet." Bill McDougall led the Broncos with three goals and two assists, raising his playoff point total to 40. Rod Kavanagh, with his first two goals of the playoffs, Corey Belitski, Morgan Watts, Dave Shyiak and Brad Bergen also scored for Humboldt. Jeff Smith had Selkirk's only goal. "In close games we're able to take the man out of the play," said Bergen, who had the honor of putting the latest mark on the Broncos' chart. "That's something we didn't always do last year. Sometimes that might have cost us some goals." The Broncos led 2-0 after one period, but forced the Steelers to melt under the heat of intense forechecking. Humboldt scored four goals in a span of 3:34 in the second and led 6-1 when the period ended. "We're an aggressive hockey team," said Lynch. "If we win 5- 1, it means the other team is doing a good job of keeping the puck out of the net. That aggressiveness is a reflection of our style. Our players don't quit. They never want to quit.' Next rung on the ladder for the Broncos will mean stepping over the winner of the Richmond Sockeyes-Red Deer Rustlers series, which is tied 2-2, in the Western final. The first two games will be in Humboldt on Monday and Tuesday. "The only way to get to

Canadian Press VIENNA - Al Eagleson is going to put his theory to the test. Claiming that Canadians can never get enough of good hockey, Eagleson has released a schedule of 34 Canada Cup games to be played this summer featuring the best players in the world. Team Canada opens camp in Montreal on Aug. 3. There are 14 exhibition games and 20 tournament one, if the final goes the maximum three games. Hamilton gets 10 games. Vancouver gets none. The six-team round-robin tournament begins Aug. 28 and ends Sept. 6. The playoffs are scheduled to begin Sept. 8. Starting tomorrow, Atlantic champion Dartmouth, N.S., Fuel Kids open against the host Humboldt Broncos. Then on Sunday, the Richmond Sockeyes play Dartmouth, and Humboldt meets the Pembroke, Ont., Lumber Kings. The round-robin concludes with Pembroke against Richmond on Monday, Dartmouth playing Pembroke on Tuesday and Richmond meeting Humboldt on Wednesday. The third-and second-place finishers will face each other in a semi-final with the winner facing the first-place finisher on Saturday,

By DARRON HARGREAVES Sun Staff Writer HUMBOLDT - The Humboldt Broncos are known to have searched at least a couple of the world's corners for talent. No fewer than 14 players on their 23-man roster hail from places other than Saskatchewan. They come from B.C., Ontario, P.E.l., Boston. The most extravagant of these imports calls Lulea, Sweden, home.

A 9-3 thrashing at the Uniplex last night all but ended Selkirk's chances of advancing past the Humboldt Broncos in Centennial Cup quarter-final play. "We've got too many good players hurt, too hard a series to get here,' said dejected Steeler coach Frank Porco. "It's not enough. It's as simple as that. "We'll come out tomorrow and give our best shot. That's all we can do.' The win gives the Broncos a 3-0 series lead, following two wins in Selkirk. "This was a very pivotal game for us," said Bronco coach Bernie Lynch. "We know about the Steelers' history of coming back after being down 2-0 and winning. "Now we've gotta go for the jugular." This game was all Humboldt. "We came out hard and kept it up," said Kevin Luke, who had three goals for the Broncos.

The victory gave Humboldt a 4- 0 sweep over the Lancers in the best-of-seven series. Bill McDougall, Brett Stewart, Brad Bergen and Garnet Kazuik scored for the Broncos. Darryl Plandowski, Todd Cornish and Travis Gould replied for Lloydminster. The Broncos will now meet the winner of the Manitoba Junior Hockey League in the Western Centennial Cup semi-final.

(Canadian Press) Dave Tomlinson scored three goals to pace the visiting Richmond Sockeyes to a 9-1 thrashing of Red Deer Rustlers on Thursday to win the Centennial Cup Western best-of-seven semifinal 4-3. Richmond exploded for five goals in the second period. Rob Clarke, Mike Claringbull, Jason Phillips, Jim Gunn, Mike McCormick and Tony Bobbitt also tallied for the Sockeyes, who outshot the Rustlers 39-34. Red Deer's Pete DeGraaf spoiled goaltender Jamie Stewart's shut-out bid at 13:17 of the third period. Richmond now plays Humboldt Broncos in the Western best-of-seven final.

Dave Tomlinson scored three goals to pace the visiting Richmond Sockeyes to a 9-1 win over the Red Deer Rustlers in the seventh and deciding game of the Centennial Cup Western semi-final on Thursday. The teams were tied 0-0 after the first period, but Richmond exploded for five goals in the second. Richmond now meets the Humboldt Broncos in the Western best-of-seven final. The opening game in the series is Monday in Humboldt.

The best-of-seven Western Canadian final is tied 2-2. Dean Rutledge replied for Richmond. The teams meet here tonight and Tuesday.

SPORTS REPORTER Ken Juba sends a tip of the hat out to George Hitchings of R.R. 3. After covering the Centennial Cup game between the Humboldt Broncos and Richmond Sockeyes on Wednesday, Juba was returning home to file his story. He was about seven miles from Saskatoon, when without warning, a deer came out of the ditch. He couldn't avoid it and the result of the collision damaged the frontend of the car so extensively he couldn't drive it. Three motorists passed Juba by before Hitchings, who was travelling in the opposite direction, stopped and drove him into the city to get some assistance.

Sockeyes lose first in overtime Canadian Press HUMBOLDT, Sask. The Humboldt Broncos scored twice in an overtime period to beat the Richmond Sockeyes 5-3 Monday night in the opening game of the -of-seven Centennial Cup Western Canada junior hockey final. Curtis Chamberlain had three goals, including the winner, for the Saskatchewan champion Broncos. Brad Bergen and Bill McDougall had the other Humboldt markers. The Sockeyes - British Columbia champions - had goals from Bryan Moller, Matt Hervey and Jason Phillips. Richmond led 2-1 after the second period and the teams were tied at 3-3 after regulation time.

Tony Bobbitt's goal at 1:04 of a 10-minute overtime period gave the Richmond Sockeyes a 4-3 win over the Humboldt Broncos in a Centennial Cup junior hockey playoff game Wednesday night in Humboldt. The victory gave the Sockeyes a 2-1 lead in the best-of-seven Tier 2 western final series. The fourth game will be played in Richmond on Saturday. Dave Tomlinson, Mike Claringbull and Rob Clarke scored the other goals for the Sockeyes, who were o outshot 39-23 and trailed 1-0 after the first period and 2-1 after the second. Bill McDougall had two goals and Curtis Chamberlin one for the Saskatchewan champion Broncos. Richmond, the British Columbia champion, took 13 of 25 minor penalties and the only major. In the eastern final, the host Pembroke Lumber Kings defeated the Nickel Centre Power Trains 8- 2 to lead their best-of-seven series 3-1. George Dupont and Mike Eastwood each scored twice for Pembroke and singles came from Bruce Coles, Karlo Pavich and Geoff Stanton. Gord Davy and Doug Parenteau scored for Nickel Centre. The next game in the seriestis set for tonight in Pembroke.

LLOYDMINSTER (CP) - The Humboldt Broncos won their second consecutive Saskatchewan Amateur Junior Hockey League title Wednesday with a 4-3 win over the Lloydminster Lancers. The victory gave Humboldt a 4-0 sweep over the Lancers in the bestof-seven series. Bill McDougall, Brett Stewart, Brad Bergen and Garnet Kazuik scored for the Broncos. Darryl Plandowski, Todd Cornish and Travis Gould replied for Lloydminster. The Lancers outshot the Broncos 35-28 and led 1-0 after the first period. The second frame ended in a 2-2 tie.

S-P Services Humboldt Broncos are finding out just how tough the competition is going to be in the Centennial Cup. The Broncos, who coasted to the Saskatchewan Amateur Junior Hockey League title, were dumped 5-3 by the Richmond Sockeyes on Sunday and fell behind 3- 2 in their best-of-seven Abbott Cup series with the British Columbia champions. Both teams will be participating in the Centennial Cup tournament which begins Saturday in Humboldt. "The guys have stopped playing hockey," said Broncos coach Bernie Lynch. "We haven't come close to reaching our potential in this series. We're going to have to hand these guys a loss and a convincing one - before we get to the Centennial Cup. "Nobody likes to lose. Sunday's game was delayed 30 minutes as fights broke out on and off the ice. RCMP Sgt. Bob Williams said the man was arrested for breaching the peace. He was released without being charged, but Williams said charges may : still be laid. There were four fights on the ice and a fracas behind the Sockeyes' players bench that police say was started by a group of disorderly fans who threw eggs and other debris at the Richmond players. The winner of the series is slated to meet the Humboldt Broncos.

One way or another, Richmond Sockeyes are going to the Centennial Cup. If they beat Humboldt Broncos in their best-of-seven western final, the B.C. junior A hockey champion Sockeyes take the western berth into the -team national championship. But the Broncos also happen to be hosts of the Cup and if they win the final, the Sockeyes will still round out the tournament entry. The western final starts tomorrow and Tuesday in Humboldt. If there's a split, a third game will be played Wednesday with Thursday and Friday off for travel days and the best-of-seven resuming Saturday at Richmond Arena. If either team sweeps the first two games, the travel days are Wednesday and Thursday and play opens Friday at Richmond Arena. Richmond advanced to the final Thursday in Red Deer when Dave Tomlinson scored three goals to pace the Sockeyes to a 9-1 thrashing of the Alberta champion Rustlers, thereby taking the best-ofseven western semifinal 4-3. Rob Clarke, Mike Claringbull, Jason Phillips, Jim Gunn, Mike McCormick and Tony Bobbitt also tallied for the Sockeyes. Red Deer's Pete DeGraaf spoiled Jamie Stewart's shut-out bid. To get an idea of how the hockey lives of Division 1 and Division 3 players differ, the Globe accompanied a player from each division to a practice at a school from the other division and had him comment on various aspects of the program. On one side was Dave Tomlinson, a senior center from Division 1 Boston University, taking in a practice at Division 3 Curry. On the other side, Brian Horan, a senior center from Division 3 Suffolk, attended a Boston College practice session at Conte Forum.

MWC shot poorly from all areas in a 75-47 loss to Massachusetts Institute of Technology in the first round of the Eastern Invitational Tournament in Washington. The Eagles (4-20) hit only 32 percent (21-of-66) from the field, including two-for-15 from three-point range. MWC also struggled at the foul line, converting just three of 10 opportunities. MIT (11-11) scored nine straight points for an 11-2 lead and never looked back. Trae Shattuck led the Engineers with 19 points, Chris Sonne and Dave Dellagrotte had 15 each and Dave Tomlinson scored 14. Eddie Fields was the only MWC player to recah double figures, scoring 12 points. Teammate Alvin Buchanan had 11 rebounds. MIT will play host Catholic today, while the Eagles are relegated to the consolation bracket. In another first-round game, Emory topped Babson 82-76. MIDDLETON The Poynette Home Talent baseball team couldn't hold an early -run lead, as Middleton rebounded to capture an 11-5 victory in a nightleague game Thursday. Poynette took a 5-1 lead into the bottom of the fourth inning, but Middleton erased its deficit by scoring six times to take a 7-5 lead. Mike Passini had a two-run double that tied the game and Middleton took advantage of four walks in the inning. Middleton iced the win with three runs in the fifth inning and another in the sixth. Dave Tomlinson hit his fourth home run of the season in the first

inning to put Poynette up, 2-0. Tomlinson was 2-for-4 with three RBI. Jeff Gorman was 3-for-4 and Dan Tomlinson was 2-for-4, and each added run-scoring singles. Middleton remained undefeated with a 3-0 record in the night league, while Poynette slipped to 2-1. Poynette will host Baraboo Sunday. PORTAGE - Lee Realty scored seven runs in the first three innings and made it stand up for a 7-3 victory over Village Pub Thursday in Portage Fastpitch, Softball play. Donn Trinrud and Dave Tomlinson led the winners with two hits each. Mike Thorton had a double for Lee Realty's only extra base hit. Loyal Rierson led Village -Pub with two hits, while Glenn Schultz had a double. In the second game, Party House outslugged Service Drugs 11-7 despite being outhit 10-7. Jack Schaper led Party House with two hits, including a double. Terry Raimer was the winning pitcher. Todd Beeman, Al Fredrick, Mike Wenker and Ralph Ciolkosz all had two, hits for Service Drugs.
Archaeologists are excited about the finds made at the Anglo-Saxon site near Flixborough. Dig supervisor Dave Tomlinson studies one of the items, a bronze square-headed brooch.

the site were incredible - more than 400 pins and 600 loom weights with everything dating to the Middle Saxon period. Around eight or nine archaeologists under the supervision of Dave Tomlinson and Humberside's principal archaeologist Ben Whitwell have been working on the site and they have discovered post holes and wall trenches which indicate the position of 14 timbered buildings including two which had been rebuilt several times during the 200 year life of the settlement. Perhaps one of the most unusual finds was a small lead plaque inscribed with seven AngloSaxon names, several of which might be associated with the religious establishment of the 7th to 8th century Kingdom of Lindsey. Aid to memory Mr. Leahy added that a gilt bronze ring, inscribed with the first half of the alphabet, could have been used as an aid to memory or prayer was also found nearby. An impressive collection of writing implements has also been found with an elegant silver stylus - possibly unique - giving evidence of the high status of its owner. Middle Saxon material has been found at other locations in the area and the Flixborough settlement probably fitted into a network of monastic or aristocratic settlements within the Kingdom of Lindsey. Some of the finds and other dislays associated with the dig have just been displayed at a special preview at Normanby Hall where all those associated with the dig were able to view the finds as well as take the opportunity of visiting the site itself.

Last week the row between sandwich seller Dave Tomlinson and Princess Square came to a head when police were called. Mr Tomlinson, who runs George's Sandwich Service, said the centre management asked him to leave the square when he tried to deliver last week. He claims this means he is being denied the right ! to compete fairly with stores i in square which sell sandwiches. But centre manager Valerie Nowroz says the management can exclude "hawkers" from the square. She said: "Any trading activity the within discretion Princess Square is at of the landlord. "Although not obliged to do so I have attempted to accommodate Mr Tomlinson as I felt he might provide a service. "The facility of delivering in the normal manner through the service entrance was offered to him but he declined this offer." She said she considered taking legal action as it was not the policy of the management to allow "unauthorised hawkers to trade in a manner which is contrary to the interests of tenants".

Sandwich seller has marching orders. A SANDWICH seller feels he's been left with nothing but crumbs after being stopped from selling to shopkeepers in Princess Square - even though he has orders • Dave Tomlinson is with a full basket from them. left

With new director Tommy Garner, secretary Ken Hilton who joined in the close season, and chairman Dave Tomlinson, they are aiming to restore Skem United t to their former glory. The plans already include: DA new match programme which all the advertising space has been sold. [Publicising all matches on the cable system operated by Tawd Valley Cable, believed to be the first time this has happened with a Bass North West Counties club. [Displaying of match posters on 30 sites around the town, concentrating on the area around Concourse. [Continued effort to find firms wishing to take advertising boards around the ground, or to act as match and ball sponsors. Chairman Dave Tomlinson said this week: "We are delighted that it is a local company which is sponsoring us and we are extremely pleased to be carrying their logo on our strip. "We look forward to King Ford being our guests this Saturday on what is a big day for the club in more ways than one." Certainly United face a monumental task in Saturday's 1st. qualifying round match which kicks off at 3 p.m. With several players on the injured list, the start to the season with only four points from five matches up to Saturday has been a poor one by the standards expected, though it's very early days yet. The biggest loss is leading scorer Paddy Scoggins who is likely to be out for several more weeks with suspected ligament trouble, Colin Knox is also on the casualty list, Neil Payne made his first appearance in the 3-1 defeat at Darwen last weekend, and O'Neill himself was unavailable for the first four games. But the Skem player manager plans to have a real go at the Vauxhall Conference side. "We have proved already this year against Southport and Marine that we play better against the -called superior sides and that we're capable of raising our game. "They will bring a lot of support with them, the atmosphere should be just right and the adrenalin will be flowing, and we will be trying really hard to do our stuff on the field of play now that things are starting to happen off it," he said. Admission for Saturday's match will be increased to £3.50 for adults and £2.50 for pensioners and children at the request of Macclesfield as the senior club. That's the standard charge in the Conference, and in any event the extra 'gate' money received from both home and away fans will be to Skem's benefit after the final share-out. If any replay is needed it will take place at Moss Rose next Tuesday night, but in the event of a definite result this Saturday, Skem will entertain Vauxhall GM on Tuesday, kick-off 7-30 p.m. Burscough too are in F. A. Cup action this Saturday with a visit to HFS Loans League side Buxton, a tough test for Russ Perkins' team, though confidence should be at a premium after three successive victories.

PORTAGE - League-leading Lee Realty made the most of six hits Thursday evening. Lee, riding home runs by Dave Tomlinson - a pair - - and Dave Mills, clipped Party House, 8-7, in Portage Fastpitch Softball Association play. In the second game, Village Pub exploded for seven runs in the fourth inning of its 9-3 triumph over Trecek Chev-Olds. But Thursday's action belonged to Lee, particularily Tomlinson and Mills. Tomlinson, a left fielder, smacked a two-run homer in the second inning and added a grand slam in a five-run fourth that gave Lee a 7-2 cushion. However, Party House rallied in the top of the fifth. Brad Condon, Todd Beeman and Tim Larkin clubbed doubles, while teammates Rick Lobenstein and Neil Hardt added singles as Party House knotted the game at 7-7. In Lee's half of the frame, Mills

THE budding soccer stars of the Junior Alex showed their skills as they brushed aside opposition from Preston, Rotherham and Watford in a five-a-side competition at the weekend. Out of four competitions involving teams of 10's, Under 12s, Under. 148 and Under16s the Alex youngsters emerged victorious in every group apart from the Under 12s. The day proved very successful with the good weather encouraging a crowd of about 300 to cheer on the teams. It was organised by the Junior Alex and sponsored by Barclays. managed by Andy MarThe Under 108; were riot and John Ford, the Under 12s Alex keeper Dean Greygoose, the Under 14s by Dave Tomlinson and the Under 16s by Terry Wood. After the event Dave Tomlinson thanked all those who supported the youngsters and Barclays their sponsorship.
