BC got untracked midway through the period and tested Fish, who made some sensational saves. It was a physical period, with referee Frank Cole calling five penalties against each team. Territorially, the Terriers also had the upper hand in the second period, outshooting BC, 14-9, but BC scored twice and BU once. -BC tied the game at 8:13 on : a power play goal by Dan Shea, who hit on a high, screened shot that hit Fish's glove and slipped into the cage. BU got that one back in a hurry when Kelfer scored from the middle of the right faceoff circle at 9:20 with help from Dave Tomlinson and Tom Dion. BU continued to carry the play, Littman continued to play tough and the Eagles tied it at 2 at 17:45 when Steve Scheifele intercepted a BU pass and scored from the mouth of the goal. BC's Bill Nolan was caught tripping at 6:00 of the third, and eight seconds later Dion scored from the right | point on a screened shot. Kelfer and Tomlinson assisted on the goal. BC tied it at 13:07 when Jeff O'Neill picked up a Mark Dennehy rebound and scored from 15 feet out. BU played stronger in the third period. Sacco and his mates were sensational.

PORTAGE After watching its first two games get wiped out by inclement weather, Village Pub finally opened its fastpitch softball season Thursday and with a victory. The Pub, riding a three-run second inning and a four-run third, topped Trecek Chev-Olds, 8-1. Village Pub managed only two hits in the contest, but was aided by numerous walks. Dave Howe smacked a double, while Jim Staveness belted a single for the winners. Dan Paul and Kevin Stillson each scored two runs. Wayne Sainsbury went 2-for-4, including a triple, for Trecek, now winless in three attempts. In one other game, Party House kept its record unblemished with a 7-6 triumph over Lee Realty. Todd Beeman cracked two doubles, while teammate Brad Condon ripped a triple for 3-0 Party House. Dave Tomlinson collected three safeties in four at-bats for Lee Realty. Action resumes tonight with Fishermen's Cove battling Party House at 7 p.m. and Service Drug facing Village Pub at 8:30. had to come the length of the floor to get it. Coast Guard dropped back into its zone and didn't challenge the Engineers until they had moved the ball over the midcourt line. With one second to go, Arik Brown fired from just outside the three-point range and made it. In the five minute overtime, Oditt gave the Bears the lead at 80-78 at 1:12, but Chris Sonne tied things with a pair of foul shots at 1:40 and Dave Tomlinson, who spent a good part of the night at the foul line and hit 11 for 14 there, put his team ahead for the first time since the 4:34 point with a shot from under the hoop. From then on the Engineers took control as the Bears lost three starters - John Murphy, Oditt and Matt Perciak - via the five foul route. The Bears, who shot a blistering 76 percent on 19 baskets in 25 first half tries, did an about face in the second half when they were 29 percent on 10 for 34 shooting. Barry, who gave his team a pep talk after the game, said he was disappointed with the lack of leadership in the final stages. "Nobody seemed to want to come forward and take charge out there when the going got tough at the end," Barry said. "It was the same way in the Norwich game when we fell apart at of a game we should have won. I've lost tough theames before, but this one tonight had to be just about the toughest I've experienced in my coaching career." While the Bears went from riches to rags in the game, the Engineers went the other way. Coach Leo Osgood, who said he laid it on the line for his charges at halftime, noted it might have done some good. "I think, though, a couple of three-pointers we hit at the end of the first half got us going and we hit some key three-point baskets near the end of the game when we needed them,' Osgood said. 3-pointers pace Chancellors Worcester, Mass. - Ten three-pointers helped St. Thomas More to a 94-89 defeat of the Holy Cross junior varsity Monday. Cashman responded big-time after being yanked Friday after giving up seven goals to the Sioux through two: periods. "I like a game where the pressure is on me," he said. "When I'm really nervous going into a game, I play better." North Dakota never gave his power play Sunday and PO co 3-for-17 for nerves a test, going 0-for-6 on the the three games after coming into the series convering at about 30 percent. UND did have a 5-on-3 power play for 23 seconds midway through the second period, but managed just one shot on net, a blast from the left point by Jason Herter. After that, BU wrapped it up in the third period on Amonte's power-play goal at 6:48 and Dave Tomlinson's howitzer at 19:20, both of those goals coming against Sioux senior goalie Chris Dickson. Dickson replaced Couture late in the second period after Couture drew a 10-minute misconduct for words to referee Steve Piotrowski. Couture and Dickson combined for 28 saves, 15 by Couture. PORTAGE - Lee Realty strengthened its hold on first place, while Rio's Village Pub picked up a victory in Portage Fastpitch Softball Association play Thursday evening. 0 Riendeau turns aside host Detroit to force Game 7 DETROIT (AP) St. Louis Blues, fighting for a small piece of NHL history, evened their Norris Division series at 3-3 on goals by Dave Tomlinson, Jeff Brown and Dave Lowry in a 3-0 victory over Detroit Red Wings on Sunday night. It was the fifth shutout this season for Vincent Riendeau, who handled 23 shots. The Blues had 21 shots on Tim Cheveldae. It was the first time Detroit had been held scoreless in a playoff game since 1978 and the first shutout by the Blues since 1984. The seventh and deciding game of the National Hockey League series will be Tuesday night in St. Louis. The Blues are bidding to become the eighth team to rally from a 3-1 deficit to win a playoff series. It was last done

Freshman Ed Peterson scored 23 points, including a crucial three-pointer late in the second half, to propel Yale to a 77-68 victory over George Washington in Washington. Peterson made two free throws with 5:57 remaining to break a 57-57 tie and give Yale the lead for good. Then, with Yale nursing a 63-61 lead, he hit a three-pointer from the top of the key for a 66-61 advantage with 3:03 left. George Washington, which scored only two baskets in the final 6:18, never cut the gap below 4 points the rest of the way. Mike Ryan and Travis McCready each scored 11 for Yale, which made 32 free throws, including 9 in the final two minutes. UMass-Boston got 29 points from Stephen Donnelly and captured the Harbor Invitational with a 98- 83 win over Clark in Dorchester. Clark held a 27-26 lead when UMass-Boston reeled off 21 straight points en route to a 53-32 halftime lead. Vaughn Ames had 25 points and 15 rebounds to lead Old Westbury over Emerson, 87-60, in the consolation game. David Brown showed the way with 18 points and Anton Brown added 15 as Massachusetts crushed Southern Connecticut, 84-61, in Amherst. Jim Barton had 34 points and John McKay added 24 as Dartmouth nipped Kent State, 88-86, in Charlottesville, Va. Ed Cirane's game-high 24 points enabled Suffolk break away from a 41-41 halftime tie for an 85-73 to decision over Framingham State in Cambridge. Leonard Bass had 24 points as Westfield State took a 92-89 overtime decision over Southeastern Mass. in Westfield despite 36 points by Waldemar Sender. Matt Hancock had 21 points and Tom Dorion added 17 to boost Colby over Roanoke in Salem, Va. Gary Bestman and Bill Shepard each had 18 points to lift Western Connecticut over Fitchburg State, 86-67, in Danbury, Conn. Jay Fraser led the way with 16 points and Dave Tomlinson came off the bench to score 14 as MIT cruised to a 69-58 triumph over Gordon in Cambridge. John Schmuck had 24 points and Jim Ferrer 23 as Bentley topped New Hampshire College, 88-76, in Manchester, N.H. Orlando Vandross scored 32 points and four other AIC players were in double figures in a 95-77 romp over host Lowell. Lowell's Leo Parent led all scorers with 39. Amherst routed host Worcester State, 91-55, behind Scott Saft's 22 points and 21 by Yram Groff. Pace nipped Merrimack, 54-52, in New York with only Matt Baldini in double figures with 13 points. Steve Scott led Merrimack with 15.

Boston University beat Providence 7-5 in the Hockey East semifinals at Boston Garden Friday night. BC had just regained a 4-3 lead with 2: 17 left in the middle period on an, Ed Ronan goal, but Amonte's goal really took the steam out of the Friars, who had rallied from 2-0 and 3-2 deficits to tie it 3-2. Peter Ahola took a drive from the point that sailed wide of Providence goalie Brad Mullahy. It deflected off the boards, came out the other side and Amonte jammed the puck in. Dave Tomlinson's power play goal at the 4:50 mark of the third period made it 6-3 and the Friars couldn't close to within two goals the rest of the e way. Rob Gaudreau and Shaun Kane sandwiched Friar goals around one by BU's Chris McCann to close out the scoring. BU, now 24-10-2, will play the winner of the Maine-Northeastern game in Sunday's 5 p.m. final. Providence finished at 21-10- 2. BU had bolted to a 2-0 lead in the first period on goals by Ahola and Dave Tomlinson. Providence rallied to tie it in the second period on goals by Brian McCarthy and Gaudreau before Keith Tkachuk regained the lead for BU and Larry Rooney equalized for Providence. Ed Ronan regained the lead for •BU with 2:17 left in the middle period as he was alone in the slot, took a pass from Petteri Koskimaki and fired a 20-foot slap shot past Mullahy's blocker side. The Friars were still very much alive, but then came the Amonte goal.

CONGRATULATIONS to Aldershot soccer club's chief groundsman Dave Tomlinson (above) who marked out a double celebration this week, writes PETER HUTCHINSON. Hard-working Dave chalked up 25 years of keeping the pitch in shape - and this year is also his 25th wedding anniversary! On top of that his son Chris scored for Aldershot Youth in the 1-1 draw with Horndean at the Recreation Ground on Tuesday i in an FA Youth Cup match. Father Dave is also manager of Aldershot's Youth team! Dave lives with his wife Janet at Beaumont Park in Aldershot. They have two daughters and two sons. EMPLOYEE Dave first took charge of the Recreation Ground as a council employee 25 years ago last Saturday. He was born in the town and has supported Aldershot since a youngster. And Dave - age 46 - made i it clear: "Aldershot soccer club is the love of my life. When the team suffers, I suffer." He added: "I cannot speak highly enough of the club. I get on well with all of them and my big ambition before I retire is to set up the pitch for Aldershot's first match in Division One. People might laugh - but that is my hope." 'PROUD' And his wife Janet confirmed: "I am very proud of Dave. He would do anything for Aldershot Football Club." Certainly the Recreation Ground pitch is regarded as one of the best in the Football League. * A lot of the credit must go to that loving care of Dave Tomlinson. Keep up the good work Dave - and we all hope your dreams of Division One turn into reality! by EDDIE TRUSLER. Picture

RUGBY UNION White was stopped just short of the line, but his inside pass to Neil Chapman put the centre clear for Mold's second try. This brought Loughor to life and they replied with a penalty from fly half Frazer Lloyd. Loughor squandered four penalties before Mold punished them with a penalty from Gareth Jones. Mold extended their lead before the interval. A crunching tackle from Dave Tomlinson sent the ball loose and replacement Andrew Smith picked up and fed Roger Bold who went over from close range for a converted try.

In the second half, the Clwyd side maintained their momentum and after nine minutes Dave Tomlinson caught the Loughor winger in possession. From the ruck that followed Neil Chapman found Gareth Jones who cut inside a defender for a try which he converted. Loughor salvaged some pride with a try soon after from flanker Mike Davies, but this served only to inspire Mold further. Winger Brynley Wynne outpaced the Loughor defence for a 60 metre try. 3-1 in the series after finishing the regular season 20 points ahead of their Alberta rivals. Calgary has faced heavy criticism in the past for bowing out of the playoffs early including last year when the Los Angeles Kings beat the Flames. A loss to Edmonton Sunday would have been hard for the team and fans to take. "It obviously wasn't a great play on my part," said Messier, the Oilers' captain, in a solemn dressing room that was almost deserted. "Fleury got the puck on the fly and all our guys were just standing still. didn't have a chance of catchBut we've got to forget about it now and regroup and go at it for game seven. It should be a great game." •Brett Hull never got a shot on goal. Instead, the St. Louis Blues relied on defence to stave off elimination in their Norris Division series with the Detroit Red Wings. Fighting for a small piece of NHL history, the Blues evened the bestof-seven series at 3-3 on goals by Dave Tomlinson, Jeff Brown and Dave Lowry in a 3-0 victory over the Red Wings. "When they see Adam Oates throwing his body around like he did tonight, the other guys pay attention," Hull said. "You know, good defence from us fires us up the same as good offence does." Hull led the NHL with 86 regularseason goals. But the Blues were third in the NHL on defence, allowing only 3.13 goals a game. "I think my reputation is as a goalscorer, but this team's reputation is a pretty good defensive club." Hull said." It was the fifth shutout this season for Vincent Riendeau, who handled 23 shots. The Blues had 21 shots on Tim Cheveldae. It was the first time Detroit had been held scoreless in a playoff game since 1978 and the first shutout by the Blues since 1984. The seventh and deciding game will be Tuesday night in St. Louis.

CONGRATULATIONS to Aldershot soccer club's chief groundsman Dave Tomlinson (above) who marked out a double celebration this week, writes PETER HUTCHINSON.

Hard-working Dave chalked up 25 years of keeping the pitch in shape - and this year is also his 25th wedding anniversary! On top of that his son Chris scored for Aldershot Youth in the 1-1 draw with Horndean at the Recreation Ground on Tuesday in an FA Youth Cup match. Father Dave is also manager of Aldershot's Youth team! Dave lives with his wife Janet at Beaumont Park in Aldershot. They have two daughters and two sons. EMPLOYEE Dave first took charge of the Recreation Ground as a council employee 25 years ago last Saturday. He was born in the town and has supported Aldershot since a youngster. And Dave - - age 46 - made: it clear: "Aldershot soccer club is the love of my life. When the team suffers, I suffer." He added: "I cannot speak highly enough of the club. I get on well with all of them and my big ambition before I retire is to set up the pitch for Aldershot's first match in Division One. People might laugh - but that is my hope." 'PROUD' And his wife Janet confirmed: "I am very proud of Dave. He would do anything for Aldershot Football Club." Certainly the Recreation Ground pitch is regarded as one of the best in the Football League. * A lot of the credit must go to that loving care of Dave Tomlinson. Keep up the good work Dave - and we all hope your dreams of Division One turn into reality! Picture by EDDIE TRUSLER.

PORTAGE Lee Realty made it official Tuesday night - the final evening of competition. Lee, riding Clark Schlender's five-hitter, blanked Village Pub 5-0 and in the process won the Portage Fastpitch Softball Association league championship. Lee finished the season with a 16-4 record. Service Drug Store took second with a 15-5 mark. The game was scoreless until the fourth inning when Lee erupted for a pair of runs. Jerry Kabele and Steve Jacobs led off with singles. After Donn Trinrud sacrificed both runners, Dave Tomlinson reached on an error. Bryan Albrecht then followed with a run-scoring double. Lee added two more in the fifth. Jeff Schlender and Tom Gray co collected base hits and both came home on a single by Jacobs. Mike Barreau's single drove home the final run in the sixth. Meanwhile, Clark Schlender struck out four Village Pub batters in going the distance. He allowed two doubles to Dave Howe. Gastineau may try boxing VANCOUVER, British Columbia (AP) Former NFL Pro Bowl defensive end Mark Gastineau may announce Wednesday that he is planning a career in boxing, according to a family friend. **T think he's made up his mind he wants. to fight,' said Murray Pezim, a local businesseman and family friend who has offered his services as Gastineau's manager. Pezim said Tuesday the flamboyant lineman and his girlfriend, actress Brigitte Nielsen, were en route to Vancouver. The New York Jets placed Gastineau, who was known for his sack dance over fallen quarterbacks, on the reserve-retired list last October but still retain his playing rights. The 6-foot-5, 275-pound lineman, who entered the NFL in 1979, recorded 107½ sacks and was a five-time Pro Bowl selection. Gastineau is the second football player in recent months to contemplate a foray into the boxing ring. Offensive lineman Tony Mandarich, taken second overall in the NFL draft this year by the Green Bay has indicated a desire to Packers, take on world heavyweight champion Mike Tyson. Tyson's manager said Tuesday he was unimpressed by such offers.

Four minutes later, Fisher's Paul Collins almost managed a spectacular won-goal. Trying to clear Dave Tomlinsons free-kick from the right edge of the box, the big defender's full-length diving header rebounded to safety off the post. Only Askey's goal separated the teams at half-time, although Macc could have had at least three more. Burr, put through by Askey, blasted over from a good position after 34 minutes, and soon after, he was too high again after Bastock could only parry Paul Kendall's powerful leader from Tomlinson's corner. And a minute later, with the home defence straded, Steve Hanlon's angler effort was turned round the post by Bastock. Hanlon and Kendall both went close after the break, but it was Burr, ironically following Fisher's best spell, who got No2 in the 62nd minute.

CONGRATULATIONS to Aldershot soccer club's chief groundsman Dave Tomlinson (above) who marked out a double celebration this week, writes PETER HUTCHINSON.

CONGLETON only just held on to their 100 per cent record in the Men's Division 1 of the Crewe and District Badminton League in their recent fixture at home to strugglers Ruskin. An early injury to David Marshall meant he and Shing Sven had to concede their first game to Ruskin's Peter Barber and Mike Hitchem. Gavin Simpson and Andy Coppack brought the match level by beating Steve Wrench and Nigel Downes 21-14. Dave Tomlinson and Tony Ford restored Ruskin'% lead in beating Alan Rogers and Gary McDonald 21-14. The lead changed sides throughout the night until at 4-4 Gavin Simpson and Andy Coppack finally gave Congleton the victory by defeating Dave Tomlinson and Tony Ford 21-8. This win makes Congleton clear favourites for the title with a record of nine wins out of nine played.

PORTAGE - Two of the top three teams in the Portage Fastpitch Softball Association kept up its winning ways in competition Wednesday evening. Lee Realty, riding atop the league with a sizzling 10-2 record. posted a 12-5 triumph over Trecek. Chev-Olds. In the second game, Party House cruised to a 13-6 victory over Village Pub. Party House, which banged out 13 hits, improved to 7-4. In the opener, Lee Realty, winners of nine straight, tallied four runs in the first frame and never looked back. Jeff Schlender drew a one-out walk in the first. Steve Jacobs followed with a run-scoring double. Bryan Albrecht and Dave Mills then cracked back-to-back home runs. Lee added one run in the third before exploding for seven in the fourth of the five-inning contest. Jacobs clubbed a triple, while Dave Tomlinson smacked a homer to highlight the big inning. Lee collected seven extra base hits. • Devin Beeman, Dan Daley and John Walker picked up two hits apiece for winless Trecek. Party House wasted little time in controlling its win over Village Pub. The House scored six times in the second inning. Rick Lobenstein belted a triple and Jim Schumann added a double in the uprising. A three-run fourth for Party House featured consecutive roundtrippers by Dan Fearing and Lobenstein. Fearing's was a tworun blast. Loyal Reierson and Ken Staveness were perfect in three trips to the plate for Village Pub, which rapped out 13 hits. PORTAGE - Both winners in Portage Softball Association fast pitch action Wednesday made the most of their two hits. Lee Realty, paced by Dave Tomlinson's - two-run homer, topped league-leading Service Drug Store, 2-1. After Service grabbed a 1-0 lead in the fifth, Bryan Albrecht of Lee Realty drew a one-out walk in the bottom of the fifth. After a pop out, Tomlinson belted his round-tripper. The only other hit for Lee was a single by Adam Stein. Service managed five safeties off of Lee starter and winner Clark Schlender, but they were scattered over seven innings. In the nightcap at Veteran's Park, singles by Dave Hamilton and Scott Bublitz were all Party House could muster, but that was enough as they downed Trecek Motors, 9-0. The Party House was aided by nine walks and three hit-batters. Trecek garnered three singles off of winning pitcher Steve Drost. Party House improved to 4-11,

PORTAGE Dave Tomlinson drilled three hits to power leagueleading Lee Realty to a 7-1 victory over Service Drugs Wednesday in Portage fastpitch softball play. Lee Realty moved to 7-1, a full game ahead of Martin Security at 5-1. Service Drugs dipped to 2-5 with the loss. Lee Realty led 2-1 after 4½ innings, but put the game out of reach with two runs in the fifth and three more in the sixth. Tomlinson, the team's left fielder, had two singles and a double. Steve Jacobs added a triple in Lee's eighthit attack. Southpaw Tom Blum pitched the first five innings and gained the win. Clark Schlender pitched shutout ball the final two frames. Jeff Vaningan and Bill Greene each had two hits for Service Drugs. Tom Hilgendorf's double was the team's only extra base rap. Left-hander Al Fredrick suffered the pitching defeat. In the second game, Fort Tavern scored in each of the first four innings and went on to an 8-6 triumph over Party House. Fort Tavern improved to 2-4,

Dave Tomlinson, the organiser, said: "God is not a theologian, a fundamentalist or a Liberal. God is freedom." Everyone joined in for the hymn, It's a Wonderful World by Louis Armstrong. Anyone in a cassock might have felt a touch uncomfortable at Harry having to rub shoulders with the punks, goths, skinheads and bikers in attendance. Shaven-headed Dave Tomlinson, 43, who founded Harry five years ago and roars around the showground on his 1200cc Kawasaki motorcycle, said it was an attempt at common-sense Christianity, in contrast to the "nonsense" found in many churches: "Jesus Christ would feel more comfortable at Harry than in the cosy, rather smug church we've got today. In 144 NHL games with the Quebec Nordiques and New York Rangers, he has scored 31 goals and 50 assists. He has scored everywhere he's gone, but NHL totals haven't been enough to keep him his there. Last season, he skated for Flint, Mich., and Phoenix in the IHL. He won the Ontario Hockey championship in 1985-86 with 131 League scoring points while playing for Belleville and Hamilton. Kent Manderville • Scoring 26 points in 26 games helped him earn the Rookie-of-the-Year award in the Eastern Collegiate Athletic Conference last year, During the same season, he helped Canada's national junior team to the world gold in Finland. The Calgary Flames own his NHL rights after taking him 24th overall in the 1989 draft. He turned 19 years old in April and is the youngest player on the Canada roster. Pat Murray - He was drafted in the second round by Philadelphia in 1988 but has spent three years developing his skills at Michigan State University. He seems to have perfected his passing game, which produce a school-record 60 assists last seahelped son. His 84 points over last year's 45-game schedule made him the No. 3 scorer in the Central Collegiate Hockey Association. Scott Pellerin - At only 20 years old, he has a more impressive resume than many hockey players twice his age. He won the Centennial Cup with the Notre Dame Hounds in 1987-88 and helped Canada win a national junior title in Helsinki this winter. In 1988-89 at the University of Maine, he was the counfreshman scorer with 29 goals and was voted try's top East Rookie of the Year. In 1989-90, he had Hockey 22 goals and 34 assists for Maine. The New Jersey Devils spent a third-round draft choice on him in 1989. Gary Shuchuk - When the Saskatoon Blades' Scott Scissons bowed out of the Goodwill Games, Canada made this high-scoring 23-year-old his replacement. Last season, he was named NCAA AllAmerican while playing for the University of Wisconsin. In 46 games, he scored 41 goals and 40 assists for 81 points. Shuchuk is a 5-foot-10, 190-pound forward who will wear jersey No. 14. Dave Tomlinson - Look for him to shadow the optop scoring threat. His record says he has ponent's the smarts and defensive mentality to shut down franchise player, Last year at Boston University, he was selected the league's best defensive forward and the school's top student athlete. He's also a threat to having collected 15 goals and 22 assists in 43 score, games last season.

Boston University beat Providence 7-5 in the Hockey East semifinals at Boston Garden Friday night. BC had just regained a 4-3 lead with 2:17 left in the middle period on an Ed Ronan goal, but Amonte's goal really took the steam out of the Friars, who had rallied from 2-0 and 3-2 deficits to tie it 3-2. Peter Ahola took a drive from the point that sailed wide of Providence goalie Brad Mullahy. It deflected off the boards, came out the other side and Amonte jammed the puck in. Dave Tomlinson's power play goal at the 4:50 mark of the third period made it 6-3 and the Friars couldn't close to within two goals the rest of the way. Rob Gaudreau and Shaun Kane sandwiched Friar goals around by BU's Chris McCann to close out the scoring. BU, now 24-10-2, will play the winner of the Maine-Northeastern game in Sunday's 5 p.m. final. Providence finished at 21-10- 2. BU had bolted to a 2-0 lead in the first period on goals by Ahola and Dave Tomlinson. Providence rallied to tie it in the second period on goals by Brian McCarthy and Gaudreau before Keith Tkachuk regained the lead for BU and Larry Rooney equalized for Providence. Ed Ronan regained the lead for •BU with 2:17 left in the in the middle period as he was alone slot, took a pass from Petteri Koskimaki and fired a 20-foot slap shot past Mullahy's blocker side. The Friars were still very much alive, but then came the Amonte goal.
Dave Tomlinson with a working

Edmonton In the days after Marc Lepine slaughtered 14 female Montreal university students, many Canadian men questioned their own courage. What would they have done if they had been in the classroom when Lepine fired his semi-automatic rifle into the air and ordered the men to leave? Would they have followed everyone else out the door? Or would they have confronted the gunman? One man with no doubts Dave Tomlinson, 56, of Edmonton, president of the National Firearms Association, a small but vocal Canadian gun lobby. Tomlinson says that as soon as Lepine fired, he would have known the e killer, was serious about using the 100-percent, all out attack® using* anything and everything I've got because even if he gets me, the chances are I can knock him out of action long enough so that somebody else can pile on." His decision to attack would have been automatic, Tomlinson says, since he's trained himself to respond in this manner. "If you wait until the day when you're looking at a situation and saying, *My God, in the next few minutes I'm going to be mutilated or killed,' and you try to make your decision to attack then, it's far too late because you temporize and fiddle around until he's had you." One thing in his favor, Tomlinson says, would be that Lepine would get only one, clear shot at him because the Sturm Ruger semi-automatic .223-calibre rifle which Lepine used is extremely difficult to aim after one shot, a fact Tomlinson would have instantly realized from his long years of studying guns.

Boston University overcame a 3-1 deficit with four seond-period goals to turn back Colgate, 5-4, last night in Hamilton, N.Y. Jamie Cooke's goal eight seconds into the second period was the third straight for Colgate after freshman Dave Dahlberg gave the Terriers a 1-0 lead at 6:29 of the opening period. BU began its comeback with Joe Sacco's goal at 3:25 and the Terriers tied it on Shawn McEachern's goal at 10:05. Marc Dupere's second goal of the game at 12:29 regained the lead for Colgate before Dave Tomlinson and freshman Tony Amonte got it back for BU with late second-period scores. Lowell 6, Kent State 4 - Craig Charron and Scott MacPherson each scored two goals to spark Lowell in Kent, Ohio. Lowell took charge with three consecutive second-period goals by MacPherson, Charron and Gerry Daley that broke a 1-1 tie. Kent State got back in it when Ross Antonini's goal late in the second period, and Pete Calliri's score early in the third period cut Lowell's lead to 4-3. Maine 6, Michigan-Dearborn 3 - Martin Robitaille collected a goal and three assists to spark Maine in Orono. Freshmen Jim Montgomery and Jean-Yves Roy and sophomores Mike Barkley, Keith Carney and Scott Pellerin also scored for Maine. Carney broke open a 2-1 game when he beat MichiganDearborn goalie Dave Church on the short side from the top of the faceoff circle. Mark Spease, Dave O'Kragly and Brian Dawson scored for Michigan-Dearborn. Scott King stopped 16 shots in the Maine net while Church had 19 saves. New Hampshire 5, Vermont 3 - UNH snapped a 2-2 tie with three third-period goals in Durham, N.H. Mark McGinn, Joe Flanagan and Chris Winnes scored for UNH, with Winnes' goal giving the Wildcats a two-goal edge with 1:09 remaining. Ricker Love scored a pair of second-period goals for Vermont that gave the Catamounts a short-lived 2-1 lead. Merrimack 4, St. Cloud 3 - Ed Locke's second goal of the game with 1:04 remaining provided Merrimack with the victory in St. Cloud, Minn. Yannick Gosselin was busy in goal for Merrimack, making 46 saves. Clarkson 11, McGill 2 - Mark Tretowicz had a hat trick, and eight other players scored for Clarkson at Potsdam, N.Y.

DETROIT - The St. Louis Blues, fighting for a small piece of NHL history, evened their Norris Division series at 3-3 on goals by Dave Tomlinson, Jeff Brown and Dave Lowry in a 3-0 victory over the Detroit Red Wings last night. It was the fifth shutout this season for Vincent Riendeau, who handled 23 shots. The Blues had 21 NHL shots on Tim Cheveldae. It was the first time Detroit had been held scoreless in a playoff game since 1978 and the first shutout by the Blues since 1984. The seventh and deciding game of the series will be played tomorrow night in St. Louis. The Blues are bidding to become the eighth team to rally from a 3-1 deficit to win an NHL playoff series. It was last done by the Edmonton Oilers last season in the first round against Winnipeg. The New York Islanders have done it twice and the Red Wings once. Other teams to accomplish the feat are Toronto, Washington and Los Angeles. In previous series which have gone seven games, St. Louis is 4-4, the Red Wings 7-5. Tomlinson gave St. Louis a 1-0 lead with his first NHL playoff goal at 7:00 of the first period, Brown's second goal came at 2:24 of the second period and Lowry with 1:41 left in the game. A MIDDLE Saxon settlement which was uncovered at Flixborough looks set to reveal fascinating insights on the life and times of the period. Scunthorpe Evening Telegraph reporter PHIL GOULD went to see some of the finds and talk about their significance with dig supervisor Dave Tomlinson.

The discovery has already been hailed as a Saxon Pompeii as many of the finds have been preserved in sand which over the years had been windswept across the site. Humberside archaeological unit dig supervisor Dave Tomlinson believed the uncovering of the site was of great significance for a number of reasons. "The Flixborough site was occupied during the Middle Saxon time between 670 and 870 AD and we don't know great deal about that period of history. "It will also give good information about the time, as it had not been occupied prior to the settlement and after the inhabitants left it remained unoccupied." By Pete Warner Of the NEWS Staff : Ellsworth's Jim Card, who helped spark the Eagles to an Eastern Maine Class B runnerup finish, has been selected as the Most Valuable Player in Penobscot Valley Conference Class B baseball. Card is one of five Ellsworth play. ers named to the squad, which was drawn from the five schools representing PVC 'B' - Ellsworth, Bucksport, Hermon, Mount Desert Island and Orono. Ellsworth Coach Jack Scott, who guided the Eagles to a 16-3 record, was named the conference Coach of the Year in balloting of the league's coaches. Bucksport placed four players on the PVC All-Star squad, while MDI accounted for three, Orono two and Hermon one. Ellsworth's other representatives are senior catcher Chad Sattler, junior outfielders Jason' Clarke and Mat Brown, and senior pitcher Joe Domagala. Bucksport's All-Star contingent is made up of senior third baseman Matt Leathers, senior shortstop Todd Gilley, senior second baseman Rick Jones and senior pitcher Mark Smith. Senior pitcher Dave Tomlinson, senior catcher Chris Linscott (utility) and sophomore designated hitter Chip Applegate were MDI's honorees. Senior first baseman Casey Garland and sophomore third baseman Chad Eddy (utility) of Orono and Hermon senior outfielder David Hannan also earned the praise •of league coaches for their efforts. Card edged out Bucksport's Leathers in the MVP balloting by virtue of his consistency in the field and at the plate. The 6-foot-1, 170-pounder led the conference with a .564 batting average, including four home runs and 19 runs batted in. Leathers was a valuable performer for Coach Dave Gonyar's Golden Bucks, The rugged 6-2, 217-pounder

Hard-working Dave chalked up 25 years of keeping the pitch in shape - and this year is also his 25th wedding anniversary! On top of that his son Chris scored for Aldershot Youth in the 1-1 draw with Horndean at the Recreation Ground on Tuesday in an FA Youth Cup match. A Father Dave is also manager of Aldershot's Youth team! Dave lives with his wife Janet at Beaumont Park in Aldershot. They have two daughters and two sons. EMPLOYEE Dave first took charge of the Recreation Ground as a council employee 25 years ago last Saturday. He was born in the town and has supported Aldershot since a youngster. And Dave - age 46 - made it clear: "Aldershot soccer club is the love of my life. When the team suffers, I suffer." He added: "I cannot speak highly enough of the club. I get on well with all of them and my big ambition before I retire is to set up the pitch for Aldershot's first match in Division One. People might laugh - but that is my hope.' 'PROUD' And his wife Janet confirmed: "I am very proud of Dave. He would do anything for Aldershot Football Club." I Certainly the Recreation Ground pitch is regarded as one of the best in the Football League. * A lot of the credit must go to that loving care of Dave Tomlinson. Keep up the good work Dave - and we all hope your dreams of Division One turn into reality! Picture by EDDIE TRUSLER.
Gallini CONGRATULATIONS to Aldershot soccer club's chief groundsman Dave Tomlinson (above) who marked out a double celebration this week, writes PETER HUTCHINSON.

Richmond has one the most potent lineups the division, boasting the top goalscorer in the league, Rob Clarke, who potted 65 goals in 49 games, as well as snipers Dave Tomlinson (43-65-108) and Brian Moller (27-64-91). While the defence is unspectacular, the Sockeyes are solid in goal, with Frank Romeo, who led the division with a goals against mark of 2.95. All that notwithstanding, Sockeye coach Orland Kurtenbach hasn't been looking farther ahead in the playoffs. "We have to take this series one game at a time," said Kurtenbach in a telephone interview with The Daily Free Press. "All the games prior to the last two were very close. We're going to have to put in a good effort.

PORTAGE •Lee Realty clinched at least a tie for the league crown after dumping Party House, 13-3, in Portage Fastpitch Softball Association action Thursday night. Lee improved to 15-3 with two games remaining - Tuesday versus Fisherman's Cove and a makeup contest against Village Pub. No date has been set for that matchup. Service Drug Store is currently second with an 11-5 mark. Service Drug has four games remaining. In one other contest Thursday, Village Pub dumped Trecek ChevOlds, 19-11, in three innings. Lee Realty put the game away in the second inning by scoring nine runs. Lee sent 15 batters to the plate. Center fielder Kris Hall and left fielder Dave Tomlinson smacked home runs, while catcher Dave Mills cracked a triple and first baseman Bryan Albrecht drilled : a double in the outburst. Lee finished with 11 hits, including a perfect 4-for-4 by Hall. Party House also smashed a couple of homers - solo shots by Dan Fearing and Rick Lobenstein. In the evening's opener, Village Pub sent 14 batters to the plate in the second inning and- -in the process scored eight runs. Ken Staveness, Donn Paul and Joe Westcott clubbed doubles.

Terriers never recovered, even though Mike Lappin's shot darted past PC's Matt Merten to tie the game, 4-4, at 10:56. Rick Bennett's power-play goal with 5:39 to play gave the Friars a 5-4 lead, which meant BU needed two goals to send the series into overtime but PC's Mario Aube scored to settle the issue. PC beat BU for the fourth time in five games this season. Mattice, who scored 13 goals his freshman season, had just 5 entering last night's game and was used sparingly Friday by coach Mike McShane. "He's got a world of talent." said McShane, whose team survived a total of 32 penalties in the two games - 16 each night. "I've tried him on different lines and I don't know if it was a matter of confidence or what. Tonight that talent really showed.' For BU coach Jack Parker, it was a matter of his team failing to mount much of anything when playing at even strength. "Three of our four goals were with a two-man advantage, said Parker, whose team had 16 power plays to PC's seven over the two games but cashed in just four times. "We had no flow through center ice and Providence not only forechecked well, they always had three men back to break us up." BU clicked early when Mike Sullivan (playing for the first time in four games because of a shoulder injury) scored five minutes into the game. So it became a 55-minute game, and Providence, which hadn't won at BU in four years until Friday night, never let the -Terriers take command. -Gord Cruikshank beat Fish from 25 feet at 9:15 for his 96th career goal, making him the school's all-time leading goal scorer. The Friars (13-16-5) made it 2- 1 on an all-freshman goal 8:35 Into the second period. Larry Rooney finished off a bang-bang passing play with Pat Madigan and lifted a liner over Fish's shoulder from 15 feet. Providence weht up, 3-1, less than two minutes later when Mattice took a Mike Dempsey pass and had Fish at his mercy. BU (14-17-3). playing without Injured Ville Kentala, a 15-goal scorer, lost forward Matt Pesklewis after the first period because of a fractured collarbone - but the Terriers mounted a comeback. Joe Sacco took a Dave Tomlinson feed and cut in front to beat Merten, and when the Terriers put on more pressure, Rooney was called for holding with 8:05 left in the period. It became a two-man advantage four seconds later when Jim Hughes was called for tripping. Tom Dion tied the game with a 50-foot shot to the corner. But then Mattice, Bennett & Co. took over. "The only thing I was worried about down the stretch." said McShane, "was the penalties. But we kept our poise. Give credit to Bennett, too. He had a broken hand earlier this season and was playing some defense, but he got a big goal tonight playing up front." But no goal was bigger than Mattice's second.

paragraphs.text: Boston University beat Providence 7-5 in the Hockey East semifinals at Boston Garden Friday night. BC had just regained a 4-3 lead with 2:17 left in the middle period on an Ed Ronan goal, but Amonte's goal really took the steam out of the Friars, who had rallied from 2-0 and 3-2 deficits to tie it 3-2. Peter Ahola took a drive from the point that sailed wide of Providence goalie Brad Mullahy. It deflected off the boards, came out the other side and Amonte jammed the puck in. Dave Tomlinson's power play goal at the 4:50 mark of the third period made it 6-3 and the Friars couldn't close to within two goals the rest of the way. Rob Gaudreau and Shaun Kane sandwiched Friar goals around one by BU's Chris McCann to close out the scoring. BU, now 24-10-2, will play the winner of the Maine-Northeastern game in Sunday's 5 p.m. final. Providence finished at 21-10- 2. BU had bolted to a 2-0 lead in the first period on goals by Ahola and Dave Tomlinson. Providence rallied to tie it in the second period on goals by Brian McCarthy and Gaudreau before Keith Tkachuk regained the lead for BU and Larry Rooney equalized for Providence. Ed Ronan regained the lead for • BU with 2:17 left in the middle period as he was alone in the slot, took a pass from Petteri Koskimaki and fired a 20-foot slap shot past Mullahy's blocker side. The Friars were still very much alive, but then came the Amonte goal.
Intelligencer Journal photo by Suzette Wenger Winning horticultural awards at the Pennsylvania State Farm Show were, from left, Dave Tomlinson, Kevin Peters, Sheri Drumm and Jim Lease.

tests. Other winners included: Dave Tomlinson, third place, geraniums; Drumm, third place, terrariums; Mark Cramer, third place, hospital arrangements; and Tina' Hynicker, fifth place, fruit/vegetable baskets. Dan Helwig, who teaches floral greenhouse landscape technology at Mount Joy, said about half of his students are from IU-13. IU students, he said, accounted for nearly half of the 17 awards garnered by his class. The horticulture course, he said, is a useful program for students who have difficulties in a standard academic track. "A disability isn't going to get in their way," he explained. "You can turn out a beautiful floral design and, if you have a calculator, you can figure out the price.... And there are enough jobs in the horticulture fields.' Compiled from Staff and Wire Reports STONY BROOK, N.Y. - Massachusetts Institute of Technology beat Trinity, 72-51, Saturday to capture third place at the USB/Polera Tip-Off Classic. Dave Tomlinson and Mike Duffy each scored 18 points to lead MIT and two other Beavers were in double figures, also. Senior wing Mackey Smith led Trinity with nine points. The Tigers were defeated by Swarthmore, 77-62, in a late game Friday night. Trinity, 0-2, will again be on the road Tuesday, when the Tigers take on Austin College in Sherman. 2 IN THE NEWS Compiled from the Associated Press Rangers ink deals with four The Rangers signed defensemen HOCKEY Joby Messier and Brad Tiley and left wings Craig Duncanson and Dave Thomlinson yesterday. Terms of the contracts were not disclosed. Messier, a cousin of Rangers captain Mark Messier, played four seasons at Michigan State. Selected in the seventh round of the 1989 draft, the 22-year-old had 21 goals and 48 assists in 171 games for Spartans. He had 13 goals and 15 assists in 41 games last season. Tiley, Duncanson and Thomlinson were free agents. Bruins acquire Kimble: The Boston Bruins acquired right wing Darin Kimble from the expansion Tampa Bay Lightning for center Ken Hodge and defenseman Matt Hervey. Kimble, 23, has played four seasons in the NHL with Quebec and St. Louis. The 6-foot-2, 205-pounder had a goal with three assists in 46 games for the Blues last season. In 177 NHL games, Kimble has 12 goals, 15 assists and 747 penalty minutes. Olajuwon threat Hakeem Olajuwon's agent has BASKETBALL threatened legal action against the Houston Rockets unless his client is traded and the team retracts statements it made about him during an injury controversy, according to a published report. Rockets general manager Steve Patterson confirmed Thursday he received the letter from agent Leonard Armato. Jazz sign Crotty: The Utah Jazz signed former Virginia guard John Crotty to a one-year contract. Crotty, 23, played for the Jazz in the Rocky Mountain Revue in Salt Lake City and in the California Summer League, where he averaged 15.0 points and 11.6 assists. The 6-foot-1 Crotty averaged 20.3 points and leagueleading 6.1 assists for Greenville of the Global Basketball League last season. Blancas sets pace Homero Blancas shot a 6-under-par 65 to take the lead after the first round of the Senior PGA Tour's $400,000 First GOLF of America Classic at the Elks Country Club's Highlands course in Grand Rapids, Mich. Blancas' round, two strokes off the course record held by Miller Barber, held a one-stroke lead over Barber, Lee Trevino, Tommy Aaron and Isao Aoki. The 54-hole, no-cut tournament runs through tomorrow. Waiting in the wings: The chairwoman of the Crestar-Farm Fresh LPGA Classic said she has several prospective sponsors to replace the tournament's namesakes, which are pulling out. Judy Bray said she asked the LPGA to give her until the end of next week to firm up arrangements for the 54- hole event. "I'm real encouraged," Bray said. Crestar and Farm Fresh said last week their decision to stop supporting Virginia's lone LPGA stop was based on rising costs associated with the event, held at Greenbrier Country Club in Chesapeake. LPGA event to stay: Area business and community leaders announced that Youngstown will retain its LPGA tour event. The future of the tournament was in doubt because of the financial troubles suffered by its former sponsor, PharMor Inc. The former Phar-Mor at Youngstown will be renamed the Youngstown LPGA Classic. The 54-hole, $500,000 tournament will be played some time in

Meet the stars of the Tampa Bay Lightning - Matt Hervey, Stan Drulia, Keith Osborne, Shayne Stevenson and J.C. Bergeron. Well, they're not the stars this year. But, if Lightning General Manager Phil Esposito has a say - - and he does - the Fab Five will be stars for the Lightning, maybe as early as next season. Currently, they're stars with the Atlanta Knights of the International Hockey League. With 19 games remaining, those five and others could see action in Lightning uniforms before the season is over. All have played at one time this season with the Lightning except for Hervey, who may be the best prospect the Lightning have in Atlanta. Osborne is with the Lightning now. Two weeks ago, before Osborne was called up by Tampa Bay, Atlanta 1 coach Gene Ubriaco said, "This is an outstanding minor-league team. We have scorers, great goalies and solid defensemen. Matt Hervey is going to be a star in the NHL. Mark it down. All he needs, all they all need, is NHL experience." And, according to Esposito, that's exactly what they will get within the year.

Penguins 4 PITTSBURGH Jaromir Jagr, his Bruins 3 face angelic and his hands demonic, broke over the blue line last night and ended Boston's five-game playoff winning streak with a killer's wrist shot that handed the Bruins a 4-3 loss at 9:44 of overtime. Sometimes, the right effort brings the wrong answer. The Bruins outplayed the Penguins most of the night - as in a 38-25 shot lead through regulation - but finally fell prey to one of the most gifted young talents in all of hockey. "[Jagr] just needs one chance," said an admiring teammate, rookie Shawn McEachern. "He gets the puck at the red line and, on the bench, you just stand up and watch him." And that was about how last night came to a screeching halt for the Bruins. By Raad Cawthon STAFF WRITER Atlanta Knights center Jock Callander is looking forward to this weekend, when the Peoria Rivermen come to town for two games. In two previous games against Peoria both Atlanta victories - Callander totaled five points, including three goals. Atlanta will play Peoria tonight and Saturday in The Omni. Both games begin at 7:35. Like the Knights, Peoria is a good skating team. The Rivermen have the IHL's fewest penalty minutes - 845. However, they're 0-2 against the Knights and are coming to Atlanta for the first time this season. The Knights opened their season in Peoria in October with a 6-5 victory and returned in November, winning 4-1. But the Rivermen are 7-3 in their last 10 games, including a one-point loss to league-leading San Diego. Peoria is in second place in the Midwest, 15 points behind Milwaukee. This weekend the Knights will find out if they have righted themselves from a difficult month-long stretch when defensemen Rick Lanz and Matt Hervey were injured and Atlanta played most games with an allrookie defense. During that period the Knights were 7-5.

In Buffalo, Buffalo Sabres center Dale Hawerchuk signed a multi-year contract that will pay him $1.2 million per season, the team announced Friday. The length of the contract was- not released. The agreement makes the 29-year-old Hawerchuk the second-highest paid Sabre behind Pat La Fontaine. Don MacAdam, who coached the Cape Breton Oilers to three playoffs in the American Hockey League, Friday was named head coach of the New Haven Senators. MacAdam, 41, had two second-place and a third-place finish in his three seasons with the Oilers. He was an assistant coach with the Detroit Red Wings for three seasons before heading to Cape Breton. The New York Rangers signed defensemen Joby Messier and Brad Tiley and left wings Craig Duncanson and Dave Thomlinson Friday. Messier, a distant cousin of Rangers captain Mark Messier, played four seasons at Michigan State. The Boston Bruins acquired right wing Darin Kimble from the expansion Tampa Bay Lightning Friday for center Ken Hodge and defenseman Matt Hervey. Kimble, 23, has played four seasons in the NHL with Quebec and St. Louis. The 6-foot-2, 205-pounder had a goal with three assists in 46 games for the Blues last season. In 177 NHL games, Kimble has 12 goals, 15 assists and 747 penalty minutes. Hodge, 26, was named to the NHL's all team following the 1990-91 season. He split last season between the Bruins and their AHL affiliate in Maine.

Farrish said Sunday's game was a perfect example of not having enough power to finish strong. "The score was 4-2 after the second but we came out strong and had a couple of good chances in the third. But, in the last 10 minutes there just wasn't enough left to keep it up. When you're going with five defenceman and some of them are hurting, too, something just has to give. I feel for the players. You can only give what you've got." Hawks now have four days off and Farrish is hoping some of the injured players will be ready when the teams returns to action next Friday in Rochester. They play in Newmarket, Ont. Saturday. "Maybe Matt (Hervey), Dallas (Eakins) and a couple guys who are playing with a lot of hurt will be better by then," he said. TAMPA - Rob Ramage said he wanted to finish his career with the Tampa Bay Lightning. Instead, he may have to settle for the Stanley Cup. The 13-year veteran defenseman, considered to be Tampa Bay's team leader, was traded to the contending Montreal Canadiens following the Lightning's Saturday. The Lightning received minorleague defensemen Eric Charron, 22, and Alain Cote, 25, and future considerations - a draft pick next June or a player off the Canadiens' non-protected list. Although Charron and Cote are needed in Tampa Bay, neither will join the club for its game tonight in Chicago. Instead, the Lightning have recalled four players - goalie David Littman, forward Jason Lafrenier and defensemen Chris LiPuma and Matt Hervey - from their Atlanta farm club. "It's all part of the game," said Ramage, 34. "I'd heard the rumors lately and I kind of expected it. They [the Lightning] have a lot of young guys in the minors and Roman [Hamrlik] will start playing more and more and I'm not sure I would've had a role here next season. I'm thankful to have played

Tampa Bay Lightning recall forward Tim Berglund and right-winger Keith Osborne from Atlanta of the International Hockey League; reassign defenceman Matt Hervey and leftwinger Jason Ruff to Atlanta.

The Tampa Bay Lightning acquired center Ken Hodge and defenseman Matt Hervey from the Boston Bruins, in exchange for right wing Darin Kimble and future considerations. Hodge was a 30- goal scorer in NHL 1990-91 and is the son of Ken Hodge Sr., who was a teammate of Lightning president and general manager Phil Esposito in the 1970s. Hodge had 30 goals and 29 assists as a rookie in 1990-91, earning

a berth on the all-rookie team. He struggled last season, splitting time between Boston and its minorleague affiliate in Maine. Hodge had six goals and 11 assists while with Boston. Hervey played in 16 games with the Bruins, recording one assist. Both Hodge and Hervey are 26. "In Ken Hodge, we are acquiring a player who has proven goal-scoring ability at the NHL level," Esposito said. "And Matt Hervey has shown consistent improvement in his professional career and provides added depth at defense." Hodge and Hervey will attend the Lightning's first-ever training camp, which begins Friday at the Lakeland Civic Center. Kimble, 23, has played four seasons in the NHL with Quebec and
Tampa Bay got Ken Hodge and Matt Hervey from Boston in return for Darin Kimble. Staff and wire reports
Hockey was a way to avoid trouble for Matt Hervey; now he's trying to make problems for Lightning opponents.
VA Times photo - MIKE PEASE Defenseman Matt Hervey was called up from the Lightning's minor-league club in Atlanta on Monday. The sport makes a save

TAMPA - The trek from Palm Springs, Calif., to 29 Palms is 35 miles all uphill. Three summers ago, the story goes, one of the top 10 triathletes in Southern California challenged Matt Hervey to a bicycle race along that route, which begins at sea level and ends 3,500 feet higher. "Matt beat him with ease," recalled Art Block, who has been like a father to Hervey for the past five

years. "I would tell you the name of the triathlete, but I wouldn't want his name to show up in the papers. It would be too embarrassing for him. "But it just shows how incredibly competitive and well trained Matt is. Some professionals get lazy in the off-season, but not Matt." For Hervey, a defenseman trying to earn a regular job with the Tampa Bay Lightning, his entire life has seemed like an uphill climb. Hervey grew up in Whittier, Calif., a suburb of Los Angeles where boys were more apt to be in gangs than boy scout troops. When Hervey was 14 and 15 he often went to the "Saturday night fights in the park." "It was the Caucasians against the Mexicans," Hervey said. "It wasn't like the gangs today with the knives

CONNECTICUT POST Saturday, BRIEFLY A wire staff, report MacAdam named Senators coach Don MacAdam, who coached the Cape Breton Oilers to three playoff appearances in the American Hockey League, was named head coach of the AHL's New Haven Senators Friday. MacAdam, 41, had two second- and a third-place finish in his three seasons with the Oilers. He was an assistant coach with the Detroit Red Wings for three seasons before heading to Cape Breton. MacADAM MacAdam started his coaching‹ career at the University of New Brunswick. During his last four seasons coaching varsity hockey he published three books on hockey conditioning and doubled as assistant coach with the Federicton Express of the AHL. BOXING/Great day for Danes Jimmi and Johnny Bredahl of Denmark made pro boxing history Friday night as they won World Boxing Organization titles in successive bouts. The brothers, both undefeated European champions, also became Denmark's first pro boxing champions in modern times. Johnny Bredahl scored a unanimous 12-round decision over WBO junior bantamweight champ Jose Quirino of Mexico, and less than two hours later, his older brother Jimmi outpointed veteran Daniel Londas of France after 12 rounds for the WBO junior lightweight championship. HOCKEY/Bruins deal Hodge The Boston Bruins acquired right wing Darin Kimble from the expansion Tampa Bay Lightning for center Ken Hodge and defenseman Matt Hervey. • The New York Rangers signed defensemen Joby Messier and Brad Tiley and left wings Craig Duncanson and Dave Thomlinson. Messier, a distant cousin of Rangers captain Mark Messier, played four seasons at Michigan State and had 13 goals and 15 assists in 41 games last season. The Winnipeg Jets have been served with a lawsuit launched by Russian recruit Alexei Zhamnov. Zhamnov wants damages out of his recently signed contract with the Jets, which guarantees him a reported $1.25 million over three years. He apparently grew dissatisfied with the deal after the Jets signed Finnish prospect Teemu Selanne for a reported $2.7 million, including a $1.5-million signing bonus. BASKETBALL/Jazz ink Crotty The Utah Jazz signed former Virginia guard John Crotty to a one-year contract. Crotty set a Virginia record for career assists with 683. He averaged 15.5 points as a senior. Hakeem Olajuwon's agent demanded that the Houston Rockets trade his client and retract statements the team made about Olajuwon during an injury controversy. Olajuwon was out for five games this spring after the center said he could not play because of a left hamstring injury. Speculation surfaced that Olajuwon was faking the hamstring injury to gain leverage for a contract extension. Moncton's Ron Presti had to be taken off the ice on a stretcher at 17:41 after the blueliner slammed into the post on the Springfield end. Moncton coach Rich Bowness inserted Dave Quigley in place of Essena at the start of the second period. par Pierre TURGEON MONCTON Auriez-vous quitté Los Angeles et la vie californienne pour espérer faire carrière au hockey professionnel? C'est pourtant ce que Matt Hervey, un robuste défenseur des Hawks de Moncton a choisi de faire. Il a •de plus quitté le domicile familial alors qu'il venait à peine de célébrer son 16e anniversaire de naissance pour s'expatrier au Canada où il avait de meilleures chances de s'améliorer et de se faire remarquer. Cette année, il a réalisé un autre pas dans la bonne direction quand les Jets de Winnipeg lui ont d'abord consenti un essai de 25 matchs avant de lui offrir un contrat d'un dans la Ligue Américaine afin de voir ce qu'il pouvait faire. C'est déjà un bon pas quand on réalise qu'après des séjours à Victoria et à Lethbridge dans la Ligue junior de l'Ouest, il lui a fallu évoluer dans un circuit tiers avec les Sockeyes de Richmond, en banlieue de Vancouver, où il a tout de même remporté la coupe Centennial, em-

Matt Hervey is battling more than agent without compensation at season's end, free to sign with any other team. That means how well he does this year will dictate signing and earning power next season. So far, he's been one of the steadiest Hawks, playing solid defence and amassing 10 points in 25 games. Hervey, the last cut at Winnipeg's camp, is not happy to be in Moncton. Before training camp, he requested a trade or reassignment to either Phoenix or San Diego of the IHL. "This is my fourth season in Moncton and I just don't think Winnipeg's given me a fair chance," he says. "I understand it takes a couple of seasons in the minors to mature, but Winnipeg still hasn't had a good look at me yet.

By NEIL HODGE The Telegraph- Journal MONCTON Matt Hervey knows heartache. A year ago, the Moncton Hawks defenceman's mother was in a car accident in northern California. Today, she is still in a coma in his native Los Angeles. "It's tough to keep my mind on hockey," he says quietly. *'There's not a day go by that I'm not thinking about her and hoping that she comes out of her coma. "I try my best to stay busy because if I just sit around, it bothers me even more. Mom would want me to continue playing. She always was my biggest supporter." At age 24, Hervey, in his fourth American Hockey League season, has faced more heartache than many ever do. Unlike most, he can't turn to his father in these trying times because, in a way, he doesn't really have one. He hasn't seen his dad since his parents were divorced six years ago. "He just disappeared ... don't know where he is." says Hervey, whose Hawks host the Cape Breton Oilers tonight at 7:30 o'clock and the Rochester Americans tomorrow at 5 p.m. The date was Jan. 10, 1990. The night before a game against Cape Breton, Hawks coach Dave Farrish and an RCMP officer arrived at his Sydney hotel room to inform him about his mother's accident. Hervey took the next four weeks off. "Matt's been in a very difficult situation the last year," says Farrish. "I feel for him a lot. We talked about it last year and a bit this year, but he doesn't like to bring it up a lot. "To his credit, he's been able to continue playing extremely well under these circumstances. It doesn't seem to have affected his play." Farrish has been more than a coach to Hervey. He's been somewhat of a missing fatherly figure. And, like a family, his teammates have also been there to lean on. "Dave and the whole team have been great to me through this," he says. "They've always been there when I needed someone to talk to. HOCKEY: Lightning acquires Hodge Bruins acquired right wing oM The Boston Darin Kimble from the expansion Tampa Bay Lightning yesterday for center Ken Hodge and defenseman Matt Hervey. 8T. Kimble, 23, has played four seasons in the ea NHL with Quebec and St. Louis. The 6-foot2, 205-pounder had a goal with three assists in 46 games for the Blues last season. In 177 NHL games, Kimble has 12 goals, 15 assists and 747 penalty minutes. Hodge, 26, was named to the NHL's allrookie team following the 1990-91 season. He split last season between the Bruins and their AHL affiliate in Maine. He had six goals and 11 assists in 42 games with Boston and six goals and 11 assists in 19 games for Maine after being sent down on Feb. 18. He's the son of former Bruins star Ken Hodge Sr. Hodge Sr. played with Lightning executive Phil Esposito for Stanley Cup champion Bruins teams in 1970 and 1972. BOXING: Dane wins title Johnny Bredahl became Denmark's first world professional boxing champion in modern times yesterday when he scored a unanimous 12-round decision over World Boxing Organization junior bantamweight champion Jose Quirino of Mexico in Copenhagen, Denmark. Bredahl, 24, the European champion, extended his winning streak to 15. The three judges scored the fight 118-111, 119-110 and 118-110. The three first rounds were about even. Then the Dane, supported by a partisan crowd at the Parken, Copenhagen's new soccer stadium, took command. "I expected a hard fight, but I had an easy payday," Bredahl said.

Centre Scott Schneider suffered a broken hand when slashed in Fri- H day's game. Schneider had surgery performed on the hand in Hershey on Saturday. Farrish expected Schneider will be lost for three to four weeks. Because of Schneider's injury, plus injuries to Brent Hughes (checkbone), Matt Hervey (groin), Bryan Erickson (shoulder) and Tony Joseph (stomach) and the emergency recall of defenceman Bryan Marchment and left-winger Craig Duncanson by Winnipeg, Farrish signed defenceman -forward Don Dietrich to a 25-game amateur tryout on Saturday. "He can play both defence and forward so that's a big plus in his Moncton fans may recall Dietrich as a rookie with the 1981-82 Calder favor," said Farrish of Dietrich. Cup Dietrich champion played New the last Brunswick couple of Hawks. years in Europe but is currently : living in Portland, Me. Farrish has a couple of reasons to be optimistic about starting 1992 with a .500 record. 1. Ilis Hawks play eight games at home compared to just four on the road in January. Their four road games are two one-day jaunts to Sydney and one each to Halifax and Fredericton. 2. Several of his injured players are just about ready to play again. "I expect Butzy (Erickson) could be playing Wednesday (Fredericton) and Brent (flughes) and Matt (Hervey) could be just days away. too. Tony is also looking forward to playing again soon," says Farrish. Actually, the way Farrish reads it, being in second is more important than the .500 record. "Holding a top position is what hockey is really all about." he says. "Also, our 500 record is something we can work from. If you're a .500 team you're normally in the playoffs but we have a glorious opportunity within the next six weeks to really pick up on our record.' Farrish is very high on left-winger Don Barber. assigned to the Hawks by Winnipeg last Friday. • "He's a highly skilled player who's just lacking a little confidence," * says Farrish. "Normally when a player of his skills comes down it's to get ice-time and regain his confidence. "We'll give him plenty of work and try and get his offensive skills going again. He's got an outstanding touch but he just lost some of it in Winnipeg.' Farrish said he played Francois Gravel in goal on Saturday to give Draper a rest. Signed as an emergency replacement from the N.B. Senior League Miramichi Gagnon Packers last week, Gravel faced 48 shots in Saturday's game. "lie made some excellent saves but I knew Tom was going back in + "I just wanted to give Tom a rest Saturday. Ile's played a lot and he Sunday," said Farrish. looked a little weary on Friday night (7-5 loss). "When we arrived at 5 a.m. Saturday I just figured he better take the day off and recouperate a little." ••. IN THE NEWS Compiled from the Associated Press Rangers ink deals with four The Rangers signed defensemen Joby Messier and Brad Tiley and left HOCKEY wings Craig Duncanson and Dave Thomlinson yesterday. Terms of the contracts were not disclosed. Messier, a cousin of Rangers captain Mark Messier, played four seasons at Michigan State. Selected in the seventh round de of the 1989 draft, the 22-year-old had 21 goals and 48 assists in 171 games for Spartans. He had 13 goals and 15 assists in 41 games last season. Tiley, Duncanson and Thomlinson were free agents. Bruins acquire Kimble: The Boston Bruins acquired right wing Darin Kimble from the expansion Tampa Bay Lightning for center Ken Hodge and defenseman Matt Hervey. Kimble, 23, has played four seasons in the NHL with Quebec and St. Louis. The 6-foot-2, 205-pounder had a goal with three assists in 46 games for the Blues last season. In 177 NHL games, Kimble has 12 goals, 15 assists and 747 penalty minutes. Olajuwon threat Hakeem Olajuwon's agent has BASKETBALL threatened legal action against the Houston Rockets unless his client is traded and the team retracts statements it made about him during an injury controversy, according to a published report. Rockets general manager Steve Patterson confirmed Thursday he received the letter from agent Leonard Armato. Jazz sign Crotty: The Utah Jazz signed former Virginia guard John Crotty to a one-year contract. Crotty, 23, played for the Jazz in the Rocky Mountain Revue in Salt Lake City and in the California Summer League, where he averaged 15.0 points and 11.6 assists. The 6-foot-1 Crotty averaged 20.3 points and leagueleading 6.1 assists for Greenville of the Global Basketball League last season. Blancas sets pace Homero Blancas shot a 6-under-par 65 to take the lead after the first round GOLF of the Senior PGA Tour's $400,000 First of America Classic at the Elks Country Club's Highlands course in Grand Rapids, Mich. Blancas' round, two strokes off the course record held by Miller Barber, held a one-stroke lead over Barber, Lee Trevino, Tommy Aaron and Isao Aoki. The 54-hole, no-cut tournament runs through tomorrow. Waiting in the wings: The chairwoman of the Crestar-Farm Fresh LPGA Classic said she has several prospective sponsors to replace the tournament's namesakes, which are pulling out. Judy Bray said she asked the LPGA to give her until the end of next week to firm up arrangements for the 54- hole event. "I'm real encouraged," Bray said. Crestar and Farm Fresh said last week their decision to stop supporting Virginia's lone LPGA stop was based on rising costs associated with the event, held at Greenbrier Country Club in Chesapeake. LPGA event to stay: Area business and community leaders announced that Youngstown will retain its LPGA tour event. The future of the tournament was in doubt because of the financial troubles suffered by its former sponsor, PharMor Inc. The former Phar-Mor at Youngstown will be renamed the Warren- LPGA Classic. The 54-hole, $500,000 tournament will be played some time in

By Gerard McLaughlin Sports Reporter Remember Moncton? Matt Hervey does. "My time in Moncton was both good and bad," offered the candid Hervey Monday as he prepared to return for the first time with his new team the Maine Mariners - for an American Hockey League game Prior to signing a two-year contract with the NHL Boston Bruins with Moncton Hawks tonight. during the summer, the 25-year-old Hervey, a Whittier, Cal. native, Hervey was a free-agent walk-on with the youthful 1987-88 Hawks, spent four seasons on the Hawks blueline. the first fully staffed farm-team that the NHL Winnipeg Jets established here. The 1987-88 Hawks struggled to win only 27 games but then made Hervey says he made some "great friends" in Moncton, but he the playoffs in two of the next three seasons. candidly claims he was never really happy with the hockey side of life. "It was frustrating playing in Moncton year after year and not getting a real chance in Winnipeg,' he says. "It wasn't only me either. Hockey is my profession and playing four years without getting a chance just builds up a lot of frustration. You get so irked. I needed a change. After playing the first five games of the current season in Boston, Hervey was then demoted by former Hawks boss and now Bruins coach Rick Bowness to Maine. But, he's not sulking or disappointed now. In fact, he's hoping for another opportunity in Beantown before the season concludes. "Rick explained that right now it's a numbers game and I have to wait my chance,' says Hervey. "He told me to work hard and something will eventually happen. I'm not here counting the days but I'm confident I can play in Boston when my time comes. I think the Bruins defensive style, 'don't be flashy. . move it up,' is the kind of hockey I play best.' Meanwhile, Hervey is still somewhat frustrated over the treatement tendered him by the Jets. "I just like to know where I stand and very few players knew where they stood in the Winnipeg organization. I don't feel we were treated fairly," he says.

Rangers ink deals with four The Rangers signed defensemen Joby Messier and Brad Tiley and left HOCKEY wings Craig Duncanson and Dave Thomlinson yesterday. Terms of the contracts were not disclosed. Messier, a cousin of Rangers captain Mark Messier, played four seasons at Michigan State. Selected in the seventh round of the 1989 draft, the 22-year-old had 21 goals and 48 assists in 171 games for Spartans. He had 13 goals and 15 assists in 41 games last season. Tiley, Duncanson and Thomlinson were free agents. Bruins acquire Kimble: The Boston Bruins acquired right wing Darin Kimble from the expansion Tampa Bay Lightning for center Ken Hodge and defenseman Matt Hervey. Kimble, 23, has played four seasons in the NHL with Quebec and St. Louis. The 6-foot-2, 205-pounder had a goal with three assists in 46 games for the Blues last season. In 177 NHL games, Kimble has 12 goals, 15 assists and 747 penalty minutes. paragraphs.text: The professional careers of several Moncton Hawks should end in 25 days. Incapable of playing in the American Hockey League, many players will be released by the NHL parent Winnipeg Jets at the conclusion of the regular season schedule. In most cases, Winnipeg's decisions on who to re-sign are easy ones. High-scoring forwards Randy Gilhen, Peter Douris and Alfie Turcotte and defencemen Todd Flichel and Paul Boutilier are valuable players the Jets want back in the organization next season. What about the rest of the Hawks? With a team as bad as this year's Hawks, the NHL club can't be too enthusiastic about many prospects. In the minor leagues, there are always surprises, but this year it has only meant, one another. disappointment There is one great exception - rookie defenceman Matt Hervey. The California native played Tier II junior last winter in Richmond, B.C. and was recommended to the Jets by coach Orland Kurtenbach. Hervey impressed the Jets so much at training camp that he was assigned to the Hawks with an amateur tryout. It didn't take Hawks coach Rick Bowness long to convince the Jets that Hervey was worth signing to a pro deal. After a few AHL games, Hervey showed he enjoyed the physical play and he was eager to learn. Besides the offensivelyskilled players (Gilhen, Douris and Turcotte), Hervey has been Moncton's steadiest player. He's played the point on the power play, killed penalties and taken extra shifts. At plus 2, Hervey is the only plus player on the Hawks roster. "He's been very consistent," coach Bowness noted. "He's one player r who has been consistent at home and on the road. "We have a lot of players who don't bother showing up on the road. That's why we're in last place. Hervey is one guy who comes to play every night....It doesn't matter if we're at home or not." Bowness also says the 22- year old defenceman is the most improved player on the team. "He wasn't even being considered at training camp," Bowness explained. "His enthusiasm caught our eye. He's played hard and practised hard to improve himself. "He's proven that he can play in this league. We've used him in every situation possible and he's proven himself. He's also got more than 200 minutes in penalties so that tells you something about his character. He is a real competitor." Inviting many players from this year's team back to the Hawks next season would be disasterous. However, Matt Hervey is one player who has earned that right. NOTES: Calgary Flames and Edmonton Oilers, the two best teams in the NHL's Smythe Division, improved themselves and their farm teams prior to Tuesday's trade deadline, but the Winnipeg Jets did nothing. Jets general manager John Ferguson hand forced by the "Flames and Oilers, but no were made. The Jets have a surplus of goaltenders and defencemen. According to one NHL source, Ferguson was "asking for too much" in efforts to make trades....Nova Scotia Oilers added some muchneeded muscle Tuesday. Thug Alan May, who was second in the AHL with 357 penalty minutes in 1 Maine, was traded for scorer Moe Lemay, who had floated through most of the season. ...Maine also acquired veteran Steve Tsujiura from New Jersey. The Mariners have been after the tiny centre all year.

Many within the Tampa Bay organization believe Bergeron is their goalie of the future, but like Littman, he lacks NHL experience. Look then for the Lightning to protect Jablonski, whom many scouts believe will blossom into one of the league's best goalies, and take a chance that no one selects Bergeron. Defensemen: The Lightning can protect five in this category and four spots will automatically go to Joe Reekie, Shawn Chambers, Marc Bergevin and Bob Beers. As a first-year pro, Roman Hamrlik is exempt and does not have to be protected, so losing him is not a concern. Likewise, Chris LiPuma and Shawn Rivers are also exempt, so Tampa Bay can therefore decide between Matt Hervey, Eric Charron and Alain Cote for the fifth spot. Charron and Cote are new to the organization and probably won't get much consideration. So look for the club to protect Hervey, whose offensive capabilities are highly regarded. Forwards: Brian Bradley, John Tucker, Chris Kontos, Rob Zamuner and Adam Creighton are givens here. That leaves four open spaces on the protected list and three of them almost assuredly will go to Rob DiMaio, Marc Bureau a and Mikael Anderson. That leaves one spot to be filled by either Danton Cole, Steve Maltais, Steve Kasper, Randy Gilhen or any one of six Atlanta prospects. Chances are that none of the players from Atlanta - Jason Ruff, Dave Capuano, Shayne Stevenson, Keith Osborne, Tim Bergland and Jason Lafreniere will make the protected list. At 32, Kasper probably won't either. Age won't keep Maltais off the list. Statistics will. After a promising exhibition season, the winger has produced only y six goals and 12 assists through 54 games. Cole has similar numbers (nine goals, 12 assists) but he and Gilhen are both considered excellent penalty killers, so they will likely be the two most heavily considered for the final spot on the protected list. And after long consideration, don't be surprised if Esposito opts to protect the 30-year-old Gilhen instead of the 26-year-old Cole. After all, Esposito has wanted Gilhen on his team for more than a year. He isn't likely to let him go very easily.

Talk about playing games. The Tampa Bay Lightning did that figuratively so some Atlanta Knights could do that literally. The International Hockey League playoffs begin April 17, but rosters had to be set last week. The Knights have qualified for the playoffs, but with so many Lightning injuries of late, most of Atlanta's top players are playing with Tampa Bay. The IHL rule states that postseason players must be in the same city as the rest of the team on the day rosters are set. So players such as J.C. Bergeron, Jason Lafreniere, Jason Ruff, Matt Hervey, Keith Osborne, Dave Capuano, Shawn Rivers and Tim Bergland flew into Atlanta last week on the day rosters were set. Then, some of them flew right back out to join the Lightning. "It's a crazy rule," Lightning President/General Manager Phil Esposito said. "They have to be there. Most importantly, though, we want those players to be able to play in the playoffs for Atlanta." The Knights' postseason roster looks like this: Goalies: Bergeron, David Littman, Mike Greenlay. Defense: Hervey, Jeff Buchanan, Alain Cote, Eric Charron, Scott Boston, Chris LiPuma and Cory Cross, who is currently with the University of Alberta. Forwards: Osborne, Capuano, Ruff, Bergland, Jock Callander, Brent Gretzky, Shayne Stevenson, Stan Drulia, Jean Blouin, Herb RagIan, Christian Campeau and Marc Tardiff, who is with Sherbrooke of the Quebec Major Junior Hockey League.

paragraphs.text: Ernie Harwell, fired in 1991 after 32 years as the radio voice of the Detroit Tigers, is expected to be back next season as a member of the team's broadcasting crew. Mike llitch, the new Tigers owner, said yesterday he had offered Harwell a job sharing broadcasting duties with Rick Rizzs and Bob Rathbun, the two men hired to replace Harwell. Details of the new deal were incomplete, but llitch said he hoped to be able to announce the whole package late next week. Bruins acquire Kimble The Boston Bruins acquired right wing Darin Kimble from the expansion Tampa Bay Lightning yesterday for center Ken Hodge and defenseman Matt Hervey. Kimble, 23, has played four seasons in the NHL with Quebec and St. Louis. The 6-foot-2, 205-pounder had a goal with three assists in 46 games for the Blues last season. In 177 NHL games, Kimble has 12 goals, 15 assists and 747 penalty minutes. Hodge, 26, was named to the NHL's
