After winning three team awards at a banquet Monday night, the reaction was vintage Ron Wilson. "It's nice did you hear that Matt Hervey got called up to Winnipeg?" asked the player/assistant coach of the American Hockey League's Moncton Hawks. Wilson has always been n more interested in team successes than personal glory. Wilson is the Hawks leading scorer with 90 points in 77 games, but he'd be more comfortable talking about the achievements of others. His teammates honored him by naming him their most valuable player. He was also honored for being the team's top scorer and the booster club voted him most popular player. "The awards are nice, but not as nice as winning games," Wilson, 32, said. "I got the most points because I'm the only guy here to play all the games so far," he added. "Other guys could have won it, but there were call-ups and injuries. "It's nice to be recognized. The award that came from the team means a lot. It's special when your peers give an award." Sophomore centre Scott Schneider was named the team's most improved player. After scoring 12 goals and 23 assists last season, Schneider has fired 29 goals and 34 assists this year. He's a also been one of the team's best centres on face-offs. He is expected back this weekend after missing several weeks with a shoulder injury. Slick centre Alfie Turcotte was named the Hawks most sportsmanlike player. He has 64 points in 55 games with only 55 penalty minutes. Second-year defenceman Todd Flichel, who has led the plus/minus

statistics for much of the season, was named unsung hero of the team and goaltender Tom Draper, second in the AHL with 26 victories,, was named rookie of the year. An award for the team's best defenceman will be presented at the final home game of the season. The 'Hawks will play their final three regular season games this week, with Flichel, a goal Robin of avoiding Bartel, major Markku injuries. Kyllonen, Luciano Borsato, Scott Schneider and Mike Warus are all ailing with an assortment of injuries. Manager Larry Haley is attempting to fill out the roster with players from amateur teams in the area. Defenceman Matt Hervey will play for the parent Winnipeg Jets tonight and Wednesday in Los Angeles against the Kings. Hervey, a Whittier, Calif., native, is expected to rejoing the Hawks Thursday or Friday. The Hawks play Wednesday against Utica, Friday against New Haven and finish the season Sunday afternoon against Halifax. All games are at the Coliseum.

paragraphs.text: By Dwayne Tingley Staff Reporter Matt Hervey had never heard of Langley, British Columbia. But, as a 15-year-old he hitchhiked north from his broken home in Los Angeles. He stopped in a small .Canadian town, where he hoped to forget his t troubled past and begin pursuing a hockey career. "It wasn't a good situation," said Hervey, now a third-year defenceman with the Moncton Hawks. "If I had stayed in L.A., I'd probably be working construction somewhere with an expensive drug habit to support.' Being Matt Hervey hasn't been easy.. paragraphs.text: waiver draft on Monday to see if they, are selected. "I understand their positions. It would be tough to come here and then find out they are picked by somebody and have to move again," said Farrish. "There's a chance somebody could take anyone of them so I'm willing to wait a few extra days to accommodate them." Moncton won their first exhibition in Bridgewater, N.S. last Wednesday, defeating Halifax Citadels 4-1. Moncton's final pre-season is next Tuesday in Fredericton when they'll tangle with the Canadiens. With only 15 players in training camp, Farrish is limited in things he can do.. "Improving our conditioning and working on basic systems and things like breakouts and the powerplay," answers Farrish t to what the team is working on most. "Getting the little things down so they just become automatic is something you can't practice enough," said Farrish. Look for something to break on the Johnny LeBlanc talks between the Jets and the Edmonton Oilers this weekend. Jets general manager Mike Smith and Oilers GM Glen Sather met on Thursday and Friday and the LeBlanc case was certainly discussed. LeBlanc, the Campbellton native who scored 53 goals with the Oilers farm team in Cape Breton last year, opted for free agency over the summer and the Jets are definitely after .his services. But, so far the talks have stalled and LeBlanc is sitting home in Campbellton. _"I have a gut feeling something is about to break," said Farrish Friday. "I don't really know what the problem was but I'd certainly like to see it get straighted out and get him in our lineup soon.' •• Following are the 23 players that have been assigned to the Hawks: Goal (1): Mike O'Neill (Yale two years ago, Finland last season). Defence (10): Daryl Stanley (Vancouver, NHL), Bryan Marchment (returnce), Matt Hervey (returnee), Grant Richison (returnee), Todd Flichel (returnee), Dallas Eakins (returnee), Chris Norton (returnee), Rob Bateman (University of Vermont), Denis Larocque (Cape Breton Oilers), Don McLennan (University of Denver). Forwards (12): Brent Hughes (returnee), Guy Larose (returnee), Steve Martinson (Sherbrooke, AHL), Craig Duncanson (New Haven, AIL), lain Duncan (returnce), Sergei Kharin (Wings, Soviet Union), Stu Barnes (Tri-Cities Americans, junior), Lee Davidson (University of North Dakota), Peter Hankinson (University of Minnesota), Scott Schneider (returnee), Larry Bernard (returnee), Tony Joseph (returnee). A GOOD ONE A training camp report on Kharin by a Winnipeg television station recently drew outstanding praise from Jets fans. Jets' defenceman Peter Taglianetti took Kharin under his wing during training camp and the two were inseparable. Taglianetti was supposed to be teaching Kharin how to speak English and Kharin teaching Taglianetti Russian. When the reporter asked Kharin what english words he had learned, he replied: "Peter Taglianetti is the best player in Winnipeg camp." The real truth is, Kharin speaks very good English.

paragraphs.text: Rivermen come to town for two games. In two previous games against Peoria - both Atlanta victories - Callander totaled five points, including three goals. Atlanta will play Peoria tonight and Saturday in The Omni. Both games begin at 7:35. Like the Knights, Peoria is a good skating team. The Rivermen have the IHL's fewest penalty minutes 845. However, they're 0-2 against the Knights and are coming to Atlanta for the first time this season. The Knights opened their season in Peoria in October with a 6-5 victory and returned in November, winning 4-1. But the Rivermen are 7-3 in their last 10 games, including a one-point loss to league-leading San Diego. Peoria is in second place in the Midwest, 15 points behind Milwaukee. This weekend the Knights will find out if they have righted themselves from a difficult month-long stretch when defensemen Rick Lanz and Matt Hervey were injured and Atlanta played most games with an allrookie defense. During that period the Knights were 7-5.

By Gerard McLaughlin Sports Reporter Talk about a team going hot'and cold. That was the Moncton Hawks on the weekend. Winless in five games, Hawks came out with fire in their eyes in Halifax on Saturday night and scorched the rival Citadels 4-1 in front of 4,107 fans at the Metro Centre. But, it was a much different story at the Coliseum Sunday. Although Moncton scored first, visiting Newmarket Saints then took complete control, triggering seven straight goals, defeating the Hawks 7-3 in front of 2,713 fans. Saints 7, at Hawks 3 Mike Stevens powered the Saints attack with three goals, two of them shorthanded. Mike Miller added two goals and two assists while Greg Johnston and Bob Reynolds each had goals. Moncton, which outshot the Saints 40-31, got goals from Simon Wheeldon, Todd Flichel and Matt Hervey. The Saints, now unbeated in their past five games (4-0-1) improved their overall record to 12 wins, 12 losses and three ties while Moncton's record dropped Moncton's record to 11-12-8.

paragraphs.text: day, Rick Bowness loaded the gun, and yesterday he pulled the trigger. The Bruins coach threatened lineup changes after a 6-3 loss to the Los Angeles Kings Monday and he has followed through. Center Ken Hodge and defenseman Jack Capuano rode the bus from the team's hotel to the practice rink before finding out they had an early afternoon flight back East. Hodge's demotion was termed a twoweek conditioning assignment. This season has been a bitter disappointment for Hodge, who was coming off a rookie year filled with accolades, among them the Seventh Player Award and a spot on the NHL all-rookie team. He had 30 goals and 29 assists, which was second among NHL rookies in scoring and the second-highest rookie goal total in club history. His replacement, Barry Pederson, who was called up yesterday with Scott Arniel *and Matt Hervey, holds the record with 44. "Maybe it should've happened before, I don't know," said Hodge. "I'm disappointed in the way I've played. Hopefully I'll go down and work on some things and hopefully get back in the right frame of mind." Hodge has found it especially tough on the road, earning only 1 assist in the last eight road games. Overall he has 6 goals and 11 assists in 42 games. "Seventeen points this year, that's ridiculous," said Hodge, 25. "I'm disappointed and everything, but I'll think positive, play hard for two weeks and see what happens. I can't be bitter; the only person I can be bitter at is myself for not being prepared from the very beginning." Hodge had a shaky training camp, which he candidly admitted

By Nancy L. Marrapese GLOBE STAFF CALGARY, Alberta - Rick Bowness hasn't ruled out anything in his quest to make the Bruins a better team, and the changes started yesterday. Forwards Barry Pederson and Scott Arniel and defenseman Matt Hervey were recalled from Maine while center Ken Hodge and defenseman Jack Capuano were sent to the Mariners. That was before practice. During practice, Bowness, who had been reluctant to split up center Vladimir Ruzicka and right wing Steve Leach, did just that, putting Leach on a line with Adam Oates and Bob Carpenter. Andy Brickley was put at left wing on Ruzicka's line, with a right wing to be named later, possibly Peter Douris. The coach also moved Brent Ashton from left wing to center on a line with Dave Reid and Lyndon Byers. The fourth line consisted of injured center Dave Poulin between* Lou Crawford and Chris Winnes. But tonight, when the Bruins play the Calgary Flames at the Saddledome, it's anybody's guess who will be playing with whom. "Sometimes changes are made just to get the team going and you have no control over it," said Leach, who added he's not exactly losing anything by playing with Oates. "I'll just communicate with him, find out what he likes to do and try to play off of that," said Leach, who missed Monday's game because of the flu. Bowness said the Ashton switch was an attempt to get him out of a slump. In the past 11 games, Ashton has I goal and 1 assist. Poulin, Wiemer close Poulin and Jim Wiemer arrived just before the team Monday night. Wiemer is possible for Friday's game in Edmonton while Poulin will skate with the team but won't play until after the road trip. Poulin, who has missed the entire season with a groin problem, said he needs to build more

paragraphs.text: HOCKEY Sabres sign Hawerchuk: Buffalo Sabres center Dale Hawerchuk signed a multi-year contract Friday that will pay him $1.2 million per. National Hockey League season. Hawerchuk, 29, is now the second-highest paid Sabre behind Pat LaFontaine, who earns $1.8 million per year. Boston acquired right winger Darin Kimble from Tampa Bay for center Ken Hodge and defenseman Matt Hervey. The New York Rangers signed defensemen Joby Messier and Brad Tiley and left wings Craig Duncanson and Dave Thomlinson. Messier, a distant cousin of Rangers captain Mark Messier, played four seasons at Michigan State.

eryone wants to play some games that mean something. Once the regular season starts, every game is important." Hawks coach Dave Farrish also had "good feelings" about his roster and he analyzed it, position-byposition. At goal, the Hawks will have rookie Rick Tabaracci and thirdyear veteran Bob Essensa. "These two have great futures in the game, but they have to play well here,' Farrish said. "The team should be confident with these two goaltenders." Hawks are currently carrying seven defencemen and all "are big, physical and they move the puck well." "It would seem like there's a little more depth at defence this year," the coach said. "There's no one offensive force like a Paul Boutilier, but there's great balance among the seven.' Moncton defencemen are: Chris Norton, Matt Hervey, Guy Gosselin, Todd Flichel, Dallas Eakins, Bryan Marchment and Grant Richison. The Hawks be lacking a little depth at forward, but another player or two could soon be reassigned to Moncton from the NHL parent Winnipeg Jets. The Schendier-Hughes-Kulak line will be as good as any in the league, but the team's other lines could be lacking scoring punch. Other Moncton forwards are: player/assistant coach Ron Wilson, Guy Larose, Luciano Borsato, Neil Meadmore, Larry Bernard, Danton Cole and Brian Hunt. NOTES - Right winger Ron Jones, who played junior in London last season, has been released by the Hawks..... ..Goaltender Bob Essensa won't arrive, in Moncton until Sunday so former University of Moncton netminder Joel Drolet will back-up Rick Tabaracci tonight…...Forward Gary Dickie, who scored 106 points for Regina Pats in the Western junior league last winter, will practise with the Hawks in the next few days in an attempt to land a contract. He was recently released by Washington Capitals......Cape Breton Oilers have halted season ticket sales at 3,900. They also have a lengthy waiting list for season tickets.

The 6-foot2, 205-pounder had a goal with three assists in 46 games for the Blues last season. In 177 NHL games, Kimble has 12 goals, 15 assists and 747 penalty minutes. Hodge, 26, was named to the NHL's allrookie team following the 1990-91 season. He split last season between the Bruins and their AHL affiliate in Maine. He had six goals and 11 assists in 42 games with Boston and six goals and 11 assists in 19 g games for Maine after being sent down on Feb. 18. He's the son of former Bruins star Ken Hodge Sr. Hodge Sr. played with Lightning executive Phil Esposito for Stanley Cup champion Bruins teams in 1970 and 1972. BOXING: Dane wins title Johnny Bredahl became Denmark's first world professional boxing champion in modern times yesterday when he scored a unanimous 12-round decision over World Boxing Organization junior bantamweight champion Jose Quirino of Mexico in Copenhagen, Denmark. Bredahl, 24, the European champion, extended his winning streak to 15. The three judges scored the fight 118-111, 119-110 and 118-110. The three first rounds were about even. Then the Dane, supported by a partisan crowd at the Parken, Copenhagen's new soccer stadium, took command. "I expected a hard fight, but I had an easy payday," Bredahl said.

The winning team in the fiveman Texas scramble tournament featured defencemen Matt Hervey and Chris Mills, scout Charlie Hodge, radio reporter Les Lazaruk of Winnipeg and TimesTranscript sports reporter Gerard McLauglin. They were six-underpar 63. Hawks new assistant coachplayer Ron Wilson, Steven Fletcher, Laurie Boschman and Andrew McBain were second.

Most Winnipeg Jet regulars will be in the lineup Friday night when the NHL team faces off in an exhibition game at the Coliseum against its American Hockey League affiliate, the Moncton Hawks. Well-known Jets like Dale Hawerchuk, Randy Carlyle, Brent Ashton and Andrew McBain will play the game, general manager John Ferguson said Wednesday. Friday's game time, is 7:30 p.m. Ferguson said he wanted to attract a large crowd to the game. "We want people to see the Winnipeg Jets.....the veterans and some of the players coming up through the system," he said. "'We've had a good week here and we'd like to top it off with a good exhibition game." Others in the Jets lineup will be: goaltenders Daniel Berthiaume and. Pokey Reddick; defencemen Bryan Marchment, Frederik Olausson, Teppo Numminen, Brad Berry and Peter Taglianetti; and forwards Gilles Hamel, Alfie Turcotte, Darren Boyko, Teemu Selanne, Markku Kyllonen, lain Duncan, Hannu Jarvenpaa; Ray Newfeld and Brad Jones. Playing for Moncton will be goaltenders Tom Draper and Bob Essensa; defencemen Todd Flichel, Matt Hervey, Sean Clement, .Jamie Husgen, Paul Boutilier and Matt Cote; and forwards Craig Endean, Guy Larose, Peter Douris, Stu Kulak, Randy Gilhen, Brent Hughes, Scott Schneider, Neil Meadmore, Steven Fletcher, Ron Wilson, Mike Warus and Ryan Stewart. When the AHL Moncton Hawks camp opens at the Coliseum Monday morning, six additional players will join the cast of hopefuls. Attending from the University of Moncton Blue Eagles will be leftwinger-centre Eric Galarneau, rightwinger Daniel Larin and defenceman Patrick Black, all from Laval, Que. paragraphs.text: WINNIPEG (CP) - The Vancouver Canucks chased the Winnipeg Jets goal-for-goal through a National Hockey League exhibition game at the Arena on Monday night and came away with a 3-3 tie. Dale Hawerchuk, Matt Hervey and Dave McLlwain scored for the Jets, while the Canucks, who never held a lead, replied with goals from Rich Sutter, Tony Tanti and Tim Lenardon. Vancouver played with a split squad and were without Soviet stars Igor Larionov and Vladimir Krutov. "In preseason games the wins and losses don't count, obviously, but the way you play the game is important," said Vancouver coach Bob McCammon. "'We had to come back three times with a team split in half so you don't have much cohesiveness. "It's encouraging and I think when we merge the two teams we'll be pretty strong.". Winnipeg, playing for an announced crowd of is 9,829, 1-1-1. raised its preseason record to 4-3-1, while Vancouver's record The Canucks outshot their Smythe Division rivals 27-24. Daniel Berthiaume, looking for a starting role in the Winnipeg net, kicked out 24 of 27 shots and was named the game's first star. "I just want to play my game and I don't want to say who will be No. 1 and who will be No. 2," said the diminutive goaltender who ended up with the Jets' minor league affiliate in Moncton during a rocky 1988-89 campaign. I keep playing the way I am right now I might have a chance. " The teams scored nine seconds apart in the opening period. Sutter fired a shot through traffic that eluded Jets netminder Daniel Berthiaume at 3:59, and Hawerchuk blasted a shot from the left wing between the legs of Vancouver starter Kirk McLean at 4:08. The scoreless second period was a clinic in poor power-play execution. The Jets stickhandled away a five-minute manpower advantage to open the period with Vancouver's Mike McWilliams off for high-sticking, and the Canucks squandered a seven-minute power play when Brent Hughes was sent to the lockerroom for high-sticking and instigating a fight. Hervey broke the deadlock with a blast from the point at 2:54 of the third period, but Tanti restored the tie when he lifted his own rebound .over Berthiaume with Vancouver enjoying a man advantage at 9:04. McLlwain snapped a rebound over fallen rookie Canuck netminder Gus Morschausera at 12:53, and Lenardon converted a pass from Barry Pederson at 15:35 to send the game into overtime.

reported to Moncton. "I called him and he told me he had some personal things to clear up before coming to Moncton," Farrish said. "I told him he should get here soon because we've got three games in four nights." Cole is a rookie from Michigan State University who scored 62 points in 47 games last season. Third -year centre Scott Schneider and freshman defenceman Grant Richison, a Detroit native who played at University of Calgary last winter, are expected to meet the Hawks at the Halifax airport today and possibly play tonight's game in Wolfville. Meantime, the NHL parent Winnipeg Jets still have four goaltenders in Winnipeg. Battling for jobs are: Daniel Berthiaume, Tom Draper, Bob Essensa and Pokey Reddick. There are still nine defencemen with the Jets. Fighting for the final two jobs are: Bryan Marchment, Matt Hervey and Shawn Cronin. At least one, maybe two, of those defencemen will be assigned to the Hawks. Looking for the last three forward spots are: Mark Kumpel, Brian McReynolds, Brad Jones, Brent Hughes, lain Duncan, Laurie Boschman and Stu Barnes. Farrish expects the Jets to assign more players today. It will be another six or seven days before he gets a full roster for the Hawks. 000 NOTES - Look for Maine Mariners to be strong this season. Among the players currently in the Mariners camp are: Peter Douris, Jim Wiemer, John Blum, Ray Neufeld, Billy 0'Dwyer, John Carter and goaltender Frank Caprice. Former Hawks centre Alfie Turcotte is still with Boston and is given a good shot at landing a job witht he Bruins.....Veteran Lane Lambert has left the Bruins camp because they wouldn't guarantee him a contract. He may join the Canadian Olympic team u unless another organization can sign him..... Veteran defenceman Steve Richmond, a standout with New Haven last year, is looking for a job....High-scoring 1 Dale Krentz of Adirondack is looking for a trade because he believes he belongs in the NHL. However, his $75,000 AHL salary has ended many trade talks.....For the first time in three years, Halifax's AHL games will be carried on radio. Play-by-play opf the Citadels road games is expected to be carried by CKO…....Edmonton officials say Mark Lamb has had an outstanding training camp and it's doubtful if he'll be re-assigned to Cape Breton.

By Raad Cawthon STAFF WRITER Stan Drulia has replaced Keith Osborne as one of the Atlanta Knights forwards in tonight's. International Hockey League All-Star Game. Osborne, who leads the league in goals with 34, was called up to the Tampa Bay Lightning Tuesday along with defenseman Chris LiPuma and forward Tim Bergland. For Osborne and Bergland it is the first callup of the season. LiPuma played one game with Tampa Bay two weeks ago. Drulia will join the four Atlanta players already in Phoenix for the game. Goalie David Littman, defensemen Shawn Rivers and Matt Hervey and forward Jock Callander already had been picked to represent the Knights. Drulia started the season in Tampa Bay but was sent to Atlanta in December. Since then he has played in 16 Atlanta games, scoring 24 points on 13 goals and 11 assists while accumulating 16 penalty minutes. His on-ice score is plus-10. The game, in Phoenix's Veteran's Memorial Coliseum, will be televised on tape-delay in Atlanta on SportSouth at 11 p.m. IHL owners also voted to expand Tuesday as a team from Las Vegas was added to the 12-member league. Defencemen Matt Hervey and Jamie Husgen were converted into right wingers Wednesday night and the results were dramatic. Led by the pair's hard-hitting, the Hawks rallied with five in the third period to defeat Baltimore Skipjacks 6-4 before 1,799 spectators at the Baltimore Arena. ... HOCKEY: Lightning acquires Hodge The Boston Bruins acquired right wing Darin Kimble from the expansion Tampa Bay Lightning yesterday for center Ken Hodge and defenseman Matt Hervey. Kimble, 23, has played four seasons in the NHL with Quebec and St. Louis. The 6-foot2, 205-pounder had a goal with three assists in 46 games for the Blues last season. In 177 NHL games, Kimble has 12 goals, 15 assists and 747 penalty minutes. Hodge, 26, was named to the NHL's allrookie team following the 1990-91 season. He's the son of former Bruins star Ken Hodge Sr. Hodge Sr. played with Lightning executive Phil Esposito for Stanley Cup champion Bruins teams in 1970 and 1972. BOXING: Danes win titles Jimmi and Johnny Bredahl of Denmark made pro boxing history last night in Copenhagen as they won World Boxing Organization titles in successive bouts. The brothers, both European champions and undefeated, also became Denmark's first pro boxing champions in modern times before a boisterous crowd of 6,000 at the Parken, Copenhagen's new soccer stadium. Both fights went the distance. Johnny Bredahl scored a unanimous 12-round decision over WBO junior bantamweight champ Jose Quirino of Mexico. It was his 15th consecutive win. Less than two hours later, his older brother Jimmi outpointed veteran Daniel Londas of France after 12 rounds for the WBO junior lightweight championship. ETC.: Dolphins split pair Farley Snow recorded three aces as Jacksonville University beat Davidson 15-8, 15-11, 15-9 at the Coastal Carolina Labor Day volleyball tournament in Myrtle Beach, S.C. JU was beaten by Virginia Commonwealth 15-7, 15-4, 15-4. ... Ricky Huggins hit three home runs and drove in eight runs and Roy Eppley drove in five runs to lead Vernon's to a 28-16 victory over the Armed Forces All-Stars at the ASA Major National softball tournament at Drew Park.

Now the Canucks have settled onWatt, providing they can convince the Toronto native and agent Alan Eagleson to sign a contract. Watt will inherit a troubled team from Neale, who joined the Canucks in 1978 and saw Vancouver make the playoffs every season until 1984-85. Vancouver finished 23 points behind fourth-place Los Angeles Kings and had the only losing record in the Smythe Division. The Canucks are getting old in goal with Richard Brodeur, are too young on defence to be effective and didn't have a 40-goal man on the forward lines. Neale, Bill LaForge, Roger Neilson, Orland Kurtenbach, Phil Maloney, Bill McCreary, Vic Stasiuk and Hal Laycoe have coached the Canucks during their first 15 years in the NHL. Watt will attempt to be successful where they have failed; no Vancouver coach has had a winning record, although Neilson came close at 51-61-21. Watt left the Canucks on good terms in 1981 when he e had the c opportunity to become head coach of the Jets. Winnipeg improved by 48 points that winter while the Canucks went to the Stanley Cup final with Neilson behind the bench after a league suspension to Neale.

By ELLIOTT PAP Richmond Sockeyes are showing the kind of consistency any coach would love - when they play overtime, they win. The Sockeyes are four-for-four in extra time during the B.C. Junior Hockey League playoffs and, not surprisingly, they're undefeated as well. After sweeping both Nanaimo and Delta in four straight, Orland Kurtenbach's troops have vaulted to a 2-0 lead over Kelowna Packers in the best-of-nine final, taking game two 5-4 on defenceman Matt Hervey's goal at 3:37 of the first overtime period Wednesday night. The series now shifts to Kelowna for games three and four Friday and Saturday. Game five is back at Richmond Arena on Tuesday. Last night's thriller was in sharp contrast to the series' opener, which saw Richmond waltz to a 7-0 win. It was 2-2 after one period and 4-3 Richmond after two. "They came out flying and took the body, I think we were a little surprised at that," said Hervey. "It was really anybody's game."

Hervey, a native of Los Angeles, is one of nine 20-year-olds being used by the Sockeyes. He began the season as a free-agent tryout with the NHL Kings, went to Flint of the International League and was gassed when later demotions left him without a spot. He also saw duty with Seattle of the major junior Western League. "I could have gone to the Atlantic Coast League but I had heard a lot of good things about the Sockeyes and that Orland Kurtenbach was a good coach, so I decided to play my last year of junior," said Hervey, who had already spent three years in the WHL with both Victoria and Lethbridge. Hervey's heroics last night came when he pounced on a Dean Rutledge rebound after Rutledge and Paul Rutherford broke in two-onone on Kelowna netminder Dusty Imoo. Other goal scorers for Richmond were Rutledge, Rutherford, Bill Hardy and Dave Tomlinson while Jamie Kurki, Pat Mangold, Kelly Hurd and Mike Murinko replied for the Packers.

They don't wear badges, carry sidearms or read people their rights, but be assured that left wing Jay Miller and defenseman Marty McSorley are policemen for the Los Angeles Kings. Their job: to protect the Hope Diamond of the National Hockey League - Wayne Gretzky. Every team has one or two of them. One of Pittsburgh's is right wing Jay Caufield. His primary role is to make sure the opposition knows that if they intend to mess with the Penguins' superstar, Mario Lemieux, they'll have to deal with him, too. They used to be called goons. Now they're known as enforcers or bodyguards or policemen. Regardless, they are the guys who occasionally give the NHL -and some of its players - a black eye. Not long ago, the standard joke was: "I went to a fight and a hockey game broke out." Ted Green, Dave "Hammer" Schultz, Orland Kurtenbach, Dave "Tiger" Williams and players of their ilk were slow to score and quick to anger, dropping their gloves at the slightest provocation, brawling their way through the season. Sometimes their role was to protect the less volatile, more valuable players on their teams. Sometimes it was simple intimidation. There was a time when Schultz and his Philadelphia Flyers teammates were known collectively as the Broad Street Bullies - and not without good cause. As Bobby Clarke, then their star skaterscorer and later their general manager (and now general manager of the Minnesota North Stars) said of those penalty-laden Flyers of the 1970s: "We take the most direct route to the puck and arrive in ill humor.' In 1988-89, the Penguins set the NHL record in penalty minutes with 2,670. That averages out to 33 minutes a game, or more than half of each game with one or more players in the penalty box. In recent years, the NHL has taken steps to reduce the mayhem on the ice. It penalizes the third man in a fight, penalizes players who might engage in a secondary fight (away from the original one), and is more likely to penalize the instigator of a fight. And, in the event a player leaves the bench to join or start an altercation, the league automatically suspends him for 10 games, his coach for five, and fines the team $25,000. Two years ago, Pittsburgh left wing Troy Loney and then-coach Gene Ubriaco were penalized (and the Penguins were fined) for just such an off-the-bench infraction. Last year there were no such penalties. Bryan Lewis, for 18 years an NHL refer- • ee and now the league's director of officiating, said he was at a dinner recently and found himself seated next to Marty Springstead, the American League's supervisor of umpires.

Pagnucco. Prentice-Hall, 332 pp. $12.95. - The subtitle of this Where-Are- They-Now? hockey reference suggests that the book covers only the stars of hockey's golden era. Actually, anyone who played at least three season's in the NHL between 1952 and 1968 receives an entry. Heroes recalls a time when the Uke line of Bronco Horvath, Johnny Bucyk, and Vic Stasiuk terrorized opposing defences and Glen Hall flopped into a butterfly formation to fend off shots from the likes of Claude Larose and Orland Kurtenbach. If these names send you reeling thrillingly into the past, you'll want to snatch up this book. Though it could use more action photos and some of the 173 player bios provide as scant information as the back of a hockey card. Heroes is and well-researched. Pagnucco has a fine ear for hockey talk, and the book is best when

ANDY SPRUCE Hockey Left winger with the Vancouver Canucks and Colorado Rockies from 1976 to 1979. Career record — 31 goals, 42 assists in 172 games. Spruce played junior for the London Knights, was a fifth-round draft pick of the Vancouver Canucks in 1974, and played in the minors at Seattle and Tulsa. "I can remember how happy I was the third year," Andy recalled recently. "When our coach, Phil Maloney, told me I had made the team and to find an apartment in Vancouver." He signed with the Rockies after becoming a free agent. "We were really a bunch of misfits," he said. "They didn't have a big budget and signed everybody else's fourth-liners. Doug Favell had a great year in goal and Wilf Paiement and Paul Gardner. were our best scorers, and we had Barry Beck, who was just a rookie but was so good, he was our equalizer." Going into the final day of the season, the team was fighting for a playoff spot. "We had to beat St. Louis at home and then hope that Vancouver would tie or lose to Los Angeles on the coast. We had to wait because of the time difference but Los Angeles won 4-3 and that put us in the playoffs. The first round was only a best-of-three series then and Philadelphia beat us 3-2 in overtime, and then 3-1 in Colorado, But just making the playoffs was a thrill. It's the only time that franchise has made it, dating back to Kansas City and to the team that is now in New Jersey." His second coach at Vancouver was Orland Kurtenbach. At

And the piece de resistance: four of the five Canuck skaters sent off for something outrageous so that Darcy Rota could show off his penalty killing skill against five opponents. He proved he can still skate, even if the wind wasn't quite what it might have been a year ago. Rota collapsed finally, in the Alberni defensive zone, and the Oldtimers went down the ice and hammered away at Bromley gleefully until they finally scored. Also surprising to this fan, who was viewing the Canuck oldsters for the first time, was the sheer size of Orland Kurtenbach. No wonder he was treated with respect in his playing d days. Kurt was a gentle giant on this occasion, however, turning in the odd burst of speed and making nifty passes, but by a and large staying in the background and leaving things to fleeter teammates. Those teammates turned it on at will, as you would expect from expros. They would score a few, let Alberni back into it a little, and then slam the door with a few more goals. Clearly, they could have scored at will, even given Community Arenatype lighting.

Young Trevor Ruel gets away a drive past a watchful Orland Kurtenbach in -periods play Saturday at Community Arena. Young Trevor was part of the J and P Meats mites team which humbled the pros 5-1. While the wind lasted, nobody touched him as he ragged the puck the length of the ice.

paragraphs.text: few years ago, and it involved a nine-pin break with a good deadwood in front. Just as he was about to bowl his second ball, a ball flew over from an adjoining lane and knocked down the pin and knocked away the deadwood. The bowler was. very •insistent about being credited with a spare and was rather up tight when told he e could not claim such. This all came under the interference rule and meant a re-set for the bowler, which he did not want...he only wanted the spare. In such cases, you as the bowler in question cannot under any circumstances claim anything that is knocked down by another method or ball, or another bowler. By the same token, the bowler that rolled the errant ball also cannot claim anything on the hit pin. Only pins knocked down by the bowler himself can be claimed. It is very unfortunate that such things like this happen, but like anything else, strange things to happen in this game. The bowler originally. can get a re-set when this happens and I am sure that if that bowler doing the complaining had knocked out a single pin, then had the rest messed up by a stray ball or pin, would be most pleased to be due a re-set. In short, you have to hit them and knock them down yourself without help from any other bowler, or source in order to claim pinfall yourself. Now let's look at that matter of Living hockey fantasies VANCOUVER (CP) - Adult hockey players who dream of taking a feathery pass from the Flower or skating on the same line with the Golden Jet will have that opportunity this summer when the Legends on the Pacific camp debuts at Parksville on Vancouver Island. It's an adult hockey week which features some of the all-time National Hockey League greats, including Guy (Flower) LaFleur and Bobby (Golden Jet) Hull. "It's an opportunity to live your hockey fantasies," says organizer Paul Shmyr, ' who played seven seasons in the National Hockey League and several more in the World Hockey Association. "It's patterned after the adult baseball camps which have been so successful in the (United) States.' The Legends' experience is scheduled for Aug. 24-29 and the old-star lineup also includes Doug Harvey, Bill Gadsby, Johnny Bower, Orland Kurtenbach, Hall of Fame referee Red Storey, Dunc Wilson, Larry Cahan and Shmyr. The adult roster will be limited to 64 players, who want to spend $1,586 each to rub shoulders with some of the game's greatest. Besides hockey, the camp also features charter-boat salmon fishing, tennis, golf, sailing, water skiing and canoeing as activities. "Ever since I retired (in 1982), I've wanted to get back with the players I used to chum around with," Shrnyr says. "'The old-time players think this is a great concept.' Shmyr, who manages Diego's Restaurant in Burnaby, B.C., emphasizes that the the accent will be on having fun for both the rank amateurs and the old-time stars.

paragraphs.text: said with a shrug.* For all the notoriety of that fight, Don's favorite incident involved Orland Kurtenbach. "This was really funny," Cherry says. "He had arms about two miles long. He chipped my tooth with one blow, and when I swung back, I accidentally buried my finger in his eye. It started puffing up right away. "Honestly, I didn't mean to do it. I was really scared. Then the fans started giving it to me, so I had to act like I did it on purpose." herry's tough image C hard didn't family growing times or stem up from in in from a his "Bad Street" environment in Kingston, Ontario. It resulted from a brawl during his rookie year in Hershey. "I was suckered, but good, by Bobby Baun," he says. "I was knocked out cold. When I woke up, there were 10 bodies on top of me and he (Baun) was choking me. I was prepared to die at center ice that night. "Believe me, from then on, I did the suckering." As a player, Cherry averaged nearly one penalty minute per game during his 6½ seasons here. Cherry played just one game in the NHL. He suited up for the Boston. Bruins as a 20-year-old rookie during the 1955 Stanley Cup Playoffs. "My downfall was baseball," he explains. "They knew I liked the game, so they told me not to play it during the off-season. So, of course, I went out and broke my shoulder playing ball. "In those days, if you crossed your team, you were finished. I was a confirmed minor leaguer after that.' Cherry saw more back roads than Rand McNally. In addition to Rochester, he played in Hershey, Springfield, Kitchener, Sudbury, Spokane and Three Rivers. He never made a lot of money. "I got $5,000 my first year in Rochester," he says. "Jack Riley (the GM) got me up to $6,000 the next year. I thought that was great until I learned all the rookies in the league made $6,000. Here I was, in the league 12 years, and I'm making the same as all the rookies. I enjoyed it, though. By ELLIOTT PAP The weekend dethroning of the Richmond Sockeyes as B.C. Junior Hockey League and Centennial Cup national champions has got head coach Orland Kurtenbach thinking - thinking he wants another crack at tier two hockey's biggest prize. The Sockeyes were eliminated by the Vernon Lakers Saturday, losing the best-of-seven BCJHL final 4-1. "I told Bruce Taylor (Sockeyes' owner) I'd let him know in a month to six weeks whether I'd be back," said Kurtenbach, the former Vancouver Canuck captain and coach. "However, I would say it's a fair assessment on your part if you predicted I'm not ready to stop coaching. I have my insurance business and the driving range, but my S enjoyment is still in hockey." The 51-year-old Kurtenbach was lured to the Sockeyes for the 1986-87 season and won everything in sight as a rookie junior coach. The loss to Vernon was Kurtenbach's first playoff defeat after eight straight series victories, plus last May's Centennial Cup tournament.
ORLAND KURTENBACH: decision pending

So I decided to take it on for a year." Fennelly Get Ready For Hockey In Florida omebody up there has a sense of S is humor, For that the one years? past you - the have several to Tampa love. months Bay area or has been offering up first borns right and left for the chance at professional baseball. Domes have been built, junkets have been made, feelers have been sent, and recently, Bay Area significant humans gathered for an odious We-are-the-World singalong. The Mayors of Tampa and St. Pete led the charge. Join the team, buy a ticket! Everybody, join the team, buy a ticket. And it's worked. A group of businessmen from Detroit want to bring a team here. An ice hockey team. No kidding. There is a hockey game tonight in Orlando, town which has itself been bidding for sports as if this was slave auction. The Minnesota North Stars will play the New Jersey Devils. A group out of Detroit, spearheaded by former NHL goalie Jim Rutherford, will be watching closely. Gotta laugh. Go ahead and smirk, baseball brats. But years from now, when you guys are playing out the string with that song (".. . So Kasey, could you please play Join the Team' for Martha in Dubuque.") and the Florida Everblades or whatever are packing some downtown arena, perhaps you'll change your tune. For you Floridians who accept your hockey-before-baseball fate and want to make the most of this cruel joke, some pointers are in order. Baseball and ice hockey, you see, are different from each other. The ice tipped me off. Mind you, there are similarities. Both involve men whacking objects with sticks. Both make important use of organists. Both: are better to watch than "The Love Connection." Unless it's the Braves. From there, however, the two sports diverge. Names. Baseball has guys like Rusty and Peanuts and Lefty. Hockey has Toe and Punch and Gordie. A baseball guy is Sid Bream or Bobby Cox or Don Zimmer. A hockey guy is Rejean Houle or Orland Kurtenbach or Orest Kindrachuk. Regular season. Baseball respects its regular season, allowing only four teams in postseason play. Hockey follows it's 80- game slate by allowing 29 teams into a playoff format, including several NBA

Broncos' 19th consecutive road victory, a blanket - uh, string that stretches back to January. It also allowed the Saskatchewan champs to tie the teams' best-ofseven Western Canadian championship Abbott Cup series 2-2. ORLAND KURTENBACH

Scotty Bowman, Al Arbour, Keith Allen and who knows how many other experienced NHL executives. Watt wasn't the team's first choice to be coach, either. Dave King of the Olympic team accepted the job, but gave it back, all five years on an $800,000 contract. Lorne Henning, an assistant coach under Arbour with New York Islanders before spending a year in the minors as a head coach, could have had the Vancouver job, but turned it down when the Canucks apparently wouldn't match his minor league salary. Now the Canucks have settled on Watt, providing they can persuade the Toronto native and agent Alan Eagleson to sign a contract. Watt will inherit a troubled team from Neale, who joined the Canucks in 1978 and saw Vancouver make the playoffs every season until 1984-85. Vancouver finished 23 points behind fourthplace Los Angeles Kings and had the only losing record in the Smythe Division. The Canucks are getting old in goal with Richard Brodeur, are too young on defence to be effective and didn't have a 40-goal man on the forward lines. Neale, Bill LaForge, Roger Neilson, Orland Kurtenbach, Phil Maloney, Bill McCreary, Vic Stasiuk and Hal Laycoe have coached the Canucks during their first 15 years in the NHL. No Vancouver coach has had a winning record. Watt left the Canucks on good terms in 1981 to become head coach of the Jets. Winnipeg improved by 48 points that winter while the Canucks went to the Stanley Cup final with Neilson after a league suspension to Neale. It was downhill after that for both Watt and the Canucks. Watt eventually was fired by the strong-minded Ferguson after his coach had a run-in with scoring star Dale Hawerchuk. one of the NHL's most gifted players.

HOCKEY Ex-NHLers let dreams .come true VANCOUVER - Adult hockey players who dream of taking a feathery pass s from the Flower or skating on the same line with the Golden Jet will have, that opportunity this summer when the Legends on the Pacific camp debuts at Parksville on Vancouver Island. It's an adult hockey week which features some of the all-time National Hockey League greats, including Guy Lafleur and Bobby Hull. "It's an opportunity to live your hockey fantasies," says organizer Paul Shmyr, who played seven seasons in the National Hockey League and several more 1 in the World Hockey Association. "It's patterned after the adult baseball camps which have been so successful in the (United) States.' The Legends' experience is scheduled for Aug. 24-29 and the old-star lineup also includes Doug Harvey, Bill Gadsby, Johnny Bower, Orland Kurtenbach, Hall of Fame referee Red Storey, Dunc Wilson, Larry Cahan and Shmyr. The adult roster will be limited to 64 players, who want to spend $1,586 each to rub shoulders with some of the game's greatest. Besides hockey, the camp also features charter-boat salmon fishing, tennis, golf, sailing, water skiing and canoeing as activities. "Ever since I retired (in 1982), I've wanted to get back with the players! used to chum around with," Shmyr says. "The old-time players think this is a great concept." Shmyr, who manages Diego's Restaurant in Burnaby, B.C., emphasizes that the accent will be on having fun for both the rank amateurs and the old-time stars. "It won't be like getting in shape or anything like that," he says. "We want everyone to have fun and find out what it's like to play hockey with some of the greats of the game." Shmyr said the camp organizers hope to realize a modest profit and plan to expand the Legends' concept to a Florida site next winter, possibily Fort Lauderdale, where several former NHL players reside. Shmyr also plans to begin publication of an Oldtimers Illustrated hockey magazine to keep retired players informed of such events as Legends on the Pacific. FromCanadianPress

time convincing the NHL that he was, indeed, big enough to play the game. Pat, who was born on July 4, 1940, broke in with Boston in 1961. After two seasons with the Bruins he was traded to Toronto with Orland Kurtenbach and Andy Hebenton for Ron Stewart on June 8, 1965. The Maple Leafs failed to protect him in the NHL waiver draft the next day and he was snapped up by the Chicago Blackhawks. Stapleton went on to become one of the greatest defensemen in Blackhawks' history. Three times a second team NHL All-Star defenseman, Stapleton was selected as a member of the Blackhawks' alltime team in "Chicago Blackhawks: A 60-year History," by author Gerald L. Pfeiffer.

By KEN GILES National Hockey League veteran defenceman Arnie Brown, new coach of the Bramalea Blues, doesn't have aspirations of promoting himself back into the NHL. Brown, who logged 10 years in the NHL, almost seven with the New York Rangers, isn't looking at his Blues' coaching job as a stepping stone to anywhere. "I've done that route," said Brown, Tuesday, following a training camp session at Terry Miller Recreation Centre. Brown, the Ontario Sales manager for Monsanto Plastics Division, Mississauga, was assistant coach of the Vancouver Canucks in 1977-78. He and Canucks' coach Orland Kurtenbach, a former teammate, ed following the '77-'78 season. were replac-

in which the Red Sox overcame adversity to reach their first World Scries in 11 years. Few forecasters had predicted Boston would win its division. Orland Kurtenbach Returns RICHMOND, B.C. (CP) - Orland Kurtenbach is back in hockey coaching at t the Tier Two junior level, far from the pressure-packed National Hockey League. The former NHL player and coach of the Vancouver Canucks for two years is behind the bench of the Richmond Sockeyes this season in the British Columbia Junior Hockey League. He finds the days hectic between his regular job and coaching fuzzy. cheeked teenagers. The man who became the first member of the NHL Canucks in 1970 - he captained and later coached the team - wears three hats. His average working day is 8 a.m. until midnight. Now 50 and only five pounds over his playing weight, Kurtenbach is building his own general insurance business, developing a golf driving range and coaching his favorite game. "I had no intention of getting back into hockey until Bruce Taylor (owner of the Sockeyes) called me in July to find out if I was interested in coaching the team," Kurtenbach said this week. "I told him I didn't have time, what with the problems of getting my insurance business and driving range off the ground. "But, I'd missed being involved in the game at different times, I guess, and took a look at the schedule. All the practices are nights and the travelling isn't too bad . .. so I decided to take it on for a year." Kurtenbach, whose last coaching job was with the 1982-83 Springfield Indians of the American Hockey League, will re-evaluate his priorities next spring before deciding whether to continue coaching. "This is nothing but a hobby for me," said Kurtenbach, who was one of the NHL's toughest forwards during his 11 seasons with the New York Rangers, the Boston Bruins, the Toronto Maple Leafs and the Canucks. "There's no way I want to get back into coaching full-time. I'm doing some long-range planning, soliciting (insurance) clients and getting the driving range ready for next spring.' After his fourth and final season with the NHL Canucks in 1973-74, Kurtenbach turned to coaching Vancouver's Central League farm teams in Seattle and Tulsa. Mid-way through his second season at Tulsa (1976-77), he was summoned to replace Phil Maloney as coach of the Canucks. He also handled the team for all of 1977-78 but was then moved aside with the arrival of Harry Neale. Frances, Ont. Orland Kurtenbach, C - A father of six kids and grandfather of three, Captain Kurt, 53, runs a golf driving range, an insurance business and is coach of the B.C. Junior Hockey League's Richmond Sockeyes. Wayne Maki, LW - Maki endured hell whenever the Canucks played the Boston Bruins, who were intent on avenging a sickening and much publicized stickswinging incident involving the Bruins' Ted Green in 1969. Three years later, after complaining of excruciating headaches, Maki was placed in hospital and a tumor was detected in his brain. He died on May 12, 1974, at age 29. A few days later the Canucks retired his number 11. Maki is the only Canuck player to have been so honored. Rosaire Paiement, RW - When

goals in the 1969-70 season. Poile, surveying the list of nonames, put his arms around coach Hal Laycoe and said, "There you are Hal, there's $6 million worth of talent. Don't mess it up.' The Canucks got off to a quick start that year and were in fourth place two nights before Christmas. Then, in a game in Toronto, captain Orland Kurtenbach was crunched by the Leafs' Bobby Baun, Kurtenbach's knee was shredded and he missed the next 27 games. Without their leader, the Canucks floundered. Vancouver ended up sixth with a 24-46-8 record for 56 points, one better than the Detroit Red Wings. But the placing cost the Canucks a chance to draft either Guy Lafleur or Marcel Dionne, the first two picks in the 1971 draft. When Poile was stocking the fledging Canucks he made sure he had a team you didn't mess with. In addition to Kurtenbach, the Canucks had Quinn, rock -hard Gary Doak, John Schella, Ted Taylor, Bobby Schmautz, a 165-pound buzzsaw, and Rosie Paiement, a heavy puncher who had two decisions over Bobby Orr in front of an appalled I Boston Garden crowd. The first -year Canucks had six 20- goal scorers and kept Pacific Coliseum jammed by winning 17 of their 24 games at home and drew 653,898 fans, 97.5 per cent of total capacity.

Golden oldies invade PoCo ice It'll be a brew bash. Port Coquitlam Rec Centre will be the site of the Molson's Oldtimers Hockey Tournament starting Thursday and running through Sunday. Four teams from the PoCo Oldtimers Hockey League will join 18 squads from throughout the Lower Mainland and a pair from Vancouver Island in the four-day affair. Games start at 6 p.m. Thursday and Friday, 8 a.m. Saturday and 9 a.m. Sunday, when the finals will be played. And the following Saturday, Dec. 5, PoCo Rec Centre will host the CKNW Orphans' Fund game between PoCo Molson's and the CKNW 98ers. The PoCo team will be coached by Ernie "Punch" McLean and will feature National Hockey League veterans Darcy Rota, Gary Lupul, Chris Oddliefson, Orland Kurtenbach, Paul Shymr and Dennis Kearns.

A hockey strike? Please, say it's SO After taking our shot at pro football (upper left hand corner of page), let's do hockey now. I used to be a big hockey fan, used to live at the old Garden in the early 1960s, where the Rangers usually stunk up the place, but they usually beat up on folks. Guys like Vie Hadfield and Reg Fleming, Arnie Brown and Orland Kurtenbach. Boy, could they fight. Then I found out that wasn't the way you were supposed to play hockey. Then I learned the right way. the way the Russians do it, which usually proves embarassing to the NHL teams that chase their passes around the rink. All of which brings us to Alan Eagleson, leader of the NHL players' union. Today, hockey negotiators return to the bargaining table with Eagleson, "hopeful but not confident" of averting a September strike. You know what's coming next here, don't you? Go out on strike. Don't play your 80 games for a whole season at an average of only about $2,000 per game, plus per diem. All of you, go out on strike. See if I care, 'cause I won't.

omebody up there has a sense of S is humor, For that the one years? past you - the have several to Tampa love. months Bay - area or has been offering up first borns right and left for the chance at professional baseball. Domes have been built, junkets have been made, feelers have been sent, and recently, Bay Area significant humans gathered for an odious We-are-the-World singalong. The Mayors of Tampa and St. Pete led the charge. Join the team, buy a ticket! Everybody, join the team, buy a ticket. And it's worked. A group of businessmen from Detroit want to bring a team here. An ice hockey team. No kidding. There is a hockey game tonight in Orlando, a town which has itself been bidding for sports as if this was slave auction. The Minnesota North Stars will play the New Jersey Devils. A group out of Detroit, spearheaded by former NHL goalie Jim Rutherford, will be watching closely. Gotta laugh. Go ahead and smirk, baseball brats. But years from now, when you guys are playing out the string with that song (".. . So Kasey, could you please play Join the Team' for Martha in Dubuque.") and the Florida Everblades or whatever are packing some downtown arena, perhaps you'll change your tune. For you Floridians who accept your hockey-before-baseball fate and want to make the most of this cruel joke, some pointers are in order. Baseball and ice hockey, you see, are 'different from each other. The ice tipped me off. Mind you, there are similarities. Both involve men whacking objects with sticks. Both make important use of organists. Both are better to watch than "The Love Connection." Unless it's the Braves. From there, however, the two sports diverge. Names. Baseball has guys like Rusty and Peanuts and Lefty. Hockey has Toe and Punch and Gordie. A baseball guy is Sid Bream or Bobby Cox or Don Zimmer. A hockey guy is Rejean Houle or Orland Kurtenbach or Orest Kindrachuk. Regular' season. Baseball respects its regular season, allowing only four teams in postseason play. Hockey follows it's 80- game slate by allowing 29 teams into a playoff format, including several NBA squads that missed out. Continued on 10E

paragraphs.text: By Bruce Lowitt St. Petersburg Times They don't. wear badges, carry sidearms or read people their rights, but be assured that left wing. Jay Miller and defenseman Marty McSorley 'are policemen for the Los Angeles Kings. Their job: to protect the Hope Diamond of the National Hockey League - Wayne Gretzky. Every team has one or two of them. One' of Pittsburgh's is right wing Jay Caufield. His primary role is to make sure the opposition knows that if they intend to mess with the Penguins' superstar, Mario Lemieux, they'll have to deal with him, too. They used to be called goons. Now they're known as enforcers or bodyguards or policemen. Regardless, they are the guys who occasionally give the NHL and some of its players a black eye. Not long ago, the standard joke was: "I went to a fight and a Ted hockey Green, game Dave broke "Hammer" out.". Schultz, Orland Kurtenbach, Dave "Tiger" Williams and players of their ilk were slow to score and quick to anger, dropping their gloves at the slightest provocation, brawling their way through the season. Sometimes their role was to protect the less volatile, •more valuable players on' their teams. Sometimes it was simple intimidation. * There was a time when Schultz and his Philadelphia Flyers teammates were known collectively as the Broad Street Bullies - and not without good cause. As Bobby Clarke, then their star skater-scorer and later their general manager (and now general manager of the Minnesota North Stars) said of those penalty-laden Flyers of the 1970s: "We take the most direct route to the puck and arrive in ill humor.". In 1988-89, the Penguins set the NHL record in penalty minutes with 2,670. That averages out to 33 minutes a game, or more than half of each game with one or more players in the penalty box. * In recent years, the NHL has taken steps to reduce the mayhem on the ice. It penalizes the third man in a fight, penalizes players who. might. engage in a secondary fight (away from the original one), and is more likely to penalize the instigator of a fight. And, in the event a player leaves the bench to join or start an altercation, the league automatically suspends him for 10 games, his coach for five, and fines the team $25,000. Two years ago, Pittsburgh left wing Troy Loney and thencoach Gene Ubriaco were penalized (and the Penguins were fined) for just such an off-thebench infraction. Last year there were no such penalties. Bryan Lewis, for 18 years an NHL referee and now the league's director of officiating, said he was at a dinner recently and found himself seated next to Marty Springstead, the American League's supervisor of umpires. "You've had more benchclearing brawls in one year than we've had in the last five," Lewis told Springsteadr:with a grin. "Baseball's such a violent sport.". Thereal goons, A they guys. whose sole job was to mix it up. on the ice and maybe

Orland Kurtenbach is back in hockey coaching at the tier-two junior level. The former NHL player and coach of the Vancouver Canucks is behind the bench of the Richmond Sockeyes in the British Columbia Junior Hockey League. "I had no intention of getting back into hockey until Bruce Taylor (owner of the Sockeyes) called me in July to find out if I was interested in coaching his team," Kurtenbach, 50, said. "I told him I didn't have time, what with the problems of getting my insurance business and golf driving range off the ground. "But, I'd missed being involved in the e game at different times, I guess, and took a look at the schedule. All the practices are at nights and the traveling isn't too bad. ... So I decided to take it on for a year." Kurtenbach became the first member of the Canucks in 1970, first as captain and later as coach of the team.

joining them will be Ivan Boldirev, Ron Sedlbauer, Bobby Schmautz and Orland Kurtenbach. The eastern line-up boasts several of hockey's best-known personalities, from the fun-loving Eddie Shack Paul Henderson, whose goal for Team Canada against the Soviets in 1972 made him a national hero. Team East also includes such legendary figures as Yvan Cournoyer, Darryl Sittler, Frank Mahovlich, Stan Mikita and Gilbert Perreault, the recently-retired Buffalo Sabre.

Quesnel crushed Grande Prairie four games to one to claim the Peace Cariboo Junior Hockey League championship on the weekend. The Millionaires thus became the seventh PCJHL team to go after the Mouat Cup for the provincial junior A championship. But the PCJHL, a combination of teams from the Peace Junior Hockey League and the Cariboo that began in 1975, has never captured the provincial title against B.C. Junior Hockey League representatives. "'We haven't won a game yet," admitted PCJHL commissioner Bob Leer. In fact, the closest a PCJHL team came to winning was three years ago in Prince George when the Spruce Kings lost in double overtime to the Langley Eagles. The Millionaires take on the veteran-loaded Richmond Sockeyes at 7:30 p.m. tomorrow at Minoru Arena. The best-of-three series continues at 7:30 p.m. Saturday and again Sunday, if necessary. The Sockeyes are undefeated in the playoffs, winning 13 consecutive games to sweep three series. Richmond coach Orland Kurtenbach hadn't seen any film or heard many reports on Quesnel earlier this week but plans to get some more data on what he knows to be a young team. He expects the Sockeyes to play their own game, a physical, defensive one that is also capable of exploding for goals when needed.
Staff photo by Greg Osadchuk Coach Orland Kurtenbach and his Sockeyes begin their playoff round with Quesnel tomorrow night in Richmond.

By ARV OLSON He is stirring a cup of coffee, trying to study the league schedule and listening to two separate conversations. His assistant, Mike O'Brien, enters the crowded Richmond Arena office and asks him to circle the starters on the lineup sheet. Orland Kurtenbach glances at his wristwatch and gasps, "it's like this all the time for me these days ... hectic." These days the man who became the first member of the NHL Canucks in 1970, captained and coached the team, is wearing three hats. His average working day is 8 a.m. until midnight. Now 50 and only five pounds over his playing weight, Kurtenbach is building his own year-old general insurance business in New Westminster, developing a golf driving range in Surrey and coaching a junior hockey team in Richmond. "I had no intention of getting back into hockey until Bruce Taylor (owner of the Richmond Sockeyes) called me in July to find out if I was interested in coaching the team," says Kurtenbach. "I told him I didn't have time, what with the problems of getting my insurance business and driving range off the ground. "But I'd missed being involved in the game at different times, I guess, and took a look at the schedule. All the practices are

Canadian Press Tom Watt is expected to become the ninth head coach of the bumbling Vancouver Canucks on Thursday. Watt apparently will give up the cozy confines of the University of Toronto campus for the second time in five years to join the Canucks. He left the Blues the first time in 1980 to become an assistant coach in Vancouver. His initial visit to the West Coast lasted a year. Watt bolted from the Pacific Coliseum to become head coach in Winnipeg, was named coach of the year in the NHL in 1982 and by November 1984 had been fired by Jets' general manager John Ferguson. You'd think Watt would have learned that pro hockey is a dirty business. Just ask Harry Neale, the affable general manager of the Canucks for the final 60 games last season. Neale was hung out to dry by management while the owners searched for a new superboss to become the director of hockey operations. After six weeks in limbo, Neale was finally fired and replaced by his silent assistant, Jack Gordon. Jack Who? Gordon is the new superboss after the Canucks were turned down by Sam Pollock, Scotty Bowman, Al Arbour, Keith Allen and who knows how many other experienced NHL executives. Watt wasn't the team's first choice to be coach, either. Dave King of the Olympic team accepted the job, but gave it back, all five years on an $800,000 contract. Lorne Henning, an assistant coach under Arbour with New York Islanders before spending a year in the minors as a head coach, could have had the Vancouver job, but turned it down when the Canucks apparently wouldn't match his minor league salary. Now the Canucks have settled on Watt, providing they can pursuade the Toronto native and agent Alan Eagleson to sign a contract. Watt will inherit a troubled team from Neale, who joined the Canucks in 1978 and saw Vancouver make the playoffs every season until 1984-85. Vancouver finished 23 points behind fourth Los Angeles Kings and had the only losing record in the Smythe Division. The Canucks are getting old in goal with Richard Brodeur, are too young defence to be effective and didn't have a 40-goal man on the forward lines. Neale, Bill LaForge, Roger Neilson, Orland Kurtenbach, Phil Maloney, Bill McCreary, Vic Stasiuk and Hal Laycoe have coached the Canucks during their first 15 years in the NHL. Watt will attempt to be successful where they have failed; no Vancouver coach has had a winning record, although Neilson came close at 51-61-21. Watt left the Canucks on good terms in 1981 to become head coach of the Jets. Winnipeg improved by 48 points that winter while the Canucks went to the Stanley Cup final with Neilson after a league suspension to Neale. It was downhill after that for both Watt and the Canucks. Watt eventually was fired by the strong-minded Ferguson after his coach had a run-in with scoring star Dale Hawerchuk, one of the NHL's most gifted players.
Roberts is the eighth Indians' coach in the 1980s. The other seven — Johnny Wilson, Tom Webster, Orland Kurtenbach, Doug Sauter, Lorne Henning, Fred Creighton and Gord Lane.

of wearisome travel and constant clock-changing. Playing Los Angeles in back-to-back: games last week, the Canucks travelled 3,000 miles. By contrast, if the Leafs and Detroit play back-to-back, it's 500. That's one reason there's always talk of coaching changes in Vancouver. As owners do everywhere, they reconcile losses by firing coaches. This Is the 17th National Hockey League season for the Canucks and the cerebral Tom Watt, the incumbent, is the ninth coach. That's about average for losers, a new victim every two seasons, The Canucks began with Hal Laycoe in 1970, followed by Vic Stasiuk, Bill McCreary, Phil Maloney, Orland Kurtenbach, Harry Neale, Roger Neilson, Bill Laforge and now att. Last week, the papers suggested Watt was spared the inevitable when the Canucks won the back-toback games with the Kings. Watt, headstrong and outspoken, a trifle testy before those games. "Sure, they're important, but whether we win both or lose both doesn't mean I'll be any better or worse as a hockey coach 48 hours later." Every now and then Watt takes off on his oppressors among new. shounds. He's emotional by nature, a 51-year-old native of Toronto who won nine national intercollegiate MEDIA - Page D3

like all the others, probably wouldn't last under Bowman. For the record, Crisp wanted to be considered for the Springfield job a couple of years back when the Black Hawks were looking for a replacement for Orland Kurtenbach here. Howver, Chicago didn't pursue Crisp, opting for. Doug Sauter. Speaking of Kurtenbach, he still has his own insurance, business in the Vancouver. area. and is playing regularly for the Vancouver oldtimers team. They recently played eight games in eight days against the touring Toronto old-timers. Kurtenbach, a close follower of the Smythe Division, predicts a dogfight for the playoff spots. "Vancouver should make it, but Winnipeg has a favorable schedule the rest of the way," he said. His picks for the Stanley Cup are Edmonton or Philadelphia. He plans to take in the last 10 Canuck home games and is lookforward to seeing his old goaltender, Bob Janecyk when Los Angeles comes to town. ... From the fans: William Santanello of Springfield writes that he feels there are two additional reasons Indians' attendance is down. 1. The ticket price increase over last year. 2. The increased prices for concessions. "I realize the team's expenses go up every season like they do for the rest of us," writes Santanello, but when hockey, which is a family game, becomes too high on a local minor league level for the family to afford, then attendance will decline." In response, Indian ticket prices are $6 and $7. Last year, they were $5.50 and $6.50. So there was a 50 cent increase in prices. However, when the building opened in 1972, the Springfeld Kings charged $3, $4, and $5. So the top ticket has gone up only $2 in 13 years, or an inflation rate of just over three percent per year. So while the ticket increase was probably not timely in view of the team's recent on-ice performance and the competition with other entertaiment vehicles, the team's past performance has probably been more of a problem than the price.

Saturday set for stars Punch and company come to Port Coquitlam this Saturday. PoCo Molson's will take on CKNW 98ers in the CKNW Orphans' fund game at PoCo Rec Centre. The PoCo team will be coached by Ernie "Punch" McLean and will feature National Hockey League veterans Darcy Rota, Gary Lupul, Chris Oddliefson, Orland Kurtenbach, Paul Shymr and Dennis Kearns. The CKNW squad will feature the faces behind the voices of the New Westminster radio station. Game time is 6:15 p.m. and admission is by donation.

omebody up there has a sense of S is For humor, that the one years? past you - the have several to Tampa love. months Bay - area or has been offering up first borns right and left for the chance at professional baseball. Domes have been built, junkets have been made, feelers have been sent, and recently, Bay Area significant humans gathered for an odious We-are-the-World singalong. The Mayors of Tampa and St. Pete led the charge. Join the team, buy a ticket! Everybody, join the team, buy a ticket. And it's worked. A group of businessmen from Detroit want to bring a team here. An ice hockey team. No kidding. There is a hockey game tonight in Orlando, a town which has itself been bidding for sports as s if this was slave auction. The Minnesota North Stars will play the New Jersey Devils. A group out of Detroit, spearheaded by former NHL goalie Jim Rutherford, will be watching closely. Gotta laugh. Go ahead and smirk, baseball brats. But years from now, when you guys are playing out the string with that song ("... So Kasey, could you please play, Join the Team' for Martha in Dubuque.") and the Florida Everblades or whatever are packing some downtown arena, perhaps you'll change your tune. For you Floridians who accept your hockey-before-baseball fate and want to make the most of this cruel joke, some pointers are in order. Baseball and ice hockey, you see, are different from each other. The ice tipped me off. Mind you, there are similarities. Both involve men whacking objects with sticks. Both make important use of organists. Both are better to watch than "The Love Connection." Unless it's the Braves. From there, however, the two sports diverge. Names. Baseball has guys like Rusty and Peanuts and Lefty. Hockey has Toe and Punch and Gordie. A baseball guy is Sid Bream or Bobby Cox or Don Zimmer. A hockey guy is Rejean Houle or Orland Kurtenbach or Orest Kindrachuk. Regular season. Baseball respects its regular season, allowing only four teams in postseason play. Hockey follows it's 80- game slate by allowing 29 teams into a playoff format, including several NBA squads that missed out. Continued on 10E

By Grant Kerr VANCOUVER (CP) - Adult hockey players who dream of taking a feathery pass from the Flower or skating on the same line with the Golden Jet will have that opportunity this summer when the Legends on the Pacific camp debuts at Parksville on Vancouver Island. It's an adult hockey week which features some of the all-time National Hockey League greats, including Guy (Flower) Lafleur and Bobby (Golden Jet) Hull. *'It's an opportunity to live your hockey fantasies," says organizer Paul Shmyr, who played seven seasons in the National Hockey League and several more in the World Hockey Association. *It's patterned after the adult baseball camps which have been so successful in the (United) States." The Legends' experience is scheduled for Aug. 24-29 and the old-star lineup also includes Doug Harvey, Bill Gadsby, Johnny Bower, Orland Kurtenbach, Hall of Fame referee Red Storey, Dunc Wilson, Larry Cahan and Shmyr. The adult roster will be limited to 64 players, who want to spend $1,586 each to rub shoulders with some of the game's greatest. Besides hockey, the camp also features charter-boat salmon fishing, tennis, golf, sailing, water skiing and canoeing as activities. "Ever since I retired (in 1982), I've wanted to get back with the players I used to chum around with," Shmyr says. "The old-time players think this is a great concept." Fun Times Shmyr, who manages Diego's Restaurant in Burnaby, B.C., cmphasizes that the accent will be on having fun for both the rank amateurs and the old-time stars. A typical camp day will begin with breakfast, a morning practice session, lunch, fishing or golfing in the afternoon, dinner and two scrimmage games at night before a

(CP) The British Columbia Junior Hockey League is investigating an incident which cost the Richmond Sockeyes the services of their top goaltender while he was on emergency loan to a rival team. Willie Popp, 20, of St. Albert, Alta., suffered a broken wrist last Saturday while playing for the Chilliwack Eagles and will be sidelined for at least six weeks, Richmond coach Orland Kurtenbach said Tuesday. Popp broke the wrist of his catching hand when checked by a member of the Juan De Fuca Whalers during the BCJHL game in Victoria. Chilliwack requested the use of a 1 replacement goaltender when Glen Pencer was injured and two other Eagle affiliate netminders were under suspension.

paragraphs.text: Army. The drive will be carried out between 6 and 8 p.m. on April 28 throughout the Greater Vancouver area. Funds raised during the Red Shield Appeal campaign will help fund programs that provide meals and accommodation for the homeless, residential care for youths, family assistance and counselling, care for senior citizens, guidance programs and residential centres for drug and alcohol abusers, court and prison rehabilitation worker and support for single expectant mothers. The Salvation Army has also added new services in the past year, including a residence for young offenders and a home for battered women and children. Those willing donate time to the Red Shield Appeal are asked to call Shirley Muir in the Coquitlam area (937-0758), Elma Stewart in Port Coquitlam (942-5378) R.B. Shelton in Port Moody (591-2793) or Orland Kurtenbach in New Westminster (421-5805).

ORLAND KURTENBACH Hockey Contre with the Now York Rangers, Boston Bruins, Toronto Maple Loats and Vancouver Canucks botween 1961 to 1974; coach of the Canucks from 1976 l0 1978. Caroor record — 119 goals, 213 assists in 630 games as a player: 36-62-27 in 125 games as a coach. Kurtenbach played junlor for the Prince Albert (Sask.) Mintos and was the rookie of the year with Vancouver of the Western League in 1958. He playod for 10 teams in his first 10 seasons as a pro. "Il was hard to make the National Hockey League whon there were only six clubs," he recalled recently, "When I was at San Francisco in 1966, 1 was ready to quit, find a job and stay there. But we won the championship and the following year I went back to Boston," He finished high school at the age ol 28, taking correspondence courses, and later attended the UnIversity of Toronto. He was the first player taken by the Canucks in the 1970 expansion draft, served as team captain for four seasons, then retired atter a knee injury. He coached the Seattle Totems and Tulsa Oilers and was the Central League's coach of the year atter Tulsa won the championship in ") 1976. That December, he look over as coach of the Canucks, replacing Phil Maloney, who stayed as general manager. Kurtenbach was the team's titth coach, following Hal

STEVE BOSCH ORLAND KURTENBACH: putting in 16-hour days

By ARV OLSON He is stirring a cup of coffee, trying to study the league schedule and listening to two separate conversations. His assistant, Mike O'Brien, enters the crowded Richmond Arena office and asks him to circle the starters on the lineup sheet. Orland Kurtenbach glances at his wristwatch and gasps, "it's like this all the time for me these days These . . hectic." the man who became the first member of the NHL Canucks in 1970, captained and coached the team, is wearing three hats. His average working day is 8 a.m. until midnight. Now 50 and only five pounds over his playing weight, Kurtenbach is building his own year-old general insurance business in New Westminster, developing a golf driving range in Surrey and coaching a junior hockey team in Richmond. "I had no intention of getting back into hockey until Bruce Taylor (owner of the Richmond Sockeyes) called me in July to find out if I was interested in coaching the team," says Kurtenbach. "I told him I didn't have time, what with the problems of getting my insurance business and driving range off the ground. "But I'd missed being involved in the game at different times, I guess, and took a look at the schedule. All the practices are

old defenceman at the time. "We were a ragtag bunch. We weren't even what you'd call adequate by the standards of the NHL at that time. But we were good workers and I think every one was good people." For $6 million, general manager Bud Poile got the right to select 18 players in the expansion draft who had had scored a grand total of 37 goals in the 1969-70 season. Poile, surveying the list of nonames, put his arms around coach Hal Laycoe and said, "There you are Hal, there's $6 million worth of talent. Don't mess it up." The Canucks got off to a quick start that year and were in fourth place two nights before Christmas. Then, in a game in Toronto, captain Orland Kurtenbach was crunched by the Leafs' Bobby Baun, Kurtenbach's knee was shredded and he missed the next 27 games. Without their leader, the Canucks floundered. Vancouver still ended up sixth with a 24-46-8 record for 56 points, one better than the Detroit Red Wings. But the placing cost the Canucks a chance to draft either Guy Lafleur or Marcel Dionne, the first two picks in the 1971 draft.

VETS CHARITY CHALLENGE A host of hockey legends will take to Pacific Coliseum ice March 27 all in aid of Rick Hansen's Man in Motion World Tour. The 7 p.m game "Legends of Hockey: the East-West Challenge" will features some fimilar names to fans in the Vancouver area. Long-time Canucks Bobby Schmautz and Dune Wilson will join former teammates Gregg Boddy, John Grisdale, Ivan Boldirev, Gary Bromley, Jiri Bubla, Orland Kurtenbach, Bobby Lalonde, Chris Oddliefson, Gerry O'Flahery, Darcy Rota, and Dennis Ververgaert in a battle against a star-laden eastern squad. Gilbert Perreault, Bobby Hull and Yvan Cournoyer will suit up in East team uniforms. Tickets to the game are $6, $9, and $12 and are available at all VTC/ CBO outlets. All proceeds go to Rick Hansen's fund for spinal cord research. The game is sponsored by the PNE, the Vancouver Canucks and other groups, including CP Air, Molson's, CKNW, the Town of Whistler, the B.C. Hotels Association and Olympic Fitness.
