car he was riding in, is being held in jail without bond. RICHMOND, B.C. (CP) Orland Kurtenbach, who coached Richmond to a national championship last season, has agreed to return to the Sockeyes for another year, the British Columbia Junior Hockey League team announced Monday. Kurtenbach guided the Sockeyes to the Centennial Cup title during his first season behind the Richmond bench. The former National Hockey League player was the first captain of the NHL Canucks in 1970 and later coached the team for two years.

paragraphs.text: By ELLIOTT PAP Richmond Sockeyes are showing the kind of consistency any coach would love - when they play overtime, they win. The Sockeyes are four-for-four in extra time during the B.C. Junior Hockey League playoffs and, not surprisingly, they're undefeated as well. After sweeping both Nanaimo and Delta in four straight, Orland Kurtenbach's troops have vaulted to a 2-0 lead over Kelowna Packers in the best-of-nine final, taking game two 5-4 on defenceman Matt Hervey's goal at 3:37 of the first overtime period Wednesday night. The series now shifts to Kelowna for games three and four Friday and Saturday. Game five is back at Richmond Arena on Tuesday. Last night's thriller was in sharp contrast to the series' opener, which saw Richmond waltz to a 7-0 win. It was 2-2 after one period and 4-3 Richmond after two. "They came out flying and took the body, I think we were a little surprised at that," said Hervey. "It was really anybody's game.'

Hervey, a native of Los Angeles, is one of nine 20-year-olds being used by the Sockeyes. He began the season as a free-agent tryout with the NHL Kings, went to Flint of the International League and was gassed when later demotions left him without a spot. He also s saw duty with Seattle of the major junior Western League. "I could have gone to the Atlantic Coast League - but I had heard a lot of good things about the Sockeyes and that Orland Kurtenbach was a good coach, so I decided to play my last year of junior," said Hervey, who had already spent three years in the WHL with both Victoria and Lethbridge. Hervey's heroics last night came when he pounced on a Dean Rutledge rebound after Rutledge and Paul Rutherford broke in two-onone on Kelowna netminder Dusty Imoo. Other goal scorers for Richmond were Rutledge, Rutherford, Bill Hardy and Dave Tomlinson while Jamie Kurki, Pat Mangold, Kelly Hurd and Mike Murinko replied for the Packers.

Molsons to battle old NHLers In a event that's bound to bring back memories, the Port Coquitlam Molsons Oldtimers hockey club will play the CKNW 98's this Saturday. Bolstered by the likes of former-Canucks Darcy Rota, Orland Kurtenbach, Dennis Kearns, Paul Shymr and guest coach Ernie "Punch" MacLean, the 98's should prove formidable opposition. The game goes at 6:50 p.m. Saturday in the Port Coquitlam Rec Centre. There is no admission charge, but donations will be accepted with all funds raised going to the CKNW Orphan's Fund and PoCo minor hockey.

Bottay and Look teamed up to win the 11-12 A duet title ahead of Fleury and Lyman. Andrea Adams and Theresa Grigg finished third. In B duet, Sara Doherty and Melanie Yeats finished second. Crews and Thoeny combined to win the 13-14 duet title while Coquitlam's Leanne Startin won the 13-and-over B solo title. In team competition, the Coralinas 11-12 A team won their class while in the 13-14 year old team event, Coquitlam's L and F squads finished second and third respectively. The under-12 Coralinas S and P teams finished one-two in the B event and the over-13 B team finished fourth. Diplomats grab B One team won their division and two other lost out in title game overtime as six Coquitlam-area squads participated in the weekend's Maple Ridge Icemen's 18-team Oldtimers Hockey Tournament. Playing in the B event, the Coquitlam Diplomats won three straight in round-robin play and then got two goals from Scott Steele enroute to a 7-2 win over Burnaby's College Place in Sunday's title game. Two less fortunate teams were the C Division Coquitlam Senators and the D Division Empire Pizza men, who both lost finals in overtime to Maple Ridge squads. The Senators went down 2-1 to the Green Growers with their only goal coming from John Gillies while Empire Pizza fell 5-4 to the Mountain Boys despite goals from Bryan Farquharson, Doug Brown, Jerry Gottschalk and Glen Birth. Other local teams participating in the fifth annual tournament included the A Division's Port Moody Fog Duckers, the C side Coquitlam Shifty 50's, with former Vancouver Canuck Orland Kurtenbach in their lineup, and the D Division Coquitlam Senators.

By KEN GILES Arnie Brown, a 10-year National Hockey League veteran defenceman with five different teams, has been named the new coach of the Bramalea Blues. He replaces Metro Junior B Hockey League Fullan Division coach-of-theyear Mark Logan. Pat Maloney, who assisted Logan last season, will continue as an assistant coach with Brown. Brown first contacted Blues' owner Rick Hay regarding the possibilty of coaching junior hockey during the past playoffs against Henry Carr. Blues' 1986-1987 coaching arrangements were finalized Thursday lowing a third meeting between Hay and Brown. Hay has known Brown Ince their junior playing days with the Toronto Marlboros and St. Michael's ollege Buzzers. "I selected Brown because I believed he was the type of coach I wanted. le's not in it to further a coaching career, but he wants to help young layers whether its scholarships or to go to the OHL," said Hay. Brown, who has been coaching minor hockey in Mississauga, was an assistant NHL coach with the Vanbuver Canucks when the club was uided by Orland Kurtenbach. A family emergency, in New York, ept Brown from attending the Blues' anquet, Saturday, Brown, who was part of the fivelayer deal which brought Bramalea esident Andy Bathgate to the Toronto Maple Leafs in 1964, played six years with New York Rangers.

important to have a team effort. He singled out Bob Murray, John Grisdale and Gregg Boddy as being the best of the Canucks on the night. The Firefighters were understandably pleased with their showing after the game. "Our finish was better than what we normally do," said the Fighters' coach in the dressing room during a brief post-game celebration. He said the team was pretty scrambly in its own end at times, blaming that on the fact they play full contact hockey in the Delta Sr. Men's League. And felt the game was a good tune up for the Western Canada Fighfighters tournament. One line in particular (Bruce Debbitt, Perry Schnarr and Doug Bannon) played extremely well for the younger squad picking up 11 points including five goals. Schnarr, who the coach said may someday be back with an NHL team after being a former draft pick of Washington, scored twice and looked to be the best of a determined team. Catching the Canucks flat-footed, the Firefighters opned up a 4-1 lead after 20 minutes.. A scrambly second period was highlighted by a Canuck resurgance, as Gregg Boddy set up Orland Kurtenbach mid-way through the period to pull the Canucks to within one. After Brolmley stopped a breakway attempt halfway through the final period when the score was 6-5, the Delta league crew broke the game wide open en-route to their 9-6 victory.

NHLers such as Orland Kurtenbach, Rick Blight and Gregg Boddy in their lineup, were bounced 9-6 by the Vancouver Firefighters in an exhibition game which featured some tremendous individual efforts. In fact when the Canucks started to put things together they too showed they still had some of the magic left in those worn out old frames. "It was a very enjoyable game," said Canucks' mentor Larry Cahan. "They've got a good team and you have to give them credit." While the Canucks knew the Firefighters had a good team Cahan was further impressed after the game, praising them for playing their positions, as well as skating and passing which he said was "excellent." Which player impressed the expros most? "Numbers one through 30," quipped Blight. Cahan said a lot of kids expect them to win all the time, but even Edmonton Oilers need a good lesson once in a while. He added, "Don't forget we're spotting them 25 years in a lot of positions." By ELLIOTT PAP The weekend dethroning of the Richmond Sockeyes as B.C. Junior Hockey League and Centennial Cup national champions has got head coach Orland Kurtenbach thinking - thinking he wants another crack at tier two hockey's biggest prize. The Sockeyes were eliminated by the Vernon Lakers Saturday, losing the best-of-seven BCJHL final 4-1. "I told Bruce Taylor (Sockeyes' owner) I'd let him know in a month to six weeks whether I'd be back," said Kurtenbach, the former Vancouver Canuck captain and coach. "However, I would say it's a fair assessment on your part if you predicted I'm not ready to stop coaching. I have my insurance business and the driving range, but my enjoyment is still in hockey." The 51-year-old Kurtenbach was lured to the Sockeyes for the 1986-87 season and won everything in sight as a rookie junior coach. The loss to Vernon was Kurtenbach's first playoff defeat after eight straight series victories, plus last May's Centennial Cup tournament.
ORLAND KURTENBACH: decision pending

Former Vancouver Canuck Darcy Rota scored late in the game to snap a tie and give Port Coquitlam's Molsons Oldtimers a 5-4 win over the CKNW 98's in a charity hockey game played Saturday night. Both teams were bolstered by a bevy of former NHLers including Rota, Orland Kurtenbach, Gerry O'Flaherty, Bob Murray and Paul Shymr, who played for Molsons, while the 98's had the help of Gary Monohan, Dennis Kearns and Jim Holt. The $600 raised at the PoCo Rec Centre went to CKNW's Orphan's Fund and Port Coquitlam's minor hockey association, said Molsons team representative Gary Elgear. Elgear explained that Molsons had the advantage with local folk hero Ernie "Punch" MacLean behind the bench. MacLean repeated his famous 'garbage can on
Providing some style in the charity oldtimers' game Saturday was former Canuck Orland Kurtenbach, who helped Molsons to a win over the CKNW 98's. NEWS Photo by Craig Hodge

SAGINAW - Even though it may seem as though Mike Stapleton was destined to become a professional hockey player, the Saginaw Hawks' center says it's not true. Stapleton grew up in a hockey atmosphere because his father, Pat Stapleton, played 15 years in the NHL and World Hockey Association. "But we've never been what you would call a real hockey family, though," said Mike. "We all tried not to bring the game home with us. f "My father never pushed me (to be a hockey player). I could have just as casily been an airplane pilot. He's always been there for us kids." Stapleton has two hockeyplaying brothers and three sisters. His older brother, Tom, 24,; is playing in Sweden. A younger brother, Chris, lives on the family's farm outside of London, Ontario. "Chris is a great little kid," said Mike. "Not so little really. He almost towers over me right now and he just turned 14. "He's a goaltender and he's going to be a good one. And why not? His godparent is Tony Esposito.". Mike, a 5-foot-10, 183-pounder, takes after his father, who was a very successful defenseman despite standing just 5-foot-8 and weighing 185. Pat Stapleton had a dickens of a time convincing the NHL that he was, indeed, big enough to play the game. Pat, who was born on July 4, 1940, broke in with Boston in 1961. After two seasons with the Bruins he was traded to Toronto with Orland Kurtenbach and Andy Hebenton for Ron Stewart on June 8, 1965. The Maple Leafs failed to protect him in the NHL waiver draft the next day and he was snapped up by the Chicago Blackhawks. Stapleton went on to become one of the greatest defensemen in Blackhawks' history. Three times a second team NHL AllStar defenseman, Stapleton was selected as a member of the Blackhawks' alltime team in "Chicago Blackhawks: A 60- year History," by author Gerald L. Pfeiffer. His six assists on March 30, 1969 against the Red Wings is still a NHL record for most assists by a defenseman in a single game.

MONCTON As Moncton Hawks' coach Rick Bowness sifts through his statistics from this American Hockey League season, the negative happenings heavily outweigh the positive things but one stat that stands out in his mind is the play of rookie defenceman Matt Hervey. The Hawks are in last place in the American Hockey League's Northern Division with a record of 21-39-7 and 51 points, 10 points out of the last playoff spot, which is being held down by the Nova Scotia Oilers. Tonight, Moncton will host the Sherbrooke Canadiens at 7:30 o'clock at the Coliseum. On Wednesday, the same two teams fought to a 2-2 overtime tie. Remember Hervey, the guy who came to the Winnipeg Jets training camp as a walk-on and in his second shift in Winnipeg, he "smacked Doug Smail," recalled Winnipeg scout Peter Sullivan. "Then Laurie Boschman came off the bench and took into him." "You get noticed that way," said Bowness. "He's our only plus player. That says it all." Last season, Hervey played in the British Columbia Tier II Junior Hockey League with the Richmond Sockeyes, under coach Orland Kurtenbach, a former NHLer. **We didn't have a clue what he was like," said Bowness, adding that, "Orland Kurtenbach recommended him. Atoms spend holidays in B.C. The Ackland's Atom AAs will spend the better part of their holiday in Langley, B.C. at an invitational hockey tournament. The atoms take off today for event, which features 16 teams from B.C. and the United States, along with the Yukoners. The team plays its first game tonight at 6:30 p.m. against Portland, Oregon, and further game times will be determined by virtue of a win or loss in the opener. Clubs are guaranteed four games. Coaching the youngsters will by Trevor Haggard, who says his club should be competitive. "By no means will we get blown away," said Haggard. "We have a good bunch of kids that may be small, but they work hard." Haggard said tournament organizers turned down several clubs wanting into the event, but chose Whitehorse and others, such as the Los Angeles Junior Kings, for a novelty approach. The tournament is labelled for Atom Bs, as they used in the U.S., but that is the same ranking as the double 'A' used in the Yukon and B.C. Making up the team are: Defence - Rick McLeod, Michael Eberts, Jeff Kormos, Jimmy Coxford, Patrick Kenny and the female representative, Nancy Sholtz; Forward - Ryan Kinney, Charro Osmond, Wes Marsh, Chris Nash, Jamie Cairns, Willie Mason (captain), Garret Wheeler (assistant captain), Jonathan Carlick and Ben Harper; Goaltender Chad Warren. 6,

The toughest part for beginners is mastering skating and then tackling the moves and stick-and-puck work of the game. Without knowing how to skate well, trying to play hockey is like trying to play tennis without knowing how to walk. For players that progress beyond the novice level, there is the Lane Amateur Hockey Association, a six-team league that plays on Wednesday nights from 7 to 11:30. There are also a pair of 40-years and older teams that play on Sunday nights. Players from the latter league frequently play in the LAHA as well. "We're now generating hockey people from the community and not relying on people who move here," Smith said. "That's great." To help cultivate the future hockey lovers, Lane County Ice runs youth hockey sessions on Wednesdays and Saturdays (for more information call Lane County Ice at 687-3615).

The twice-weekly sessions allow longtime hockey players and fans from other parts of the country to get back in touch with their sport. They also help Oregonians wet their frigid feet in a somewhat foreign sport.

"The sessions we have are nice for everybody because it would be pretty much dead time on the ice anyway," Bahls said. One of the rink's most dedicated hockey players is 67-year-old Hector Smith, who grew up with the sport in
drop hockey. That number is respectable given the odd times for the pickup sessions.

gathered up some skis and a couple of wet suits and traded them in for some used hockey gear. "It took one time and I was hooked," he said. The players all bring their own sticks and gear - the rink provides the ice and nets - and split into teams. After a warmup period, a scrimmage starts and goes on for the majority of the session.

"Working there all the time I saw everybody playing (hockey) and it looked like too much fun to let everybody else have all the fun." He borrowed equipment the first time he played, but then went home,

Before the opening of Lane County Ice three years ago, the last time Smith played an organized hockey game was for the Canadian Navy in 1945. "There's hockey all around us here, it's just that it only found its expression when this rink opened," Smith said. "There's a surprisingly large number of people in this town from Minnesota, Illinois, Wisconsin and especially Canada who love the game and were just waiting for a place to play." Smith and Bahls both believe there's an undiscovered appeal to hockey for newcomers. The sport combines the aerobics of nearly constant movement with the fun and skill of a game - in a way like basketball. "I've played both," Smith said, "and basketball doesn't hold a candle to the kick you get out of hockey."
HOCKEY

"It helps so much to get out and stick-handle or practice shots," said Tom Bahls, rink manager at Lane County Ice and an avid drop-in player himself. "*(Drop-in hockey) is really the only venue that people have to practice and work on their skills before playing in games.'
HOCKEY ® Continued

"We have guys who come every time, they're real troupers," said Trish Massey, a staffer at Lane County Ice. "We get a lot of college students, a lot of professional people that come in and do it on their lunch hour - a real variety of people." The winter schedule for drop-in hockey at Lane County Ice is 11:15 a.m. to 12:45 p.m. on Mondays and from 6 a.m. to 8 a.m. on Fridays.

TORONTO — Mike Murphy, an assistant coach with the Toronto Maple Leafs the last three seasons and once the head coach of the Los Angeles Kings, joined the New York Rangers yesterday as an assistant. Murphy will work with new Rangers coach Colin Campbell, who was an assistant last year under Mike Keenan. "Leaving Toronto is really tough, it was a hard choice," Murphy said. "I was quite happy here. It's the Mecca of hockey. "The Rangers made me an offer that was impossible for me to turn down. That's the bottom line. It was just a , fiscally sound decision." Murphy, 43, has also been an assistant with the Kings and Vancouver Canucks.

NEW YORK (AP) - Commissioner Gary Bettman got approval from the NHL Board of Governors on Friday for league players to participate in the 1998 Olympics at Japan, and he gained an extra two years of labor peace in the bargain. A few details involving the world championships and Canada Cup competition still were to be worked out with the International Ice Hockey Federation and the International Olympic Committee, but it appeared the NHL participation at Nagano was set. NHL senior vice president and chief operating officer Steve Solomon attended Friday's meeting of the IIHF in Budapest, Hungary. "There are some items still open, some details to be solved," IIHF spokesman Kimmo Leinonen said. As part of the Olympic agreement, both the league and NHL Players Association agreed to waive the reopener clause in their Collective Bargaining Agreement, reached after an acrimonious 105-day lockout that disrupted last season. The original deal allowed either side to reopen the contract after the 1997-98 season. Without it in place, the current agreement stretches until the year 2000. "All sides have given up reopeners," Bettman said. "Both sides concluded it made sense in the context of building the sport. If the Olympics are as beneficial as we think they'll be, let's take advantage of it. It says a lot about how relations between the players association and the league have evolved in the last year. "There's nothing wrong with labor peace." Bettman and Bob Goodenow, head of the players association, canceled plans to fly to Budapest late Friday. Goodenow said the Olympic participation was not a done deal because of details still being worked on in Hungary. "There's still work to be done," he said. "If everything falls in place, we've agreed to the reopener." Norman Green, owner of the Dallas Stars, said waiving the reopener clause was a positive for both sides. "We've been talking about Olympic involvement for 20 years," he said. "Finally, it's been accomplished.'

Bill to protect schools approved TALLAHASSEE, Fla. Florida universities would be able to sue athletes, boosters, agents and others responsible for damages caused by violations of NCAA rules, under legislation approved Monday by a state House subcommittee. Under the legislation, a university that loses television or bowl revenue due to probation for an NCAA violation could sue to recover those losses from the players or others responsible for the violation, including boosters or agents who induce the player to violate the rules. Soviets clinch first place STOCKHOLM, Sweden Rookie Valery Shiriaiev scored at 7:04 of the third period as the Soviet Union edged Sweden 3-2 to clinch first place in the preliminary round of the World Hockey. Championships Monday night. The Soviets finished the single round-robin portion with a perfect 7-0 record. Dale Hawerchuck had one goal and one assist as Canada beat Czechoslovakia 4-2 to finish third with a 5-0-2 wontied-lost record.

lection on Nebraska quarterback Steve Taylor, who may play in Canada, and the Pittsburgh Steelers took a wrestling champion, defensive end Carlton Haselrig of Pit in the same round. The Chicago Bears, hoping bloodlines count, went on the 10th round for running back Byron Sanders of Northwestern, older brother of Barry Sanders, the Heisman Trophy winner from Oklahoma State who was the third pick of the draft by Detroit. The Seattle Seahawks, apparently believing that to forgive is divine, drafted Derrick Fenner of North Caro-| lina, who led the Atlantic Coast Conference in rushing in 1986, then was charged in 1987 with a drug-related murder in Maryland. The murder charges were dropped and Fenner eventually pleaded guilty to one count of cocaine possession in a plea bargaining agreement. And Detroit, hoping it had a man to fit the run-and-shoot, used its 10thround pick on wide receiver Jason Phillips of Houston, who led the nation with 108 catches in a similar offense. There was also a bit of nepotism of sorts. Jimmy Johnson, the new coach of the Dallas Cowboys, used his 10th-and 11th-round picks on two linebackers who played for him at the University of Miami. Johnson chose Rod Carter and then Randy Shannon for the small Hurricane reunion at Cowboys training camp. Overall, as with any draft, it was tough to assess what teams did well. But based on pre draft scouting reports, Miami and Chicago seemed to do well- -the Dolphins by getting running back Sammie Smith and safety Louis Oliver on the first round; the Bears with the first-round picks of defensive end Trace Armstrong and cornerback Cornell Woolford and the second-round choice of linebacker John Roper. All could be immediate starters. If Peete turns out to be of first-round value rather than sixth, Detroit could also fare well once it gets used to the run-and-shoot. The Lions, a slow, plodding team for years, improved dramatically on defense last year added speed this year in Sanders and second-round pick John Ford, a wide receiver from Virginia.

Tennis Championships in Newport, R.I. • Russian Andrei Chesnokov breezed into the second round of the $313,750 Swedish Open in Bastad by beating Ola Kristiansson of Sweden 6-3, 4-6, 6-2. Police continue to investigate shooting Police have questioned a man who said he and a friend were target shooting near the site where professional golfer Kim Williams was shot. Police would not release the name of the Howland Township man who said he may have accidentally hit Williams. GOLF Police said the man told them he was •firing his gun in woods near his home and one of the bullets from his 9mm semiautomatic pistol may have hit Williams. Howland Township Police Chief Steven Lamantia said it is possible the bullet that struck Williams was not fired from the man's gun. Williams was shot around 9 p.m. Saturday as she was walking into a discount store in Niles, Ohio, about 60 miles southeast of Cleveland. She was in town for the YoungstownWarren LPGA Classic. The bidding for Madison Square Garden and its two home sports teams, the Knicks and the Rangers, has been narrowed to five prospective buyers, according to published reports. The five are: Liberty Media Corp.; Nike Inc.; a partnership of Cablevision Systems Corp. ITT Hartford Insurance; a partnership of The New York Times Co. and Delaware North Cos., a sports concessionaire whose holdings include the Boston Garden; and Laurence A. Tisch, chairman of CBS Inc.

Jason Phillips added three hits, including a pair of doubles, while Emry drove in two as well as earning the victory by striking out three and walking just one. Payette (4-1, 1-1) committed seven errors in the contest. Jeff Valenzuela had two RBIs, while Matt Burke drove in three and Ciriaco Franks had two RBIs. SPIRITS CONTINUED FROM D1 O'Halloran assessed a 10-minute match penalty to Fiorentino for a deliberate attempt to injure, an infraction which will earn him a suspension from International Hockey League Commissioner Tom Berry. Afterward, Fiorentino expressed remorse for his action. • "IT WAS a stupid play on my part," Fiorentino said. "I put myself ahead of the team. I shouldn't have done it. I'm embarrassed. But it's over with and I can't do anything about it. "I should have used my head before I used my stick. It was a dangerous thing I did. I could have injured him." Evans suffered a bad cut inside his mouth, but didn't require stitches. He returned to take Kalamazoo's first shot of the shoot-out and missed. Jamie Elfstrom's fourth goal of the game with 1:53 remaining gave Lake Fenton High a 9-9 hockey tie against Mt. Morris Sunday at IMA Sports Arena. Mt. Morris had rallied to take a 9-8 lead on two short-handed goals by Mark Trombly in a 55-second span. Trombly and Elfstrom each scored three goals in a nine-goal third period. Trombly finished with a career five for the Panthers (2-3-1). Lake Fenton suffered a severe blow when starting goalie Adam Bryant was injured at the 2:36 mark of the first period. P.J. Verhille, who had never played in a high school game, replaced Bryant and allowed nine goals on 25 shots. Brian Sousa had three goals and two assists, while Bertil Jonak had two goals and two setups for Lake Fenton (2-2-1). The game was marred by a fight between Mt. Morris' Tony Dawson and Lake Fenton's Tony Carr with 5:43 remaining. Saginaw Nouvel 3, Carman-Ainsworth 2 Mike Jones scored with 5:14 left in the game to

Jason Phillips. John Ford threw a great block downfield to spring me for 6. "I knew I had an opportunity (to go all the way), it depended on what John did. I think the safety took a bad angle. Ford was being watched by cornerback Milton Mack and free safety Dave Waymer was taking a bead on Johnson. "The one guy (Mack) was on me and the safety came across," Ford said. FROM THE ASSOCIATED PRESS WINNIPEG, Manitoba Bob Murdoch was fired as head coach of the Winnipeg Jets Monday after the team falled to make the NHL playoffs for the second time in three years. Murdoch was named the NHL coach of the year In 1989-90 for turning around a dismal Winnipeg performance the previous season, when the team also falled to make the playoffs. Winnipeg closed the season Sunday night with a 6-3 loss to the Edmonton: Oilers, finishing 29-43-11. Murdoch knew he might get fired. "First of all, you have to realize that you're a hockey coach and the life ex-: pectancy isn't great," he said during the weekend.

Bills: Buffalo waived offensive lineman Moe Elewonibi, whom they wanted as insurance for their offensive line. The Bills also waived two wide receivers - Kevin Knox, a sixthround draft choice from Florida State, and Damon Thomas, a free-agent rookie from Wayne State. The Bills signed Elewonibi, an Outland Trophy winner in college, this month after right tackle Jerry Crafts struggled in his attempt to replace Howard Ballard, who left as a free agent. But Elewonibi, released by the Redskins, developed a back problem soon after signing and never appeared in an exhibition game for the Bills. Falcons: Atlanta released four players, including six-year wide receiver Jason Phillips. John Heidenreich; linebacker Also cut were free agent tackle Mitch Davis, a fourth-round draft choice from Georgia; and rookie free-agent wide receiver Corey Dixon of Nebraska. Phillips played in 55 games with the Falcons and Detroit Lions. He had seven catches for 76 yards and one score in four preseason games with the Falcons.

M Barry Sanders and Rodney Peete, leading candidates for the Heisman Trophy, were named to the 1988 Kodak Coaches All-America Team. Sanders, a junior from Oklahoma State, became the third running back in college football history to rush for more than 2,000 yards. He also set single-season records for points scored and touchdowns. Peete, quarterback for Southern California, is the sixthranked passer in the nation. The senior has 185 completions in 294 attempts for 18 touchdowns and 2,429 yards. Also chosen were running back Tim Worley of Georgia; wide receivers Erik Affholter of Southern Cal and Jason Phillips of Houston; tight end Mary Cook of Iowa; offensive linemen Tony Mandarich of Michigan State, Anthony Phillips of Oklahoma, Mark Stepnoski of Pittsburgh. Mike Utley of Washington State and Steve Wisnewski of Penn State, and placekicker Kendall Trainor of Arkansas. On defense were linemen Tracy Rocker of Auburn, Mark Messner of Michigan, Broderick Thomas of Nebraska, Derrick Thomas of Alabama and Bill Hawkins of Miami; linebackers Britt Hager of Texas and Jerry Olsavsky of Pittsburgh; backs Deion Sanders of Florida State, Louis Oliver of Florida, Darryl Henley of UCLA and Donnell Woolford of Clemson, and punter Pat Thompson of Brigham Young. Football * Woody Widenhofer, 45, resigned under pressure as Missouri football coach with a four-year record of 12-31-1. Widenhofer's resignation followed weeks of speculation that his contract, which expires Jan. 31, would not be renewed after finishing 3-7-1 overall in his fourth season. The Tigers showed consistent improvement each season until this one. His first year the Tigers were 1-10, and that was followed by marks of 3-8 and 5-6. Chancellor Haskell Monroe told reporters a search would begin immediately and he hoped •to have the successor identified by Dec. 1. He agreed to expedite the usual 30-day hiring period. University affirmative action guidelines allow for exemtpions when a search for the full period specified would cause a serious disruption of a program.

• Russian Andrei Chesnokow breezed into the second round of the $313,750 Swedish Open in Bastad by beating Ola Kristiansson of Sweden 6-3, 4-6, 6-2. Police continue to investigate shooting Police have questioned a man who said he and a friend were target shooting near the site where professional golfer Kim Williams was shot. Police would not release the name ofthe Howland Township man who said he may have accidentally hit Williams. GOLF Police said the man told them he was firing his gun at junk cars in woods near his home and one of the bullets from his 9mm semiautomatic pistol may have hit Williams. Howland Township Police Chief Steven Lamantia said it is possible the bullet that struck Williams was not fired from the man's gun. Williams was shot around 9 p.m. Saturday as she was walking into a discount store in Niles, Ohio, about 60 miles southeast of Cleveland. She was in town for the YoungstownWarren LPGA Classic. Williams, 31, of Boca Raton, Fla., left the hospital Monday. Canucks re-sign defenseman Lumme Defenseman Jyrki Lumme has signed a multiyear contract with the Vancouver Canucks, the team said. Lumme, 27, had 13 goals and 42 assists last season. He also accounted for 13 points - two goals and 11 assists - in the playoffs. HOCKEY The native of Finland has played for Vancouver for four seasons after being acquired from the Montreal Canadiens in March 1990. Falcons sign draft pick, veterans The Atlanta Falcons signed their fourth-round draft pick and two veteran free agents. The free agents are quarterback Bob Gagliano, 35, who has played eight NFL seasons and two in the USFL, and wide receiver Jason Phillips. MISC. The rookie is quarterback Perry Klein, a 6-foot-2, 218-pounder who set NCAA Division II passing records with 38 touchdown passes and 3,757 yards at C.W. Post. Former Mississippi linebacker Pete Harris said he received cash from coach Billy Brewer and an assistant coach while he was a Rebel player. Harris said he received about $50 from Brewer on one occasion and similar amounts from then assistant coach James "T" Thomas on several occasions. Harris' charge is the first public allegation of specific wrongdoing by Brewer.

The Fresno Bee Goalie Lance Carlsen's nearly flawless performance Saturday night against Los Angeles sent the Fresno Falcons into the Pacific Southwest Hockey League championship game. Carlsen stopped 51 shots, shutting out the Bandits in three

Continued from Page C1 Bennett said. "But what you have to do is win while that's happening and we did that tonight." Giuseppe Chiaramonte's tworun single with the bases loaded gave the Bulldogs a 2-1 lead in the third. In the fourth, Pete Hennecke hit a one-out double to left-center field, and Ben ReynoSO hit an RBI single to center. The Aztecs, who left runners in every inning but the eighth, made it 3-2 in the sixth with a two-out rally. Jason Phillips doubled, Kalin Foulds singled to left to move Phillips to third and Jeff DaVanon hit an RBI single. The Aztecs loaded the bases on an error by Jim Fisher, but Gunther got Travis Lee to ground out. Gunther got two outs in the seventh then walked Brian Kooiman and gave up a sharp single to center to Tony Zaragoza. Farfan retired Jason Phillips on a grounder to end the inning. Vancouver Canucks sign defenceman Garth Butcher to a multiyear contract; release Jack Bowkus, Barry Burkholder, Mike Estey, Bruce Gilles, Ron Goodall, Lou Kiriakou, Wayne MacPhee, Gary Moscaluk. John O'Connor, Scott Rawson, Tony Smith and Ken Strong; return Steve Herniman, Jeff Noble and Jason Phillips to their junior teams.

In 1 Chicago, Steve Larmer's goal with 13 seconds left in the third period earned the Blackhawks the draw after they'd pulled rookie goalie Jimmy Waite in favor of an extra skater. Steve Thomas, Curt Eraser and Denis Savard had Chicago's other goals, while Rick Meagher, Greg Paslawski, Tony McKegney and Brian Sutter scored for the Blues. •Vancouver Canucks released 12 players Sunday and assigned three more: to junior hockey clubs to cut their National Hockey League roster to 46 players. Five-year veteran Garth Butcher signed a multi-year contract, the club said in news release. The six-foot-two, 205-pound defenceman scored five goals, assisted on 15 others and had 207 minutes in penalties in 70 league games last season. The club also announced that defenceman Jin Agnew is out indefinitely with a torn rib cartilege. Released were: Jack Bowkus, Barry Burkholder, Mike Estey, Bruce Gilles, Ron Goodall, Lou Kiriakou, Wayne MacPhee, Gary Moscaluk, John O'Connor, Scott Rawson, Tony Smith and Ken Strong.
Steve Herniman® and Jeff Noble were sent to Ontario Hockey teams while Jason Phillips has been assigned to a Western Hockey League team.

bruise, the size of a fist and mostly hidden by her white blouse, spreads out toward her throat from just below her right collarbone.' These are the spots where a bullet sliced into her body Saturday night and where it remains today. "Obviously, I feel lucky," Williams said yesterday at a news conference at the Ladies Professional Golf A'ssociation's Jamie Farr Toledo Classic, which she said she intends to play this week. "There's really no explanation for it. It's pretty much of a miracle that a bullet can go through your neck and not hit anything.' Williams, 31, was shot as she walked toward a PharMor drug store in Niles, O., while playing in the Youngstown-Warren LPGA Classic last weekend. Police originally believed they were investigating a drive-by shooting, but according to Howland Township Police a man has told authorities he might have shot Williams as he and a friend were target shooting on his property in Howland, O., nine-tenths of a mile from the drug store. No arrests have been made. "The caliber bullet that is in me (9 millimeter) is what he was shooting," Williams said. She said doctors told her they would leave the bullet where it is,. lodged against her esophagus, because it could cause more damage to take it out. Football The Atlanta Falcons signed their fourth-round draft pick and two free agents. The free agents are quarterback Bob Gagliano, 35, who has played eight NFL seasons and two in the USFL, and wide receiver Jason Phillips. Gagliano joined the Falcons last Oct. 7, but did not play in a game. Phillips, a five-year player, has played in 56 games with 49 catches for 578 yards and two touchdowns. He joined Atlanta in 1991 after two seasons in Detroit. The rookie is quarterback Perry Klein, a 6-2, 218-pounder who set NCAA Division II passing records with 38 touchdown passes and 3,757 yards at C.W. Post. The Falcons also released. rookie defensive lineman Jonathan Guiles, a free agent from South Carolina State. 1 Tennis Second-seeded Jason Stoltenberg beat Kevin Ullyett, 6-4, 6-3, to lead three seeded players into the second round of the Hall of Fame Championships at Newport, R.I. Seventh-seeded Javier Frana defeated Brian MacPhie, 6-4, 7-6 (7-3), and No. 6 Byron Black downed Jim Grabb, 7-6 (7-2), 4-6, 6-2. Wild-card entry Guy Forget stunned secondseeded Andrei Medvedev, 6-3, 7-5, , in the first round of the $485,000 Swiss Open at Gstaad. Medvedev's downfall was sealed after he sprained the little finger of his racket hand in a fall during the second set. De•fending champion and top-seeded Bruguera had an easier time, trouncing Patrick Rafter, 6-4, 6-2. Third-seeded Andrei Chesnokov breezed into the second round of the $313,750 Swedish Open at Bastad by beating Ola Kristiansson, 6-3, 4-6, 6-2. Chesnokov is the highest-seeded player remaining in the event. Hockey - Defenseman Jyrki Lumme has signed a multiyear contract with the Vancouver Canucks. Lumme, 27, had 13 goals and 42 assists last season. He also accounted for 13 points - two goals and 11 assists - in the playoffs. The native of Finland has played for Vancouver for four seasons after being acquired from the Montreal Canadiens in March 1990. Details of the contract were not disclosed. Glen Sather said he's too busy to decide who will take over as the coach of the Edmonton Oilers and he doesn't expect to name his replacement until next month. Sather added the role of coach to his duties as general manager and president last November when he fired Ted Green. He said from the start he would relinquish the coaching duties after the season. There were reports he would name his replacement yesterday, but Sather said he's going fishing next week and wants to wait for owner Peter Pocklington to return from Europe before he makes an announcement.

HONOLULU (AP) - Protection of the quarterback figures to be the key in the Aloha Bowl between Houston and Washington State, two pass-minded teams. Two of the nation's most potent offenses meet at Aloha Stadium Christmas Day. No. 18 Washington State and No. 14 Houston are ranked third and fourth, respectively, in the nation in total offense. "I believe it will be a shootout," said Washington State quarterback Timm Rosenbach, the nation's most efficient passer. "I don't think : 30 or 40 points is out of the question for both teams." Houston, 9-2, is favored by four points over Washington State, 8-3. "The defense that's successful at rushing the quarterback and not giving the quarterback time is going to win," Houston coach Jack Pardee said. "The strength of our football team is our offensive line," Washington State coach Dennis Erickson said. "Houston is good up front. That matchup will be a key to the game." Rosenbach, a junior, was rated first in the nation in passing efficiency this season. He completed 66 percent of his passes for 2,791 yards and 23 touchdowns, helping Washington State to only its fourth bowl appearance in the school's 93-year football history. Rosenbach led the Pac-10 Conference and was third in the nation in total offense, averaging 286 yards pera game. He finished seventh in the Heisman Trophy balloting. Steve Broussard rushed for 1,141 yards and Rich Swinton ran for 980 yards. "Our defense is going to be tested against Washington State," Pardee said. "When you've got the topranked quarterback in football and a rusher who gained over 1,000 yards in a season ... the tribute goes to the offensive line. "The key is getting past them, not giving Rosenbach time to throw," Pardee said. Sophomore quarterback Andre Ware leads Houston's run-and-shoot offense, which ranked first in the Southwest Conference and second in the nation in passing offense, averaging 377 yards per game. Ware, who started Houston's final seven games, passed for 2,501 yards and 25 touchdowns. "I'm in love with this offense," Ware said. "There's no one else in the country that runs the run-and-shoot the way we run it." The offense helped two Houston receivers compile impressive statistics this season. All-American Jason Phillips, who caught 108 passes for 1,444 yards and 15 touchdowns, led the nation in receiving for the second straight season. Phillips is only the fourth receiver in NCAA history to lead the nation

two goals and three assists (pLaymaker). Freshman Jason Echols had one goal and one assist. Jason Krell also earned a Playmaker with four assists. Jason Phillips added a single assist. Due to adverse weather and illness, only nine players were able to make the game. The Vikings also defeated their rivals, the Cougars from Cherry Hill East, 5-1, at the Coliseum. The team outshot the Cougars, 29-19, with Siitonen again in goal. Adler scored a hat trick with Locke and Tom Martinovic scoring singles. Locke had two assists and Krell added one. The Viking Varsity also played two Suburban League games at the Face Off Circle Rink in Warminster, Pa. In the first game against Central Bucks East, the Vikings won the match, 5-3, with shots on goal at 30-23 in the Vikings favor. Senior goalie Justin Tonczyczyn was in net. Three

•The Viking Ice Hockey Club Varsity team, comprised of students from Eastern High School, is now 15-6-2 in league play. After losing 8-3 to William Tennent, the Vikings defeated the Minutemen of Washington Township, 3-2, at the Twin Rinks of Pennsauken. The Vikings outshot their opponents 34-24 with Jason Siitonen tending goal. Scoring was done by Rakhal Bhalla, Jeremy Nichols and Jeff Locke. Jason Phillips had two assists with Single assists from Jason Krell and Steve. Passalacqua. In earlier action, the Viking Varsity defeated Archbishop Wood, 12-1, at the Face Off Circle Rink in Warminster, Pa. The -Vikings outshot their opponent, 49-19. Jason Siitonen tended goal. who witnessed the brawl said fighting broke out after one of the Marines made an uncomplimentary remark about Bol's height. Bol is the NBA's tallest player at 7-foot7. Two chairs and several glasses were broken. Boxing Lennox Lewis, the WBC heavyweight champion, has rejected a deal to fight Riddick Bowe for the undisputed title. "The deal was $21 million for Bowe and a 50-50 split if the final purse went above $32 million," Frank Maloney, who represents Lewis, said Tuesday. Lewis was guaranteed $11 million. We declined that, and the Bowe fight won't happen until the summer of 1994," Maloney said. Lewis was awarded the WBC title last year after Bowe was stripped for failing to make his first defense against the British fighter. Football The chances of luring the New England Patriots to Hartford, Conn., have improved, Gov. Lowell P. Weicker said Tuesday. Weicker said he will write to Patriots owner James Orthwein in support of Philadelphia businessman Francis Murray's proposal to buy the team and move it to Connecticut. "My thought a month or so ago was this stood less than a 50-50 chance," Weicker told reporters after meeting with Murray, a former minority owner of the Patriots. "I can only assure all of you that this stands a better than 50-50 chance today." Murray has made a $133 million offer to buy the Patriots and move them into a 65,000-seat stadium that would be built at one of five sites in or near Hartford using state bonds. Hockey Saying they've outgrown the makeshift but intimate arena that contributed to a successful inaugural season, the Tampa Bay Lightning is moving to the spacious Florida Suncoast Dome. Tuesday's announcement ended months of speculation about where the team will play next season, but it didn't answer the lingering questions of if, when or where a permanent home will be built. The Lightning played its first season in the 10,400-seat Expo Hall at the Florida State Fairgrounds in Tampa. Nine months after leaving town, the North Stars will return to Minneapolis for an NHL game - this time as the Dallas Stars. They will play the Ottawa Senators on Dec. 9 in the first of six games to be played in Minneapolis as the league stages regular-season contests in neutral cities for the second straight year. Minnesota had an NHL franchise for 25 years before the North Stars moved to Dallas following last season. Phoenix, Cleveland, Sacramento, Calif., and Hamilton, Ontario, will each hold four -site games games, while Halifax, Nova Scotia, and Saskatoon, Saskatchewan, will each play host to two. Tennis Two weeks after winning her fifth Wimbledon singles title, Steffi Graf returned to competition Tuesday in what turned out to be a cameo appearance in Frankfurt, Germany. Bothered by a new injury, an aching shoulder, Graf was upset in the Federation Cup, and her defending champion German team was ousted in the first round by Australia. The United States advanced to the second round with a 3-0 sweep over Switzerland.

FROM WIRE REPORTS As expected, the news was not good for Charles Barkley and the Philadelphia 76ers yesterday. The 76ers' All-Star forward will be out for at least two weeks with a slightly torn left knee ligament, the club announced. Barkley underwent a magnetic resonance imaging test, which confirmed that suffered a second degree sprain of the medial collateral when he collided with teammate Rick Mahorn in the third quarter of a 110-101 victory over the Cavaliers on Sunday. Team doctor Jack McPhilemy said the fall caused the injury, which will keep Barkley sidelined until at least April 14. The 76ers (40-32) have already clinched a playoff berth. Dallas Mavericks forward Roy Tarpley was charged with driving while intoxicated, and the district attorney's office said it would ask that his probation for a 1989 conviction be revoked. Tarpley, sidelined since Nov. 9. with a knee injury, is serving two years' probation on a DWI conviction in 1989. Dale Ellis, acquired by the Milwaukee Bucks in a Feb. 16 trade with the Seattle SuperSonics, could be out for the rest of the season because of a back injury, Dr. David Haskell, said. The Chicago Bulls suspended reserve Stacey King for one game without pay for missing practice. He will sit out tonight's home game against Orlando. Hockey The proposed North American Hockey League awarded franchises to Hamilton, Ontario, and Oakland-Sacramento. NAHL President Gordon Stenback of Cleveland made the announcement after a conference call involving franchise owners in Cleveland, Miami, Cincinnati, Atlanta and Dallas. An NAHL meeting has been scheduled in Cleveland on May 3-4 to complete plans for a player draft. "All of the owners will begin looking for coaches and to get their staffs on board," said Stenback. "We're stronger now than we were one month ago or one week ago. We're going ahead." The league plans to begin a 70-game schedule in November. Nine months after Bob Murdoch was named coach of the year, he was fired by the Winnipeg Jets, one of five clubs in the 21-team NHL to miss the playoffs. The Chicago Blackhawks said that winger Michel Goulet will be sidelined for 10 days to two weeks because of a sprained right knee. He was hurt Sunday in a pileup during the first period of a 5-1 victory over Detroit. Tennis I Singles play in the first round of the $500,000 Family Circle

Nine high school seniors from District 5AAA were selected to play for the South in the 40th Shrine All-Star football game Aug. 8 at Civic Stadium in Portland. South Eugene and North Eugene landed two players each - linebacker Mark Koenig and fullback Geno Zaharie of South and linemen John Niemela and Mike Davenport of North. Zaharie and Niemela both have indicated they will sign letters of intent with Oregon. Quarterbacks Andy Johnson, the league most valuable player from Marshfield, and Andy Gray of Willamette both could end up as defensive players as Jeff Barry of South Medford and Rich Ekman of Bend also were selected as quarterbacks. The other players selected from 5AAA were offensive guard Bryan Pinney of Sheldon, running back Javan Yriarte of Churchill, and offensive tackle George Racette of Thurston. State-playoff runner-up Corvallis will be represented by three players - Dan Blus, Nick Todorovich and Jason Phillips. Pendleton, Lebanon and Bend also had three players each. Three all-state selections from state-champion Glencoe - running backs Brett Collins and Kyle Kozak and linebacker Todd Sahlfeld topped the North team team. Jason Palumbis of Lakeridge, the all-state quarterback bound for Stanford, was the lone quarterback chosen to play for the North. South Roster Steve Utter, Dave Husko, and Rick Shimmel, Pendleton; Rich Ekman, Jake Firkus, and Derek Hambiin, Bend; Leonard Troxel, Jason Grizzle, and John Hutley, Lebanon; Dan Blus, Nick Todorovich, and Jason Phillips, Corvallis; Ken Stout, Hidden Valley; Jeff Barry, South Medford; John Niemela and Mike Davenport, North Eugene; Jason Hawk, Ashland; Geno Zaharie and Mark Koenig, South Eugene; Taylor Vallerga, Mountain View; Todd Doughton, Roseburg; Bud Bowie, North Medford; David Blume, Eagle Point; Tim Woock, South Salem; Oscar Veal, South Medford; Andy Johnson, Marshfield; Bryan Pinney, Sheidon; Neal Forrester, Redmond; Javan Yriarte, Churchill; Andy Gray Willamette; George Racette, Thurston; Jason Hisey, Klamath Falls; Todd Gydeson, McKay; Ryan Hall, Crescent Valley; Robert Miller, North Salem; Jim Klug, Mountain View.

The Atlanta Falcons signed third-year free safety Tracey Eaton to a Plan B contract Monday, before the midnight deadline, bringing the club's total number of veteran signees to nine, six by Plan B. Earlier Monday, the Falcons announced signing Plan B free agents Jeff Donaldson, an eight-year safety from the Chiefs; Harper Le Bel, a third-year tight end/long snapper from the Eagles; Jason Phillips, a third-year wide receiver from the Lions; Benji Roland, a secondyear defensive lineman from the Buccaneers; and Naz Worthen, a second-year wide receiver from the Chiefs. The team also announced the addition of veteran kicker Rich Karlis and first-year players Mike Williams, a wide receiver, and Pat Chaffey, a running back. Morris signs with Browns CLEVELAND Joe Morris, a two-time Pro Bowl running back with the New York Giants who did not play last season, was among 10 players who signed with the Cleveland Browns on Monday. Morris had three 1,000-yard seasons with the New York Giants and holds franchise records in total yards rushing (5,296) and touchdowns (48). He's also their career in career attempts (1,318) and set a team record with 21 rushing touchdowns in 1985. Morris suffered a broken foot in New York's last preseason game in 1989, was placed on injured reserve and missed the entire season. He rehabilitated the injury but was placed on waivers in September 1990 and went unsigned for the entire season.

NFL FOOTBALL Falcons activate Rade, Phillips The Atlanta Falcons placed linebacker John Rade and wide receiver Jason Phillips on the active roster and put defensive end Mike Gann on injured reserve. Rade, who has had five consecutive 100-tackle seasons, was on the physically unable to perform list because of knee surgery last January. He is in his ninth NFL season... Efforts continued toward resolving the confused ownership of the New England Patriots after Fran Murray rejected Victor Kiam's bid for a 30-day extension to buy out his minority partner. The deadline for Kiam, who owns 51 percent of the team, to make a $38 million payment to buy out Murray passed without resolution Thursday. Murray holds the remaining 49 percent. of the franchise. Neither Murray nor Kiam was available for comment.

tournament at Al-Mar Lanes. There were no change on the leader board of the women's tournament. Varsity Lanes #1 still leads the men's tournament with 3,309 pins while Stor-All is first on the women's leader board with a 3,042 total. Mick Starkey led Deshler Lanes with a 232 game and 660 series. Jim Beaupre, Sr. added a 224 game and narrowly missed his 600 series with a 597. The team compiled a 2,851 scratch total and took over second place with 3,133 including handicap. Maumee Bait & Tackle fell to third with 3,104. Luca Pizza (3,048) and Old Milwaukee N/A II (3,035) round out the top five. Willie Hall rolled the top game in the tournament to date with a 286 opening game and ended with a 683 series on Thursday night. Other individual highlights of the most recent action include Eric Miller 253, 638; Bill Jeffers 246, 616; Bob Korsnack 233; Jim Smith 221 and Larry Bagrowski 220. The tournaments continue through Sunday at A1-Mar Lanes. Falcons' Glantz out 3-6 weeks Bowling Green senior left wing Tom Glantz is out three to six weeks with a shoulder injury suffered Wednesday in practice. He is BG's third-leading scorer with 13 points and leads the team with six goals. Freshman Kevin Armbruster, who has not played during BG's first five games, is expected to replace Glantz in the lineup this weekend at Illinois-Chicago and Western Michigan. PASS adds games to schedule ANN ARBOR - PASS Sports has announced an expanded college hockey game of the week schedule, including one Bowling Green game. The BG at Michigan State game on Nov. 9 will be televised live, starting at 7 p.m. In all, PASS Sports has added 12 college hockey games, including 1 the Great Lakes Invitational and the College Hockey Showcase, to the previously announced Prime national college game of the week schedule. ESPN2 offered the Central Collegiate Hockey Association a limited number of games, however they conflicted with television broadcasts already scheduled. *ESPN's interest in college hockey did not extend beyond the NHL strike and they were not prepared to make any commitments,"" said Bill Beagan, CCHA commissioner. Beagan also said that PASS has committed to schedule several additional CCHA games this season as well as next season. All -NLL tennis team selected There are two players from Wood County high schools on the -team All-Northern Lakes League tennis team. Bowling Green's. Martha Polus and Perrysburg's Jill Johnston are on the first team. Also on the first team are Southview's Candy Klingelheber, Kari Barcus and Shannon Long; Springfield's Laura Fraunfelder; and Anthony Wayne's Erika Mix. Lake's Tami Cutler and Perrysburg's Abby Downs are on the second team. Also on the second team are Southview's Amy Diamond and Kari Richard; Springfield's Tracie Fraunfelder and Kim Felton; Anthony Wayne's Mariam Pecsok; and Maumee's Kim Frederick. On the honorable mention list from the county are: Bowling Green's Jackie Hartman and Heather Boylan; Lake's Angie Cherry and. Tara Wozniak; Perrysburg's Kara Stahl and Molly Finkbeiner; and Rossford's Sharon' Hustwick and Evonne Lacumsky. Also on the honorable mention list are: Anthony Wayne's Jennifer Hughes and Lauren Kramer; Maumee's Anisa Saleh and Melanie Gentry; Springfield's Mandi Haiduck and Martha Price; Southview's Kara Tashima and Jenny Moore. Owens volleyball wins ROSSFORD - Owens Community College wrapped banner first season Thursday night with a 15-11, 15-10, 15-13 victory over Bluffton in -league action. The Express finished 15-9 overall, including a 7-3 record in Ohio Community College Athletic Conference play. Owens plays at 3 p.m. today

Saying it briefly From The Star-Ledger wire services HOCKEY: Wayne Gretzky won his ninth Art Ross Trophy, Ed Belfour captured his first William Jennings Trophy and the Chicago Blackhawks took their first President's Trophy as the National Hockey League's regular season came to a close. vyv BASKETBALL: The Chicago Bulls suspended reserve Stacey King for one game without pay for missing practice yesterday. He will sit out tonight's home game against the Orlando Magic. Because starters were exhausted after Sunday's 135-132 double overtime loss at Boston, they were excused from yesterday's regular practice, which followed a meeting about NBA policy on drugs and other matters. King, a forward-center who didn't play Sunday, left with the starters after the meeting, giving no reason for skipping practice. ... FOOTBALL: Minnesota Vikings kicker Donald Igwebuike went to trial alone on drug smuggling , charges yesterday after a second co-defendant in the case agreed to plead guilty to importing $1 million worth of heroin. Unlike Ibezim Ofedu, who pleaded guilty last month to conspiring with Igwebuike, Maduwuba Ibekwe will not be asked to testify for the government under a plea agreement that awaits court approval today. Meanwhile, jury selection began for Igwebuike, 31, who faces up to 120 years in prison and $6 million in fines if convicted of smuggling conspiracy, conspiracy to possess with intent to distribute and importation of heroin.. vu SOCCER: Soccer star Diego Maradona wants to return to his native Argentina from Italy to escape the furor over his testing positive for cocaine, his personal manager said yesterday. "He needs to relax, to have a change of scene," Marcos Franchi told a crowd of journalists gathered outside Maradona's villa in Naples, where he plays in Italy's professional soccer league. Maradona, who inspired Argentina's 1986 World Cup victory, had originally planned to leave Italy on Sunday with his wife Claudia and their two daughters, Franchi said. But they left without him, arriving in Buenos Aires yesterday. Although he remained in virtual seclusion at his villa yesterday, Maradona was visited by several of his Napoli teammates, many of whom earlier offered sympathy and support for their embattled captain. vyv MISCELLANEOUS: The Nevada Assembly yesterday gave final legislative approval to a bill that would require the NCAA to give due process to coaches and athletes. The lower house action yesterday sent the bill to Gov. Bob Miller for his signature two days after UNLV's 79-77 loss to Duke in the NCAA semifinals. There was little comment on the bill or Saturday's game as the Assembly concurred in Senate amendments to the bill. But after the vote, Assemblyman Gene Porter, D-Las Vegas, griped briefly about officiating at Saturday's game. Porter, one of several lawmakers who attended the game in Indianapolis, said that after the game he found his car had been towed by police. The bill was backed by supporters of UNLV coach Jerry Tarkanian, who has been involved in a -decade dispute with the NCAA. Several other states also are considering such proposals, many

Center. The hospital refused comment and HE Karen hosiptal Waldbillig spokeswoman said the family asked that no information be released. Other relatives told the DOUGLAS station Douglas was taken to the hospital Monday and had slipped in and out of a coma since then. It was not known whether Douglas previously had been diagnosed as a diabetic or which form of the disease he might have. Douglas, a native of Columbus, was the heavyweight champion for only eight months. He was a 42-1 underdog when he won the title in February 1990 with a 10th-round knockout of then-undefeated Mike Tyson in Tokyo. TENNIS/Medvedev upset Wild-card entry Guy Forget of France stunned second-seeded Andrei Medvedev in straight sets during the first round of the $485,000 Swiss Open. The Ukrainian, ranked eighth in the world, was beaten 6-3, 7-5. Defending champion and top-seeded Sergi Bruguera of Spain had an easier time, trouncing Australian Patrick Rafter 6-4, 6-2 in his first match of the tournament. Russian Andrei Chesnokov breezed into the second round of the $313,750 S Swedish Open by beating Ola Kristiansson of Sweden 6-3, 4-6, 6-2 Tuesday. At No. 3, Chesnokov is the highest-seeded player remaining in the event. No. 1 Magnus Larsson of Sweden withdrew with to a shoulder injury. Second-seeded Jason Stoltenberg of Australia beat Kevin Ullyett of South Africa 6-4, 6-3 to lead three seeded players into the second round of the Miller Lite Hall of Fame Tennis Championships in Newport, R.I. Mark Kaplan of Greenwich defeated Kenny Thorne of Richmond, Va., 7-6 (8-6), 4-6, 6-3, while Lou Gloria of Orange lost his first-round match 7-6 (7-3), 6-3 to Michael Tebbutt of Australia. HOCKEY/O'Connell promoted The Boston Bruins turned to an old friend , naming former All-Star defenseman Mike O'Connell as assistant general manager. O'Connell, 38, played for the Bruins from 1980 to 1986, representing the team in the 1984 National Hockey League All-Star game. For the past two seasons, he has coached at the Bruins' minor league affiliate in Providence. Edmonton Oilers President and General Manager Gien Sather said Tuesday he's too busy to decide who will take over as the coach of the Edmonton Oilers and he doesn't expect to name his replacement until next month. Ron Low, an Oilers assistant coach for the past five seasons, and George Burnett, coach of the Oilers' AHL affiliate in Sydney, Nova Scotia, are considered frontrunners for the position. Defenseman Jyrki Lumme has signed a multiyear contract with the Vancouver Canucks, the team said. Lumme, 27, had 13 goals and 42 assists last season. He also accounted for 13 points - two goals and 11 assists - in the playoffs. ALSO/Tour de France Belgium's Johan Museeuw took the lead in the Tour de France after the team time trial , and 1 American Lance Armstrong moved into fifth place while heading a surge by his team. In the battle of the two favorites, three-time winner Miguel Indurain of Spain gained a slight edge over Tony Rominger of Switzerland. The team

time trial, the Tour's third stage, covered 41 miles from Calais to the Channel Tunnel.Museeuw took the overall lead from Britain's Chris Boardman, who had held it since the prologue Saturday. Armstrong moved into fifth place, 22 seconds behind. The Atlanta Falcons signed their fourth-round draft pick and two veteran free agents. The free agents are quarterback Bob Gagliano, 35, who has played eight NFL seasons and two in the USFL, and wide receiver Jason Phillips. Gagliano joined the Falcons last Oct. 7, but did not play in a game. The rookie is quarterback Perry Klein, a 6-2, 218-pounder who set NCAA Division II passing records with 38 touchdown passes and 3,757 yards at C.W. Post. Dave D'Entremont saved his best game for last and won his first PBA title since 1992 when he captured the $100,000 PBA Oregon Open in Portland, Ore. D'Entremont beat tournament leader Joe Firpo 235-214 to earn his second career title and the winner's share of $16,000. The 33-year-old righthander qualified fourth and had to win four matches for the victory. REGION/Thomas honored Tom Thomas, a recent graduate of Fairfield Prep, has been chosen the No. 1 diver (for non-public schools) in the United States by the National Interscholastic Swimming Coaches Association. Thomas, who was named All America for the third straight season, will attend South Carolina in the fall. • Lenn Johns was appointed head women's volleyball coach at Quinnipiac College, according to Athletic Director Burt Kahn. Johns replaces Ann Marie Bahantka, who resigned following the 1993-94 season. Johns coached at St. Micheal's from 1987-91. Ted Haley of Greenwich shot a 74 in the first round of the Las Vegas Founders' Legacy junior golf tournament in Henderson, Nev. Haley is five shots behind leader Charlie Woerner of Sunnydale, Calif., in the boys 15-18 division.

HOCKEY York Pee Wees hand Cap Boys an 8-1 loss Bill Gwilt pumped in four goals as the York Ice Hockey Club Pee Wees beat the Cap Boys 8-1 last weekend. Justin McClain chipped in with a goal and five assists. Chris Leber, Dayne Grove and Mike Harper also scored for York. The York Squirts took home a secondplace trophy from the Haverford Hawks Invitational Tournament. Wintersport beat York 7-2 in the title game. Goaltender Kevin Hare made 19 saves in a scoreless tie with Haverford. York beat Springfield 7-1. For the tournament, York got three goals each from Chris Shreiner and Mark Hall, two from Jared Hynd and one apiece from David Gleason and Adam McCreesh. The York Mites beat Hershey 5-1 behind a pair of goals from Matt Daggett. Danny Smith, John Lutz and Jared Eberly also scored for York.

I JASON PHILLIPS: a positive attitude is showing up in WHL scoring statistics Alex Barta photo

By Jeff Rud Times-Colonist staff Jason Phillips has waited five years, but it's finally happened. confidence level has caught up to his talent and the combination is paying big dividends for the Victoria Cougars this season. For the first time since he made his initial stab at the Western Hockey League five seasons ago, Phillips is scoring consistently. He has 21 goals and 15 assists in 23 games and is the leading scorer for coach Wayne Naka's Cougars. "Confidence is the big difference," Phillips said this week. "I have the ability to play in this league and Wayne has brought it out. He puts a lot of confidence in me." Actually, Phillips' surge of confidence began late last November. It came after one of the bleakest periods of his young life when he quit the Seattle Thunderbirds and considered giving up on hockey altogether. He had been bounced around from Kamloops, to Brandon, to Seattle and had never had a real shot at playing regularly in the WHL. "My confidence was at it's worst when I quit Seattle," recalled the 20-year-old Coquitlam native. "I felt like I wasn't getting on with life - that I was falling behind." Then, all of a sudden, things started going his way. He got a call from Bruce Taylor, the owner of the B.C. Junior Hockey League's Richmond Sockeyes, asking if he'd like to finish out the season there under coach Orland Kurtenbach. He took the offer, and it led to a key role in the Sockeyes' Centennial Cup championship drive. At Richmond, Phillips scored 29 goals in just 34 games. He also graduated from high school. "I had figured when I went back to the BCJHL, that that was it for my
TOMORROW COUGAR HOCKEY VS PORTLAND 7:30 P.M.

hockey," said the 5-foot-11, 170-pound left winger. "But as it turned out, Kurt and Bruce really turned things around for me. Things just went great in Richmond. It was the best thing that could have happened to me." His on-ice performance rekindled the interest of the Cougars, who added Phillips as an overage for this season. Then came yet another confidence booster - an invitation to the Vancouver Canucks' National Hockey League training camp. "To me, that was the thrill of a lifetime when I think about it," Phillips said. "It was my goal when I left midget, but I never really thought it would happen. It was like when you're a kid out in the carport and you pretend you're Stan The Steamer Smyl, shooting on Richard Brodeur. I couldn't believe I was actually there with those guys." The NHL experience served to elevate Phillips' confidence that much more heading into his last WHL season. And finally, after languishing on the bench in Brandon, Kamloops and Seattle, he is getting a chance to play a regular shift and on the power-play and penalty-killing units for Naka. "Everybody in the WHL had given up on him," said Naka, who first saw Phillips as a 15-year-old at training camp while he coached the BCJHL's Kelowna Buckaroos. "But he's always had the skill. We went after him because we knew he was going to score. I just wish I'd had him three years ago." Naka felt Phillips was too young for junior hockey after that first meeting but Phillips moved on to play with the Vernon Lakers and split time between there and the WHL Blazers during his first two junior seasons. He was traded to Brandon prior to the 1985-86 season and dressed for 66

Phillips is seeing ice time in pressure situations, like on the powerplay where he has five goals. His confidence has steamrolled to the point where he now feels he is a bona fide WHL scorer with a shot at playing in the American Hockey League next year. "It's just rising and rising as I go on," Phillips said of his confidence. "I'm continuing to surprise myself. It's a much different feeling than when I was sitting on the bench.'

"I'm not here to work miracles. But I have six to eight good years left in me and the first thing I want to do is help Pittsburgh get in the playoffs." The Penguins have missed the NHL playoffs the last five seasons, even with the multi-talented Mario Lemieux the past three years. Simpson, 20, has emerged as the Penguins' second-best player to Lemieux. Playing centre and both wings, the Penguins' 1985 first-round draft pick has 13 goals and 13 assists in 26 games. "I wasn't really expecting it, but that's hockey," Simpson said. "It's only once in a lifetime you get a shot at a player like Paul Coffey," Johnston said. "You can't afford to let it go by." Coffey "is really going to give us a shot in the arm," said Johnston, who hopes he will strengthen the Penguins' unproductive power play, currently the NHL's second-worst. McNabb to coach Clippers The Canadian Press NANAIMO - Larry McNabb has returned to coach the Nanaimo Clippers of the British Columbia Junior Hockey League, team president Vern Benedictson said Tuesday. McNabb, 48, became the Clippers' winningest coach when he was behind the bench between 1974 and 1982, capturing three BCJHL titles. The Clippers are tied for second place with a record of 12-11-1 in the Coastal Division and have been without a coach since Rich Dutka was fired Nov. 18. McNabb left the Clippers in 1982 when new ownership moved the team to Esquimalt. He has since spent his time as an alderman and regional district director in Nanaimo and runs his own sporting goods store. His son, L. D. McNabb, plays for Nanaimo and was a factor in McNabb's decision to return. "I had a long talk with L.D.," McNabb said. "He says he supports me 100 per cent. "Then I looked at the club, wondered who else they could have had as a coach and then I decided I wanted a little more excitement back in my life.'

He was fifth in scoring among defencemen with 17 goals and 50 assists last season despite missing 21 games due to injury and was a consecutive Norris Trophy winner in 1985 and 1986. A five-time all-star, Coffey holds or shares 18 Oilers' regular season and playoff records and holds the NHL record with a 17-game assist scoring streak. He also shares the NHL record for most points in a game by a defenceman with eight on March 14, 1986 against Detroit. He is the thirdleading scorer in Oilers history. Philadelphia, the New York Rangers, Detroit and St. Louis reportedly had bid for Coffey, who had helped lead the Oilers to three Stanley Cup championships in the last four seasons. SPORTS DIGEST Tackle Jason Phillips named Miami's MVP FROM WIRE AND STAFF REPORTS Jason Phillips, a senior defensive tackle from Detroit, was named Most Valuable Player of the Miami University football team during a banquet Monday at the Shriver Center on the Oxford campus. Other award winners were: Jeff Chabot (nominee, for Mid-American Conference MVP award); Gary Layton (special teams MVP); Johnnie Williams (outstanding junior); Skip Tramontana (senior, scholar athlete); Marcus Stepp (outstanding freshman); and Jeff Cheeks (most valuable walk-on). Bill Atha, Eric Henderson and Deland McCullough captured Redskin Warrior Awards. Phillips, and Chabot won captain's awards. Chabot, a center, was named first-team offense on the All-Mid-American Conference team. Firstteam All-MAC defensive players from Miami were, Dee Osborne, Atha, Phillips, Layton and defensive back David Thomas. McCullough and Williams were named secondteam All-MAC picks, while kicker Chad Seitz received honorable mention. I Liyi Wang, the Wright State University diving, coach, has been named an assistant coach on the U.S. Olympic diving squad. Cedarville College sophomores Michelle Burson and Julianne Pletcher became the first NAIA All-Americans in the Yellow Jackets' women's cross country history at last weekend's NAIA Na-, tional meet in Kenosha, Wis. Burson placed 26th, and Pletcher 27th out of 329 runners. The top 30 finishers are All-Americans. Jennifer Zenner was named an NAIA All-America Scholar-Athlete, which recognizes junior and senior varsity runners who carry a cumulative grade point average of 3.50 or higher. I Darren Heidenreich, a junior at Wright State from Cincinnati, set the Raiders' 1,000-yard freestyle record with a 9:34.92 clocking during a meet at Bowling Green. The old mark of 9:36.35 was set by, Scott Troutwine in 1985. Kristin Cusimano, a senior swimmer at Satellite High School in Indian Harbour Beach, Fla., has, signed a national letter-of-intent to attend WSU. Football: Buckeye tackle honored Ohio State tackle Korey Stringer and Wisconsin defensive back Jeff Messenger were named Big Ten players of the week. Ohio State's Marlon Kerner, was named special teams player of the week. A Virginia man accused of running naked across the field during halftime of the Ohio StateMichigan game Saturday has pleaded innocent in court. Daniel John Walsh, 21, a James Madison University sophomore, is charged with criminal trespass and public indecency. I Christopher Bell, a 6-foot-7, 290-pound offensive guard on the Northmont High School football team, has made a verbal commitment to play at the University of Cincinnati. Auto racing: Pemberton joins Penske The Penske South team of NASCAR driver Rusty Wallace has hired Robin Pemberton as its crew chief for 1995. Pemberton, who held the same post at Roush Racing with driver Ted Musgrave this' year, succeeds Buddy Parrott. Car owner Richard Petty is expected to name a driver for '95 in the next day or two. The search has been narrowed to Bobby Hamilton and Lake Speed, according to crew chief Robbie Loomis. at Cleveland 8½ 8½ Houston at Minnesota 13 13½ Tampa Bay HOCKEY at N.Y. HOCKEY N.Y. Rangers sign Healy -From wire service reports Glenn Healy, the star of the New York Islanders' surprising playoff success last spring, signed a contact with the New York Rangers on Tuesday. Healy's contract was worth a reported $3.5 million over four years. New York Rangers general manager Neil Smith said he expects Corey Hirsch, 21, the team's goalie of the future, to remain with Binghamton of the AHL this season. Healy took a circuitous route from Long Island to Manhattan. After leading the Islanders to the Wales Conference finals, Healy was left unprotected in the expansion draft and was selected by Anaheim. The next day, though, Tampa Bay -took him in the second phase of the expansion draft. The Lightning traded Healy's rights to the Rangers for a third-round pick. Healy, 31, is entering his seventh NHL season. He played two seasons for Los Angeles before signing with the Islanders in 1989 as a free agent. Last year, Healy was 22-20-6 with a 3.30 goalsagainst average, then was sensational in the playoffs. He started all 18 games and posted a 3.19 average and an .887 save percentage. Dunham loses in net New Jersey Devils signee Mike Dunham was in goal for the U.S. national hockey team's 3-1 loss night to Sweden's Vastra Frolunda club at Gothenburg, Sweden. Dunham, of Endwell, made 32 saves for the U.S., which is 5-3 on its European tour. The team's next action is in the Epson Cup on Thursday and Friday at Dusseldorf, Germany. The U.S. tied Vastra Frolunda, 1-1, midway through the second period when Jim Storm of Milford, Mich., picked up the rebound of David Sacco's slapshot and sent it past goalie Mikael Sandberg. Vastra Frolunda broke the tie late in the second period. Sandberg stopped 31 shots.

"I think it's very important," said Steve Hertzberg, president of the Lane Amateur Hockey Association with more than 200 adult and youth members. "We want to be a citizen advisory board and want to recognized as such by the Fair Board. At our last meeting on Tuesday, the new assistant fairgrounds manager, Vern Thompson, seemed very receptive. "We just want to keep our ice arena, and we don't want it operated without an opportunity for us to have some input. The rink manager, Tom Bahls, has always been supportive of the user groups. We just want to go beyond that because he has to answer to (Fairgrounds Director) Steve McCulloch and the Fair Board. "There's really no one on the Fair Board who's an ice enthusiast, no one you can point to and say he's an advocate."

For more information about the Lane Amateur Hockey Association, call Steve Hertzberger at 741-2221; about the Eugene Figure Skating Club, call Carolyn Brown 343-7195; about the Lane Speedskating Club, call Steve Lader at 343-3249; about the Lane Amateur Broomball Association, call Brian Niemeyer at 343-1248.
Lane Amateur Hockey Association At Lane County Ice Adult League

the Fair Board see the rink in five years?" said Carolyn Brown, president of the Eugene Figure Skating Club. "I think the user groups need to know about that plan, the marketing strategy for the rink. We're spending a lot of time and money, so we would like some input. We also need to know where we fit in. How do they see us?" The rink manager since April 1990 after predecessor Kim Brusegaard resigned, the 27-year-old Bahls says he understands both sides of the equation. He also is a player for Eugene Blues hockey team and a former broomball player. "The user groups are our backbone financial, enthusiasm and awareness," Bahls said. "They are the ice arena. They really are. I'm also including public session skaters in that as well, but the ones with the voices and who get things done are four clubs. I fully understand where they're coming from."
