success and records will always take a back seat to his memorable performance in the first inning of the 1934 All-Star Game. After the first two batters reached base, Hubbell fanned Babe Ruth, Lou Gehrig and Jimmie Foxx and followed that be striking out Al Simmons and Joe Cronin to start the second. After a single by Bill Dickey, Hubbell struck out pitcher Lefty Gomez. Boxing Ray (Boom Boom) Mancini and Hector (Macho) Camacho will stage their comeback fight March 6 under a formal agreement reached Saturday. The Reno-Sparks Convention & Visitors Authority in Reno, Nev., reached the agreement with promoters Joe Gagliardi of San Jose, Calif., and Lou Falcigno of New York for the bout's site rights. Under the agreement, the authority paid $650,000 for the site rights and will receive the first $750,000 in gate receipts. The promoters will receive the next $50,000, with the authority and promoters splitting the money after that. The authority will not share in any television money. Awards Barry Sanders and Rodney Peete, leading candidates for the Heisman Trophy, were named to the 1988 Kodak Coaches All-America Team. Sanders, a junior from Oklahoma State, became the third running back in college football history to rush for more than 2,000 yards. Peete, quarterback for Southern California, is the sixthranked passer in the nation. The senior has 185 completions in 294 attempts for 18 touchdowns and 2,429 yards. Also chosen were running back Tim Worley of Georgia; wide receivers Erik Affholter of Southern Cal and Jason Phillips of Houston; tight end Marv Cook of Iowa; offensive linemen Tony Mandarich of Michigan State, Anthony Phillips of Oklahoma, Mark Stepnoski of Pittsburgh, Mike Utley of Washington State and Steve Wisnewski of Penn State, and placekicker Kendall Trainor of Arkansas. On defense were linemen Tracy Rocker of Auburn, Mark Messner of Michigan, Broderick Thomas of Nebraska, Derrick Thomas of Alabama and Bill Hawkins of Miami; linebackers Britt Hager of Texas and Jerry Olsavsky of Pittsburgh; backs Deion Sanders of Florida State, Louis Oliver of Florida, Darryl Henley of UCLA and Donnell Woolford of Clemson, and punter Pat Thompson of Brigham Young. Football Woody Widenhofer. 45, resigned under pressure as Missouri football coach with a four-year record of 12-31-1. Widenhofer's resignation followed weeks of speculation that his contract, which expires Jan. 31, would not be renewed after he finished 3-7-1 overall in his fourth season. The Tigers showed consistent improvement each season until this one. 7 Center Jeff Uhlenhake won three awards, including most valuable player, at the Ohio State football team banquet. Uhlenhake, a senior co-captain from Newark, also won the Archie Griffin Award for outstanding offensive player and was honored as outstanding offensive lineman. Cross country I Indiana's Robert Kennedy, a freshman from Columbus, and Michelle Dekkers, a barefoot runner from South Africa, ran to NCAA titles, the first time one school has produced the men's and women's champions in the same year. field goal and four converts and punter Scott Player scored a single. Winnipeg had a field goal from Troy Westwood and a single from Steve Cameron. Allan Boyko received Winnipeg quarterback Sammy Garza's pass for the Bombers' only touchdown. Birmingham's second big break came two minutes after Strode's TD. Klingler threw to rookie Jason Phillips, who nimbly picked his way down the field for a 95-yard TD. At this point, the crowd was booing Garza and Winnipeg coach Cal Murphy was shaking his head and stamping his feet from the sidelines. The final first-quarter blow came when Birmingham faked a punt. Player threw a 52-yard pass to Thurman to take the ball to the three-yard line. McClendon ran it in. Garza was replaced by Terry Dean early in the second quarter after the 'Cudas got their second TD on an interception. Thurman picked off Garza's pass and ran for a 65-yard TD. The second half began with fresh quarterbacking on both sides - Steve Pelluer for Winnipeg and Reggie Slack for Birmingham. But Pelluer was knocked down late in the third with a dislocated left shoulder after being knocked down by linebackers Angelo Snipes and Shonte Peoples. Garza returned for a second try at quarterback. His TD pass to Boyko was made with three minutes left. Birmingham replied when Klingler, who returned to the field in the fourth quarter, threw a three-yard pass into the end zone.

D As they make CFL debut WINNIPEG (CP) - The expansion Birmingham Barracudas made their CFL debut with a decisive 38-10 victory over the veteran Winnipeg Blue Bombers Tuesday night. In a game that astonished the announced crowd of 22,200, the untried Alabama club walked all over the Bombers without any help from star quarterback Matt Dunigan. Dunigan, formerly a Bomber, faced his old teammates from the sidelines as he nursed a broken finger that also kept him out of action in the pre-season. Pivot Jimmy Klingler, a 22-year-old who spent last season with Saskatchewan, led Birmingham in its first regular season CFL game. But it was defensive back Andre Strode who scored the 'Cudas first touchdown on an interception. "When a defensive player does that, it kind of sets the tone for the night," said Barracuda coach Jack k Pardee. He was happy with the quarterbacking too. "For Jimmy Klingler to step in like that, and Reggie Slack, well, it makes me feel good." In the other dressing room, Bomber veteran Chris Walby decried the lack of "chemistry" on his team. "It don't take long to go from the penthouse to the outhouse and we're sinking in that mire," said the despondent offensive lineman. The Bombers are now 0-2. Birmingham also had touchdowns from Jason Phillips, Willie McClendon, Junior Thurman and

By Canadian Press Denis Savard found new legs in the playoffs for the Chicago Blackhawks and now he's found a new opportunity to sell his services. The 34-year-old Savard was among 72 National Hockey League players who were offered termination contracts by their clubs and were set free to start testing the market on , Tuesday. A player offered a termination contract has 30 days to negotiate a contract with a rival team. If he fails to catch on with another club, his team can buy out the option year of his contract and release him or keep him for one more season. Others offered termination deals included St. Louis Blues goaltender Jon Casey, Toronto

By JONATHON JACKSON For The Sun Times Growing up in small-town New Brunswick had an insulating effect on Shane Kenny. The big Owen Sound Platers defenceman always dreamed of making it to the National Hockey League but never imagined it was possible until he arrived in the Ontario Hockey League in 1993. He was 16. "I'd heard about it (the OHL) but that was it," Kenny said in a recent interview, adding the Maritime provinces had no major junior outlet at that time.

"Owen Sound's all right. It's a good hockey town," Kenny said. He added he won't mind coming back to the Scenic City for another year or two of junior - and meeting new coach Ric Seiling. "I've heard he's strict. I need a little discipline," Kenny said with a laugh. Seiling toiled for 10 years in the NHL. It won't be Kenny's first time being coached by a former NHLer. His midget coach was Fredericton native Danny Grant, who played parts of 13 NHL seasons with four teams and who scored 50 goals for the 1974/75 Detroit Red Wings. Another former coach, Jerry Harrigan, believes Kenny has the tools to be successful in the pros. "Kenny's got three things going for him," said Harrigan, whose contract was not renewed this past spring after three seasons behind the Platers bench. "He's a big boy with a good shot,

Kenny, now 18, is one step closer to his dream. The Oromocto native's ranked 42nd among North American prospects for Saturday's NHL entry draft in Edmonton. "I'd like to be a little higher," Kenny said of his second-round ranking. "But I didn't have that good of a year." The si 230-pound rearguard got off to a slow start this past season and saw his stock drop accordingly. Once a projected first-round he feels he'll even go a bit lower than what Central Scouting has predicted. "I think I'll end up a third-rounder. I have a feeling.' He racked up 13 goals and 32 points in 65 games as a Plater sophomore in 1994/95, up from his four goals and 13 points in 54 games as a rookie. Kenny started skating at age two and began playing competitive hockey at age five. He said he's always been a defenceman, following in the footsteps of his father - an allstar blue liner in the New Brunswick senior leagues. The younger Kenny played midget in nearby Fredericton and caught the

to 40 travelling rugby players and four matches. Two clubs from Hanham High School of Bristol, England, will take to the pitch Friday night in a pair of exhibition matches against the junior and senior Bruce secondary school all-stars. The all-star clubs will feature players from Kincardine, Port Elgin Saugeen, Walkerton and Sacred Heart. Games are 6 p.m. and 7:30 p.m. / Admission is free. Saturday at 1 p.m. the under-19 girls Bruce all-star squad will face the Orillia Dingos and the Kincardine Barbarians men's team will follow, challenging Forest City of London. DYNAMOS WIN PORT ELGIN - Brian Dolan scored the winner on penalty kicks Tuesday as the Owen Sound Dynamos nipped the Port Elgin Flyers 3-2 in its first game ! of MidWestern Ontario S Soccer League Competitor Cup play. Ryan Traynor backstopped the victory in the Dynamo net as the Flyers missed their first penalty kick after an Owen Sound goal. Darren Elkerton opened the scoring for the Dynamos, heading a corner from Rob Blundell 10 minutes into the game. Roloff Carstensen knotted the score five minutes later but Blundell headed a throw-in from Sean Dantas before the end of the half, putting Dynamos ahead 2-1. Carstensen got the equalizer on a deflected free kick 25 minutes into the second half. Recently signed Will Valdez, John Walsh and Jan Bohlmark shored up the Dynamo line-up which was short six players. Owen Sound returns to league play this Sunday when it plays host to Hanover at 4 p.m. HOCKEY TRYOUTS Grey Bruce Highlanders triple-A hockey organization Tuesday announced tryouts will begin July 29 in Ayton. The tryouts for the organization's seven squads, minor atom through midget, will cost $20 per player. That guarantees three practices. A registration fee of $529 will apply to all signed players. Executive member Steve Leal anticipates team rosters will be finalized by the Labor Day weekend with the league schedule beginning on Oct. 1. Area residents will get a chance to "hear" what the Grey Bruce Highlanders triple-A hockey organization is all about Thursday. Host Tony Ambrogio and Highlanders executive members Miles McCauley, Leal, Denis Longpre and Bob Stride will be on CFOS Sportsticker at 6 p.m.. The Highlanders are still seeking volunteers, coaches and managers. Interested applicants can write Steve Leal at Box 65, Markdale, NOC 1H0 or fax (519) 924-3073. NOVICES WIN THIRD ELORA - Dylan Stoneman had two goals Tuesday as the Owen Sound RIS Xerox novices got their third win of the season in a West Central Minor Lacrosse zone 8 6-5 decision over Elora. Mike MacLeod, Chris Osborne and Brandon Earnest also scored for Owen Sound. RBW DOWNS ELORA ELORA - Brian Kazarian netted a pair of goals Tuesday as the Owen Sound RBW bantams downed Elora 7-3 in West Central Minor Lacrosse zone 8 play. Geoff Potter, Scott Luckhardt, Rob Milnes and Brad Slater also score. Owen Sound plays host to Milton tonight at the Lumley Centre at 7 p.m. CRESCENTS FALL ELORA - Jeff Nickel potted four goals Tuesday in a losing effort as the Owen Sound Crescent Club midgets fell 11-9 to Elora in West Central Minor Lacrosse zone 8 action. Adam McKeachnie with the hat trick and Andrew Reid with two also scored. Owen Sound plays host to Milton tonight at 8 p.m. at the Lumley Centre.

victory over the Havre North Stars late Sunday night in the first round of the state American Legion Class A baseball tournament. The win lifted Dillon to 37-5 for the season and the Cubs will next play Tuesday evening at 7:30 against the Bitterroot Valley Bucs. Monday games saw the Great Falls Sparkies blank Miles City 3-0 and Laurel knock off Havre 9-6 in loser-out games. Fort Macleod, Alberta, beat Libby 5-2, and Bitterroot Valley skunk Scobey 14-0 in winners' bracket contests. Dillon Coach Gail Whitworth said he plans to start left-hander Nick Miller on the mound Tuesday night, a spokesman said. Walker struck out seven North Stars and walked nine. The Cubs trailed 2-0 until taking a 3-2 edge in the third inning. Brothers Brian and Jason Phillips beat out consecutive infield hits. Sam Buchanan's single scored Brian Phillips before Ryan Bralette's single loaded the bases. Back-toback consecutive sacrifice flies by Josh Hegstad and Walker scored

However, Havre came back to score a run in the fourth. So, the Cubs came to the plate in the top of the fifth with the scored knotted 3-3. Hegstad reached on and error and then Walker smacked his home run over the left-field fence for a 5-3 Cubs lead. Dillon built a steady command. The Cubs got three runs in the sixth. Jason Phillips doubled, Buchanan walked and then Bramlette singled to knock in Phillips. Hegstad walked and the fourth ball was a wild pitch. Buchanan was thrown out at the plate as he tried to score. Bramlette wound up on third base and Hegstad on second. Consecutive wild pitches allowed Bramlette and Hegstad to score. Jason Phillips and Buchanan added RBI singles in the seventh inning and Loran Laknar whapped a run-scoring double in the ninth.
CITY - Shaun Walker Jason Phillips with the tying run a complete game and wal- and Buchanan with the go-ahead tie-breaking two-run homer tally.

Tunnel. Museeuw took the overall lead from Britain's Chris Boardman, who had held it since the prologue Saturday. Armstrong moved into fifth place, 22 seconds behind. The Atlanta Falcons signed their fourth-round draft pick and two veteran free agents. The free agents are quarterback Bob Gagliano, 35, who has played eight NFL seasons and two in the USFL, and wide receiver Jason Phillips. Gagliano joined the Falcons last Oct. 7, but did not play in a game. The rookie is quarterback Perry Klein, a 6-2, 218-pounder who set NCAA Division II passing records with 38 touchdown passes and 3,757 yards at C.W. Post. Dave D'Entremont saved his best game for last and won his first PBA title since 1992 when he captured the $100,000 PBA Oregon Open in Portland, Ore. D'Entremont beat tournament leader Joe Firpo 235-214 to earn his second career title and the winner's share of $16,000. The 33-year-old righthander qualified fourth and had to win four matches for the victory.

said the family asked that no information be released. Other relatives told the DOUGLAS station Douglas was taken to the hospital Monday and had slipped in and out of a coma since then. It was not known whether Douglas previously had been diagnosed as a diabetic or which form of the disease he might have. Douglas, a native of Columbus, was the heavyweight champion for only eight months. He was a 42-1 underdog when he won the title in February 1990 with a 10th-round knockout of then-undefeated Mike Tyson in Tokyo. TENNIS/Medvedev upset Wild -card entry Guy Forget of France stunned second-seeded Andrei Medvedev in straight sets during the first round of the $485,000 Swiss Open. The Ukrainian, ranked eighth in the world, was beaten 6-3, 7-5. Defending champion and top-seeded Sergi Bruguera of Spain had an easier time, trouncing Australian Patrick Rafter 6-4, 6-2 in his first match of the tournament. Russian Andrei Chesnokov breezed into the second round of the $313,750 Swedish Open by beating Ola Kristiansson of Sweden 6-3, 4-6, 6-2 Tuesday. At No. 3, Chesnokov is the highest-seeded player remaining in the event. No. 1 Magnus Larsson of Sweden withdrew with to a shoulder injury. Second-seeded Jason Stoltenberg of Australia beat Kevin Ullyett of South Africa 6-4, 6-3 to lead three seeded players into the second round of the Miller Lite Hall of Fame Tennis Championships in Newport, R.I. Mark Kaplan of Greenwich defeated Kenny Thorne of Richmond, Va., 7-6 (8-6), 4-6, 6-3, while Lou Gloria of Orange lost his first-round match 7-6 (7-3), 6-3 to Michael Tebbutt of Australia. HOCKEY/O'Connell promoted The Boston Bruins turned to an old friend, naming former All-Star defenseman Mike O'Connell as assistant general manager. O'Connell, 38, played for the Bruins from 1980 to 1986, representing the team in the 1984 National Hockey League All-Star game. For the past two seasons, he has coached at the Bruins' minor league affiliate in Providence. Edmonton Oilers President and General Manager Gien Sather said Tuesday he's too busy to decide who will take over as the coach of the Edmonton Oilers and he doesn't expect to name his replacement until next month. Ron Low, an Oilers assistant coach for the past five seasons, and George Burnett, coach of the Oilers' AHL affiliate in Sydney, Nova Scotia, are considered frontrunners for the position. Defenseman Jyrki Lumme has signed a multiyear contract with the Vancouver Canucks, the team said. Lumme, 27, had 13 goals and 42 assists last season. He also accounted for 13 points - two goals and 11 assists - in the playoffs. ALSO/Tour de France Belgium's Johan Museeuw took the lead in the Tour de France after the team time trial, and American Lance Armstrong moved into fifth place while heading a surge by his team. In the battle of the two favorites, three-time winner Miguel Indurain of Spain gained a slight edge over Tony Rominger of Switzerland. The team time trial, the Tour's third stage, covered 41 miles from Calais to the Channel

TAAC West and winner of 11 straight, will play TAAC East champion Georgia State at 1 p.m. CST Saturday in Atlanta for the conference championship. It will be the third straight championship meeting between GSU and Centenary. GSU won 2-0 two years ago in Atlanta. Centenary won 3-1 last year in Shreveport. Centenary qualified for the conference final Monday by playing its best game of the season. The Gents attacked from all areas of the field and kept HardinSimmons off-balance. Led by sophomore striker Curtis Partain's school-record four goals - three in the first half - the Gents took a 4-0 lead at intermission and led 6-0 after 60 minutes. Except for the last seven minutes of the first half and the last 20 minutes of the game - when the lead was unreachable -, Centenary rarely gave HardinSimmons players space to work the ball and build an attack. Hardin-Simmons (9-8 overall, 4-2 in TAAC West) had a 31-21 advantage in shots, but most were wild 20- 25-yard chances. "We knew they would come in here pumped, high," said Centenary Coach Glenn Evans. "What we wanted to do was knock the ball around and slow them down, and quickly counter against them. "We wanted to bury them early." The Gents did that with four goals in the first 22 minutes. The first came at 9:00 when Greg Woodbridge, on the right side, passed to Jamin DePrang at the right post. DePrang then passed to Partain who headed the ball past HSU goalkeeper Alan Chandler. Partain got his second goal at 14:00 when he took a pass in midfield from Jason Phillips, dribbled into the penalty box and kicked a low 16-yard shot inside the left post. Partain added No. 3 at 20:00 when he took a pass from DePrang, beat a fullback and kicked a shot past Chandler. Woodbridge made it 4-0 on a penalty kick at 22:30 that was set up when he was tripped by Chandler on a breakaway attempt inside the penalty box. Partain got his fourth goal at 52:00 when he got a loose ball, dribbled into the goal box and kicked in a shot. The four goals gave Partain a team-high 14 goals. "I never scored four in college," said Partain, a transfer from UCLA. "In high school, I had five in one game. Everytime they'd (Hardin-Simmons) try to pass to their outside fullbacks, there would be Woody (Woodbridge) and me breathing down their necks. They made a lot of bad back passes." DePrang, who did an excellent job of midfield marking and finding the open man on offense, scored his seventh goal of the season at 60:00 when he intercepted a Hardin- back pass and kicked in a low shot. Michael Henderson closed out Centenary's scoring at 79:00 with a shot off a pass from Steven Zeiller. Hardin-Simmons' goal came at 64:00 when Jason Bates, following up a shot by Todd Redfearn that was deflected by Ramsey, kicked a shot inside the near left post.

The team time trial, the Tour's third stage, covered 41 miles from Calais to the Channel Tunnel. Museeuw took the overall lead from Britain's Chris Boardman, who had held it since the prologue Saturday. He dropped to the 20th position, 54 seconds behind. Forget stuns Medvedev at Swiss Open Wild-card entry Guy Forget of France stunned sec Andrel Medvedev in straight sets during the first round of the $485,000 Swiss Open in Gstaad. The Ukrainian, ranked eighth in the world, was beaten 6-3, 7-5. Medvedev's downfall was sealed after TENNIS he sprained the little finger of his racket hand in a fall during the second set. Defending champion and top-seeded Sergi Bruguera of Spain had an easier time, trouncing Australian Patrick Rafter 6-4, 6-2 in his first match of the tournament. Second-seeded Jason Stoltenberg of Australia beat Kevin Ullyett of South Africa 6-4, 6-3 to lead three seeded players into the second round of the Miller Lite Hall of Fame Tennis Championships in Newport, R.I. • Russian Andrel Chesnokov breezed into the second round of the $313,750 Swedish Open in Bastad by beating Ola Kristiansson of Sweden 6-3, 4-6, 6-2. Police continue to investigate shooting Police have questioned a man who said he and a friend were target shooting near the site where professional golfer Kim Williams was shot. Police would not release the name of the Howland Township man who said he may have accidentally hit Williams. GOLF Police said the man told them he was firing his gun in woods near his home and one of the bullets from his 9mm semiautomatic pistol may have hit Williams. Howland Township Police Chief Steven Lamantia said it.is possible the bullet that struck Williams was not fired from the man's gun. Williams was shot around 9 p.m. Saturday as she was walking into a discount store in Niles, Ohio, about 60 miles southeast of Cleveland. She was in town for the YoungstownWarren LPGA Classic. Williams, 31, of Boca Raton, Fla., left the hospital Monday. Canucks re-sign defenseman Lumme Defenseman Jyrki Lumme has signed a multiyear contract with the Vancouver Canucks, the team said. Lumme, 27, had 13 goals and 42 assists last season. He also accounted for 13 points - two goals and 11 assists - in the playoffs. HOCKEY The native of Finland has played for Vancouver for four seasons after being acquired from the Montreal Canadiens in March 1990. Falcons sign draft pick, veterans The Atlanta Falcons signed their fourth-round draft pick and two veteran free agents. The free agents are quarterback Bob Gagliano, 35, who has played eight NFL. seasons and two in the USFL, and wide receiver Jason Phillips. MISC. The rookie is quarterback Perry Klein, a 6-foot-2, 218-pounder who set NCAA Division II passing records with 38 touchdown passes and 3,757 yards at C.W.

The Whalers and Phoenix Mutual once again co-sponsor the CIAC hockey finals at the Civic Center March 13. "I think it's important to showcase athletes in a facility such as the Civic Center, to emphasize the positive side of kids because athletics develop self-discipline, determination and sportsmanship," Robert Fiondella, president of Phoenix Mutual, said. In the previous two years, the finals have drawn between 4,000 and 5,000 people. "Fans should be aware that this is high hockey as evidenced by the fact that three of the four title games in the last two years have gone to overtime," CIAC Executive Director John Daly said. paced the Dofasco Steelhawks to a 7-3 win over Amherst, N.Y. in an atom exhibition hockey game on the weekend. Justin Cannon, Jason Phillips and Steven Miinarevic also scored for Dofasco. In a peewee exhibition action, Terry Shamoon and Paul Riley fired two goals apiece as the Dofasco Steelhawks posted a 7-6 decision over West Seneca, N.Y. Jamin Shaw, Joey Katkic and Jeff Michaud also scored for the Steelhawks. Earlier, Dofasco trimmed Bowmanville 4-3 led by a hat trick from Terry Shamoon. Kyle Boucher added a single. In novice exhibition action, the Dofasco Steelhawks routed Saltfleet Fortino's Supermarkets 7-2. In other novice exhibition play, Dundas Laidlaw dropped a 5-2 decision to Flamboro last night. Brandon Travaris led the winners with two goals while singles went to Ryan Hone, Richard Toule and Scott Keller. Matt Hickling and Bobby Joe Worrall scored for Dundas. On the weekend, Mike Watters scored with nine seconds left as Dundas Laidlaw tied Georgetown 3-3. Worrall scored the other Dundas goals. 000 TREVOR MUCKLE and Jimmy Wall each scored as Good Times beat G.J.'s 2-1 in Dundas Minor Hockey Association novice house league action last night: Andrew Waltman answered for G.J. 'S. 000 JOEY McLEAN poured in seven goals and added four assists as the Penguins trounced the Bruins 9-1 in Scott Park Minor Hockey Association tyke house league action on the weekend. Jay Dumont added the other two goals for the Pens. 000. BRAD WHALEN, Duncan Martin and Michael Avery each scored as Hamilton Welco Protective Devices trimmed Guelph 3-2 to even their six-point Ontario Minor Hockey Association Southwestern League minor atom AAA divisional playoff series 5-5. 000 SINGLE GOALS by Jason Haardeng, Craig Aulenback and Steven Hatcher lifted Stoney Creek Dairy to a 3-1 win over Hamilton Wings last night, sweeping their best-of-five OMHA Provincial Playdowns minor atom AA first-round series 3-0. In major atom AAA action, Hamilton Davidson Electric lost two games to Whitby on the weekend to fall behind 2-0 in the best-offive quarter-final. Hamilton lost 5-2 in the first game with goals going Frank Taksas and J.J. Cybalski. Hamilton dropped the second contest 5-3 with goals going to Brian Hogben, Andrew Yip and Cybalski. 000 GLABROOK JOE Hagel Sports won the atom consolation championship at the Penetanguishene Winterama Minor Hockey Tournament with a 5-1 win over Hamilton Parkdale. Chris Hart scored three times for Glanbrook while Mike Dean added a pair. Scott Smith replied for Parkdale. 000 STEVE HORVATH scored two goals as the Jets downed the Kings 4-3 in Mount Hamilton Minor Hockey Association peewee house league action last night. Mike Duncan and Jamie Woods also scored for the winners while Bobby Tykoliz, Shawn Halloran and Steve Green answered for the Wings. 000 SCOTT BEATTIE scored twice in the final minute of the game to lift the Coronation Screaming Eagles to a 3-1 win over the Coronation Whitehawks in Eastwood-Coronation Interlocking House League novice play last night. Vince Marino also scored for the winners while Arg Singh replied for the Whitehawks. 000 A GOALTENDING clinic is being offered by the Hamilton Minor Hockey Council for atom and novice-aged players at Mountain Arena on Thursday at 8 p.m. Call Charlie Richardson at 388-6852 for information. Elsewhere... ALVARO VENTURELLI was named the most valuable player of the game as the Hamilton Aquatic-Water Polo Club won the junior championship at the Jimmy Thompson Memorial Water Polo Tournament held at McMaster on the weekend with an 11-9 decision over Guelph. In the senior B group, Hamilton whipped Guelph 21-7 to win the title. Hamilton's Geoff White was named MVP. 000 HILL PARK Matt's Sports Cafe swept Huntington 3-0 in action from the Hamilton Department of Culture and Recreation Men's Volleyball League last night. Scores of the games were 15-7, 15-4 and 15-13. 000 THE HAMILTON One-Way Volleyball Club placed second at the Ontario Volleyball Association Region Three Juvenile Boys' Championships in St. Catharines on the weekend after losing 2-0 (8-15, 7-15) to West Side in the final. Hamilton picked up silver medals for its effort. 000 MARK GUROWSKI of the Hamilton Olympic Club placed second in the junior boys' 400 metres at the Ontario Bantam-Midget-Junior Indoor Track Meet on the weekend. COSENTINO and Len Dimitri fired two goals each VINCE as Joslin's Karate and Kickboxing whipped Prestige Printing 6-0 in Regional Non-Contact Oldtimers' Hockey League junior division action on the weekend. Jack Brown and Barry Carte added singles for the winners while Paul Elcombe posted the shutout. Meanwhile, Rick Puhakka recorded the shutout as Peninsula Truck Caps stopped Hamilton Carpet and Tile 4-0. Kevin O'Brien counted for two goals for the winners while singles went to Dave Dore and Cam Whiffing. John Bowker fired a hat trick as Henderson Insurance Brokers downed Whelan's Sport Cresting 5-3. Gerry Crevier and William Patter also scored for the Henderson while Joe Cipolla, Mike LaChance and Willard Knox replied for Whelan's. In intermediate division play, Gord Ross, Lorne Hart and Tom Walters each scored two goals as Abba Lock and Safe doubled Hamilton Vehicle Leasing 6-3. Russ Cobb, Moe Charbonneau and Ken Miller answered for Hamilton Vehicle Leasing. and Dave Anderson added a pair each as Saltron Television Meanwhile, Tom Carney a fired a hat trick and George Morasse outlasted Mercer's Auto Clinic 7-5: Bob Walters led Mercer's with two goals while Charlie Purich, Wayne Gibson and Len Brandino added one apiece. Bob Smith and Wayne Harron scored to lift Veteran's Taxi to a 2-1 decision over Westview Photo. Ernie Coulson countered for Westview. In senior action, Ray Murphy potted two goals to lead Tim Horton Donuts (Mohawk Road West) past Sheild Contracting 3-2. Bob Frame added a single for the winners while Barry Devins and Claire Logan answered for Shield. Larry Mitchell, Dave Baker and Bob Gibson scored as Classic Sleep Centres downed Wm. J. Video 3-1. Logan Baird replied for Wm. J. Also, Steve Murphy, Bob Whittaker and Dave Easton each scored once as Matt's Sports Cafe trimmed R.B. Car Wash Systems 3-2. Gord Kennedy and John Corby replied for R.B. 000 BAIN DONALDSON fired two goals as Wentworth Sports downed Crescent Oil 4-1 in Ancaster Men's Hockey League action on the weekend. John Galama and Pat Radigan added one each for the winners while George Revill replied for Crescent Oil. In other games, Jeff Wallace, Ron Strauch, Joe Cipolla and Mike Mcinnes scored to pace Eastside Mario's past Robin's Donuts 4-2. Bruce Earl notched both goals for Robin's. Meanwhile, Dan Karschti scored a pair of goals as Mickey Clair Drywall upended Re/Max Realty 6-4. Ted Troundou, Bill Pazeratz, Gary Reynolds and Wray Oaks chipped in with singles for the winners while Dave Lawson, Rick DeJonge, Jeff Mollen and Mike Wiggens answered for Re/Max. M.T.C. Electric and Soldaat's Poultry battled to a 1-1 tie with Carl Maragno scoring for M.T.C. and Bill Oaks relying for Soldaat's. 000 MIKE SCHATZ tallied a pair of goals as Public Works dumped Flamborough 5-1 in Civic and Regional Employees' Hockey League action last night. Pete Topolovic, Jason Kirkpatrick and Roland Dube also scored for the winners while Bill Finnamore replied for Flamborough. AI Nicholson, Steve Spicer, Tom Brown, Doug Woods and Frank Turcato each scored as DiCenzo Construction downed Parks Department 5-2. John Togmus scored both goals for the Parks Department. Meanwhile, Bill McBay, Dave Tarbutt and Vince Mercuri each scored as Stoney Creek edged Regional Engineers 3-2. Jeff Pidsadny and Pat Cosispodi scored for Regional Engineers. 000 SINGLE GOALS by Jay Russell, Victor Rolfe and Ed Mullaley sparked Mount Hamilton Local 105 I.B.E.W. to a 3-1 win over Rosedale in Hamilton Minor Hockey Council bantam hub league play last night. Aaron Sloat replied for Rosedale. In atom action, Jeff Dummond fired a hat trick as Lawfield McDonald's (Harvard Square) routed. Coronation 9-0. Jeff Neil, P.J. Van Dalen, Chris Lambert, Todd Hornby, Michael Clark and KerriAnne Rudaniecki also scored for the winners while Kevin Horvath and Paul Polak shared the shutout. In a novice division clash, Mount Hamilton David A. Pauls Real Estate got shutout goaltending from Brian Crawford and Chris Donaldson in a 5-0 win over Lawfield Queenston Chev-Olds. David Cox, Joey Tenute, Brian Mcintosh, Jason Morrison and Justin Park scored for Mount Hamilton. 000 KEVIN OSINGA scored with one second remaining in the game to lift the North Stars past the Bruins 2-1 in Dofasco Minor Hockey Association bantam house league action last night. Maybe Tony Meola should have stuck with soccer after all. The goalkeeper for the U.S. soccer team who played well during the recent World Cup was waived by the New York Jets on Tuesday. While several of his former teammates were signing lucrative contracts to continue playing soccer overseas, Meola was trying to catch on as an NFL kicker. Unfortunately for him, he was playing behind one of the best kickers in league history, 38-year-old Nick Lowery. Meola, dubbed "Captain Hook" by Jets teammates early in camp for his tendency to shank kicks, was signed as a possible designated kickoff man who could learn from Lowery. Only one of Meola's five preseason kickoffs reached the end zone, but coach Pete Carroll said the team hopes to keep him around. "We would like to get him on the practice squad,' Carroll said. "We'll have to see how the numbers fall.' Tuesday was the NFL's biggest cutdown day, with teams required to get down to 60 players. Most to go were marginal rookies and free agents, although among those cut was Nick Bell, the 43rd player taken in the 1991 draft, who was trying to switch from running back to tight end for the Raiders. Pittsburgh severed its ties with troubled guard Carlton Haselrig, a Pro Bowler two years ago, and put holdout kicker Gary Anderson in position to play only if he requests reinstatement from the commissioner's office. FALCONS: Atlanta released four players, including six-year wide receiver Jason Phillips. Also cut were free-agent tackle John Heidenreich; linebacker Mitch Davis, a fourth-round draft choice from Georgia; and rookie free-agent wide receiver Corey Dixon of Nebraska. Phillips played in 55 games with the Falcons and Detroit Lions. He had seven catches for 76 yards and one score in four preseason games with the Falcons. Davis, a fourth-round pick, had seven tackles and one quarterback sack in four preseason games. BROWNS: Antonio Langham, the Browns' No. 1 draft pick, was named starting left cornerback over Donald Frank, acquired in a trade with San Diego. Forest Park Elementary and Woodlands Junior High schools in Nanaimo, was recruited, by Dalhousie coach Dr. Ryan. Lennox received a varsity 'D' award from Dalhousie in recognition of his basketball efforts at the Nova Scotia university. Perhaps the highlight of his Lennox' career came on a nationally-televised- game featuring Dalhousie and Acadia Axemen. TSN color commentator Jack Donahue, coach of Canada's national basketball team, praised Lennox for his solid performance on the floor. Lennox is now doing post graduate work at Dalhousie. .. Sunday's Lions-Saskatchewan TV game was probably the worst display of pro football I've ever witnessed. Yes, even worse than that USFL tilt I caught live three years ago in Phoenix. Despite the Lions' sad play, Canadian wide receiver John Pankratz picked up 63 yards to close within 10 yards of. Willie Fleming (4,480) as the third alltime Lions' leader in receiving yards. Not a bad accomplishment considering Pankratz has quietly gone about his business with the Lions. Most football fans in Canada know who Fleming is, but I out East can spell Pankratyone Still with the Lions, Roy Dewalt was as bad as Michael Jackson against the Roughies, but he passed the 22,000 mark in career passing yardage. He has passed Warren Moon, Peter Liske and John Hufnagel to move into 40th place all-time in CFL history. He should pass Tom Wilkinson and Joe Kapp before the end of this season. It was rather sad to see Phil Niekro end his Major League Baseball career, earlier this week. Rod Hardy and I had the honor of seeing old 'Knucksy' in the Toronto Blue Jays' clubhouse during their late August road trip to Seattle. There he was, sitting at his stall in his tee-shirt and underwear, puffing on a cigarette and staring at the floor. He had a Bass fishing hat on and he looked more like a weary sailor than a pro ball player. I almost felt sorry for Niekro as the rest of the youthful Jays gobbled down lasagna and watched a Yankees' game on TV. My sorrow turried to happiness when I realized Niekro had broken into the Majors when I was seven-years-old. And here he was trying to help the Blue Jays win the American League East pennant 23 years later. He had' a fine and long career, playing a game he • loved and dedicated •himself to playing. No need to feel sorry for Knucksy. He was one of the best. BY AL CAMERON Staff Writer On a team with its fair share of hot dogs and headline grabbers, Corky Parkes has quietly gone about his job with little or no recognition from fans or media. Considering Parkes has scored 63 goals over the past two seasons for Nanaimo Clippers, and may be on his way to a 50- goal season in 1987-88, maybe the fans and media have been missing a good thing. He will be a key for the Clippers, who take on unbeaten Delta Flyers Friday at Civic Arena in an 8 p.m. start. Parkes, a three-year B.C. Junior Hockey League vet, is entering his fourth and final junior season, and he would like to go out with a bang. "I'm working a lot harder out on the ice," said Parkes, 20. "I reported in better shape than last year, because I want to give my best effort for my last season.' Burarkes, graduated program, from play- the ing along Midget, like goalie Ray Woodley and Jason Phillips. He joined the old Burnaby Bluehawks for the 84-85 season and scored 12 goals before being released at Christmas. He played the remainder of the season in junior B, and joined the Clippers for the 85-86 season. Parkes scored 24 goals and 55 points that year, and 39 goals and 64 points last season. the Parkes has been on a roll in the early going of this season, scoring six goals and nine points in the first five games. "I should be scoring more," said Parkes. "I've had a lot of he chances, so I just have to bear down more. The puck isn't bounto cing for the whole team.' of Parkes says he is shooting for a 50-goal season, and coach Rich is Dutka says he is more than capable. "You know he'll do no worse than last year, said Dutka. "He's always been fairly consistent. He should be able to crack following the 80-point barrier. I hope he's to right about the 50 goals.' Dutka called Parkes one of the best penalty killers in the league, and that is borne out by some pretty good statistics. Parkes scored five shorthanded goals in each of his first three seasons, and has already scored a pair this season. "I've always liked the pressure of penalty killing," said Parkes. "I've done it most of my career." There's no real secret to Parkes' methods in turning a shorthanded situation into a potential goal. "I just try and read what they're going to do," he said. "Most teams have basically the same power play so I just try and anticipate what they'll do and make a move before they do.' the by One difference in Parkes this season is his penchant towards A playing a more physical game. be "I let the other guys do the hitfor ting.' said Parkes, "but this year I feel like getting in the game more.' Parkes' hockey future is a little up in the air right now. The "I'll see how my season goes

NOTES/ANNOUNCEMENTS MARTIAL ARTS CHAMPIONSHIPS - The 1992 Martial Arts Championships will be held beginning at 10 a.m., Saturday, Oct. 10 at Willamette High School, 1801 Echo Hollow Rd., in Eugene. The entry fees for competitors are $25 by Oct. 6, $30 after. Spectators are welcome. For more information, call 344-2715. SPECIAL OLYMPICS VOLUNTEERS - The Willamalane Park & Recreation District is looking for volunteers to help staff the Special Olympics basketball programs for youth and adults. For more information, call Dorothy Van Winkle at 746-1669. SHORT-TRACK SPEED SKATING - The 2nd Annual Pacific Northwest Open short-track speed skating race sponsored by the Lane Speed Skating Club will be held at 4 p.m., Saturday, Oct. 24 at Lane County Ice. Spectators are invited and admission is free. For more information, call Bill McConochie at 686-9934. LAHA ADULT HOCKEY LEAGUE - Registration is available at Lane County Ice for the Lane Amateur Hockey Association's 4th Annual Adult Winter League. The 20-game season will be held on Wednesday nights beginning on Oct. 14. For more information, call LAHA president Steve Hertzberg at 741-2221. YOUTH HOCKEY - Registration is ongoing for the Lane Amateur Hockey Association's youth hockey program for boys and girls up to age 18. A Youth Functional Hockey Class is also available from 5:15- 6:30 p.m. on Wednesdays. The cost is $6 for each drop-in class. Full equipment is required, with some equipment available to rent. To rent equipment for $15, call Rex Meisenheimer at 747-3943. Registration is available at Lane County Ice. For more information about the program call Carole Horton at 687-9754. EUGENE PARKS VOLLEYBALL CLASSES - Registration is available for Eugene Parks beginning and intermediate instructional volleyball classes starting on Oct. 12. The beginning class will meet from 7-8:30 p.m., Mondays, at Jefferson Middle School. The intermediate class will meet from 8:30-10 p.m., Mondays, at Jefferson Middle School. The cost for each eight-week class is $20 for Eugene residents and $25 for non-Eugene residents. Registration is available at the Eugene Parks office at 22 W. 7th Ave. For more information, call 687- 5333. EUGENE PARKS TENNIS INSTRUCTION - Registration is available for Eugene Parks fall tennis classs and a series of individual clinics for beginning and intermediate players. The fee for the tennis classes is $24 for in-city residents, $29 for non-Eugene residents. Clinics are $5 each. For more information, call 687-5333. TENNIS FESTIVAL - A Wilson-Jeep Eagle Tennis Festival will be held at 6:30 p.m., Monday, Oct. 12 at the Eugene Swim & Tennis Club. Open free of charge to the general public, the event includes games, contests and instruction. Space limited to 100 adults and 20 children accompanied by adults. For more information, call 342-4414. SAILBOAT RACING - The Yaquina Bay Yacht Club in Newport will host its annual regatta on Oct. 10-11. There is a $30 fee for racing, with a skippers meeting each day at noon at the Embarcadero. For

MISSION, Texas - Most people can only dream of winning $1 million, and most golfers can only fantasize about hitting a hole-in-one, but both dreams came true at the same time for Ray Wilkerson of Harlingen, Texas. The 41-year-old made the shot during the Pizza Hut Million Dollar Hole-in-One Contest Sunday afternoon in the South Texas Health Care Foundation Golf Classic. He parlayed an eight-iron tee shot 150 yards, 2 feet, into the No. 5 cup to win the big money. The National Hole-in-One Insurance Association will pay Wilkerson, a route salesman for Rainbo Baking Co., $25,000 a year for the next 40 years, said Lee Singletary, tournament director. Hockey Nine months after Bob Murdoch was named coach of the year, he was fired by the Winnipeg Jets, one of only five clubs in the 21- team National Hockey League to miss the playoffs. The Jets fell to fifth place in the Smythe Division with just 27 wins and were eliminated from the Stanley Cup playoffs with a 3-2 overtime loss in Vancouver on Thursday. The Chicago Blackhawks said that wing Michel Goulet will be sidelined for 10 days to two weeks because of a sprained right knee. Goulet was hurt Sunday in a pileup into the boards during Chicago's 5-1 win over Detroit. Tennis Gabriela Sabatini and Jennifer Capriati, both ranked in the top 10 in the world, teamed up to eliminate Helen Kelesi and Katerina Maleeva 6-4, 6-4 in the opening round of doubles play at the $500,000 Family Circle Magazine Cup at Hilton Head, S.C. Football Minnesota Vikings kicker Donald Igwebuike went to trial alone on drug smuggling charges after a second co-defendant in the case agreed to plead guilty to importing $1 million worth of heroin. General The Nevada Assembly gave final legislative approval to a bill that would require the NCAA to give due process to coaches and athletes. The lower house action sent the bill to Gov. Bob Miller for his signature.

Carl Hubbell Rodney Peete George Irvine Baseball's Carl Hubbell dies of accident injuries Carl Hubbell, who won 253 games as a left-handed pitcher for the New York Giants, died two days after being critically injured in an automobile accident. He was 85. Hubbell, most remembered for striking out five future Hall of Famers in a row in the 1934 All-Star Game, lost control of his car Saturday while driving near his Mesa, Ariz., home, and the vehicle struck a metal pole. He died of head and chest injuries, a hospital spokeswoman said. Hubbell, nicknamed "King Carl," was one of the top pitchers of the 1930s, winning 21 or more games five years in a row and earning the Most Valuable Player Award with a 26-6 record in 1936. FOOTBALL: Barry Sanders of Oklahoma State and Rodney Peete of Southern Cal, leading candidates for this year's Heisman Trophy, were named Monday to the 1988 Kodak Coaches AllAmerica Team. Also chosen were running back Tim Worley of Syracuse; wide receivers Erik Affholter of Southern Cal and Jason Phillips of Houston; tight end Marv Cook of Iowa; offensive linemen Mike Utley of Washington State, Tony Mandarich of Michigan State, Anthony Phillips of Oklahoma, Mark Stepnoski of Pittsburgh, and Steve Wisnewski of Penn State, and placekicker Kendall Trainor of Arkansas. Named to the defensive team were linemen Tracy Rocker of Auburn, Mark Messner of Michigan, Broderick Thomas of Nebraska, Derrick Thomas of Alabama and Bill Hawkins of Miami; linebackers Britt Hager of Texas and Jerry Olsavsky of Pittsburgh; backs Deion Sanders of Florida State, Louis Oliver of Florida, Darryl Henley of UCLA and Donnell Woolford of Clemson, and punter Pat Thompson of Brigham Young. The selections were made by the American Football Coaches Association. Quarterback Rodney Peete, who led second-ranked Southern Cal to a 31-22 victory over crosstown rival UCLA last Saturday despite being weakened by the measles, now has laryngitis, it was announced Monday. Peete, who will guide the Trojans against top-ranked Notre Dame this Saturday at the Los Angeles Coliseum, has been ordered by the Trojans' medical staff to refrain from talking until Thursday. He is practicing is practicing this week, but freshman quarterback Todd Marinovich stands behind him and calls out the snap count. • Linebacker Billy Ray Smith suffered a season-ending injury, a hairline leg fracture, in San Diego's upset victory over the Los Angeles Rams on Sunday, the Chargers announced Monday. Quarterback Mark Vlasic was lost for the season when he suffered torn knee ligaments during the third quarter of the Chargers' 38-24 victory. At College Station, Texas, former Texas A & M fullback George Smith told a reporter that Aggie Coach Jackie Sherrill threatened violence and became angry during phone calls to discuss how much money the former athlete should receive for his silence, according to a published report. Smith had claimed in a story written by Dallas Morning News reporter Doug Bedell and published Friday that Sherrill payed him "hush money" to keep him quiet about NCAA violations.

starters must be replaced, as well as receivers James Dixon and Jason Phillips, offensive linemen Moser, Paul Hearn and William Gant, defensive linemen Glenn Montgomery and Keith Jenkins and defensive backs Jackson and Norwood. Houston must battle the cloud of probation. The NCAA's three-year sentence includes no bowls for two seasons, no live television for one and a loss of 10 scholarships. There may be a trip to Japan to entice recruits. Athletic Director Rudy Davalos met with Japan Bowl officials Saturday and discussed moving Houston's 1990 home game with Arkansas to Tokyo. Pardee would like to lure receivers, offensive linemen and defensive backs from the junior colleges. "When we get back, that's our first priority," he said. Only 15 scholarships are available for freshmen and they must be treated like gold. Pardee believed the exposure of playing - and beating Wyoming on ESPN proved invaluable. The Aloha Bowl on ABC, even in defeat, helped more. Now Pardee and his staff must round up freshmen capable of putting Houston back in a bowl after the Cougars have been pardoned from the NCAA's prison.

00K no further than this year's edition of the Welland Pirates for proof that records are made to be broken. The assault on the Pirates' book began with Jamison .Nuttle and shows no signs of = slowing. Nuttle broke the mark for saves with 10 before his recent promotion to Augusta of the South Atlantic. He passed Marc Pisciotta's total of eight which was established during the 1991 .: * A season. Barring a spate of call-ups over the final four weeks of the season, Nuttle won't be alone in etching his name into Welland baseball annals. No less than six other records are in clear and present danger. * Heavy-hitting Mitch House, a third baseman, leads the onslaught. House will eclipse records in both home runs and RBIs. The six-foot-two veteran has eight home runs this year as well as one left over from the 1991 season he spent in Welland for a total of nine. Antonio Mitchell of the 1991 Pirates holds the record with 10. Mitchell's other record of 38 RBIs is also in grave danger from House, who has 33.• Cent fielder Jermaine Allensworth had 50 hits after 48 games. Over a full season, that total projects to 81. Scott Bullett, currently in the big leagues with Pittsburgh, set the record of 78 in 1990. Pitchers Jason Phillips, Brian Pelka and Ramon Morel also have opportunities to join Nuttle. The Pirate record for wins by a pitcher is just six, recorded by David Bird in the inaugural 1989 season. Pelka, with five, will no doubt surpass Bird. Morel with four should while both Brian Beck and Jason Phillips with three each have outside shots. Phillips and Pelka both have over 40 strikeouts and are within striking distance of Rich Robertson's record of 80. Both Phillips and Pelka will have to increase their average of 4.5 strikeouts per start by about one-and-a-half per game. Phillips and Pelka also have outside shots at the standard for innings pitched, set last year by Frank Garcia-Luna. The safest section in the book is Valentine Henderson's 1989 record for steals.of 34. Allensworth is the Pirate leader this year; with 13: Of course, the record that might mean the most to the fans is that of first Pirate entry to finish above .500 and in the thick of a Stedler Division pennant race. Starting last night, Welland needed to go 15-11 over the final 26 games to® finish slightly above the * break-even mark. At the current rate, the Pirates will finish with 36 wins and 41 losses, them two-and-a-half alrexines off the pace of the best Welland Pirate team of alltime, the 1990 squad.

TORONTO (CP) - Gord Stellick, former general manager of the NHL's Toronto Maple Leafs, was hired as the director of operations for the Colonial Hockey League. The eight-team minorleague circuit said in a release Tuesday that the 36- year-old Stellick will work on its behalf to improve its relationship with the National Hockey League, American Hockey League and International Hockey League. Blackhawk •defenseman Bob McGill can feel them breathing down his neck. The Young Guns are out to get him, and one of them is his own cousin. Rookies Ryan McGill, Mike Peluso and Cam Russell will continue to have a chance to work their way into the Hawk defense this season, coach Mike Keenan said Wednesday. By Thursday, other prospects won't be able to say the same. "We're going to decide by Thursday who is going to the minor leagues this year," he said. "But we'll look at those three young defensemen in more preseason games and see how they develop. They're probably not ready, but it's a possibility they could make the team.' Not all three. They are probably vying for one spot among the seven defensemen Keenan is likely to retain. And Bob McGill knows he's in for a fight. "I've never been guaranteed a job any year, anywhere, said the eightyear veteran of the National Hockey League. "Ryan, Cam and Peluso will all be good players and I can see they're getting the opportunity to make the team now. "But | helped contribute to getting this team to the Stanley Cup semifinals last year and I I've shown I'm a team player, a character player." He continues to show that by helping out his three young competitors. know they're here to take my job, but I'm here to help them, too, with advice. try to do that when I see they need

By Mike Kiley Chicago Tribune BLOOMINGTON, Minn.-Minnesota, home of the North Stars and the site of the Blackhawks' second preseason game Tuesday night, is one of the National Hockey League's most intriguing contradictions. The Stars play in a state that has 2,255 registered amateur hockey teams, more than any other state. At last June's NHL draft, 17 picks coming out of high school or the junior-A level were from Minnesota, more than any other state produced in the draft. There were 146 high school hockey teams in Minnesota last year, more than 300 fewer than basketball teams. But fans generated $576,241 in hockey tournament admission compared to revenues of $388,793 for basketball, which exceeded football revenues. Minnesota people are sold on hockey, just not on the Stars. The team's average paid attendance of 8,000 a game last season was the worst in the NHL. A national sports magazine recently carried a no prediction that the Stars would win the Norris Divi-

Bits & Pieces This mite be fun: The starting wide receivers for the Detroit Lions in their "Silver Stretch" offense are 5-foot-6-inch Richard Johnson, 5-7 Stacey Mobley, 5-11 Robert Clark and huge 6-0 Troy Johnson. They are backed up by 5-7 Jason Phillips, 5-9 Mel Gray, 5- 9 Walter Stanley and the gigantic 6-2 John Ford. The Lions cut their leading receiver of last season, Pete Mandley, along with their leading passer, Rusty Hilger, and leading runner, Garry James. "They already had it in mind there were certain guys who can play in that offense and certain guys who can't--no matter what we did in practice," Mandley said. "They made the statement they didn't want any receiver over 5-7. I'm 5-10. How short do you want a guy?" Early-season quirks: The Phoenix Cardinals, who gave up 60 c quarterback sacks last season, have yielded none through the first two games. The Miami Dolphins' defense, which had only 24 sacks all last season, has 11 after two games to lead the league. Gofer broke, Tony: Green Bay tackle Tony Mandarich, football's first million-dollar offensive lineman, will make his debut Sunday against the Rams in Anaheim. He started carning his money last week when he went from the sidelines to the locker room to fetch a pair of shoes for running back Brent Fullwood. "Anything and anyway I can help this team out," Mandarich said. Mooning a teammate: Houston defensive back Cris Dishman, a second-year player from Purdue, was chewed out by Oiler quarterback Warren Moon after committing a personal foul that led to a touchdown pass by San Diego's Jim McMahon. "We have a tendency to be overaggressive at times, and I had enough of seeing that in the preseason," Moon said. The first week, Dishman stood over Minnesota tight end Carl Hilton and barked after Hilton had suffered a concussion. "That's the kind of thing that starts riots among sane men," said Viking tackle Tim Irwin. Moon Surprise, surprise: Phoenix coach Gene Stallings was unhappy when the schedule had his Cardinals starting with three road games, especially after they finished last season with five straight defeats. Stallings didn't know Phoenix owner Bill Bidwill had requested the three straight road games because he didn't want to subject home fans to the glaring sun on one side of Sun Devil Stadium at this time of year. Stallings also didn't know the Cardinals would start out 2-0 on the road. Boz out 6 weeks: Brian Bosworth, Seattle's $11 million linebacker, was placed on injured reserve Wednesday. He will miss at least six games after injuring his right shoulder making an arm tackle in a loss to Phoenix last Sunday.

You can call Lemuel Stinson a lot of names, and his Bear teammates certainly do. Sunday, the second-year cornerback will be called upon to replace Donnell Woolford at left cornerback when the Bears visit Detroit to face the Lions' helter-skelter run-and-shoot offense. "it's no big deal," Stinson said. "I played against [Lions receiver] Jason Phillips in college when he was at Houston, and he is good. Houston used the run and shoot, so I've played against it. My main worry is the crossing routes and watching who goes deep." Woolford, the No. 1 draft choice who made his first pro interception in last Sunday's 38-7 victory over Minnesota, said his separated left shoulder is improving. "There's a chance I could play some," said Woolford, who is listed as doubtful. "I'll know for sure on Friday.' "He is in pain,' coach Mike Ditka : said. "But it isn't as bad as it was Sunday. We're gearing for the following week." Stinson has been nicknamed "Lemon Head" by teammates because his resemblance to the cartoon character on the box of Lemon Head candies. Defensive tackle Dan Hampton calls him "Horsefly." Safety David Tate had a new nickname for him Wednesday. "Forget about Lemon Head," Tate said. "From now on, he'll be known as 'Quick Six.' Stinson got his first NFL interception last Sunday and returned it 29 yards for a touchdown. It's a feat he hasn't let Tate forget. They have a friendly competition going. Tate has four career interceptions but none for a TD. "I'm ready to. go," said Tate, set to resume practicing Thursday after missing the first two games with a pulled hamstring. "The hamstring feels a lot better. have to gain a little more strength. I don't want it to bother me all year. "I've got to get back out there. Even Stinson is ahead of me with interceptions. Ever since he ran that one back, I can't shut him up. used to have bragging rights. Now I've got to run one back myself.' BOXING Report says Douglas in diabetic coma - In Columbus, Ohio, former heavyweight champion James "Buster" Douglas was r reportedly hospitalized Tuesday in a diabetic coma. WBNS-TV, citing no sources, said Douglas, 34, was upgraded from poor to stable condition at Grant t Medical Center on Tuesday Douglas afternoon. The hospital refused comment and hospital spokeswoman Karen Waldbillig said the family asked that no information be released. Unidentified relatives of the former boxer had told the station Douglas was taken to the hospital Monday and had slipped in and out of a coma since then. They said Tuesday afternoon that his condition was improving. •It was not known whether Douglas previously had - been diagnosed as a diabetic or which form of the disease he might have. In some cases, if diabetes goes untreated, the victim may go into a diabetic coma, which can lead to death. BASKETBALL Top Rank fights over Wolves - Top Rank of Louisiana asked a federal judge in Minneapolis on Tuesday to › reconsider an earlier decision that stopped Louisiana court proceedings about the fight over ownership of the Minnesota Timberwolves. Top Rank, which last month agreed to buy the NBA team for $152.5 million and move the team to New Orleans only to see the league reject the deal, also asked U.S. District Court Judge James Rosenbaum to dismiss the case. The group contends that because the state of Louisiana contributed $1 million to the purchase, it is an "indispensable" part of the case. And because a constitutional amendment doesn't allow citizens from o one state to sue another state in U.S. court, Rosenbaum should throw out the federal lawsuit and let the Louisiana court handle it. HOCKEY • Sather puts hiring on hold - Glen Sather said Tuesday he's too busy to decide who will take over as the coach of the Edmonton Oilers and he doesn't expect to name his replacement until next month. Sather, the man credited with building the Oilers into the most powerful NHL team of the 1980s, added the role of coach to his duties as general manager and president last November when he fired Ted Green. There were reports he would name his replacement

Green joins Buccaneers - On Tuesday, the Tampa Bay Buccaneers signed former Detroit Lions receiver Willie Green to a one-year contract worth about $500,000. The 6-foot-2, 181-pound Green was released by the Lions last month to make room under the salary cap for Minnesota Vikings free-agent receiver Anthony Carter. An eighth-round draft pick out of Ole Miss in 1991, Green finished his college career as the school's second all-time leading receiver. The Bucs outbid the division-rival Chicago Bears for the services of Green, who would've been reunited with former Lions quarterback Erik Kramer. Falcon signees include Gagliano, Klein - The Atlanta Falcons signed their fourth-round draft pick and two veteran free agents Tuesday. The free agents are quarterback Bob Gagliano, 35, who has played eight NFL seasons and two in the USFL, and wide receiver Jason Phillips. Gagliano joined the Falcons last Oct. 7, but did not play in a game. Phillips, a five-year veteran, has played in 56 games with 49 catches for 578 yards and two touchdowns. He joined Atlanta in 1991 after two seasons in Detroit. The rookie is quarterback Perry Klein, a 6-2, 218- pounder who set NCAA Division II passing records with 38 touchdown passes and 3,757 yards at C.W. Post.

Russian Andrei Chesnokov breezed into the second round of the $313,750 Swedish Open in Bastad by beating Ola Kristiansson of Sweden 6-3, 4-6, 6-2. Police continue to investigate shooting Police have questioned a man who said he and a friend were target shooting near the site where professional golfer Kim Williams was shot. Police would not release the name of the Howland Township man who said he may have accidentally hit Williams. GOLF Police said the man told them he was firing his gun in woods near his home and one of the bullets from his 9mm semiautomatic pistol may have hit Williams. Howland Township Police Chief Steven Lamantia said it is possible the bullet that struck Williams was not fired from the man's gun. Williams was shot around 9 p.m. Saturday as she was walking into a discount store in Niles, Ohio, about 60 miles southeast of Cleveland. She was in town for the YoungstownWarren LPGA Classic. Williams, 31, of Boca Raton, Fla., left the hospital Monday. Canucks re-sign defenseman Lumme Defenseman Jyrki Lumme has signed a multiyear contract with the Vancouver Canucks, the team said. Lumme, 27, had 13 goals and 42 assists last season. He also accounted for 13 points - two goals and 11 assists - in the playoffs. HOCKEY The native of Finland has played for Vancouver for four seasons after being acquired from the Montreal Canadiens in March 1990. The bidding for Madison Square Garden and its two home sports teams, the Knicks and the Rangers, has been narrowed to five prospective buyers, according to published reports. The five are: Liberty Media Corp.; Nike Inc.; a partnership of Cablevision Systems Corp. and ITT Hartford Insurance; a partnership of The New York Times Co. and Delaware North Cos., a sports concessionaire whose holdings include the Boston Garden; and Laurence A. Tisch, chairman of CBS Inc.

overall, 6-0 in the TAAC West and winner of 11 straight, will play TAAC East champion Georgia State at 1 p.m. CST Saturday in Atlanta for the conference championship. It will be the third straight championship meeting between GSU and Centenary. GSU won 2-0 two years ago in Atlanta. Centenary won 3-1 last year in Shreveport. Centenary qualified for the conference final Monday by playing its best game of the season. The Gents attacked from all areas of the field and kept HardinSimmons off-balance. Led by sophomore striker' Curtis Partain's school-record four goals - three in the first half - the Gents took a 4-0 lead at intermission and led 6-0 after 60 minutes. Except for the last seven minutes of the first half and the last 20 minutes of the game - when the lead was unreachable -, Centenary rarely gave HardinSimmons players space to work the ball and build an attack. Hardin-Simmons (9-8 overall, 4-2 in TAAC West) had a 31-21 advantage in shots, but most were wild 20- 25-yard chances. "We knew they would come in here pumped, high," said Centenary Coach Glenn Evans. "What we wanted to do was knock the ball around and slow them down, and quickly counter against them. "We wanted to bury them early.' The Gents did that with four goals in the first 22 minutes. The first came at 9:00 when Greg Woodbridge, on the right side, passed to Jamin DePrang at the right post. DePrang then passed to Partain who headed the ball past HSU goalkeeper Alan Chandler. Partain got his second goal at 14:00 when he took a pass in midfield from Jason Phillips, dribbled into penalty box and kicked a low 16-yard shot inside the left post. Partain added No. 3 at 20:00 when he took a pass from DePrang, beat a fullback and kicked a shot past Chandler. Woodbridge made it 4-0 on a penalty kick at 22:30 that was set up when he was tripped by Chandler on a breakaway attempt inside the penalty box. Partain got his fourth goal at 52:00 when he got a loose ball, dribbled into the goal box and kicked in a shot. The four goals gave Partain a team-high 14 goals. "I never scored four in college," said Partain, a transfer from UCLA. "In high school, I had five in one game. Everytime they'd (Hardin-Simmons) try to pass to their outside fullbacks, there would be Woody (Woodbridge) and me breathing down their necks. They made a lot of bad back passes." DePrang, who did an excellent job of midfield marking and finding the open man on offense, scored his seventh goal of the season at 60:00 when he intercepted a Hardin-Simmons back pass and kicked in a low shot. Michael Henderson closed out Centenary's scoring at 79:00 with a shot off a pass from Steven Zeiller. CHARLOTTE - The expansion Carolina Panthers are not in any hurry to hire a coach. Having been turned down by Joe Gibbs, the Panthers decided to play the field. They start playing in 13 months. "What we're trying to do is sit back and observe right now," said Mark Richardson, director of business operations. "There are probably a dozen coaches that we'll keep a close eye on this year. We're watching them at all levels. There's some college coaches, there's some NFL coordinators and NFL head coaches that we're keeping track of." Trading cards show different Simpson ANAHEIM, Calif. - The latest O.J. Simpson trading cards show him running from the law, not on the football field. D & K Distributors sold thousands of the "In Pursuit of Justice" cards, which show police pursuing Simpson and arresting him. D & K plans to issue a set of 50 cards that will include a picture of the blood-splattered walkway where the slayings occurred, said Lowell Katz, a sports card dealer. "They weren't made in bad taste even though the whole subject is," Katz said. McMichael: tough goodbye to Chicago CHICAGO - Steve McMichael spent 14 years with the Chicago Bears, so it shouldn't have been a surprise when the defensive lineman had difficulty signing with the Green Bay Packers, Chicago's NFC Central rival. When McMichael started to sign the second page of his contract, the ink ran out. The pen had a Bears insignia on it. Atlanta signs pair of quarterbacks SUWANEE, Ga. The Atlanta Falcons placed linebacker John Rade and wide receiver Jason Phillips on the active roster and put defensive end Mike Gannon injured reserve. Rade, who has had five consecutive 100-tackle seasons, was on the physically unable to perform list because of knee surgery last January. He is in his ninth NFL season... Efforts continued toward resolving the confused ownership of the New England Patriots after Fran Murray rejected Victor Kiam's bid for a 30-day extension to buy out his minority partner. The deadline for Kiam, who owns 51 percent of the team, to make a $38 million payment to buy out Murray passed without resolution Thursday. Murray holds the remaining 49 percent of the franchise. Neither Murray nor Kiam was available for comment... The Cleveland Browns acquired defensive end Mike Wise, who had been waived by the Los Angeles Raiders. Wise was a fourth-round draft pick out of Cal-Davis by Los Angeles in 1986. He played in 13 games for the Raiders last season, starting two at defensive tackle.

HOCKEY N.Y. Rangers sign Healy From wire service reports Glenn Healy, the star of the New York IslandHers' surprising playoff success last spring, signed a contact with the New York Rangers on Tuesday. Healy's contract was worth a reported $3.5 million over four years. New York Rangers general manager Neil Smith said he expects Corey Hirsch, 21, the team's goalie of the future, to remain with Binghamton of the AHL this season. Healy took a circuitous route from Long Island to Manhattan. After leading the Islanders to the Wales Conference finals, Healy was left unprotected in the expansion draft and was selected by Anaheim. The next day, though, Tampa Bay took him in the second phase of the expansion draft. The Lightning traded Healy's rights to the Rangers for a third-round pick. Healy, 31, is entering his seventh NHL season. He played two seasons for Los Angeles before signing with the Islanders in 1989 as a free agent. Last year, Healy was 22-20-6 with a 3.30 goalsagainst average, then was sensational in the playoffs. He started all 18 games and posted a 3.19 average and an .887 save percentage. Dunham loses in net New Jersey Devils signee Mike Dunham was in goal for the U.S. national hockey team's 3-1 loss night to Sweden's Vastra Frolunda club at - Gothenburg, Sweden. Dunham, of Endwell, made 32 saves for the U.S., which is 5-3 on its European tour. The team's next action is in the Epson Cup on Thursday and Friday at Dusseldorf, Germany. The U.S. tied Vastra Frolunda, 1-1, midway through the second period when Jim Storm of Milford, Mich., picked up the rebound of David Sacco's slapshot and sent it past goalie Mikael Sandberg, Vastra Frolunda broke the tie late in the second period. Sandberg stopped 31 shots.

LOCAL SPORTS Rinkside... STEVEN MLINAREVIC scored twice as the Bruins trimmed the Penguins 3-2 to win the Dofasco Minor Hockey Association atom A championship Monday. Matthew Rogerson also scored for the winners. Corey Robertson and John Little replied Tor the Pens. In the B final, Shaun Lucas fired a hat trick as the Oilers doubled the North Stars 6-3. Jason Phillips added a pair for the winners while Perry Ryerson scored once. Joey A Majerovich replied with a pair for the Stars while Marco Majerovich added a single. 000 CARL MORAGNO scored four times as M.T.C. Electric drubbed Re/Max Realty 10-3 in Ancaster Men's Hockey League round-robin playoff action on the weekend. Tom Strauch and Ernie Taylor added a pair of goals each for the winners while singles went to Rob Fotheringham and Howle McCullough. Eldon Bull, Sandy Stewart and George Svarcopf answered for Re/Max. In other games, two goals each from Jeff Wallace and Ken Boyko paced Eastside Mario's to a 7-1 win over Wentworth Sports. Joe Cipolla, Mickey Mercanti and Cam McMillan chipped in with one apiece for the winners while John Galama netted the lone Wentworth Sports marker. Al Harrison and Bruce Earl scored as Robin's Donuts tied Crescent Oil 2-2. Len Dimitri and Dave Sosiak countered for Crescent. Mike Galama and Jerry Soldaat fired two goals each as Mickey Clair Drywall skated to a 5-5 tie with Soldaat's Poultry. Ted Troundou also scored for Mickey Clair while Randy Lyttle, with two, Wes Woof, Bruce Hatton and John Yanch answered for Soldaat's. 000 HAMILTON EASTWOOD Leathers came home from the Pepsi Invitational Minor Hockey Tournament on the weekend with the silver medals after losing 4-2 to Bowie, Md., in the novice division championship. Ryan Goddard scored both Leathers' goals. In preliminary action, Leathers crushed Guelph 11-0, tied Syracuse, N.Y., 3-3 and whipped West Seneca, N. Y., 10-1.. Vince Guducci led Leathers with four goals in the win over Guelph while Goddard added a pair. Singles went to P.J. Oliphant, Bobby O'Toole, Bobby Naylor, Freddie Swan and Mitch Trebilcock. The shutout was shared by Brandon Howard and Dwayne Smith. In the tie with Syracuse, Leathers' goals went to Swan, Oliphant and Goddard while Goddard (2), O'Toole (2), Swan (2). Graham Sutherland scored eight times and added two assists over the tourney while Ted Clement added four goals and six helpers. Mike Leone with four goals and three assists, Andy Love (1,2) and Mike Bester also scored for Mount Hamilton. SPORTS BRIEFS Rocker, Worley, Thomas honored by Kodak coaches Auburn defensive lineman Tracy Rocker, Georgia running back Tim Worley and Derrick Thomas of Alabama were named Monday to the 1988 Kodak Coaches All-America Team. Also named were Barry Sanders and Rodney Peete, leading candidates for this year's Heisman Trophy. Sanders is a running back from Oklahoma State; Peete is quarterback for Southern California. Also chosen were wide receivers Erik Affholter of Southern Cal and Jason Phillips of Houston; tight end Marv Cook of Iowa; offensive linemen Tony Mandarich of Michigan State, Anthony Phillips of Oklahoma, Mark Stepnoski of Pittsburgh, Mike Utley of Washington State and Steve Wisnewski of Penn State, and placekicker Kendall Trainor of Arkansas. Named to the defensive team were Mark Messner of Michigan, Broderick Thomas of Nebraska and Bill Hawkins of Miami; linebackers Britt Hager of Texas and Jerry Olsavsky of Pittsburgh; backs Deion Sanders of Florida State, Louis Oliver of Florida, Darryl Henley of UCLA and Donnell Woolford of Clemson, and punter Pat Thompson of Brigham Young. The selections were made by the American Football Coaches Association.

He compiled a 42-13-3 record with 14 career shutouts for Rochester John Marshall High School, and was a twotime member of The Associated Press all-state hockey team - first team as a junior, second team as a senior. He was drafted by the Los Angeles Kings in the seventh round (133rd player chosen overall) of the 1988 NHL Entry Draft. But when he arrived at St. Cloud State, promptly, was redshirted. I had lost my drive and desire to play," Kruesel said.

By MIKE KILLEEN Times Assistant Sports Editor Hockey quit being fun for Jeff Kruesel. So Kruesel, a redshirt sophomore goalie, has decided not to go out for the St. Cloud State hockey team this season. "I was frustrated," Kruesel said. "IfI was still enjoying it, I would have stuck with it. But the bottom line was I wasn't enjoying it. "The fact is, I'm going to make a living playing hockey. If I don't enjoy it, I'm not going to play." St. Cloud State coach Craig Dahl said, "I'm a little surprised by his decision.'

SCSU hockey

Dahl hopes to pair two junior league recruits together - center Brett Stewart and wing Fred Knipscheer. Stewart played for the Humboldt Broncos of the Saskatchewan Junior League last season, scoring 50 goals and 66 assists for 116 points. Knipscheer was the third-leading scorer in the United States Hockey League last season with the Omaha Lancers, andhe finished the season with 55 goals and 59 assists for 114 points. Other newcomers will be given a long look to pick up the productivity, including wings Brett Lievers (51 points at Wayzata High School last season), Tony Gruba (39 points, Hill-Murray) and Jeff Schmidt (60 points, Rochester Mayo). Several veterans also could figure into the picture, including senior Scott Eichstadt (5-8-13 in 26 games), senior Mike Notermann (4-5-9 in 15 games), sophomore Dan O'Shea (4-4-8 in 25 games), sophomore Dave ( Gingerich (4- 3-7 in 22 games), junior Doc DelCastillo (4-2-6 in 22 games) and sophomore Joe Larson (0-1-1 in five games). Junior Chic Pojar and sophomore Greg Hagen are transfers from Colorado College who are eligible to play the day after fall quarter ends in November. Both played for the St. Paul Vulcans of the USHL at end of last season while going to school at St. Cloud State, with Hagen scoring 26 points in 19 games and Pojar scoring 20 in 20 games. "Right now, there are a lot of options. It could take a month or 1½/ months to get it all settled out," Dahl said.

By MIKE KILLEEN Times Assistant Sports Editor The goal is to score more goals. That's the not-so-simple taskfacing the St. Cloud State hockey team, which opens practice Monday at National Hockey Center. While the Huskies prepare for their first season in the Western Collegiate Hockey Association - their first game is Oct. 12 against Minnesota at National Hockey Center - they also hope to improve an offense that was one-dimensional last season. The Huskies scored 161 goals in 38 games last season. If you plot that total against the eight WCHA teams, the Huskies ranked seventh in scoring - only Colorado College (152) and Michigan Tech (150) scored fewer goals. Worse yet, 68 of the 161 goals (42 percent) were scored by one line - center Jeff Saterdalen (24) and wings Tim Hanus (22) and Chris Scheid (22). "The area we need to improve upon is goal-scoring," St. Cloud State coach Craig Dahl said. "The players are aware of it, and they know we need to improve it.' But there's a flip side to all of this - the Huskies' defense. St. Cloud State allowed 152 goals last season. Again, if you plot that against WCHA schools from last season, that would have tied Minnesota-Duluth for fewest goals allowed. "If we can keep our shots-against and goals-against average down and pick upour offense, I think we could surprise a few people this season," Dahl said. Here's a position-by-position analysis of the Huskies:

Chesler would not disclose specifics, but said the women's hockey team is one of only 15 in the nation and "it's difficult to justify retaining sports that do not have a lot of support on a national level." Field hockey, played in this country almost exclusively by women, seems the most susceptible-to elimination. "The bottom line is that male athletic directors don't understand it," said Ohio State's Karen Weaver, past president of the national field hockey coaches assocation. Purdue, whose team was ranked in the top 20, and Southern Illinois dropped their field hockey teams in the late 1980s. Maryland-Baltimore County eliminated its team and replaced it with women's soccer. In 1988, San Jose State dropped field hockey. THE TYPICAL college football team carries 95 scholarship players. Because there is no comparable sport to offer to women, the overall result at most schools is that men outnumber women athletes by more than 2 to 1. "That means that for every woman getting a free education through sports, two men are," said Grant.
