SPORTS BRIEFS Rocker, Worley, Thomas honored by Kodak coaches Auburn defensive lineman Tracy Rocker, Georgia running back Tim Worley and Derrick Thomas of Alabama were named Monday to the 1988 Kodak Coaches All-America Team. Also named were Barry Sanders and Rodney Peete, leading candidates for this year's Heisman Trophy. Sanders is a running back from Oklahoma State; Peete is quarterback for Southern California. Also chosen were wide receivers Erik Affholter of Southern Cal and Jason Phillips of Houston; tight end Marv Cook of Iowa; offensive linemen Tony Mandarich of Michigan State, Anthony Phillips of Oklahoma, Mark Stepnoski of Pittsburgh, Mike Utley of Washington State and Steve Wisnewski of Penn State, and placekicker Kendall Trainor of Arkansas. Named to the defensive team were Mark Messner of Michigan, Broderick Thomas of Nebraska and Bill Hawkins of Miami; linebackers Britt Hager of Texas and Jerry Olsavsky of Pittsburgh; backs Deion Sanders of Florida State, Louis Oliver of Florida, Darryl Henley of UCLA and Donnell Woolford of Clemson, and punter Pat Thompson of Brigham Young. The selections were made by the American Football Coaches Association.

Elsewhere, the Moose Jaw Warriors traded veteran forward Darren Kwiatkowski, 19, to the Regina Pats in exchange for the rights to right winger Wayne Kope, 19, currently playing college hockey in Camrose, Alta. Kope will be put on Moose Jaw's list of college players.

The Jets, who have managed just one goal in over 100 minutes of hockey and failed to score on five power play opportunities in the game, remain third in the Smythe Division with a 4-4 record. Bobby Carpenter and Jim Fox scored third-period goals and goaltender Glenn Healy recorded his first NHL victory to lead the Kings over the Rangers at Madison Square Garden.

BRANDON -Outstanding and satisfying were the words general manager Al Patterson used Wednesday night to describe the Victoria Cougars' : 3-1 Western Hockey League win over the Wheat Kings. "It was an outstanding junior hockey game because it was scoreless for 2½ periods," Patterson said. Peter Fry had a spectacular game in goal for Victoria, stopping many difficult shots and winding up with 29 saves. George Maneluk was also impressive in the Brandon nets, making 33 saves.

The Canadian Press Coaches Butch Goring of the Spokane Chiefs and John Van Horlick of the New Westminster Bruins have been suspended by the Western Hockey League following a bench-clearing incident involving their teams on Oct. 24. The league announced in Calgary on Wednesday suspensions totalling 13 games and fines amounting to $2,500 over of the incident. Economic goals: City officials laud business benefits of new hockey franchise By KEVIN MILLS Sunday Staff Writer LEWISTON - A Quebec Major
Junior Hockey League team in Lewiston may mean great things f 0 r hockey

but its benefits are expected to go beyond the sport itself. "This is a plus for the whole city," said Mayor John Jenkins, who began the initial discussions about bringing a team to Lewiston. "Like any sports do for Portland or otherwise, it brings visitors to spend money in the restaurants and the hotels. It brings shoppers into our downtown. It give us a big shot in the arm. This is what we need for our economy. You've got to get people here to see what Lewiston has to offer." "We're real excited about this," said Steven Levesque, the city's development director, who has played a key role during recent ations. "This will have a wonderful effect. It's real important for the community to have this type of a class opportunity. It has a real strong community aspect. So it's really important for us." An agreement among the Lewiston City Council, officials of the Central Maine Civic Center and the Quebec Major Junior Hockey League sealing the deal is expected Monday. Long before defenceman Milan Dragicevic arrived in Regina to play in the Western Hockey League with the Pats, the praise heaped on him by scouts was enormous. The labels read like this: Can't miss National Hockey Leaguer. Sure high pick in 1988 NHL entry draft. That heady praise still may have some credence to it but Dragicevic knows he has to perform much better than he has so far this season - if he wants to be selected in the first three rounds of next year's draft. "He's a good-sized kid and he's strong but he has to play tougher. That's the way he has to play because he hasn't shown me he's an offensive player," said one pro scout. "He has to become more involved in his end and in front of the net with authority.' Said Dragicevic: "I'm off to a really slow start. It's a matter of time before I break out of my slump." And by 'slump', he said he won't be pre-occupied with being an offensive threat (he has five points so far; last season, he had 17). What he wants to do is tidy his own backyard first. "I'm going to have to stay home, play defence and be aggressive and not worry about points and going up and getting caught," he said. have to get more involved in the play. That's my biggest problem." Last Saturday in Medicine Hat, Dragicevic got "caught" with 6½ minutes left in the second period. He

severe groin injury incurred while he was playing for Team Pacific against a touring Soviet midget team put him on the 60-day injury list. "I wish I could've played 72 games instead of 41 games," said Dragicevic. "Thirty-0 games would've made a difference. I had all the confidence in the world going into that Russian series." Before the Yugoslavian native left for his stint with Team Pacific, the Pats had glowing words about his performance as a 16-year-old. Said assistant coach Dennis Sobchuk: "I marked him on his grades and he was above average and coming into his own before Christmas. He was showing some of his potential. But with the injury, he was coming back this year with only half a year of experience." Dragicevic said he was highly touted in part because he was the only 15-year-old defenceman playing in the Tier Two Alberta Junior Hockey League in 1985-86. With the Sherwood Park Crusaders, he recorded two goals and 18 assists. "That (high rating) doesn't put pressure on me," said Dragicevic. "The only pressure on me is the pressure I put on myself because I want to be the best that I possibly can be." Despite missing most of the second half of last season, Dragicevic played for Canada's under-17 team against the U.S. in a series of games last summer in Colorado Springs, Colo. "Making the team and playing with the best players in Canada was a great step for my future," he said. "I know what I'm up against in the draft."

LETHBRIDGE (CP) - The Lethbridge Hurricanes Friday announced a restructuring in their front office with the addition of Wayne Simpson as new general manager. Simpson, most recently with the New Westminster Bruins as director of player personnel, will take over a job which until now has been shared by two people. Team president Steve Arisman said Glen Hawker will continue his duties as head coach and relinquish his role as director of hockey operations, while Ray Austring will no longer be business manager but will continue to assist the club in various other areas.

"That's really done big things for us the last couple of games," said Saskatoon coach Marcel Comeau of his club's power play, which is operating at a 67-per-cent success rate in the last two games. "It's kind of a confidence thing," he said. "If you get rolling, you just seem to keep rolling. If you bog down, you tend to stay that way." It was the second successive game that the power play has helped master a Western Division foe. On Tuesday, Saskatoon scored six power-play goals on six chances en route to a 15-2 drubbing of the Thunderbirds. The Blades broke the game open during a five-minute power play in the second period. While Victoria's Gary Moscaluk was sitting out a major for high-sticking, Curtis Leschyshyn and Jack Bowkus scored to break a 2-2 tie. Tracey Katelnikoff and Mike Bukta also scored power-play goals in the second frame to make it 6-2 Saskatoon after 40 minutes. Dean Holoein added a first-period goal while Victoria was two men short and Katelnikoff contributed a thirdperiod power-play goal. Clayton Chappell and Darin Bader scored even-strength goals for Saskatoon. Jason Phillips, on a third-period penalty shot, Jackson Penney, Andrew Wolf, Greg Batters and Joe Johannson replied for Victoria. "How many power-play goals did they get?" asked Cougars' coach Wayne Naka. "Six? Then that's the difference. We're playing undisciplined, inconsistent hockey, and it's hurting us."

Simpson, brother of New York Islander coach Terry Simpson, was a scout for the Prince Albert Raiders before his stint with the New Westminster team. The community-owned Hurricanes are in their first season in the Western Hockey League. The restructuring allows Hawker to spend more time focusing on his coaching duties, Arisman said.

Kevin Clemens didn't want to leave t the Regina Pats last year but Western Hockey League president Ed Chynoweth said otherwise. When the Pats lured Doug Sauter away from his head coaching position with the Medicine Hat Tigers to become their new head coach and general manager in May, 1986, the Tigers complained. They felt tampering was involved because they claimed Sauter was still under contract to them. HONOLULU (AP) - Protection of the quarterback figures to be the key in the Aloha Bowl between Houston and Washington State, two pass-minded teams. Two of the nation's most potent offenses meet at Aloha Stadium Christmas Day. No. 18 Washington State and No. 14 Houston are ranked third and fourth, respectively, in the nation in total offense. "I believe it will be a shootout," said Washington State quarterback Timm Rosenbach, the nation's most efficient passer. "I don't think 30 or 40 points is out of the question for both teams." Houston, 9-2, is favored by four points over Washington State, 8-3. "The defense that's successful at rushing the quarterback and not giving the quarterback time is going to win," Houston coach Jack Pardee said. "The strength of our football team is our offensive line," Washington State coach Dennis Erickson said. "Houston is good up front. That matchup will be key to the game." Rosenbach, a junior, was rated first in the nation in passing efficiency this season. He completed 66 percent of his passes for 2,791 yards and 23 touchdowns, helping Washington State to only its fourth bowl appearance in the school's 93-year football history. Rosenbach led the Pac-10 Conference and was third in the nation in total offense, averaging 286 yards pera game. He finished seventh in the Heisman Trophy balloting. Steve Broussard rushed for 1,141 yards and Rich Swinton ran for 980 yards. "Our defense is going to be tested against Washington State," Pardee said. "When you've got the topranked quarterback in football and a rusher who gained over 1,000 yards in a season ... the tribute goes to the offensive line. "The key is getting past them, not giving Rosenbach time to throw," Pardee said. Sophomore quarterback Andre Ware leads Houston's run-and-shoot offense, which ranked first in the Southwest Conference and second in the nation in passing offense, averaging 377 yards per game. Ware, who started Houston's final seven games, passed for 2,501 yards and 25 touchdowns. "I'm in love with this offense," Ware said. "There's no one else in the country that runs the run-and-shoot the way we run it." The offense helped two Houston receivers compile impressive statistics this season. All-American Jason Phillips, who caught 108 passes for 1,444 yards and 15 touchdowns, led the nation in receiving for the second straight season. Phillips is only the fourth receiver in NCAA history to lead the nation

Dallas police sent the district attorney's office the paperwork on Tarpley about 10 a.m., and a formal charge of DWI, second offense, was filed immediately, Assistant District Attorney Norman Kinne said. Scott, Drexler honored NEW YORK Dennis Scott of the Orlando Magic was named rookie of the month Monday in the NBA while Clyde Drexler of the Portland Trail Blazers was selected player of the week. Scott averaged 19.4 points last month while setting a league record for most 3-pointers in a season by a rookie. He broke the record for 3-pointers, set last season by Dana Barros with his 95th. Drexler averaged 23 points, 6.5 rebounds and 6.3 assists while leading the Trail Blazers to a 4-0 record last week. Morris joins Cleveland CLEVELAND - Joe Morris, a two-time Pro Bowl running back with the New York Giants who did not play last season, was among 10 players who signed with the Cleveland Browns on Monday., Morris had three 1,000-yard seasons with the New York Giants and holds franchise records in total yards rushing (5,296) and touchdowns (48). He's also their career in career rushing attempts (1,318) and set a team record with 21 rushing touchdowns in 1985. Morris suffered a broken foot in New York's last preseason game in 1989, was placed on injured reserve and missed the entire season. He rehabilitated the injury but was placed on waivers in September 1990 and went unsigned for the entire season. Students mourn death MANCHESTER, N.H. Students stood silently and the flag at Central High School was lowered to half-staff Monday in honor of Kevin Charbonneau, the Manchester teen-ager killed in a hockey game during the weekend. "I think shock is the best description of this," principal William Burns said of students' reaction. "I mean no one is ready for anything like that. And I think realization doesn't take place for a while.' Charbonneau, 15, died Saturday night at Catholic Medical Center after blocking a shot with his chest during the Manchester Regional Youth Hockey Association Bantam Division championship game. Softball meetings set The Florence Recreational Division Softball organizational meeting for the adult leagues have been scheduled as follows: Women's City Softball League - 6:30 p.m. April 2; Co-Ed Softball League 7:30 p.m. April 2; Men's City Industrial Softball League - 6:30 p.m. April 4; Men's Industrial Softball League - 7:30 p.m. April 4. These meetings will be held at the Billy Jeffers Center in Timrod Park. Lady Vols welcomed home KNOXVILLE, Tenn. - Tennessee women's basketball coach Pat Summitt, in her Volunteer orange sweatsuit, strode to the podium Monday and looked up at the 1,500 cheering fans gathered to celebrate the Lady Vols' NCAA basketball title. "Can you believe it?" she said. Summitt has made no secret all year that she and her team wanted to atone for the end of the 1990 season, when they lost in the regional finals to Virginia, in overtime, to miss the Final Four held on their home floor in Knoxville.

'The other situation: Mississippi State, a big un- A derdog at home to No. 7 Georgia, scores with 3:15 to play. That. cuts Georgia's lead to 35-34. Mississippi State coach Rockey Felker, with everything to gain and nothing to lose, sends in ... his kicker. That makes it 35-35. This is what it's all about? Georgia ruined Felker's hope for a meaningless tie by scoring with 17 seconds to play for a 42-35 victory. Maybe both Ford and Felker figured their defenses would hold, that they'd get the ball back in time for a shot at a winning field goal. But Clemson didn't get the ball back until 32 seconds remained. And Mississippi State didn't get back until 17 - ticks were left. And both teams lost. Doesn't that say something? THE LEAGUE WITH THE BEST non-conference showing so far? The Pac-10, easily. The league is 15-1 against outside competition (Arizona lost to Oklahoma). Next is the SEC at 10-3, the ACC at 11-5; the Southwest and WAC at 10-7 and the Big Eight at 9-8 ... Think there will be some scoring in Saturday's West Virginia-Pittsburgh game at Pitt? WVU, 3-0, comes in as the national scoring leader at 54 points per game. Pitt, 2-0, is fifth nationally at 50.5 Wis: consin entered the season figuring it could tuneup or two before facing Miami this Saturday and Michigan next weekend. So Western Michigan and Northern Illinois are the first two games on the schedule. Now Wisconsin is 0-2, and obviously in quite a bit of trouble .. Houston wingback Jason Phillips, who caught an SWC record 99 passes last year (the fifth-highest total •4 in NCAA history), is on a pace to surpass that: Through two games, Phillips leads the nation with 26 catches (for 353 yards). Rutgers' Eric Young is sect. ond nationally with 22 receptions, followed by Phil-* lips' teammate, James Dixon, who has 19. .. Besides • West Virginia-Pitt, there are three other key games I this weekend: Oklahoma is at USC, Georgia is at South Carolina and UCLA is at Washington. All are: unbeaten and ranked in the Top 20. ... How refreshing to see Rutgers fans rally around their football team with that strong show of support at Giants Stadium for last Saturday's Vanderbilt game. The Rutgers athletic department is preparing individual thank notes for each of the 23,477.

Grillo says that in terms of his skating ability, he has never seen a player this size further along in his development at this age, and that includes Mario Lemieux. "He's got what we call phonebooth moves," said Grillo. "For a big guy, he's very good in small areas, very tricky." Kozlov's stock had fallen somewhat by the time the Sharks drafted him because of his reputation in Russia as a player given to periods of laziness. As recently as last month in the Moscow area, a National Hockey League scout repeated this view in tones that left little room for contradiction. Kozlov's numbers playing for Moscow Dynamo the last two seasons have not been sensational. Last season he tied for third on the team in scoring, with 16 goals and nine assists (in Russian hockey, only one assist is tallied per goal,

But Kozlov will be stepping into perhaps the best situation he could find anywhere. As a boy, his heroes were right wing Sergei Makarov and center Igor Larionov, two of the top players in the Soviet Union throughout the 1980s, and now he will be skating with them and talking to them every day. "He's a tall kid and he has good hands," said Larionov, who practiced with Kozlov when he came to San Jose in February for a brief visit. "I would say it will be easier for him to adjust to this hockey style in a short period of time when he has some old folks like us around him to make it easier." The Sharks have been high on Kozlov for a long time, so high that they pulled an elaborate draft-day deal last June. Knowing they could get Kozlov even if they traded away their second pick overall, they swapped that pick with Hartford in a package that included: the sixth pick overall, with which they chose Kozlov; a second-round pick with which they selected Czech defenseman Vlastimil Kroupa, an instant success last season; and future considerations, which ended up giving them the rights to Makarov, their leading scorer last season.

San Francisco Chronicle SAN JOSE - More than a year after they drafted him sixth overall in the 1993 draft, the Jose Sharks finally signed left wing Viktor Kozlov on Tuesday, agreeing on four-year that pay the young Russian forward $3.45 million. Kozlov, 19, has spent the summer at Sharks director of scouting Chuck Grillo's hockey camp in Nisswa, Minn., working with Coach Kevin Constantine and others in the organization. He will come to Sharks training camp next month in Minnesota with an excellent chance at making the team. "I think he'll play in San Jose this season, there's no doubt in my mind," said Grillo. "But he still has to go to training camp.' 'The Sharks like their young European prospects to spend some time playing minor-league hockey to help them adjust to life in North America. But they are so excited about Kozlov they probably will not be able to resist plugging him into the lineup right away. At 6-foot-5, 225 pounds, Kozlov has enough size to become a dangerous player in front of the net. For so large a player, he skates well and handles the puck adeptly.

SUWANEE, Ga. The Atlanta Falcons signed their fourth-round draft pick and two veteran free agents. The veterans are quarterback Bob Gagliano, 35, who has played eight NFL seasons and two in the USFL, and wide receiver Jason Phillips. Gagliano joined the Falcons last Oct. 7, but did not play in a game. Phillips, a five-year veteran, has played in 56 games with 49 catches for 578 yards and two touchdowns. He joined Atlanta in 1991 after two seasons in Detroit. The rookie is quarterback Perry Klein, a 6-2, 218- pounder who set NCAA Division I passing records with 38 touchdown passes and 3,757 yards at C.W. Post.

deer hunter killed One hunter was killed and five others were hurt on the first day of gun season for deer in Ohio, police said Tuesday. Donald McClain, 18, of Lucasville, was killed Monday in Pike County when he fell and his 12-gauge shotgun discharged, the sheriff's department said. Two hunters were killed and 14 were hurt in 1987's six-day deer gun season, the Ohio Division of Wildlife said. This year's gun season ends Saturday at sunset. Race car driver Gordon Johncock has been cited for allegedly poaching a deer on land he owns near Hastings, Mich., a state Department of Natural Resources officer said. Johncock, cited for possession of an untagged deer and for hunting with a .270-caliber rifle in a shotgunonly zone, has until today to answer the charges or face arrest, according to the DNR. Boxing: Solid gold sentiment Employees at the Pensacola, Fla., Naval Air Station gave a solid-gold medal Tuesday to boxer Roy Jones Jr. to replace the one they believe he was cheated out of at the Summer Olympics. Co-workers of the boxer's father, Roy Jones Sr., at' the base's Naval Aviation Depot contributed $650 to buy nearly 3 ounces of 14-karat gold that went into the medal inscribed "A Solid Gold Medal for a Solid Gold Champion, Roy Jones." Baseball: Gedman stays with Red Sox The Boston Red Sox resolved one of their free agent problems Tuesday by agreeing with Rich Gedman on a one-year contract that reportedly makes him the highest paid catcher in the American League. Gedman accepted a contract that reportedly will pay him slightly more than $1.1 million. Lance Parrish; at $1 million, has been the highest paid American League catcher. Colleges: Sports come first College football and basketball players spend more time on their sports than on academics, according to a study released Tuesday. The survey was commissioned by the NCAA Presidents Commission. Football and basketball players spend an average of 30 hours a week on their sports even though most do not expect to make the professional ranks. And nearly all believe that earning a college degree was "of greatest importance" or "important" when they began their studies. Basketball: No clearance for Bedford William Bedford, the Detroit Pistons' 7-foot-1-inch, center, had been expected to get clearance Monday to rejoin the Detroit Pistons, but drug c counselors at the Adult Substance Abuse Program in Van Nuys, Calif., did not give their approval. The counselors said that Bedford, who was suspended without pay last March after he sought help for cocaine addiction, lacked the proper mental attitude to return. Locally: Squirt hockey team wins The Dayton Squirt All-Stars (10-11-year olds) won the Team Michigan Invitational Thanksgiving Hockey Tournament at Detroit last weekend by winning four games: 8-0 over Livonia, Mich.; 6-2 over Burlington, Ont.; 4-1 over Hamburg, N.Y., and 6-0 over Mississauga, Ont., in the championship game. Jeff Hamilton of Englewood was named the tournament MVP as he scored 12 goals. Matt Neff of Springboro starred in goal for Dayton.

"We've had quite a few performances like that," shrugged Van Horlick, the Bruins' rookie coach. "I thought he was bad for both teams. It was obvious he was going to play a part in the game." New Westminster dropped a 4-3 decision last night after beating the Cougars 6-4 in Victoria on Saturday. The split kept the Bruins seven points behind second-place Victoria in the Western Division. The margin might be smaller today had Loraas not fingered Bruin Greg Spenrath for roughing just as teammate Mike Hawes drilled home a power play goal at 11:15 of the third period, tying the game 3-3. Two minutes later, Victoria's Jason Phillips scored shorthanded to increase the Cougar margin to 4-2 and a late Bruin rally couldn't make yp the lost ground. "Spenrath and their player (Gary Moscaluk) came together, Spenrath pushed away and he called it," said Van Horlick. "'It was very untimely." the The net as puck was Loraas already blew his on its whistle way to cancel the goal.

Although he has 2,512 committed season tickets holders for his rink under construction in Kennewick, Wash., New Westminster Bruins' owner Ron Dixon still maintains he has not decided to move his Western Hockey League team. "One thing I've learned in business is never make a decision until you have to," Dixon said Sunday night. Fall physical examinations for Central York School District will be held Aug. 10 & 12 at Central High School. Those who should report Aug. 10 are those athletes in cross country and golf (7-7:30 a.m.), field hockey (7:30-8:15), girls' volleyball (8:15-9) and varsity/jayvee football (9-10). Those reporting Aug. 12 include: frosh football (3-3:45 p.m.), soccer (3:45-4:15) and cheerleading (4:15-5). Volleyball camp The York Volleyball Camp has openings in the Youth Evening Volleyball camp, which is held at York College under the direction of Bruce and Barb Koller. The youth camp will run from 6:30-9:30 p.m. Aug. 2-5 and is designed for boys and girls in grades 4-9. It is geared towards introducing the players to proper techniques and understanding the game. Fee is $65 per player and includes a t-shirt and snacks. For more information, call Barb Koller at 757-2547.

Physicals The West York Area School District will have physical examinations for its fall sports at 12:30 p.m. both Aug. 5 and 12. The examinations will be administered by school district physican at the senior high nurse's office. All participants in football (grades 7-12), golf, soccer and cross country (grades 9-12) should report Aug. 5, while those participating in girls* field hockey, girls* tennis, girls' volleyball (grades 9-12) and football cheerleaders (grades 7-12) should attend the Aug. 12 examination.

I GOLF TYSKA RETAINS LEAD: Jason Tyska of Connellsville shot a 2-over-par 72 to retain a two-shot lead after the second round of the 80th Pennsylania Men's State Amateur Championship in Lancaster. Combined with the 5- under 65 that he shot in the first round, Tyska's total was 3-under 137. Mechanicsburg's Chip Richter, who shot a 71, for a combined 139. TIGER WOODS ADVANCES: Two-time defending champion Tiger Woods of Cypress, Calif., advanced to the third round of the U.S. Junior Championships. Woods, 17, beat Ryan Lavoie of Pasco, Wash., 6 and 5 and Chad Wright of Ventura, Calif., 4 and 2. BOWLING DICKINSON WINS SHOWBOAT: Gary Dickinson won a semifinal match over Jim Long by one pin and then beat Gene Stus 246-203 to win the $150,000 PBA Showboat Senior Invitational Tournament. Long left a 4-7-10 split in the 10th frame of the semifinal match to allow Dickinson to pull out a 212-211 victory. Dickinson, a PBA and ABC Hall of Fame member, then beat top-seeded Stus for his first senior victory and earn $24,000. AULBY TOPS WICHITA OPEN: Mike Aulby averaged 262 over six games to take a commanding lead over Park Bohn Ill in the $140,000 Wichita Open. Aulby, an Indianapolis native who has won 21 PBA tournaments, has an 18-game pinfall of 4,358 and a 137-pin lead over Bohn of Freehold, NJ., who has nine PBA titles to his credit. FOOTBALL PENN STATE GETS OSTROSKY: Doug Ostrosky, a Fox Chapel High tight end and the son of a Pitt booster, is the second Pennsylvania high school football player to make an early commitment to Penn State. Earlier, all-state wide receiver. Joe Nastasi of Northern Bedford committed to the Nittany Lions. The 6-foot-7, 240-pound Ostrosky is rated by some scouting services as one of the nation's top five prep tight ends. I TENNIS STEVEN UPSETS SAMPRAS: Brett Steven of New Zealand, ranked 45th in the world, beat Wimbledon champion and world No. 1 Pete Sampras 7-6 (8-6), 6-3 in the third round of the Canadian Open. Mikael Pernfors of Sweden shocked second-ranked Jim Courier 6-3, 6-2, 13th-seeded Todd Martin upset fourth-ranked Boris Becker of Germany 7-5, 7-6 (7-3) and 11th-seeded Andre Agassi beat fifth-seeded Michael Chang 7-6 (7-3), 6-3. NASCAR SPEED TAKES ALLISON'S SPOT: Lake Speed will take over for the late Davey Allison at Watkins Glen on Aug. 8 and likely complete the rest of the NASCAR season in the No. 28 Ford Thunderbird, team owner Robert Yates said. Speed started 11 races this season in his own Thunderbird, but his sponsorship ran out after last week's race at Talladega Superspeedway, Allison died July 14 after sustaining head injuries in a helicopter crash at Talledega. I ICE HOCKEY SAVARD GOING TO TAMPA?: Denis Savard is reportedly leaving the Stanley Cup champion Montreal Canadiens to join the Tampa Bay Lightning. Transactions Calendar

RHS Wins First NORTHFIELD Gene Campbell and Darren Coloutti scored to lift Rutland to a 2-1 victory over Northfield in high school hockey on Wednesday night, giving the Raiders their first victory of the season. John Chamberlain scored for Northfield to knot the score at 1-1, setting the stage 1 for Coloutti and his game-winner. Mark Guenther and Matt Daley were credited with the assists. Rutland, 1-4, now plays in the Rutland Holiday Hockey Tournament on Friday and Saturday. "We played very well. We outshot them by a pretty wide margin," said Rutland coach Glenn Olson. "We missed a lot of shots, we're still not finishing. "But we had a lot of kids out sick and we had a lot of kids playing sick. It was a good gut check for us."

Devils Still Perfect GRANVILLE, N.Y. The Poultney Blue Devils remained undefeated (5-0) as they beat Salem, N.Y. in the first round of the boys Granville Legion Christmas Tournament Wednesday night. They will play Granville in the finals tonight. Jason Phillips led Poultney with 31 points and 13 rebounds while Dan Foley had 18 points and 13 rebounds. John Handley also chipped in with 10. Salem got 23 from Justin Brown.

Bratt Ices U-32 BRATTLEBORO - Brattleboro scored seven second-period goals en route to an 11-2 victory over U-32 in boys' hockey on Wednesday night. Bryan Bassler and Jesse BelcherTimme had three goals apiece for the 2-2 Colonels.

Rostagno upsets Becker in 3rd round of Seiko Super Boris Becker's troubles continued Friday, when he was upset in the third round of the $1 million Seiko Super Tennis Tournament in Tokyo. The second-seeded Becker was upset by No. 7 Derrick Rostagno of the United States 7-6 (7-4), 4-6, 6-3. Fourth-seeded Andre Agassi also lost, beaten by sixth-seeded Goran Ivanisevic of Yugoslavia 6-3, 6-4. Top-seeded Stefan Edberg of Sweden beat American Michael Chang, the eighth seed, 6-2, 6-2, and third-seeded Ivan Lendl defeated the fifth -seed, American David Wheaton 7-6 (7-5), 7-5. Rinkside... DAVID HOLMES fired a hat trick as Lawfield Joe Hagel Sports doubled Eastwood Galing Construction 6-3 in Hamilton Minor Hockey Council Hub League midget action last night. Rob Whitmore, Jim Schofield and lan Kraynick also scored for the winners. In novice action, two goals each by Alexander Moffatt and Andy Secore lifted Coronation Ultramar Homenergy Bengals to a 5-1 win over Eastwood Ken Boyter Auto Sales. Ryan Preston followed with a single for the winners while Tony DePasquale answered for Eastwood. Meanwhile, Rhian Dodds scored a pair of goals as Eastwood Eaton Yale shaded Lawfield Danny Parr 5-4. J.R. Smith and Jeff Aitken also scored for the winners. In atom action, Lawfield McDonald's (Harvard Square) tied Eastwood Stanley Pack 1-1. Arden Hammell scored for Lawfield while Chris Short countered for Eastwood. .000 VINCE GUIDUCCI, Frankie Curto and Justin Park scored as Hamilton Stoney Creek Furniture shaded Milton 3-2 last night to take a 2-0 lead in a best-of-five Ontario Minor Hockey Association Provincial Playdowns major atom AA third-round series. In peewee B action on the weekend, Orangeville clipped Stoney Creek Optimists 5-2 to even the best-offive third-round serie at 2-2. Peter Walker and Amedeo DiGiammatteo scor for Stoney Creek. • Earlier, Stoney Creek beat Orangeville 7-6 in overtime. Steven Knighton scored for goals for the winners while singles went to Brandon Lipsitt, Justin Taravski and Walker. In bantam B action on the weekend, Flamboro, Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce scored a pair of wins - 6-2 Sunday and 7-4 Saturday - to take a 2-1 lead in the best-of-five third-round series. Chris Brenn scored three for Flamboro on Sunday while singles were scored by Jason Bird. Judd Belbeck and Ryan Conners: Robbie Green and Dave Dunham answered for Dunnville. On Saturday, Eric Hopkins, Nolan Venne, Ryan Jarmuth, Bird, Brenn and Belbeck scored for Flamboro while Warren Franklin, Randy MacDonald, Nathan Gordon and Green replied for Dunnville. Meanwhile, in novice A third-round play, Grimsby Davidson Staioners got four goals in the third period in a 7-1 win over Welland on the weekend. Kevin Bieska and Adam Masters fired two goals apiece for the winners while singles went to Matt Hodges, Jason Harshaw and James Romyn. Grimsby leads the best-of-five set 2- 0. 000 JUSTIN CANNON and Luke Mikalauskas scored three goals each as Bruins whipped 8-1 in Dofasco Minor Hockey Association peewee action last night. Mike Shields added a pair for the winners while Greg Perkins countered for Oilers. another game, Scott Serroul scored twice as Red Wings dumped Kings 7-4. Shaun Lucas, Jason Steeves, John Little, Corey Robertson and Jason Phillips also scored for the winners while Jason Phillips, Joey Majerovich, Paul Buch and George' Anastos replied for Kings. In novice action, David McCallum fired a hat trick while Erik Gamble added pair of goals as Kings downed Bruins 8-5. David Tivador, Brent Popovich and Jesse Wilmot also scored for the winners while Aaron Adkins, with two goals, Jeffrey Silva, Ash Gureckas and Gary Christian countered for Bruins. In other games, Michael Le scored two goals as

deen Sunoco Mighty House Ducks 10-0 in Eastwood-Coronation Interlocking League tyke action on the weekend. Derek Prodeus added a pair for the winners while Dustin Goddard, Gilles Bonds and Ryan Randazzo chipped in with singles. Devan Prince-Reid earned the shutout. In novice play, Coronation Sports Shop tied Coronation Super Mario Tile 4-4. Jason Phillips caused so much bother that he was twice blatantly taken out by Maesglas players and both Colin James and Andrew Thomas were booked by referee Howard Davies. Maesglas' best chances of the first fell to Ian Evans, but his effort was fingertipped onto the bar by home keeper Adrian Davies, while a Laurence Rees shot was also blocked by Davies. The visitors defence was far better organised in the second period and although full backs Dylan and Wyn Evans added width to the Llandysul attacks

LLANDYSUL should have had this league match wrapped up in the first half as they squandered chance after chance on Saturday. Only some poor finishing from the home strikers and some great goalkeeping from Andrew Thomas kept Llandysul to two goals at the interval when they should have been out of sight. In the end Maesglas could have snatched a draw as they missed a couple of chances in the last 10 minutes game, but that would have been rough justice on Llandysul. The kick off for the match was delayed with Maesglas 1 late turning up for the game and this was to prove costly. Llandysul had been warming up well before time and they caught the visitors cold from the start. Within a minute Jason Phillips found himself with space and he beat two Maesglas defenders before shooting past the helpless Thomas. The Cardigan based side were kept under intense pressure and 10 minutes later, Llandysul were awarded a corner. John Evans found full back Wyn Evans an and *his header was cleared off the line by Stuart Thomas for another corner which was a carbon copy of the first, but this time Evans made no mistake with powerful header. Questions must be asked in the Maesglas defence why Evans was allowed to have two free headers. Sporting summary HOCKEY.
• Cardigan rugby player Sharon Gibby from Clunderwen who will represent Wales at rugby on Sunday having already gained international honours in hockey. Picture: Huw R.D. Thomas

FEW people can boast to have represented Wales in two sports, but that's exactly what Sharon Gibby will be doing at Neath on Sunday when she wins her first Welsh ladies rugby cap at the Gnoll (kick off 3.00p.m.) Prior to playing rugby Sharon, 22, was very successful hockey player and has represented her country on 20 occasions in this sport at under 18, under 21 and senior level. Although she still plays hockey now and again for Kilgetty, former Ysgol y Preseli pupil Sharon now devotes most of her time to playing rugby for Cardigan. A very strong tackling player she has played most of her club rugby at full back, but it will be in the right wing position that she will make her Welsh debut. A natural footballer with a particularly powerful kick, she has been drafted into the side to try and combat the electrifying pace of England on the wings. She will be marking Richmond flyer Debbie Francis a former junior sprint champion, but Sharon is no slouch' and can make a real name for herself. Although she is winning her first cap, Sharon will be the No.1 goal-kicker, while Wales have also some handy moves up their sleeves involving Gibby. She could well have been capped last year but missed out on the trials owing to her work commitments as a policewoman with the Ministry of Defence in Milford. Inside her will •be former

By The Associated Press Heisman Trophy winner Barry Sanders of Oklahoma State, Outland Trophy winner Tracy Rocker of Auburn and Butkus Award recipient Derrick Thomas of Alabama were named today to a recordbreaking Associated Press All-America college football team for 1988. Sanders, Oklahoma State's junior tailback, is joined in the backfield by quarterback Steve Walsh of Miami, Fla., and Texas A & M running back Darren Lewis, both of whom shattered records this season. The wide receivers are two more record-breakers - Jason Phillips of Houston and Hart Lee Dykes, Sanders' Oklahoma State teammate. Top-ranked Notre Dame placed three players on the All-America team - offensive tackle Andy Heck, defensive end Frank Stams and linebacker Mike Stonebreaker. Oklahoma State, Nebraska and Arkansas were the only other schools to be represented by more than one player. Nebraska placed center Jake Young and defensive end Broderick Thomas on the squad while Arkansas had placekicker Kendall Trainor and defensive linemen Wayne Martin.

Games: St. Cloud State at Denver University, 8:35 p.m. today and Saturday at DU Arena, Denver. St. Cloud State at Colorado College, 8:35 p.m. Monday at Broadmoor World Arena, Colorado Springs. Radio: WJON (1240 AM) will broadcast all three games. Airtime each night is 8:05 p.m. with Greg Harrington. Series: Denver leads the series with St. Cloud State, 1-0. The Pioneers beat the Huskies 8-4 Nov. 29 at Municipal Ice Arena. This is the first meeting between the Huskies and the Tigers. Records: St. Cloud State is 14-10- overall. The Huskies swept Michigan-Dearborn last weekend, 7-4 and 11-2. Denver is 11-15-1 in the Western Collegiate Hockey Association and 12- 15-1 overall. The Pioneers beat Lowell 5-3 Saturday to end an eight-game losing streak. Colorado College 6-16-3 in the WCHA and 8-16-3 overall. The Tigers play Michigan Tech and Saturday before facing St. Cloud State. Leading scorers St. Cloud State

Hockey St. Cloud (Minn.) Times / Fri., Jan. 20, 1989

One key for the Pioneers will be goaltending. Both Chris Gillies and Lucien Carignan have been inconsistent this season. About Colorado College: Things are looking up in Colorado Springs, where the Tigers have doubled their wins from last season under first-year coach Brad Buetow. Colorado College is 3-0-1 in its last four games, all on the East Coast. "I'm real proud of the guys," said Buetow, who coached at Minnesota from 1979-85 and U.S. International from 1985-88, when that program was disbanded. "The players have played hard all season long. We're just not playing good right now; we're playing with a lot of confidence," Buetow said. The key to turnaround has been improved goaltending. Derek Pizzey began the season as the No. 1 goalie, but Jon Gustafson holds the hot hand. He has played the last three games, all wins. "Jon will start against Michigan Tech, but we'd like to get Derek in there. He's had a rough year," Buetow said. The offense has been led by the line of senior left wing Tim Budy, freshman right wing Steve Strunk and freshman center Grant Block (4-8-12). Budy, who hasplayed in 147 consecutive games for the Tigers, has eight power-play and three short-handed goals this season. Strunk, from Wausau, Wis., has nine power-play goals. He was recruited to Colorado College by former Tigers' coach Mike Bertsch. "I have to thank Mike for that one," said Buetow, who brought four U.S. International players with him to Colorado. One interesting statistic: Colorado College is 7-7-2 on the road, but 1-9-1 at home. Rapids victory bie Grendahl) 12:02; 6. W - Jim Harrison (Mike Lukkari, Tim Elay) 12:40;7. Police continue to investigate shooting Police have questioned a man who said he and a friend were target shooting near the site where professional golfer Kim Williams was shot. Police would not release the name of the Howland Township man who said he may have accidentally hit Williams. GOLF Police said the man told them he was firing his gun in woods near his home and one of the bullets from his 9mm semiautomatic pistol may have hit Williams. Howland Township Police C Chief Steven Lamantia said it is possible the bullet that struck Williams was not fired from the man's gun. Williams was shot around 9 p.m. Saturday as she was walking into a discount store in Niles, Ohio, about 60 miles southeast of Cleveland. She was in town for the YoungstownWarren LPGA Classic. Williams, 31, of Boca Raton, Fla., left the hospital Monday. Canucks re-sign defenseman Lumme Defenseman Jyrki Lumme has signed a multiyear contract with the Vancouver Canucks, the team said. Lumme, 27, had 13 goals and 42 assists last season. He also accounted for 13 points - two goals and 11 assists - in the playoffs. HOCKEY The native of Finland has played for Vancouver for four seasons after being acquired from the Montreal Canadiens in March 1990. Falcons sign draft pick, veterans The Atlanta Falcons signed their fourth-round draft pick and two veteran free agents. The free agents are quarterback Bob Gagliano, 35, who has played eight NFL seasons and two in the USFL, and wide receiver Jason Phillips. MISC. The rookie is quarterback Perry Klein, a 6-foot-2, 218-pounder who set NCAA Division II passing records with 38 touchdown passes and 3,757 yards at C.W. Post. The bidding for Madison Square Garden and its two home sports teams, the Knicks and the Rangers, has been narrowed to five prospective buyers, according to published reports. The five are: Liberty Media Corp.; Nike Inc.; a partnership of Cablevision Systems Corp. and ITT Hartford Insurance; a partnership of The New York Times Co. and Delaware North Cos., a sports concessionaire whose holdings include the Boston Garden; and Laurence A. Tisch, chairman of CBS Inc.

Canucks re-sign defenseman Lumme Defenseman Jyrki Lumme has signed a multiyear contract with the Vancouver Canucks, the team said. Lumme, 27, had 13 goals and 42 assists last season. He also accounted for 13 points - two goals and 11 assists - in the playoffs. HOCKEY The native of Finland has played for Vancouver for four seasons after being acquired from the Montreal Canadiens in March 1990.

WEDNESDAY, August 24, 1994 The Sun /C3 Jets give the boot to Cup star Meola Maybe Tony Meola should have stuck with soccer after all. The U.S. goalkeeper who played well during the recent World Cup was waived by the New York Jets Tuesday. Only one of Meola's five preseason kickoffs reached the end zone, but coach Pete Carroll said the team hopes to keep him around. In other news from NFL camps Tuesday: PITTSBURGH STEELERS: The team severed its ties with troubled guard Carlton Haselrig, and put holdout kicker Gary Anderson in position to play only if he requests reinstatement from the commissioner's office. • CLEVELAND BROWNS: The Browns released eight players, including offensive lineman Mike Withycombe. I ARIZONA CARDINALS: Arizona signed former Denver quarterback Shawn Moore and also released four players. NEW ENGLAND PATRIOTS: New England released nose tackle Ervin Collier, a third-round draft pick, and free agent linebacker Mike Kerr. PHILADELPHIA EAGLES: Philadelphia waived 15 players, including Preston Jones, last year's third-string quarterback. DALLAS COWBOYS: Dallas released eight players, including running back Judd Garrett and offensive lineman Caesar Rentie. CINCINNATI BENGALS: Cincinnati got to the roster limit by placing offensive tackle Joe Walter on injured reserve and waiving three players. BUFFALO BILLS: Buffalo waived offensive lineman Moe Elewonibi and wide receivers Kevin Knox and Damon Thomas. ATLANTA FALCONS: Atlanta released four players, including six-year wide receiver Jason Phillips. - From Sun News Services Raiders cut Bell;

Bills Buffalo waived offensive lineman Moe Elewonibi, whom they wanted as insurance for their offensive line. The Bills also waived two wide receivers - Kevin Knox, a sixthround draft choice from Florida State, and Damon Thomas, a freeagent rookie from Wayne State. The Bills signed Elewonibi, an Outland Trophy winner in college, this month after right tackle Jerry Crafts struggled in his attempt to replace Howard Ballard, who left as a free agent. But Elewonibi, released by the Redskins, developed a back problem soon after signing and r never appeared in an exhibition game for the Bills. Falcons Atlanta released four players, including six-year wide receiver Jason Phillips. Also cut were free-agent tackle John Heidenreich; linebacker Mitch Davis, a fourth-round draft choice from Georgia; and rookie free-agent wide receiver Corey Dixon of Nebraska.

Frank Romeo Hardee HEARTLAND ATHLETE OF THE WEEK

short pass over the middle that Romeo reached and grabbed with his left hand. Tucking the ball away, he started running for the DeSoto goal line. Daniels caught up with Romeo and forced him out of bounds 44 yards upfield from where Romeo had caught the ball. Romeo had a chance to cut back inside, but decided to plow through the DeSoto running back. "I knew I couldn't out run Chris," he said. "So I decided to go right through him. I like to give back to people what they give to me." The interception return set up the Wildcats' first touchdown, a sweep by Reggie Brown. Blocking for Brown on that play was Frank Romeo. Three more times DeSoto would penetrate deep into Hardee territory, and three more times they would be turned away by Romeo and company. Daniels, who had rushed for more than 1,000 yards just eight games into the season, was held to 73 yards all night. Romeo is the signal-caller on a defense that has held opponents to an average of 6.3 points per game. He and Robert Rawls make sure the adrenalin is flowing in every defender. "We try to keep them fired up," said Romeo about his and Rawls' role on defense. "We let someone know if they made a good play, and if they make a mistake, we tell them to forget it and get them back next play." With a a good combination of speed, size, desire and discipline, the 6-foot-2, 225-pound senior has been sought by many colleges. Florida State, Florida, Louisville and Miami have all contacted him through letters.

WAUCHULA Frank Romeo had been waiting for Hardee's game with DeSoto for a long time. Romeo, the Wildcats' linebacker-tight end, had to sit out last year's game with an injury. He had to watch from the sidelines as DeSoto's Chris Daniels rushed for 219 yards in the Bulldogs' 21- 17 win. The Wildcats' loss spoiled their bid for a perfect season. This year, with Hardee coming into the DeSoto game 9-0 once again, Romeo was not about to let history repeat itself. "I got fired up," he said. "We had a good pep rally and everything. I was waiting for this game all season." Romeo responded with eight solo tackles, three assists and one interception and blocked well on offense in the Wildcats' 20-0 win over their traditional rivals. Hardee decided to keep the ball on the ground, so Romeo was not a factor as a receiver. "Frank played his best defensive game Friday night," Hardee coach Bob Martin said. "And he's had some good defensive games in the past. He also blocked extremely well on offense." His interception will be talked about for a long time when the 1986 version of the rivarly is discussed. The Bulldogs had driven down to the Hardee 14, but two losses made it third-and-long from the Wildcat 26. DeSoto quarterback Tommy Henry threw a

short pass over the middle that Romeo reached and grabbed with his left hand. Tucking the ball away, he started running for the DeSoto goal line. Daniels caught up with Romeo and forced him out of bounds 44 yards upfield from where Romeo had caught the ball. Romeo had a chance to cut back inside, but decided to plow through the DeSoto running back. "I knew I couldn't out run Chris," he said. "So I decided to go right through him. I like to give back to people what they give to me." The interception return set up the Wildcats' first touchdown, a sweep by Reggie Brown. Blocking for Brown on that play was Frank Romeo. Three more times DeSoto would penetrate deep into Hardee territory, and three more times they would be turned away by Romeo and company. Daniels, who had rushed for more than 1,000 yards just eight games into the season, was held to 73 yards all night. Romeo® is the signal-caller on a defense that has held opponents to an average of 6.3 points per game. He and Robert Rawls make sure the adrenalin is flowing in every defender. "We try to keep them fired up," said Romeo about his and Rawls' role on defense. "We let someone know if they made a good play, and if they make a mistake, we tell them to forget it and get them back next play." With a a good combination of speed, size, desire and discipline, the 6-foot-2, 225-pound senior has been sought by many colleges. Florida State, Florida, Louisville and Miami have all contacted him through letters.

WAUCHULA - Frank Romeo had been waiting for Hardee's game with DeSoto for a long time. Romeo, the Wildcats' linebacker-tight end, had to sit out last year's game with an injury. He had to watch from the sidelines as DeSoto's Chris Daniels rushed for 219 yards in the Bulldogs' 21- 17 win. The Wildcats' loss spoiled their bid for a perfect season. This year, with Hardee coming into the DeSoto game 9-0 once again, Romeo was not about to let history repeat itself. "I got fired up," he said. "We had a good pep. rally and everything. I was waiting for this game all season." Romeo responded with eight solo tackles, three assists and one interception and blocked well on offense in the Wildcats' 20-0 win over their traditional rivals. Hardee decided to keep the ball on the ground, so Romeo was not a factor as a receiver. "Frank played his best defensive game Friday night," Hardee coach Bob Martin said. "And he's had some good d defensive games in the past. He also blocked extremely well on offense." His interception will be talked about for a long time when the 1986 version of the rivarly is discussed. St. John defeats Keyport ROUNDUP Press Staff Report AUDREY GOMEZ and Christa Ciriani each scored 16 points to lead St. John Vianney to a 60-46 nonconference victory over host Keyport yesterday. The win made the Lancers 19-0 on the year. Gomez' 16 points included a six-forseven day at the line. Sarah Coleman and Ellen Lenihan each added eight points for the Lancers. Colleen Hanrahan scored 11 points and Danielle Notarcola netted 10 for Keyport (12-5). NEPTUNE 40, EWING 30: Emma Reeves, scored 14 points to lead the Fliers to the non-conference win. Stephanie Sculphorpe and Tonya Bray each scored six for Neptune (15-3). Misty Cregar scored 10 points for Ewing. SOUTHERN 44, CENTRAL 34: Berri Tuttle scored 10 points to lead the Rams to the non-conference win. IN BOYS basketball: CENTRAL 41, SOUTHERN 34: Jermaine Clay scored 17 points to lead the Golden Eagles to the non-conference• win. Rodge Jerkezian added nine points for Central (14-4). Doug D'Zio went 10 of 10 at the line and scored 16 total points for Southern (11-8). POPE PAUL XI 55, LAKEWOOD 48: Frank Chillierti and Don Sipio scored 12, points each and Tom Senatorne added 11 to power Pope Paul XI to the nonconference win. Rydel Holmes scored 19 points for the Piners. ST. JOHN VIANNEY 80, KEYPORT 66: Jim Powers scored 21 points and helped key a 18-9 third -quarter surge as the Lancers evened their record with a non-conference win. The Lancers (10-10) also received scoring from Keith Telesca (18 points). and Joe Broderick (14). IN SWIMMING: SOUTH JERSEY INVITATIONAL SECTIONAL: Drew Aromando of Toms River East took first in the 50 freestyle final with a 23.1 clocking to lead Raiders to a third-place finish with 147, points yesterday at Fort Dix. Lenepe took overall honors with 265 and Shawnee finished second with 184. IN ICE hockey: CLIFTON 2, TOMS RIVER SOUTH O: J.R. Schneider scored both goals lifting Clifton to a non-conference win over the Indians at Macay Park, Englewood. Eric Peterson stopped 16 shots to. record the shutout.

MOST OF the time, when you find yourself in strong wrestling divisions like Manchester and Marlboro do, you measure advances toward respectability in inches. But last night Manchester and Marlboro took giant leaps toward reverence with major upsets. Manchester, getting key victories from Shawn Wirth (171) and Bryan Morrison (189), stunned No. 3 Lacey, 36-30, in a Shore Conference Class B South match. Marlboro, winning all five bouts between 130 and 152 pounds, shocked No. 10 Middletown South, 30-27, in a Class A North match. Manchester won four of the first five bouts _ three by pin - and held off the Lions, who wrestled without starters Justin DiNicola (103), John Stout (140) and Todd Hunt (189). With Nick Meyers (103), Josh Blair (119) and Glenn Sheets (130) scoring pins, the Hawks bolted to a 21-6 lead. Lacey won four of the next five bouts and tied the score. 27-27. But Manchester got a lift from Wirth, who pinned Gary Willis in 5:22 and then a 7-4 win by Morrison over Mike Peterson at 189 pounds. Marlboro fell behind early, 6-3, but won the next six bouts for a 28-6 lead over Middletown South, which wrestled without Doug and Bill Kirk. Jason Greiner (145) and Augie Calabro (152) had pins during the Mustangs' rush while Mike Kilayko (140) added a key 10-3 win over Dan Cartagena. By DAN STINSON Now for the hard part. Richmond Sockeyes waltzed to their first B.C. Junior Hockey League Coastal Division championship Tuesday night, but then they were playing the passive Delta Flyers, a team that simply refused to be physical. On the horizon are the Penticton Knights, a club that can hit, skate and score and must be favored to win the overall league title. "The road to the Centennial Cup (Canadian Tier Two championship) goes through Penticton," Sockeyes' co-coach. Bill Wilms said after Richmond defeated Delta 5-2 at Richmond Arena to win the best-ofseven Coastal series 4-1. "We'd better be ready." Indeed. Penticton wrapped up the Interior Division title last night with a 12-4 trouncing of Kelowna Packers, also taking the best-ofseven series 4-1. The Sockeyes were defeated in four regular season meetings against Penticton and Kelowna, and in the Knights they're facing a team that has lost only once on home ice in the last two years. Moreover, the Knights have home-ice advantage in the series, tentatively scheduled to get under way in Penticton on Monday. Muzz MacPherson, the Sockeyes' general manager-co-coach, has no illusions as to what lies ahead for his team. MacPherson coached Burnaby Bluehawks to a sevengame final series against Penticton last year, losing in overtime in the deciding contest. "We've got a fairly good skating club, but I believe Penticton is quicker," MacPherson said. "We're certainly going to have to be more physical against Penticton than we were against Delta. We'll get hammered if we don't stop their speed." Sockeyes were rarely pushed by the young Delta club. Their domination was so thorough that they successfully killed off a five-minute major penalty last night for the second consecutive game. Jim Butterworth opened scoring for Richmond just 13 seconds from the opening faceoff. By Danny Martin Times Sports Writer If Roy Henderson did not know Maurice Hall, he would question what the 5-foot-5, 14-year-old was doing on the Ben Boeke Arena ice with older and larger hockey in a scrimmage at the Spring Junior A/Collegiate Evaluation Camp. "Normally, when you come out to one of these (camps) and see kids out there two or three years younger than everyone else, you tend to say 'That kid ' * shouldn't be out there, Henderson said. "But after watching him, you say that about some of the older kids." Henderson, the Philadelphia Flyers' Western Region scout and the general manager of the Richmond Sockeyes of the British Columbia Junior Hockey League of Canada, is among six professional, junior and collegiate representatives at the camp which began Thursday and ends today. The other representatives are Rick Kozuback, head coach of NCAA Division III champion Wisconsin-River Falls; Kevin Constantine, head coach and GM of the Rochester (N.Y.) Mustangs, the United States Hockey League national Junior A champions; Chuck Grillo and Pat Funk, New York Rangers scouts; and Bruce Taylor, owner of the Richmond Sockeyes. The camp is a combination of two evaluation camps started last year by Dennis Sorenson and Tim Gorski. Sorenson is a coach of the Alaska All-Star Pee Wees and a former University of Alaska Anchorage center and Gorski is the Alaska scout for the Rangers and the head coach of the Alaska High School All-Star Select Team. "This gives Alaskan kids the chance for exposure without having to travel," Sorenson said. "Recently, a lot of kids have flown Outside to various camps. They spent $1,000 for the trip, and they wound up getting cut, and didn't know where to go from there.' Lakers oust Penticton By The Canadian Press Vernon Lakers advanced to the finals of the B.C. Junior r Hockey League playoffs with a 5-4 win over the home-ice Penticton Knights on Wednesday: The victory gave the Lakers a 4-0 sweep of the best-of-seven semifinal series. Vernon will face the winner of the other semi-final series between the Richmond Sockeyes and Delta Flyers. Richmond took a 2-0 lead in that series Wednesday. The Lakers jumped into a commanding lead with three goals in a space of two minutes, 19 seconds in the second period, ending the period ahead 5-0. The Knights came back with four straight goals in the final period, including one at 19:15 after pulling their goaltender, but couldn't get the equalizer. Greg Parnell scored twice for Vernon, with singles from Ian Alger, Duane Dennis and Mike Paller. Penticton got goals from Platzer, Cliff Furnseth, Shayne Green and Lance Momotani. Vernon outshot Pentiction 39-29. SOCKEYES 12 FLYERS4 Richmond blasted visiting Delta to take a 2-0 game lead in their best-of-seven semi-final. The teams also ran up 450 minutes in penalties, including 99 minutes handed out after a major fight in the second * period. Barry Kress and Marty Comeau each scored three times for Richmond. The Sockeyes got other goals from Jason Talo, Rob Clarke, Kirk Phare, Dan Walton, Garth Lamb and Tony Bobbitt. Delta's goals were scored by Brian Bethard, Ray Savard, Jim Kambeitz and Darren Schwartz. Richmond outshot Delta 55-24. The two teams meet again Friday and Saturday night in Delta.

Clippers 6, Sockeyes 5 (OT) By KEVIN MITCHELL Staff Writer Given one wish, there's no way Wayne Bianchin could have asked for a better end to Nanaimo Clippers' quarter-final playoff series with Richmond Sockeyes Friday night at frenzied Frank Crane Arena. Len Evans scored at 7:09 of a second s suddendeath overtime period to give the Clippers thrilling 6-5 win over Richmond before close to 1,200 B.C. Junior Hockey League fans. The Clippers voted early this morning to begin the Coastal Division best-of-seven final series against Langley Eagles Sunday night (7:30) at Frank Crane Arena. Langley can't get weekend ice time so the series will shift to the Fraser Valley for games two and three Monday and Tuesday. "Even as a pro, there's nothing bigger than tonight," said a joyous Bianchin, in his second year as Clipper coach. "You put away all the frustrations of the season for phe magic moment.' It was without a doubt the best junior hockey game played in Nanaimo in five years if not ever. The Clippers looked like the New York Islanders for the first 10 minutes of the game, but came up empty due to about four goalpost shots and some sensational goaltending by rookie Chris Dickson. As strange as it seemed, Richmond shouldn't have held period leads of 2-1 and 3-1. The Clippers were the stronger team on this night, but until defenceman Stan Czenczek rushed the length of the ice to narrow the margin to 5-4 late in the third period, couldn't get a break. Nanaimo held an overwhelming 19-8 shot advantage in the opening session, but had to settie for Gord Simpson's eighth goal of the series to offset a powerplay goal by David Street and a shorthanded effort by Eric Catchpole. Richmond took a 5-2 lead at 11:36 of the third frame when Dave Phipps' shot struck the end boards, hit Clipper goalie Ken Trousdall in the back of the leg, and trickled in the net. That shot by Phipps, his second tally of the night, stung the Clippers like a rope burn in what was a highly intense tug-of-war. However, Evans scored 33 seconds later and Czenczek followed five minutes later with a rush which resulted in a marker which would rate goalof-the-week in any league. Simpson then brought the house down when he dove at a loose puck in front of Dickson, connecting enough to send the puck over the line with just 49 second left in regulation time. That goal came with Czenczek in the penalty box for an all too obvious trip in front of referee Dave Dodson. Both clubs enjoyed great scoring chances in the first 10-minute overtime session, but Trousdell and Dickson wouldn't give an inch. On the winning goal, Evans stole the puck off Sockeye defenceman Peter Gill just inside the Richmond blueline. Al Johnston then beat Richmond forward Richard Novak to the puck and fed Evans in front of the net.

Clippers bring back memories As far as entertainment goes, you can't beat the thrills and spills of junior jockey. I learned that when as a youngster my dad used to take me to old Civic Arena in Vernon. I'll never forget the excitement I got watching the likes of Bob Gassoff, Eddie Johnstone, Wayne Dye, Ernie Gare, John Price and numerous others. Years and thousands of junior hockey games later, I still get the same excitement out of junior hockey. Especially at playoff time. There's nothing like playoff hockey to get one's spine tingling. I can still spend an hour telling whoever will listen how I enjoyed a Centennial Cup western final series between Kelowna Buckaroos and Selkirk, Man. Steelers or another one between Vernon Essos and Weyburn Red Wings of Saskatchewan back in the very early 1970s. Friday night's game between Nanaimo Clippers and Richmond Sockeyes must have injected a ton of enthusiasm into anyone who is fond of the game of hockey. Since it's been four years since this city's junior team has been in a playoff game at Frank Crane Arena, the Clippers' 6-5 win in doubleovertime was just what the doctor ordered. Coach Wayne Blanchin described it as a rink "magic felt happy moment" as and they I'm stood sure and everybody watched in the Clippers mob one another in a a a a a a a a a a victory celebration. As one of thousands of Canadians who never realized a dream of playing for the Leafs of Canadiens, I have never found myself envious of the junior or pro hockey player. Instead, I have found joy in seeing them achieve their goals, finding happiness in a big victory. Watching the beaming smiles in the dressing room and at a post-game paryy made. me feel good as a hockey fan. I realized they had gone through a long and often frustrating season and had surely deserved this moment of joy. For the local young hockey fan, the likes of Len Evans, Gord Simpson and Ken Trousdell will long be remembered as their heroes, if not for Friday's thrilling win, but for what may happen in the next series. One can only hope the best is yet to come, but if it isn't, the Clippers have something very special to be proud of nonetheless. It was a great seventh and deciding game, as good as you're going to see in any hockey league. And it will some day be a nice memory. In league action Tuesday night, Cowichan fell 8-3 to hometown Richmond Sockeyes, while Brett Hull's hat-trick led Penticton Knights to a 12-3 pounding of Summerland Buckaroos. Steve Tuttle, David Phipps and David Holmes each scored twice to pace the Sockeyes. Craig Tillberg, Kal Russell and Phil Lum replied for Cowichan. Ken Clement breaking his left thumb during his first shift in Saturday's game against Richmond Sockeyes is not good news for Nanaimo Clippers. The local B.C. Junior Hockey League club was counting heavily on the veteran nicknamed "Pope" to provide the leadership and drive in the postseason. Clement is one of the nicest and most soft spoken players on the Clipper roster, but get him in the corner battling for the puck and he resembles a combination between Atilla the Hun and the Incredible Hulk. The 20-year-old, who has made up for his lack of playing skill through his intensity and hard work, may well have played his last ever junior hockey game. That's a real pity because the Clippers needed him to pull them through upcoming clutch situations in the playoffs. Maybe the other players on the team will now decide they really have their work cut out for them and pull together. Maybe their game song will include a line about winning the big one for "Pops." Clement is still attending practises and skating with the club and who knows, he might even help do some coaching on how the rest of the guys can play tough, grinding hockey which is his forte. There still a slight possibility that "Pops" could be back for the playoffs but only if the injury is not as bad as first feared and if the Clippers can get into a couple of long playoff series. Clement could conceivably be back if Nanaimo makes it to the BCJHL championship. But this latest bit of bad luck must be extremely frustrating for him. When the playoffs start, you can bet the most attentive of the spectators will be "Pops" anar urging his teammates to play their hearts out beat the enemy. Speaking of another hockey team, Vancouver Canucks, are they finally for real or is this business of winning on the road just one big mirage? The NHL club is suddenly beating good teams regularly and with a comeback style they haven't displayed since the Phil Maloney days. Maybe Harry Neale is doing something right, although I still have a sneaking suspicion the Canucks will settle back into their mediocore groove before too long. They've done it too long to become consistent winners. Vancouver is by no means guaranteed a playoff spot and as the Roger Nelson coached Los Angeles Kings are ever improving and hot on the Canucks' tall. The next 20 or so games will show whether the Vancouver players have smartened up or they just happened to catch several of the better clubs in the pro circut on off nights. Before I disappear from the sports desk andmove into the news department permanently, I'd like to pay tribute to one of this city's most forgettable sports teams: the Daily Free Press Walleyball squad. The next time Rod Summers visits Frank Crane Arena, he'll be wearing the blue and white jersey of the Abbotsford Flyers. Nanaimo Clipper owner coach Doug Harding released the 18-year-old White Rock -blueliner from the B.C. Junior Hockey League club Tuesday, allowing Summers to join the Flyers. Harding said he is hoping the Flyers will compensate the Clippers for Summers, saying "I've told them I need a goal scoring forward. We're hoping that they'll be able to reciprocate that, but if not..." Abbotsford and Summers have been talking for some time, but when a deal to send Summers to Richmond Sockeyes didn't materialize, Harding was left with little choice in the situation when the Flyers hummed and hawed about trade bait. "What can you do? I told him I either get someone for him, or keep him out of hockey," he said. Nanaimo had hoped to land 18-yearold forward Doug McFaul in the deal, but that is up in the air. Meanwhile, in BCJHL action Tuesday night, McFaul, Rob Reid and Sean Harder all scored twice as the Flyers toppled Richmond Sockeyes 124 in Richmond. Mitch Evanish, Rick Bifaro, Mike Bulka, Kal Russell, Chris Anderson and Joe Quinn also tallied for Abbotsford. Rick Dietrich fired a pair of goals for the Sockeyes, who got, singles from Henry Reimer and Keith Streete In Summerland, David Shields produced four goals and two assists to lead Penticton Knights to a 12-3 rout of the Buckaroos. Penticton's other markers came from Murray Winnicki, Greg Cyr, Rod Ware, John De Pourcq, Ian Kidd, Mike Barzal, Derek Mäyer and Joe Murphy. Ed Tuckwood with two, and Cary Fisher replied for Summerland.

'Nanaimo Clippers are 13 points out of the fourth and final-playoff spot in the B.C. Junior Hockey League's Coastal Division with only seven games remaining, but owner-coach Doug Harding refuses to throw in the towel. "Who knows what could happen there could be a lot more games if we make the a playoffs," Harding said Tuesday. Currently on a seven game losing skein, the shipmen had best set their sights on Richmond Sockeyes, one point ahead of Abbotsford Flyers in third spot with 52 points, if they hope to make post, season play. The Flyers have been red hot since Christmas. "Richmond would have to be the team to beat. I've said since Christmas that Abbotsford was the team to beat. Abbotsford is not going to lose a game for the rest of the season," he said. Harding added later he told his club two weeks ago "not to worry about making the playoffs. Forget about making them, and go out and have fun. All the playoffs are is money, and I've decided to survive anyway.' The Clippers' attendance woes have been well documented, with an average of less than 400 fans per game at Frank Crane Arena. Despite the decrease over previous seasons, Harding still claims Nanaimo has the best attendance in the Coastal Division. by Rick Rake New ownership and management is in store for Abbotsford Falcons who are 2 and 23 following their 9-3 loss Saturday to league-leading Richmond Sockeyes. B.C. Junior Hockey League president Les Jeboult told News a presentation to "preserve the Falcons in Abbotsford" was made to the BCJHL board of governors on Sunday in Princeton. He said Del Thachuk of White Rock is the potential principal. "Nothing has been accepted or rejected by the board, but we have a couple of loose ends to take care of first," said Jeboult. "The presentation was accepted in principle.' Outgoing coach Byron Hitchens of Abbotsford told The News he is "selling out his interest" in the team and revealed that Don Berry, recent Western Hockey League Seattle Thunderbird coach, may take his place. Berry, 56, , coached the BCJHL Abbotsford Flyers to the 1983 tier two Centennial Cup final in Toronto. He had a 4 and 9 record behind Seattle's bench. Jeboult would not confirm or deny Berry's possible involvement with the team - "just wait for an anouncement from the new owner. I- don't want to steal his thunder." Junior Hockey League play Thursday night. In the only other BCJHL contest, the Nanaimo Clippers led throughout the game as they defeated the Buckaroos 8-5 in Summerland. In Richmond, the Sockeyes led 4-2 after two periods but Kelowna came back with goals from Kevin Harder and Jay Luknowsky to tie the game. Kerry Russell and Kevin Taillefer scored the earlier Packer goals. The Sockeyes built their lead on goals by Jeff Nottingham, Dave Tomlinson, Peter Holmes and Mario Desjardins, before coming up empty in the third period. The Sockeyes outshot the Packers 35-32. It was the third staight win for Kelowna and the first loss of the year for Richmond, now 2-1. In Summerland, Mike Knapton scored twice for the Clippers, while Darrin Biggs, Corkey Parks, Brett Mayor, Todd Demchuk, Craig Frankford and Joey Barszczewski added singles. Nanaimo outshot Summerland 52-35. Summerland goals came from Steve Fredrich, Darrin Harrold, Mark Valley, Tim Roux and Kevin Hoey.

Clippers' Al Johnston fends off Richmond Sockeye player Furlan's netting catches Sockeyes in BCJHL battle

Frank Furlan wasn't about to let a lucky goal by Richmond Sockeyes' David Holmes get on his nerves Saturday night at Frank Crane Arena, The goal, at 18:51 of the second period would be the last one Richmond would get as Furlan performed magic in the Nanaimo Clippers' net the rest of the way in a 5-4 Clipper victory before 350 B.C. Junior Hockey League fans. Holmes tied the score at 4-4 when he dumped the puck in off the end boards and watched in amazement as the puck took a wild bounce off Furlan and entered the Nanaimo goal. "This happens to me all the time in Beban," said Furlan. "'They don't get the edge (of the ice) properly hesitated though and made the wrong move. It's routine, I should have had it ..." The rookie goalie stopped Richmond sniper Henry Reimer twice from 'gimme' range early in the third period with the speedy Sockeye forwards foreing the Clipper defence into all kinds of shoddy clearing passes which developed into Richmond scoring chances. Furlan then made a superb pad save off. veteran blueliner Rock Baykey's blast from the slot and seconds later followed with a chest save off a Baykey wrist shot. Keith Street would later be foiled by Furlan on a clean break in front, but it was Bruce Major who shook his head the loudest when he was robbed by Furlan in the final minute. "I put my head in it and said 'I'm not going to lose it for you guys'," said Furlan of his third period performance. "You gotta be really happy with Frankie's play," said Clipper coach Doug Harding, who will start the 16- year-old again tonight when the Clippers take on Sidney Capitals in an 8 o'clock start at Frank Crane Arena. NICE GOAL Mitch Poulin's goal at 3:08 of the final frame on a breakaway stood up as the winner. Poulin made a nifty move to get past Street at the Richmond blueline and despite the fact that Street threw his stick, Poulin managed to 'manoeuvre puck around the stick and beat Sockeye goalie Glenn Pencer along the ice. • Dale Brisco with two goals, Chris Calverley with his first, BCJHL goal and 'Al Chatlain rounded out Nanaimo's scoring. Calverley's tally was a beauty as he rushed down left wing and from a sharp angle rifled a snapshot into the far side of the net.

MacPherson • returned to coaching after that, leading major junior teams until 1978 when he began as coach of the Eastern League New Jersey Devils (later the Hampton, Virginia Aces - farm team for the NHL Philadelphia Flyers). In 1980, Muzz made the move to B.C., coaching the New Westminster Bruins for one season before heading back to the Prairies to coach the Junior A Drumheller Miners, where he stayed until 1983. But B.C. soon beckoned and for two years Muzz took charge of the successful Burnaby Bluehawks before spending one season with the Richmond Sockeyes, his last coaching position before coming to Langley. In 1972-73, MacPherson was Manitoba Amateur Hockey Association coach-of-the-year and in 1975-76 was Ontario Hockey Association 1 to coach-of-the-year. Educated at Edina High School in Minneapolis, MacPherson completed one year at the University of North Dakota. He has worked as a telecine operator, master control operator and video take supervisor at CJAY-TV station in Winnipeg. He is married with two boys - one 10 years old a done 13. MacPherson said at a recent press conference that he intends to make the Eagles more open and accessible to the public and local media. He also stressed that he expects® his players to perform well in an effort to bring topcalibre hockey back to Langley.

The Langley Eagles B.C. Junior A hockey club has released its 1986-87 g game schedule. The Coastal Division team will play 53 games this season under new coach and g.m. Murray (Muzz) MacPherson, who assumed the coaching reins last month from Larry Romanchych. The• team's season opener is on Thursday, Sept. •18th, against Merritt, followed by back-to-back games against Penticton and Summerland on Friday, Sept. 19th and Saturday, Sept. 20th. The last regular season game is set for Feb. 21st against the Abbotsford Falcons, formerly the Flyers. The Eagles, and all Coastal Division sides, are again expected to face stiff competition from the Interior Division Penticton Knights this season. George Evens, of the Evens Group, has been hired as a marketing, public relations representative for the Eagles. he will work alongside MacPherson, who formerly coached the Burnaby Bluehawks and Richmond Sockeyes. Born in London, MacPherson was influenced greatly as a youth by his father Stewart Miles, who was once A CBC radio hockey broadcaster and later a war correspondent and compere of the Royal Command Variety Show. MacPherson started playing hockey in 1956 as a goalie for the Winnipeg Rangers junior A team and later played for the Flin Flon Bombers and Fort Wayne, Indiana, Komets. From 1963 to 1967, MacPherson coached midget and juvenile teams in the Manitoba Amateur Hockey Association before coaching the Junior A Winnipeg Monarchs for three years. on From 1972 to 1973, MacPherson coached the Portage La Prairie Junior A- Terriers, including

by Gord Kurenoff There's an old joke in junior hockey which suggests that defence is the thing players jump over to beat decurfew. Richmond Sockeyes, had the last laugh Saturday night after they punctured Abbotsford Falcons 'swiss cheese defence' en route to a 15-2 cakewalk at MSA Arena. "It was a little hard to take," understated Falcons' assistant coach Jack Goeson following the one-sided affair. "We came up with a pretty poor effort."
