"We've got 14 returning from last year," said coach Jack Fulton. Two years ago the club, which finished fourth last season, lost 14 players through graduation. "Near the end of last year we saw some dividends (from the rebuilding program)," added Fulton. "We're hoping we can build on that this year." Fulton is looking for better things from returnee John Wilson this season. Wilson, who had a slow start last year, finished up by playing for the senior Salmonbellies in the national Mann Cup championships. New Westminster r lost in the seventh and deciding game of the series at home. Cory Watson, who is 6-foot-4 and weighs in the vicinity of 200 pounds, has stepped up from the New Westminster intermediate club. "We think he can step up and do quite well in junior," said Fulton. Another promising newcomer is Craig Smith, a Junior B player from Ottawa who "did quite well" in the Ontario league. Richmond Sockeyes: New coach Pat Ryan, who called the shots for the Richmond intermediate club last season, said only a handful of players returned from last season. "It's not looking too bad," he said, "but most of the guys haven't played together before." The Sockeyes, who finished fifth last year, are in a recovery mode. •We're still in a rebuilding process," said Ryan. "We're the underdogs for sure." Richmond will be anchored by veterans Gary Frederickson, Dean Allan, Dave Haller and goaltender Robert Cook. Three intermediate grads - Darren Goundrey, Greg Reid and Brad Dickson - - have played prominent roles in pre-season games and workouts. Dickson was the intermediate league scoring champion last season. Surrey Rebels: "We're a somewhat improved team," said coach Bob McClelland. The Rebels, who finished "second-to-last" in the 1987 season, defeated New Westminster in the recent tournament and was only narrowly beaten by both Coquitlam and Burnaby. Edmonton, which played half of the league schedule and placed last in 1987, won't be competing this year. McClelland said his squad has seven holdovers from last season and the newcomers are looking good. "They're taking up the slack," he added. Veterans Justin Bosher and Todd Evans, who combined for 30 points in the pre-season tournament, anchor the team along with other holdovers Ken Hinds, Neil Gailey and Dennis Bull. Rookie Dan Rodrigues (from the Killarney Junior B organization) and Pat Hagel, who sat out last season with a shoulder injury, have also been impressive. McClelland has particularly high hopes for the 6-foot-3, 245-pound Hagel. "We're expecting him to be our policeman," he explained.

to the Senior B Nanaimo Timbermen during their opening pre-season exhibition game, the Legion bounced back to drop the same squad 14-6 in Nanaimo. Esquimalt also dropped a 14-6 decision to revitalized Victoria Pay Less of the Western Lacrosse Association in the annual John Crowther Memorial game Wednesday at Esquimalt Sports Centre. Legion coach Nirmal Dillon said the club is playing better with each game while adapting to his new fast-break system. Dillon said the club's running and checking improved tremendously in the second game against the Timbermen. And the tactics even gave Pay Less a little trouble at times. Several players are still missing from the lineup. Tory Thompson is nursing a broken finger suffered in the first game against Nanaimo, and three of Esquimalt's main guns, twins Gary and Paul Gait as well as Ravi Dhillon, are expected to rejoin the team about June 1 after completing studies at United States universities. In addition, Dillon expects former Victoria Cougar hockey player Greg Batters to turn out again this season. Esquimalt, which won the league playoff title and then lost in the national Minto Cup championship last year, opens its season May 7, battling the Coquitlam Adanacs at 8 p.m. at Esquimalt Sports Centre. Elsewhere in the league: Coquitlam Adanacs: Coach Les Wingrove said the team, which finished in a tie for top spot with Esquimalt and was awarded first place on the basis of games between the teams, is looking g good this year. "We've got some talent equal to last year," said Wingrove. The Adanacs only lost half a dozen players through graduation. "We're a little slow getting going, but we're getting there,' added Wingrove. Evidence of Coquitlam's strength lies with a victory in a pre-season tournament involving all the teams in the league except Esquimalt. The Adanacs beat the Richmond Sockeyes 11-9 in the final of the single-knockout tourney. Outstanding returnees include 1987 rookie-of-the-year Lorne Hotseth, Scott Parson, Ken Thomas, goaltender Jeff Dobish, Wade Dean and Wingrove's son, Trevor. Tops among the newcomers is Troy Cordingley of Brampton, who scored four goals in his first preseason game with Coquitlam. Burnaby Cablevision: "We've only lost two or three people we're looking pretty good," said coach Randy Delmonico. The Cablevision crew finished third last year behind Coquitlam and Esquimalt. "I think we're going to have a really balanced team this year," added Delmonico. One of the leading returnees in the pre-season has been Ron Anhofer, who scored four goals in a recent game against the Surrey Rebels. Tod Cullum and Steve Pongratz have also proved impressive among the veterans. Top newcomers have been Curtis Smith, who sat out last season following knee surgery after playing on the Canada Games team two years ago, and Trevor Andres, who was with the Langley Junior B club last year. Delmonico's biggest task will be to choose two goaltenders out of the four attending camp. "The four we have now are all capable of playing in this league," he said. New Westminster Salmonbellies:

McNabb. "It looked like he (Hopper) sent a guy after him. If that's going to happen, then him (Hopper) and I will go and settle things. I know he's a small guy, Hopper, whose coaching style wouldn't support such a move, was rather stunned by McNabb's threatening gesture midway through the second period. "Larry was jumping up and down at me as if had sent him (Voshell) out there,' said Hopper. "He (Voshell) was frustrated and guess he's not going to put up with whatever went on out there." Voshell shouted at his bench, "Now get going" after being ejected so perhaps was only looking to get club fired up with Nanaimo holding a 5-0 lead. As for stat night, Grant McDonald fired a pair of goals and added an assist for the Clippers, while Pat Hodgins chipped in with a goal and three assists. Captain Sean Stotts ended a seven-game scoring slump with one marker and two assists, while L.D. McNabb and Mike Knapton each produced a goal, and an assist. Joining in the fun were Finner, Wally Bzdell and Darren Biggs with two helpers apiece. McNabb S said no secret strategy was used in the Clippers first victory in seven starts against Delta. "I thought we played as a unit," he said. "I couldn't think of anybody who didn't play well. Everybody has to come to play hockey and that's how we have play every night. It was nice to take pressure off Corky's (Parkes) line and get somebody else scoring.' Parkes missed the third period after L.D. McNabb's skate cut his hand for five stitches at the Clipper bench. A tie and win over Delta to open the New Year makes the Clippers' January schedule look a better. Nanaimo still must face Richmond Sockeyes twice and Delta, Penticton Knights and Juan de Fuca Whalers once each this month. "If you checked our schedule after Christmas, it looked like a nightmare," said coach McNabb. "If we play like this against Richmond, it should be a heck of a game Friday night (Civic Arena)." by Alan Watson Sam Styles and Brian Silverson are a couple of young guys coaching a hockey team - but that team seems intent on adding a few premature greys to their hairlines. For the second game in a row, the Abbotsford Pilots bleve a third period lead then came back to win in overtime, the latest one a 7-6 win over the first-place Richmond Sockeyes, Friday night at MSA Arena. The win puts the Pilots within two points of the league leaders in the West Coast Junior Hockey League, and with one game in hand. The two teams met again last night in Richmond (no score available before deadline). "It's hard on the coaching staff but it's great for the fans," Styles said Monday of Friday night's win, which followed on the heels of a 6-5 overtime win over the cellar-dwelling Grandview Steelers five nights earlier. "It seems to be a never saga." And, true enough to recent form, the Pilots started quickly, looked like a dominant team and then presto - things were close again. An outstanding opening period, in which they outshot Richmond 14-8 and scored three times, set the stage. After giving up one early in the second, a couple of well-earned markers put Abbotsford up 5-1 and seemingly in command. Uh-uh. Richmond struck at 15:09, 16:57 and 18:56 of the second period, the latter on the powerplay, and suddenly the game was back on. Hard-working Trevor Harms, whom Styles commended for his consistent play of late, finished off a nice three-way passing play with linemates Tyler Boucher and Kirk Olischefski to put the Pilots up by two at 3:42 of the third. But back came Richmond, fueled by the efforts of centre Travis Evans, who had a five-point night. They got one back before the period was half over and notched the equalizer with less than four minutes to play, again, on the powerplay. Then it was up to Pilots goaltender Jason Holt to bar. the door, which he did. His biggest came with less than five seconds on the clock as he first stopped a partial breakaway then blocked the rebound while lying on his back in the crease. Then, at 2:24 into overtime, centre Jay Nagamatsu, the first player from the Abbotsford-Matsqui Minor Hockey Association to actually graduate to the team since it shifted operations here from Mission three seasons ago, fed a beautiful strike of a pass to a streaking Rod Spitzig. He went in alone on Richmond goaltender Jason Scott, made a couple of moves and deposited it behind him to the delight of close to 400 fans " in attendance. Styles of course has no concrete answers to his team's propensity for blowing leads, but offered a couple of theories. "It almost seems the players can't play without pressure. We don't have the killer instinct but once we're in a battle, we play well. And, it could be partly Brian and myself making rookie coaching mistakes as well. Everybody's learning at

(Herald staff writer) Vernon Lakers have never lost more than one game at a time this season. Can Calgary Canucks do the impossible and shatter that monument of consistency? The Canucks beat the Lakers 7-3 Tuesday night in the first game of a Centennial Cup quarterfinal. Game 2 goes tonight, also at Max Bell Arena. The Canucks, 51-9 in the Alberta Junior Hockey League's regular season, are trying to break the B.C. Junior League's two-year domination of the Centennial Cup, the symbol of Canadian Tier II supremacy. Richmond Sockeyes won last season while Penticton Knights took the cup the year before. No Calgary team has won the Centennial Cup. With the victory, the Canucks kept alive a streak of their own. They have yet to lose a series opener in this season's playoffs. Brandy Semchuk led the underdog Canucks with two thirdperiod goals. Dave Loewen, Dean Larson, Tim Kollman, Mike Bertamini (shorthanded) and Dennis Fleming supplied single markers. Farrell Goodwin (power play), Mark Greyeyes and Wes Elliott replied for the Lakers, who outshot Calgary 30-28. The first period was tied 1-1, but the Canucks led 4-3 after the second. Dean Larson gave Calgary a 2-1 lead with a breakaway goal at 6:46 of the second. The goal came courtesy a steal by Bertamini. Tim Kollman ripped a slapshot along the ice at 7:22, providing the Canucks with a 3-1 lead.

the Sockeyes rebounded to finish out the first period with a 4-3 advantage. With no scoring in the second period, Delta was forced to play catch-up hockey in the final frame. After Richmond scored again, the Flyers narrowed the Sockeyes' lead to 5-4. Richmond added an insurance marker in the final minutes of the game to secure the victory. Game five in the series, which Delta needed to win to stay alive in the series, was played in Richmond on Tuesday (after The Leader's press time). If necessary, the sixth game of the series will be played at Sungod Arena tonight (Wednesday) at 8 p.m. A seventh and deciding game, if needed, would be played in Richmond on Thursday. Delta opened the series with a 3-2 shaving over the Sockeyes on March 13 in Richmond. Flyers scored the winning goal early in the third period and forechecked Richmond the rest of the way to preserve the win. The following night, Sockeyes rebounded from the opening loss to narrowly squeak past the Flyers 5-4 in Richmond. Sockeyes then came to Delta and skated to a 6-1 triumph on Sunday afternoon to take a 2-1 lead in the series. Following the fourth game of the series, Flyers' general manager Frank Sever felt Delta should have won the game. He still hopes his squad can steal the series away from Richmond. "We had our chances," Sever said. "But I think we can still do it."

by Larry Pruner Delta Flyers lost control, the Richmond Sockeyes didn't and referee Tom Hansen never had it. The Flyers were hammered 12-4 by the fishmen Wednesday night in Richmond but also did some hammering of their in a second-period brawl which was responsible for a large junk of game's 450 penalty minutes. But the important figure was the score, which Delta lost and fell behind 2-0 in the best-of-seven Coastal division playoff series. The Flyers were hit for a string of 'questionable' penalties in the first period, which cost them three powerplay goals and put them behind 5-0 after 20 minutes. In fact, the Flyers didn't get a man advantage until 3:14 of the second period. At one time, Delta had nine players in the penalty box, an unofficial B.C. Junior Hockey League record. The one-sided penalty calls continued through the second frame, with Richmond adding another marker on the powerplay to increase their lead to 7-0. The fireworks were sparked in the third period, when the frustrated Flyers took on the Sockeyes in a donnybrook, which spread among the fans behind Delta's bench. by Stephen Gold It's official! The Richmond Sockeyes B.C. Junior Hockey League team will be moving , to Chilliwack starting in the 1990-91 season. The franchise, under a new name, will be playing in Chilliwack next season, officials of the club and the District of Chilliwack announced at a press conference yesterday (Tuesday) morning. (The Progress reported on Jan. 17 that the Sockeyes would be the club transferring to this community after it was announced the week before that an unnamed BCJHL team would be moving). The transfer, however, still has to be approved by the BCJHL's board of governors. Team owner Allan Brew does not expect a problem obtaining that approval either before or at the annual general meeting in June. "Definitely it's going to be a positive move for the league." The league has nine teams, six in the Coastal Division and three in the Interior. The BCJHL is a league for junior players up to the age of 20 years and differs from Junior A (for example, the Western Hockey League) in that most its successful graduates move onto U.S. college hockey before turning professional. The Sockeyes play in the Coastal division of the league but the Chilliwack-based club may be moved to the Interior grouping, current coach and general manager Orland Kurtenbach said. An official decision on which division the franchise will

Larry Hayes, most recently vice-president of the successful New Westminster Royals BCJHL team, joined the Sockeyes last week as the club's new general manager. Not all the Sockeyes' roster are expected move to Chilliwack. Kurtenbach expects a maximum of 14 of the current players to be here next year. Allan Brew, and father Cliff, through Pan Pacific Enterprises, own 100 per cent of the Richmond Sockeyes Hockey Club. The Brews bought the team before the start of the 1987/88 season. WING TIPS: Coastal Division vicepresident David Ferguson announced Tuesday that Juan de Fuca coach Dale Sproule has been handed a ninemonth suspension. The suspension was handed down after a lengthy investigation into allegations he told his players to run Richmond Sockeyes' goalie Willie Popp, who was playing for the Eagles on a loan basis on Jan. 30. Popp suffered a season-ending injury when his wrist was broken by the Whalers' Rick Cox. On Saturday (Feb. 20), the Eagles will play their final home game of the season when they host Delta at the Coliseum at 8 p.m. Eagles' defenceman Kevin Jorgenson suffered a broken ankle Tuesday night when he fell during a fight in the second period with Abbotsford's Barclay Parneta. Also of interest is the fact that coach MacPherson is turning 50 on Friday (Feb. 19), and sources say his players may have something "special" in store for Saturday.

The victory followed a 6-3 loss on Saturday to Nanaimo Clippers and 10-1 thrashing at the hands of the Richmond Sockeyes last Wednesday (Feb. 10). Heading into Tuesday night's contest, the Eagles needed to win four of their remaining games: Two against the Falcons (Tuesday and Thursday), one with Delta Flyers, and then a showdown with Juan de Fuca Whalers on Sunday on the Island. American university" recruiters will be paying much closer atterition to Nanaimo Clippers than they did a year ago. Lasy year's Clipper team consisted of several 19 and 20-year-olds who couldn't get the time of day from a college scout. This year's club will be much younger and feature several fine college prospects. "I would say that as it stands right now, there's only one player not eligible for a college scholarship (Jamie Cayford because of tier one experience)," said Doug Harding, owner of the B.C. Junior Hockey League Clippers. While some BCJHL clubs like Delta Flyers and Richmond Sockeyes will stack their rosters with Western Hockey League castoffs, the Clippers are going with a youth movement in what is definitely a rebuilding campaign. *There's always team that tries to win with the older players," said Harding. "They might get off to a fast start, but they don't always win in the end." Education is being taken seriously by the Clipper brass. If a player is cutting classes, you can bet coach Rich Dutka or general manager Vern Benedictson will hear about it from that player's school. A total of 23 players who spent last year in the BCJHL have accepted hockey scholarships in the United States. League champion Penticton Knights lead the way with eight graduates headed to the U.S., followed by Richmond Sockeyes with four and Summerland Buckaroos and Abbotsford Falcons three apiece. 1984-85 BCJHL graduates awarded U.S. college scholarships: Dean Garnett (Summerland-Miami of Ohio); Derek Pizzey (PentictonDenver); Murray Winnicki (Penticton-Ferris St.); Keith Street (Richmond-Alaska); Shawn Pettyjohn (Summerland-Alaska); Bryant Perrier (Langley-Alaska); Doug McFaul (Abbotsford-Alaska); Todd Brost (Penticton-Michigan); Ed Cristofoli (Penticton-Denver); Al Loring (Abbotsford-Maine); Brent Mowery (Summerland-Northern Arizona); Tim Comeau (Penticton-Wisconsin); Tim Coghlin (Salmon Arm-Wisconsin); Henry Reimer (Richmond-Chicago U.); Bruce Major (Richmond-Maine); Murphy (Penticton-Michigan St.); Derek Pizzey (Penticton-Colorado College); Glen Engevik (Langley-Northern Arizona); Tan Kidd (PentictonNorth Dakota); Brian Bullinger

RICHMOND, B.C. (CP) - The Richmond Sockeyes of the B.C. Junior Hockey League are moving te Chilliwack, team owner Al Brew said Tuesday. Brew said the club will move at the end of this season because of a lack of fan support in this Vancouver suburb. The move still needs league approval, expected in June when league governors meet in Chilliwack, about 100 kilometres east of Vancouver. Chilliwack has not had a team in the league since the 1988-89 season, when the Eagles moved to Ladner. Brew said Richmond coach Orland Kurtenbach will become team president next season and assistant coach Mike 0'Brian will become head coach. The Sockeyes are fifth in the sixteam Coastal Division. Meanwhile, Mike Barrie scored at 2:15 of overtime to lift the Kelowna Spartans to a 7-6 win over the Vernon Lakers in a B.C. Junior Hockey League game Tuesday night. The win capped a comeback for the Spartans who trailed 6-2 after the second period. In other games, it was: New Westminster Royals 4, Richmond Sockeyes 3 and Powell River Paper Kings 4, Cowichan Valley Warriors 3 (OT). "I thought the pass was coming out front so I just hit it," Barrie said of his first BCJHL goal. The 15-year-old was playing only his second league game since being called up from a bantam squad.

Nanaimo's Jim Young blocked 23 shots to lead Vernon Lakers to an easy 8-2 decision ove visiting Revelstoke Rangers in B.C. Junior Hockey League action Friday night. Greg Guest led the Lakers with two goals, while Kyle Johnson added one and drew three assists. In Langley, former Nanaimo Clipper Brian Silverson scored the winning goal late in the third period to give Richmond Sockeyes a 4-3 victory over the Eagles. Henry Reimer, Jeff Grosley and Mike Scardina also tallied for the Sockeyes, while Rod Ware with two and Derrick Ruppel replied for Langley. In Penticton, Dave Shields and Ian Kidd each fired two goals to lead the Knights to a 11-3 win over Summerland Buckaroos.

Chilliwack returns leading point producer Billy MacGillivray and talented winger Danny Skene, but coach Mike O'Brien is truly excited about his recruits. Among them are Joey Botskin, the top center in the Alberta Junior League and a draft pick of the Atlanta Braves, and his former linemate Mark Gagnon, who had 55 points in 27 games. Leading scorer Bob Moon is back for Kelowna, which placed second in the Interior last year. Among the new faces will be talented goalies Dallas Mix and Gord Dale and Curt Vernon, a winger who played for Spokane of the Western Hockey League. Merritt coach John "Butch" Tent has a dozen veterans back from a squad that was beaten by Vernon in the Interior playoff finals. Second-leading scorer Paul Gentes is back along with the strong line of center Warren Carter, and wings Dave Bond and Dave Zakoll, and the team's two goalies. At Penticton, first-year coach Gary Davidson started over with a team that had no returnees thanks to the financial problems that forced it to be disbanded after 27 games last season. But even with only one 20-year-old and an average of only 17 years, the Panthers have some e talented, including center Paul Kariya, who Davidson calls "unbelievable." CHILLIWACK CHIEFS Coach: Mike O'Brien Division (as the Richmond Sockeyes) Last year's o record: 21-35-3, fifth place in the Coastal Top returnees: Billy MacGillivray, center; Danny Skene, wing. Top newcomers: Joey Botskin, center; Mark Gagnon, wing; Troy Seibel, goalie. Coach's comments: "We recruited pretty hard. You can tell by our phone bill."

If the Bellingham Ice Hawks were looking for a Junior A hockey league to dominate the first season of their existence, they sure picked the wrong one. The British Columbia Junior Hockey League is generally considered the best Junior A league in Canada, which means the best in the Western Hemisphere. probably the best Tier 2 league anywhere," said Ice Hawks coach Mike Collins. It will be hard for the BCJHL to top last season, when division winners New Westminster and Vernon eventually met in the Centennial Cup final, the Super Bowl of Canadian Junior A hockey. Those two powerhouses, which have dominated the league the past two years, are back, although with new clubs since many players graduated out of the junior league because of the age limit (20). But there are new franchises and new faces galore around the rest of the league. Bellingham, of course, took over the Ladner franchise. The Cowichan Valley Warriors became the Victoria Warriors. The Richmond Sockeyes became the Chilliwack Chiefs. And the Penticton Knights stayed in Penticton, but became the Panthers when new owners took over for a a a a a a a a franchise that disbanded in the middle of last season. In addition to making additions, the league also restructured its divisional setup. Bellingham joins -New Westminster, Nanaimo, Powell River and Victoria in the Coastal Division. Chilliwack joins Vernon, Kelowna, Merritt and Penticton to form the Interior Division. Teams will face divisional rivals 10 times each and teams in the other division four times. The defending champions get the early nod as favorites again, although none of the coaches expect a team like New Westminster, which was 52-3-41 last season and broke just a about every team record on the books. "It was one of the best Junior 2 teams ever put together,' said Royals coach Harvey Smyl, who was an assistant coach last season. With five returnees - including outstanding goalie Todd Jones and center Greg Hadden, who set the league record for shorthanded goals two years ago - the Royals have a solid nucleus. New Westminster also is notorious for its recruiting and with new players like big center Phil Esposito (no relation) and left winger John Forchuk, the Royals should be in the hunt again. Victoria finished sixth last year, but has 17 players back including Pat Hodgins, one of the top centers in the league. The Warriors will be bolstered by Doug Popadynec, a Victoria native who was a first-team all-star in Manitoba, and Lorne Hadley, an all-star at Merritt last season. Powell River could have an explosive attack with center Brent Riplinger, one of seven returnees from last year's third-place squad, and center Jay McNeill, who moved up from Junior B and scored four goals in the Paper Kings' last exhibition game. Nanaimo lost four of the top five scorers in the league to colleges, but the Clippers have top goalie Paul Taylor back and a slew of defenseman. Unfortunately, injuries already have sidelined two

crash. Kay (Dutka) Brown died when the plane she was trävelling in with her husband, Dave, crashed in Washington State. Rallying around Dutka during those tough times was his wife Sally and their three children, Brooke 11, Maiggen, 9 and Kyle, 7. Sally has also helped pay the bills because other than the small wage he receives from coaching, Dutka does not have any other work- related income. Dutka says one the greatest satifactions he has received from the team so far is the steady growth of the defence. The Clippers are the only team in the league to use a completely rookie defence. "We have one of the most in-, experienced teams in the league. Our style is changing every game we play. As players get more experienced they now have the confidence to do different things. Our defence is 75 per cent better than they were in October and by March they should be 50 per cent better.' However, part of that defence process was interrupted last week after the Victoria Cougars of the Western Hockey. League borrowed the Clippers' top defenceman Sean Stotts. Stotts, who was ' selected to play in the all-star game Jan. 15 in Nanaimo, returned Monday from Victoria. The Cougars used Stotts on a trial basis and a decision on where he will finish the season is expected to be made this week. 'Stotts will accompany, the Clippers on an road trip which will see them play games in Summerland Thursday, Friday in Kelowna and Penticton Saturday. Meanwhile, Kent Lewis of the Clippers will miss the all game due to a six-game suspension he received for head-butting in Nanaimo's 10-5 loss Friday to Penticton Knights. His spot will be taken by either Jeff Crossley of Richmond Sockeyes or Todd Jennings of the Clippers.

teams, including the Knights, and took points away from Penticton, Kelowna Packers and Merritt Warriors for using ineligible players. KNIGHTS WIN Meanwhile, Penticton got four goals from Lee Davidson in a 9-4 victory Tuesday night over Merritt Warriors to give the Knights a two-point edge over Kelowna Packers at the top of the BCJHL Interior Division. In the evening's only other game, Coast-Division-leading leading, Richmond Sockeyes lost home to second-place Delta Flyers. In addition to Davidson's four goals, Penticton got singles from Cal Brown, Keven Melrose, David Shields, Chris Anderson and Scott Rawson. Replying for the fifth-place Warriors, who were outshot 64-23, were Kevin Thorlakson, Brad Smiley, Rob Revell and Mike Catchpole. Gord Macinnes scored twice as the Flyers outhustled Richmond, which Delta now trails by 10 points. It was the Sockeyes' first loss to a Coast Division tear, and only their sixth defeat in 33 games. Delta goalie Chris Dickson was a key to the win, as the Flyers won while being outshot 39-30. Delta, which took: a 1-0 edge in the first period and went ahead 5-2 in the second, got singles from Rod Ware, Rich Steniford, Gerry Mumford and Mitch Eva nish. Jim Butterworth, Mike O'Hara and Darren Matias scored for Richmond. Next league play is Thursday, with Vernon at Kelowna and Nanaimo at Summerl and.

son, a Grade 12 student at Semiahmoo senior secondary, was not required to wear a neck protector. "Canadian amateur hockey and all hockey associations, they've done so very much to help protect the boys from being injured with all the hockey equipment and all the padding and everything they have out now. "And to make them 100-per-cent protected is almost an impossibility. So there is certainly no blame." But the self-employed businessman said he believes hockey players will wear special safety equipment only if they are forced to because they don't associate equipment u used infrequently with being part of the game. He said his son, who played hockey since he was five, dedicated his life to the sport. "He lived it, he dreamt it and after every game he'd come home wake me up and give me a hug and tell me all about his hockey night. About the good things and the bad things. He just lived it ... However, Henry Reimer, 18, victim of a similar accident last year, said he will not wear a protector. "That is a freak accident," Henry Reimer said of his injury last November and the one that I killed Butler. It took 50 stitches to close Reimer's cut. "I was out a month and a half," Reimer said from Chicago where he is on a hockey scholarship at the University of Illinois. "I wore one of those football (braces) for two weeks when I came back." Reimer said he "got a chill" when he heard about Butler. But he said he would not wear a protector because he does not think such an accident is likely to happen to him again. Reimer, 18, is a Matthew McNair high school graduate and was playing for Richmond Sockeyes when he was cut. "I saw it coming but couldn't react." He said he became confused and had no idea how serious his injury was. "If it was millimetres either way, he would be a statistic," said Vic LeMire, Reimer's coach last season.

by Stephen Gold The Richmond Sockeyes are coming to Chilliwack. The B.C. Junior Hockey League team will transfer to this community next season (this fall), The Chilliwack Progress has learned. A reliable source close to the Sockeyes organization told The Progress it is the Richmond franchise that will be based out of Chilliwack and playing its home games at the Chilliwack Coliseum come the 1990-91 hockey season. The owner of the Sockeyes would neither confirm nor deny that his team was on the move. "I guess I'm in a position right now where I have to say 'no comment'," Allan Brew said in a Jan. 11 telephone interview. "We're in an awkward position right now," he said. "So I don't want to confirm or deny anything. "We'll probably make an announcement in the next month or two." Official word that an existing franchise was moving to Chilliwack came on Jan. 8 from Jack Andrews, district recreation manager. At that time, however, the district would not state which team was on the move as the transfer still had to be officially approved by the BCJHL's board of governors. Originally that approval was expected
Richmond Sockeyes moving to Chilliwack

Abbotsford would meet first place Richmond Sockeyes in the playoffs if they catch Sidney. Thachuk got his first goal at 14:21 as he picked up a loose puck in the slot and deked Nanaimo goaltender Chris Fairlie to the backhand. Thachuk made it 6-4 at 15:58 on a similar play, but

this time he elected to shoot, sliding a low shot to the stick side of Fairlie. "This win makes it interesting for us," said Falcon coach Don Berry. "We are one game behind Sidney, but we have a lot tougher schedule. I'm not confident yet though because you never know with this team. We seem to go ahead two steps and back one." Berry felt the game was won along the boards in Nanaimo's end. He lauded forward Ian Alger, who collected four assists, including two on Thachuk markers. "Ian Alger won the game for us in the corners. He made the key passes to get the goals," said Berry. On the otherhand, Dutka accepted part of the blame for the loss. He was not pleased with the refereeing but didn't feel that was the sole reason they lost. Nanaimo took 111 minutes in penalties to Abbotsford's 95. "I could have used my roster better than I did. I should have used more guys, more often, rather than going with my key guys, but it was hard with all the penalties. "All year we have played well in the third period. Tonight we just couldn't do it again.' •Ward Carlson scored three goals including his league-leading 50th of the season - to lead Kelowna Packers to a 7-4 victory over Coast-Division-leading, at Kelowna. Richmond Sockeyes In other BCJHL action, Penticton dumped Vernon 7-4 and Summerland downed Langley 7-3. Vernon took a 3-1 first-period lead before succumbing to the Interior-Division-leading Knights, who got two goals and two assists from Greg Cyr. Scott Rawson also scored twice for Penticton, including one into an empty net. The Knights were outshot 36-33 by the third-place Lakers in a fast, almost penalty-free contest, but went ahead 5-4 in the second period and Rawson did all the scoring in the third. Singles for Penticton were by David Shields, Rod Summers and Chris Anderson. Scoring for Vernon were Shawn Lofroth, Trevor Pohl, Dennis Holland and Mark McLeary.

For Jim Gunn and Jeff James, tonight's game at Frank Crane Arena against the Victoria Cougars of the Western Hockey League is much more than just an exhibition contest. The two recent additions to Nanaimo Clippers are out to play as hard as they can and show the team were let go by that they both belong. Gunn, year old defenceman, played some games with the Cougars before being sent down the local BCJHL squad to as he said "get more experience." James, an 18 year old centre, played 1½ seasons with Richmond Sockeyes before asking for his release because he felt he wasn't getting enough playing time. In September, he attended the Victoria camp as a rookie. "I'm glad to be here and think this team (Clippers) has a good chance to take B.C. title,' explained the 6'1" Gunn. "I'm impressed with the rest of the players and the calibre of the management." He intends to do as well as he can in order to have another shot

In other BCJHL games Tuesday, Langley Eagles defeated Abbotsford Flyers 4-1 and Richmond Sockeyes drubbed Cowichan Capitals 14-1. The Penticton victory sealed first place in the Interior Division for the Knights, who have one game left in the regular season. They outshot the second-place Lakers, now four points behind, 40- 32 and held period leads of 5-2 and 7-4. Hull linemate Rod Schluter collected three goals. At Langley, the Eagles got two goals from Earl Graham and one goal and two assists from Neil Reimer in their 4-1 decision over Abbotsford in the last game of the season for both clubs. Reimer's three points gave him the Coast Division scoring crown with 121 points, one ahead of Randy Douville of Abbotsford who was kept off the scoresheet.

Then they capitalized on a* couple of giveways and the next thing we know, we're in a one-shot hockey game." Newcomer Wayne Stripp, signed Monday after being released by Richmond Sockeyes, enjoyed a productive debut with three assists. "Three assists, is that what he got?" asked Bestwick. by Mike McQuillan MetroValley News Service The New Westminster Royals have been the best in the B.C. Junior Hockey League's Coastal Division for the past two years. This season the club sits in the first place again, but you can't tell that from the crowds. Club owner Bruce Taylor paints a pretty picture when he claims the club gets 350 to 400 fans per game. The numbers are likely closer to 250 or 300. The whole experience flies directly in the face of his sportsbusiness philosophy. Winners bring in the fans, right? Wrong. "Let's face it," Taylor says with resignation, "winning teams don't fill seats." And that may possibly be the hardest lesson he has learned the past three years. A long-time benefactor of junior A hockey, Taylor has had shares in two other clubs. In his six years as an owner, Taylor's teams can boast s six coastal division titles, three BCJHL championships and one Centennial Cup (1987 with Richmond Sockeyes). But winning, for Taylor, has been confined to the ice. Fickle fans, a plethora of Junior B teams, little interest from Vancouver's electronic and daily print media or overworked hockey dads - take your choice, they're all possible reasons for the current financial predicament facing metropolitian Vancouver's remaining Junior A hockey team. Other Lower Mainland junior teams like the Richmond Sockeyes, Burnaby Bluehawks (formerly of Junior A league), Ladner Peguins and Nor West Caps have all hung up their jerseys over the past 20 years. Only New West survives today and that reign could be ending, if help, in the form of fans, doesn't come soon. "I think the writing is on the wall," says Taylor. "We have to do something and I'm not sure what it would be right now." Currently, eight junior B teams compete for the hockey fan dollar across the Lower Mainland. They may only attrat 300 or so fans per game, but that means fewer fans for Royals to draw on, he says. "Every place the Junior A's have moved out of, the Junior B's have moved into." With Junior A operating costs many times higher than Junior B franchises, those missing fans translate into two years of losses for the Royals. Taylor would not say how much the club has lost since 1988 but did confirm they had always operated in the red. The community press has been a great promotional tool for New West but, Taylor says, the problem with that is they serve one community. He needs to get his message out ot fans across the Lower Mainland. Vancouver's two daily newspapers aren't interested in the team until they get to the playoffs. "'They don't mind covering it a little bit but they don't want to be forced into covering it all the time. I think that's a big insult to the sport," says Taylor. Fathers and hockey-playing sons are bread and butter to the box office, but Royals are seeing less and less of families. Where else can one take

By KEVIN MITCHELL Sports Editor Nanaimo Clippers will honor former coach Larry McNabb tonight, perhaps hoping some McNabb magic will rub off at Civic Arena. The Clippers could use the extra help as they try knock off the stubborn Richmond Sockeyes, who have won all three B.C. Junior Hockey League meetings with Nanaimo this season. Corey Jones, coming off a strong showing in the Clippers' 6-4 win Tuesday over Cowichan Valley, will see more than the 24 shots he faced from the Whalers. "They're (Richmond) pretty decent up front," said Clipper coach Bill Bestwick. "They've got a lot of good talent." The Sockeyes have outscored the Clippers 19-14 in their three victories, two of which came in Richmond. "Jones will start," said Bestwick. "We have to show confidence in him after that win because he's going to be playing in 70 or 80 per cent of our games. We have to go with him when he's got a good feeling.' Bestwick will continue an experiment started by assistant coach Ian Ramsay last game which saw rookie blueliner Tom Arkell shifted to forward.

SOCKEYES HERE The 20-year-old's value to the Clippers becomes more evident against experienced clubs like Richmond Sockeyes, the Clippers' opponent tonight at Civic Arena (8). If the Clippers are to stay close to league leading Richmond they must get leadership from older players like Cayford. "I feel I should get at least three or four points a game. That's the part I have to contribute," he said. Dutka said unreasonable to expect kind of scoring output from Cayford, one of the league's best passers. Cayford's ability to get open, create havoc in front of the net and set up plays has gained the respect of at least one BCJHL visiting coach. Richmond coach Muzz MacPherson says the key to shutting down Nanaimo is to stop Cayford. "Some people are surprised the Clippers doing well but I'm

Against Richmond, Cayford feels Nanaimo will have to play one of their best defensive games of the year. The last time the teams met, Oct. 29 in Richmond, the Clippers lost 11-0. "It's going to be a defensive game on our part," he said. "We're going to have to bug them all the time and get them frustrated.' Dutka said the Clippers are not going to make the same mistakes they did in their 11-4 loss to Richmond earlier last month on home ice. He feels goaltending was a problem and will start Chris Fairlie in goal tonight. "I didn't think we had good goaltending (Kelly Meredith). He couldn't stop the easy shots," Dutka said. Cowichan Valley Whaler coach Dale Sproule knows what he wants for Christmas - - a new referee. Leading 3-2 in Saturday's B.C. Junior Hockey League contest versus the Richmond Sockeyes, the Whalers ran into a barrage of early third period penalties courtesy the whistle of ref Dave Dodgshon. Dodgshon is a fixture at most Whaler home games, due to an apparent shortage of qualified officials. "We played two periods of great hockey," said Sproule. "But we can't get away from the penalties. We got railroaded."

Falcons lose two Abbotsford Falcons suffered from Sockeye scoring and sinbin senences last Tuesday in B.C. Junior Hockey League play at Richmond Sockeyes, Coast division leaders, led 4-0 at the end of the first period and added two in the second on the way to a 9-1 win over the last-place Falcons. -Abbotsford's Richie Dusevic scored unassisted at 11:44 of the third. - Falcon goalkeeper Darcy D'Amours made 53 saves. The heavily-penalized Falcons had some questions about the officiating. (Should we call it o-Fisher-ating? Referee Jim Fisher was the man who prompted the wonderment.) The Abbotsford club - already outclassed - suffered further from penalties "We're not ready to play a club like that shorthanded," said the team's manager and assistant coach, Byron Hitchens "We cannot afford it against a firstplace team!". Darryl Regier picked up a threegame suspension for butt -ending. Falcons fell victim to Vernon Lakers 8-4 in a well-refereed game Saturday at MSA Arena. The victory capped Coast success for the visitors who had downed Langley 7-5 Friday, after two emptynet goals by Dennis Holland in the final 65 seconds had lifted Lakers over Richmond Sockeyes 6-4 Thursday night. Meantime. Sidney's 9-6 Saturday win over Langley lowered the Eagles further into st place on the Coast, ahead of Abbotsford.

The Flyers have two games in hand on the Sockeyes. The Clippers are now 6-16 on the road, but if you don't count three wins in Sidney and one in Salmon Arm, they've only beaten Merritt and Richmond away from Home. Nanaimo will take on Merritt and Burnaby in a pair of home games this weekend. Meanwhile, Ian Kidd. set a new BCJHL record Tuesday night, scoring once and drawing three assists in Penticton Knights' 10-2 thumping of Summerland Buckaross. Kidd now has 107 points (30 goals) in 45 games, breaking the old singleseason points record by a defenceman of 106, set by Abbotsford's Craig Redmond in the 1981-82 season. Redmond is now with Los Angeles Kings of the NHL. Kidd is 20-year-old Portland product who plans to play hockey at' the American collegiate level next year. has several U.S. schools to choose from. Joe Murphy and Murray Winnicki each scored twice for Penticton, who held period leads of 4-0 and 5-2. The win was the Kinghts* 41st in 45 games.

RICHMOND, B.C. (CP) T Dwayne Wills scored three goals as the Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Chilliwack Eagles 10-1 in the only B.C. Junior Hockey League game Wednesday night. Jason Talo had two goals for the Coastal Division leaders, who held period leads of 2-1 and 7-1. Singles came from Rick Rozecki, Barry Kress, Wayne Stripp, Steve Rutledge Garth Lamb. Ray Savard was the only scorer for the Eagles, who were outshot 61-35. Meanwhile, the Victoria Cougars scored five times in the third period to break open a tight game and beat visiting Seattle Thunderbirds 7-3. Bob Foglietta led the Victoria attack with two goals, while Grant Chorney, Len Barrie, Dwayne Keller, Guy Burr and Micah Aivazoff contributed singles. Glen Goodall scored twice for Seattle, with Kent Dochuk providing the other goal. Swift Current Broncos caught the Saskatoon Blades on an off night and broke out of a brief scoring slump to beat the Blades 5-3. In other games, the Prince Albert Raiders trimmed the Brandon Wheat Kings 6-2, and Portland Winter Hawks edged the Spokane Chiefs 6-5 in overtime.

to continue playing as a member of the Eagles. However, on two occasions the BCJHL asked MacPherson not to dress Savard. The Chilliwack coach complied with both requests. The second game for which Savard did not dress was Thursday, Feb. 25 against Abbotsford, after the Eagles had been eliminated from playoff contention two days earlier. That was followed by a letter from league officials on Saturday, saying the Eagles must forfeit the eight points they had won n while using Savard, now considered an "illegal player.' The points would have gone to Chilliwack's opponents in those games, giving the Flyers first place in the Coastal Division. Richmond Sockeyes would have finished second. But the league, after talking to MacPherson again, changed its decision regarding the points and left the standings as they had been. Said MacPherson, "If my appeal goes through, and if I'm right, what do they (the BCJHL) do? It wouldn't be fair to Richmond or Delta if two weeks down the road the league comes back and says, 'Hey we were wrong. "Right now, after the season is over, I hope I'm wrong. Because that

Another best-of-seven series determines division winners who meet in the BCJHL final. From there teams vye for provincial, regional and national titles. The Centennial Cup, emblematic of junior hockey supremacy in Canada, is the end of the line. *** Penticton Knights take on Summerland Buckaroos and Merritt Centennial meet Vernon Lakers in the Interior's best-of-seven semifinals now under way. ***

Coast playoffs are scheduled to start today (Wednesday) in the B.C. Junior Hockey League. But with who, where and at what time were all a mystery for Abbotsford Flyers until today (after -press deadline). Abbotsford took on cellar-dwelling Sidney Capitals Tuesday to determine whether they finish second, third or fourth •in the Coast standings. The difference between Langley Eagles, Richmond Sockeyes and the Flyers is no more than one point. Flyers' standing also rests on the outcome of Richmond's last league game. MSA Arena (859-8030) should have information about Flyers' playoff schedule today, says arenas manager Vince Kreiser. Henry Reimer scored four goals and added an assist to power Richmond Sockeyes to a 10-5 win over Burnaby Bluehawks in B.C. Junior Hockey League play in Richmond Tuesday night. In Abbotsford, Derrick Ruppel scored twice to lead Langley Eagles to a 9-4 win over the Flyers. In Richmond, the Sockeyes dominated the game with period leads of 3-1 and 7-3. Elliot Kagna scored twice for the winners and singles came from Mike Scardina, Lee McGowan, Rod Philpot and David Holmes. Rick Dietrich, Mike O'Brien, Glen Engevik, Pat Austin and Mike Moree replied for Burnaby. Richmond goalie Scott King made 43 saves while Ron Boyd and Ray Woodley combined to make 28 saves for Burnaby. In Abbotsford, Langley led 5-1 after the first period and 6-2 after 40 minutes. Scoring for the Eagles were Mark Recci, Richard Sloan, John Basco, Rod Ware, Keith Fair, Rob Clarke and Dean Duprey. Doug McFaul scored twice for the Flyers, while singles came from Peter Gill and Tony Carter. Langley's Jason Palmer made 24 saves, while Abbotsford's Bob Romano kicked out 55 shots on goal. BCJHL play resumes Thursday, when Nanaimo visits Burnaby, and Revelstoke plays host to Vernon.

A powerplay goal by Scott Edwards at 8:30 of the final period broke a 7-7 tie and propelled the Abbotsford Pilots to a back-and-forth 8-7 seasonopening victory over the expansion Richmond Sockeyes Friday night at MSA Arena. As might be expected, it was early season West Coast Junior Hockey League form on display, before less than 200 fans. "It showed us we have some weak spots to work on," said Pilots coach Sam Styles, who quickly added, "but it also showed us some promising points, too." Abbotsford took a 3-2 lead in the first period but Richmond, led by former B.C. Junior Hockey League star Jason Talo, who had a four-point night, stormed back with a pair of early goals to move into a 4-3 lead. However, it was now the Pilots turn to fly. Veteran defenceman Todd Geddes, named the team's offensive player of the game for the night for his playmaking and puck movement skills, started the comeback at 6:15. He helped set up newcomer Andrew llaender for the go ahead goal, on the powerplay, at 14:01. Then another newcomer, Jason Murrel, put the Pilots up by two with his second goal of the game at 17:10. In the third, second-year Pilot Shawn McAdie boosted the Pilots' lead to three when he struck at 3:13 - but then came the Sockeyes. Three goals in 2:17 brought them back even

sport ...Nanaimo minor hockey product Frank Furlan made his collegiate hockey debut with Michigan Tech Huskies Oct. 11 at Denver. Furlan, 18, turned in: a stellar performance with 38 saves as the Huskies fell 4-3 to the defending champion Pioneers. Denver got three of their goals on the power play. Michigan Tech bowed 8-3 to Denver the night before with former BCJHL goalie Dave Roach (Burnaby native) going the distance in goal for the Huskies. Furlan, who •spent last year with Sherwood Park Crusaders of the Alberta Junior League after one season with the Clippers, is a freshman at Michigan Tech. Todd Decker (Delta Flyers), Shawn Harrison (Kelowna Packers), Kip Noble (Kelowna) and Richard Novak (Richmond Sockeyes) are other BCJHL graduates with the Huskies. .Ron Butlin, chairman of the B.C. Games, sent us a copy of"a by BCTV pro-director en Ross Sullivan which proves the Games are taken seriously by people in this province. Sullivan notes that the BBM spring ratings showed that 284,00 people tuned in to the Terrace Winter Games Saturday, March 1 at 6:30 p.m., while 268,000 watched Hockey Night in Canada. Star Trek drew 63,000 viewers, while Laurier's People attracted 4,000. On the Sunday, the ratings showed 281,000 people watching the Winter Games, 191,000 taking in Walt Disney, 72,000 Star Trek and 34,000 Droids. Said Sullivan, "You've got to know that all the folks back home are watching, when you see a program on amateur sports is outperforming the professionals on Hockey Night in Canada." ...The Canuck Reunion Committee is asking for help in trying to locate past players, coaches, trainers, management and booster club members for a Moose Jaw Canuck hockey reunion next summer. Donna Kasper, secretary of the reunion committee, says the team started in the early 1930s and was in Moose Jaw until folding in 1984. The reunion is slated for July 31-Aug 2. Anyone associated with past Canuck teams is urged to write to: Canuck Reunion Committee, Box 1954, Moose Jaw, Sask., S6H 7N7. ...Vernon Daily News sportswriter Jim Judd was in Edmonton last week and interviewed Nelson native Danny Gare, a member of the Oilers' taxi squad of sorts this year. Gare, 32, said, "That is primarily the reason I came here...to get a Stanley Cup ring before I retire. I'm really enjoying it here. The talent level is high and the tempo, speed, etc. is much quicker than it was in Detroit or Buffalo.' Oiler coach-GM Glen Sather appears to have signed Gare as an unofficial assistant coach. Said Gare, "He (Sather) more or less told me to be a good man, both on and off the ice, and to be good with the younger players. He also wants me to stay i in shape and be ready anytime he figures he needs me out there.' .. Perhaps the best quote of the year so far comes from flaky Houston reliever •Charlie Kerfeld. It was just another night at the office for Richmond Sockeyes of the British Columbia Junior League. The Sockeyes whipped Merritt Warriors of the Interior Division 15-3 and coach Bob Wilms said his club must continue to look to Interior clubs for opposition the Coast Division can't provide. "We've gone two games scoring 13 and 15 goals," said Wilms. "We're probably playing with the best intensity and puck movement that we've had all year. "*Our intensity is up to the level to where it has to be for us to beat the Interior teams. We're running away with the Coast but we have to gear our game to the Interior teams." Larry McNabb said he would bet the farm on a Nanaimo Clipper victory Wednesday night at Civic Arena. And Delta Flyer coach Rick I Hopper, given the chance, probably wouldn't have taken McNabb up on such a bet. However, the Flyers lived up to their nickname in rolling to a relatively easy 6-1 win before 957 B.C. Junior Hockey League fans. Delta took the Coastal Division semi-final series 4-2 and now face Richmond Sockeyes in the final. The Sockeyes dusted off Juan de Fuca Whalers 6-1 to clinch their series. "I wasn't too optimistic coming over here," said Hopper. "We did some excellent forechecking and showed a lot of character and heart. The guys really dug down for this one. Everybody played well. (Goalie) Brian Tessier played exin his first game of the series.' "We just, weren't into the game tonight,' said McNabb. "There few guys I was disappointed in. I'm not going to mention any names...We had problems in our own end. They were sending two and three men in and didn't give us a chance to set up. I thought that was the best game they've played here all year." McNabb was at a loss to explain how his club could drop three contests on home ice. "Losing three games at home isn't smart hockey," he said. "'We did our job over there by winning two games." Two Clippers who looked Wednesday like they had learned hockey by correspondence, were linemates Todd Finner, on the ice for all but one 1 Delta goal.

Abbotsford Falcons are 2 and 21 after their 7-2 loss to the Interior Division's cellar-dwelling Shuswap Blazers on Saturday, but coach Byron Hitchens isn't about to give up. "December is the deciding month, and looking at some of the teams we have to meet, we still have a run at " the Coast playoffs," he said. Hitchens was trimming his 23-player roster down to 18 on Monday, the deadline for BCJHL squads to reduce to 25. cards. "We don't want to use all of our cards up right away," he said. We still have another deadline in January." He reported that some Falcons "are starting to throw their hands up" because of the losses. Falcons arrived home emptyhanded from their first Okanagan road trip over the weekend, also losing against Vernon Lakers on Friday and Summerland Buckaroos on Thursday. Murray Lawrence was coaching the team. Summerland Buckaroos led 4-2 and 5-3 by periods on their way to a 9-4 victory. Falcons'- Trevor Tigar poked two goals into the net while Ian Alger and Sheldon Sabad notched singles. Vernon jumped on the Falcons for a 10-4 win, outshooting the visitors 73-18. Sabad scored on three powerplay opportunities for the Falcons while Doneau Menard added a single. In order to provide fans with some idea of how the teams in the provincial Midget AAA hockey championships stack up, the Daily Press contacted various scouts and Western Hockey League coaches for their opinions on the teams. Here's how they assessed the teams, in the order they figure the teams will finish. 1. RICHMOND. . Fast team, with good forwards and good discipline Big, but immobile defence . Below average goaltending ... . May struggle in big rink. PLAYERS TO WATCH FOR - Defenceman Gary Hawkins, forward Kelly Matsuo, goalie Paul Taylor. 2. PRINCE GEORGE .. Small, quick forwards • average defence, below average goaltending . . . Had to go through tough competition to get to provincials. PLAYERS TO WATCH FOR - Centre Mark Merritt, goaltender Darren Aksenchuk, defenceman Ernie Boudreaul. 3. NANAIMO Fast team, but not much size. ... Excellent .... discipline and good coaching . • Excellent goaltending, defence not outstanding but gets job done . . . Home ice will help .. . Team is on a roll. PLAYERS TO WATCH FOR - Forwards Mike Biggs and Dwain Finner, goaltenders Kevin Romando and Danny Mattice. 4. KAMLOOPS - ... Very big team, both defencemen and forwards ... Average average ... defence, below goaltending Good grinding team, needs to win early games, will be able to grind other teams down in late games. PLAYERS TO WATCH FOR - Defenceman Dewayne Gallant, forwards Davis Payne and Chris Abraham. 5. COQUITLAM - ... Good team spirit, great work habits. Well-disciplined ... Had to beat out some good teams in Fraser Valley to advance ... Dark horse team... Won North Shore Winter Club tournament. PLAYERS TO WATCH FOR - Goaltender Ken Johnstone, forwards Greg Allen, Alan Vermeullen. 6. NELSON - .. Quick team, lots of finesse players . . . Not much size ... Defence is suspect ... Lack of competition in league may hurt. PLAYERS TO WATCH FOR - Centre Scott Hargreaves, right-wing Dean Mowery.

Nanaimo will face the Kootenay champion in first-round play under a seven-team format designed for the B.C. Midget AAA Hockey Championships, March 15-19 at Frank Crane Arena. The round-robin tournament could feature just six teams if Nanaimo wins the Vancouver Island title. Should Nanaimo not win Island crown, they would join the tourney as the host team. Under the seven-game format, Nanaimo would also meet Vancouver Island on the first day. Nanaimo is scheduled to play Fraser Valley in their lone March 16 contest, while battling Greater Vancouver 17, OkanaganMainline March 18 and PeaceCariboo March 19. Under the six-game format, Nanaimo will take on Cariboo in firstround play and then meet Fraser Valley, Kootenays, Vancouver and the Okanagan in the next four days of play. The tournament banquet will be held on the Tuesday night at the Tally-Ho.

Clippers 2, Sockeyes 7 By MICHAEL RHODE The Daily Free Press RICHMOND - Nanaimo Clippers know they can play hockey with the Coastal Division champion Richmond Sockeyes. It's just a case of breaking down and winning a game. Unfortunately for the Clippers, that didn't happen Wednesday night in Richmond as Matt Hervey's goal at 11:12 of the third period broke open a tight game and enabled the

Moller evened the score at 15:47 on a nice individual effort, deking Flaherty to the left side. Former Clipper captain Bill Hardy then scored the eventual winner at 17:43 as he drilled a low shot from gimme range past Flaherty. Kozak set play up from behind the net. "We took some pretty silly penalties in the first period and they really cost us," said Richmond coach Orland Kurtenbach. "Flaherty came up with some really fantastic saves. He really kept them in the game." Kurtenbach wasn't surprised at the goal scoring prowess shown by McCormick. "Mike's played better in the last two weeks. has a big league shot and he's big league strong. He should be able to put the puck in the net." Clipper coach Rich Dutka praised his club's effort despite a 10th straight loss to Richmond in front of 200 fans. "I don't think there were any letdowns tonight," said Dutka. "One thing that we didn't do as well tonight was our forechecking. Some missed scoring opportunities really hurt us as well.' Knapton, Finner and Corky Parkes. provided the Clippers with excellent penalty killing. Knapton and Finner both missed great scoring chances on the same penalty killing shift with Nanaimo up 2-1 in the second period. The Sockeyes outshot Nanaimo 56-39 and took 18 of 33 penalty minutes. Manton took a major and minor for instigating a fight with Sockeye toughie Jim Gunn, who wanted no part of Manton and drew a minor penalty. Clipper penalty king Craig Frankford received a 10-minute for verbally abusing. an official. LAKERS WIN Vernon Lakers took a 2-0 lead in their -seven BCJHL Interior Division se series Wednesday night by dumping Penticton Knights, the defending Centennial Cup champions 6-3. In other games, Langley Eagles edged Delta Flyers 4-3 in overtime and Kelowna Packers nearly had to go the overtime route but beat the Merritt Warriors 5-4. Vernon captain Doug Spooner scored two goals to lead the Lakers •past the Knights in Penticton. Also scoring for Vernon were Shawn Howard, Todd Demchuk, Craig Bedard and Campbell Blair, who scored the winner at 15:05 of the second period on the power play. Penticton goals came from Greg Guest, Ken MacArthur and Todd Hilditch. Eagles 4 Flyers 3 Brad Woitas scored his second goal at 6:44 of death overtime to give Eagles the first game of their series with the Flyers. Jeff McLean and Bruce Kenward also scored for Langley while Bruce Pritchard, Randy Olson and Nanaimo minor hockey product Aaron Nosky provided the Delta goals. Eagles goaltender Jamie Stewart stopped 31 shots while Chris Dickson made 39 saves for the Flyers who will be in Langley again Friday night for Garne 2. Packers 5 Warriors 4 Kevin Taillefer scored with 14 seconds remaining in regulation time to give the Packers a 2-0 lead in their quarter-final series ¡against the Warriors. Brent Mowery, Kerry Russell, Jamie Kurki and Bruce Bianchini were the other scorers for Kelowna which led 1-0 after the first period and 3-2 after 40 minutes. Steve Manuel and Frank Biller led the Warriors with two goals apiece. Manuel had a chance to win the game in the final minute when Merritt was awarded a penalty shot when Kelowna was called for delay of game. Goalie Dusty Imoo, who finished the night with 36 saves, refused to be fooled by any of Manuel's fakes and smothered the penalty, shot. Merritt goalie Randy Beekhuizen blocked 57 shots. Third game in the series is Friday in Kelowna.

By FRANK LUBA Sports Reporter They weren't expecting Richmond Sockeyes to sweep the Kelowna Packers but, after all, what do they know? The defending Coastal Division champion Sockeyes surprised the experts and coach Orland Kurtenbach, and delighted more than 1,100 fans at Richmond Arena last night by edging the Interior Division title- Packers 3-2. That allowed Richmond to win the best-of-nine B.C. Junior Hockey League championship series in five games, and stretch their playoff winning streak to 13.

Clippers 0, Sockeyes 7. By KEVIN MITCHELL Staff Writer Richmond Sockeyes may as well bring out the shades. The Sockeyes' future looks pretty bright after four-game sweep of Nanaimo Clippers in the best-ofseven Coastal Division semi-final series. "I think they'll go all the way," said Clipper all-star blueliner Grant McDonald: "They've got a pretty strong team and they've got good coaching. They may be a little weak in their goaltending. They tend to give up the odd soft goal." Said Sockeye coach Orland Kurtenbach after Richmond waltzed to a 7-0 win Saturday night at Civic Arena, "In order to get to the Interior we have to win the next series. We have to take them one a time. It's going to be a challenge...I would say our club has been increasingly playing better over the last month and a half as we have been getting players back from injuries and suspensions.' Richmond, with an awesome defensive corps headed by former Western Hockey Leaguers Trevor Dickie and Matt Hervey and exMaine Black Bear University star Stan Czenczek, beat the Clippers 12 straight times, including the four playoff victories. "You've got to be disappointed losing 7-0 in the final game" said Clipper captain Sean Stotts, who had a rare bad game in front of 600 fans. "They forechecked us really well and we just couldn't get anything going." Stotts figures the Clippers will stay basically intact next year. "There's no doubt about it, I'll be back here next year, Stotts. "Hopefully everybody be back said. here next year. There were a couple of guys who weren't really trying (in the playoffs) but you have to live with it and pick up the slack." Clipper coach Rich Dutka, whose club fell to Delta Flyers (six games) in last year's semi-final series, is excited about the 1987-88 season. "I think we've started something here that will be successful ih the (future,' said Dutka. "We gave our fans, who were the greatest in the league, an honest effort in 24 of 26 games. Added Dutka, "Last year we were fairly healthy going into the playoffs, but this year we had our work cut out for us with three or four guys who shouldn't have been playing due to injuries.' NHL in the 1940s, Mariucci was surprised by the lack of knowledge of the sport outside the four American NHL cities. Mariucci set out to change that trend. One of his biggest contributions was his almost total commitment to recruiting U.S.-born players when he was coach at the University of Minnesota 1952-66 while almost every other successful college team was bringing in Canadians. "We recruited Minnesota kids. We played Minnesota kids. We became respectable with Minnesota kids. We won with Minnesota kids, maybe not as much as y we would have liked, but to win more we would have had to destroy our basic principles," said Mariucci, who was 215-148-18 in 14 years with the Gophers. His teams never won a national championship, but Minnesota was runner-up in 1953- 54 and 1954-55 and finished third in 1960-61. Mariucci's "godfather" tag was a tribute to the many great hockey minds he fostered. Some of the people who either played or coached under him were: Herb Brooks, former New York Rangers and 1980 U.S. Olympic coach; Lou Nanne, Minnesota North Stars general manager; Bob Johnson, current Calgary Flames and former Wisconsin and 1976 U.S. Olympic coach; Dave Peterson, 1988 U.S. Olympic coach; Glen Sonmor, former Gophers and North Stars coach; Doug Woog, current Gophers coach, and Willard Ikola, current Edina high school coach who is the second-winningest prep hockey coach in Minnesota history.

MINNEAPOLIS (AP) John Mariucci, the beloved "godfather" of Minnesota hockey, died Monday night after a long bout with prostate cancer. He was 70. Mariucci didn't begin playing hockey until he was. a junior at Eveleth high school, one of only a handful of Minnesota schools to play the sport.

Blues 8 North Stars 5 BLOOMINGTON, Minn. (AP) Doug Gilmour had one goal and three assists and Bernie Federko had a pair of goals and one assist as the St. Louis Blues, behind four power-play goals, trounced the Minnesota North Stars 8-5 National Hockey League victory Monday night. Gilmour's 34th goal of the season came during a three-goal spurt in a 3:42 span midway through the opening period to give the Blues a 3-2 lead. Minnesota had enjoyed a brief 2-0 lead on goals by Keith Acton and Brian MacLellan. The Blues scored at 11:06 when Mark Hunter put in his 35th goal of the season on NHL the power play. • Gilmour tied the game 2-2 just 28 seconds later when he grabbed Tim Bothwell's wide shot behind the net and circled in front to beat North Stars goalie Don Beaupre. The Blues went ahead 3-2 at 14:48 on Federko's first goal on : a rebound shot off the boards by Greg Paslawski. St. Louis got another power-play goal at 9:15 of the second period to take a 4-2 lead. Mark Reeds got the goal after taking a pass from Federko on a 2-on-1 break. The North Stars pulled within 4-3 at 12:29 of the second period on Dirk Graham's goal. But the Blues got four goals in the third period, including Federko's second on the power play. St. Louis's other goals came from Paslawski and Ron Flockhart, and an open-net goal by Rick Meagher. The beleaguered Edmonton. Oilers did their absolute best Monday night to give the appearance of a team heading for disaster. Before the game, the Oilers ran the risk of thinking their appearance against the New Jersey Devils might prove something. Whatever Edmonton wanted to think - whatever the players think now - they proved nothing positive. They can't be blamed for seeking a crutch, I suppose, but any player who claims to notice a similarity between the Devils and the Calgary Flames is fooling himself. Sure, the Oilers still have a bit of a cushion in their ongoing bid to stay ahead of the Philadelphia Flyers and the hated Calgary Flames for first place overall. Staying there will mean extra money, but beating the weakest team in the Patrick Division isn't calculated to build confidence something the Oilers sorely need since their self-esteem was shaken by losses to Calgary in back-to-back weekend games. Besides, finishing first in the regular season is feeble consolation for the prospect of taking an early power from the Stanley Cup playoffs for the second year in a row. Events of the last few days have placed the Oilers under intense pressure. Much is self-imposed. They remain the most taiented team in the National Hockey League. Man for man, none of Edmonton's rivals is close. But hockey isn't played mano a mano. It was designed as a team event. At their best, the Oilers understand the concept. They handle the puck unselfishly. They cover up for each other. Win or lose, they spread the praise and share the blame. At their worst, it's a different story. Outsiders are left to search for any real semblance of closeness. A lot of media guys guess that it hasn't. The impression of conflict goes back to last spring, when coaches k kept insisting the Oilers refused to follow instructions in the disaster against Calgary. Things don't seem much better this time around. Boss Glen Sather maintains the Oilers are lovers not fighters although he's convinced they can look after themselves. First he talks about the need for increased team discipline. Then he wonders whether there's any point in counting on officials to call even the most blatant of penalties against Oiler opponents. He should talk more, I submit, about the dismal checking that surfaces sometimes as it did when Rich Chernomaz punched home a loose puck for an important New Jersey goal in the third period after Andy Moog had turned in a spectacular save. He should talk about the number of times Oiler players have been left free to avoid interviews - especially after they play poorly. He should talk about the fact that the Oilers have a choice. They can follow instructions, work hard and give themselves a* chance to win their third Stan-: ley Cup in four years and go down in history as an outstanding team. Or they can pout when things get tough, blame events beyond their control and label themselves for all time as

men's fastball team for several years. Dickey also leaves his mother-inlaw, Joan Drayton, parents, Peg John Dickey, of Nova Scotia, and four brothers. The funeral is set for 2 p.m. Tuesday in Ryerson United Church.

Bruins get blown out Spokane Chiefs scored three second-period goals in two minutes and 14 seconds to down New Westminster Bruins 7-1 in Western Hockey League action at Queens Park Arena Sunday night. Cal Zankowski scored the lone goal for the Bruins, who lost for the 18th time in their last 19 games. The Bruins were also losers on Saturday, falling 9-4 to the Winter Hawks in Portland. Mario Desjardins, Scott Daniels, Zankowski and Darren Schwartz tallied for the Bruins, who were outshot 44-33 by Portland. In Kamloops, WHL scoring leader Rob Brown picked up a goal and five assists to lead the Blazers to a 10-2 victory over Victoria Cougars. Brown broke Bruce Eakin's single-season assist record of 125 in the contest. Delta ousts Langley Delta Flyers knocked Langley Eagles out of the B.C. Junior Hockey League playoffs Sunday, winning the deciding game in their -seven Coastal Division semi-final series 5-3. Peter Holmes scored three goals, including the winner, as the Flyers advance to the division final against Orland Kurtenbach's Richmond Sockeyes. In Interior Division final action, Vernon Lakers took a 2-1 lead in their best-of-seven series against Kelowna with a 5-2 victory over the Packers.

Bisons, Bears top all-stars EDMONTON (CP) - The Manitoba Bisons and the Alberta Golden Bears dominated the 1986-87 Canada West University Athletic Association all-star hockey teams announced Wednesday. Alberta had three players on the first team and Manitoba had two players on each of the first and second teams selected by the eight coaches. Alberta players named to the first team were defenceman Parie Proft and forwards Stacey Wakabayshi and Dennis Cranston. Joining them are goaltender Larry Dyck and defenceman Chris Saint-Cyr of Manitoba and forward Paul Geddes *of Calgary, the record-breaking conference scoring champion.

The loss means the Kings, who have allowed 32 goals in the last three games, are all but eliminated from the playoffs. Kings coach Marc Pezzin said that could make a little easier for the team. "We've got to keep the game fun at this point," Pezzin said. "'There's no use putting pressure on them right now. By The Canadian Press The race for second place in the Western Hockey League's East Division tightened up Wednesday as the Saskatoon Blades defeated the Prince Albert Raiders. In other games, Brandon downed Regina 8-7, Swift Current defeated Calgary 4-3 and Portland edged Spokane 6-5. Blades 5 Raiders 3 The Blades need only a tie Friday to clinch a spot in the second round of the playoffs. Darryl Daignault fired two third-period goals to snap a 3-3 tie and move Saskatoon within one point of the second-place Raiders. Jack Bowkus, Shaun Van Allen and Blaine Chrest also scored for the Blades. Steve Gotaas, Mike Modano and Wayde Bucsis replied for Prince Albert. "It was intense right from the start," said the Daignault, Blades who was picked up by as an over-age player in late January. Hawks 6 Chiefs 5 The Portland Winter Hawks scored two quick goals midway through the third period to beat the Spokane Chiefs. Trevor Pohl and Dennis Holland each had two goals for Portland, with singles from Jim Latos and Jamie Nicolls. Mike Rickert scored twice for Spokane while Mike Berger, Rob Friesen and Gregg Delcourt added singles. Portland held a 36-34 edge in shots over Spokane.

Clippers 1, Richmond 9 BY MICHAEL RHODE For The Daily Free Press His Nanaimo Midget A Clippers, had performed so well in Island playoffs and in their stirst three provincial final games, ran into a sizzling hot hockey team in day four of the five day provincial Midget AAA hockey championships Wednesday at Frank Crane Arena. Richmond at 3-0 Nanaimo 2- 0-1 were the only two teams who still had a chance of claiming the provincial title, so in effect, Wednesday night's game was the championship final. Richmond, Greater Vancouver champions, proved too much for the Island champions in coasting to 9-1 victory to clinch the B.C. title, and earn a spot inte B.C./Alberta series to decide the Pacific representative in the Air Canada Cup. In other games Wednesday, Coquitlam beat Kamloops 7-3 and Prince George beat Nelson by the same score. "I'm elated," said Richmond coach Wayne Tulloch. "If some one had said to me at the start of the year that we would win the B.C. championship probably would have laughed. But we have progressed through the year with hard work and determination. I'm really happy for the guys." It wasn't so much the final score that hurt. It was the fact that the game for all intents and purposes was over very early. It was just :12 into the game that Richmond took a 1-0 lead and 1:06 when the winner was scored. The combination of Brad Costa and Kelly Matsuo did heavy damage early as they were in on Richmond's first three goals. Costa made it 1-0 as he picked up a rebound in front of Clipper goalie Kevin Romando and tucked it in the left side, and just 54 seconds later Matsuo, the Richmond captain, got the winner as his point shot trickled in past Romando. Matsuo made it 3-0 at 5:14 with a shot past Clipper goaltending replacement Dan Mattice.

Hervey swooped in from the point to grab the rebound after Kelowna netminder Dusty Imoo made a spectacular save point-blank shot by Sockeye forward Dean Rutledge. Tucker injured BUFFALO, N.Y. (AP) Centre John Tucker, the Buffalo 'Sabres' leading scorer, is out of the lineup indefinitely with a back ailment, a spokesman for the National Hockey League team said Saturday.

EDMONTON (CP) - Edmonton Oilers' general manager Glen Sather said Saturday left winger Jaroslav Pouzar has been invited to try out for the National Hockey League team and will join the club on the current road trip. Pouzar played for three seasons for Edmonton before leaving to play in West Germany for the last two years. During the current season, the 35-year-old former star with the Czechoslovakian national team, finished third in league scoring by firing 26 goals in 30 games with Iserlohn of the West German first division.

Park's goal sparks Team Canada vets Canadian Press HAMILTON - Defenceman Brad Park scored with 3:43 left in the third period to give the National Hockey League veterans a 6-5 victory Friday night over the Soviet Union in the first game of a rematch of the inaugural 1972 NHL-Soviet series. The teams received a standing ovation from the 12,114 spectators at Copps Coliseum following the skillful, clean game commemorating one of international hockey's greatest events. The line of Bobby Clarke, Bill Barber and Reggie Leach, all former Philadelphia Flyers, inspired the Team Canada oldtimers. Barber scored twice, with other goals by Don Awrey, Bill Goldsworthy and Mickey Redmond.

The Kelowna Packers earned a berth in the B.C. Junior Hockey League final Friday night, getting two goals from Kevin Taillefer en route to a 6-3 win over the Vernon Lakers in the sixth game of the best-of-seven Interior Divison final. The Packers meet the winner of the Richmond Sockeyes-Delta Flyers Coastal Divison semi-final. The Sockeyes lead that matchup 3-0 and could wrap up the divison title tonight in Delta. In Delta last night, Mike McCormick scored what turned out to be the winning goal at 18:21 of the second period as the Sockeyes defeated the Flyers 4-1. Richmond got its other goals from Brian Kozak, Jason Phillips and Bryan Moller. Delta's lone goal came from Tim Kranjc.

FLINT, Mich. (AP) - Frank Perkins, a player and assistant coach for the International Hockey League's Flint Spirits, has died from what apparently were natural causes. He was 27. Perkins, who came to the team this season, was pronounced dead Tuesday night at St. Joseph Hospital. He was discovered at his home by his fiancee, Cathy Stoker, at about 6 p.m. Tuesday. An autopsy was planned for today. Team members would make a decision about whether they'd take the ice tonight in Flint in an IHL game against Salt Lake City. Perkins, who played junior hockey briefly in Kitchener, Ont., in 1977-78 before moving on to the Sudbury Wolves, appeared in 53 games for the Spirits this season. He scored eight goals and had 13 assists. Spirits coach Rick Dudley said Perkins had a back problem that he didn't talk about. "He would get bumped and come back for more, even though you knew he was hurting," Dudley said. "That kind of desire rubbed off on the entire team and that's one of the reason we have been successful."
