VICTORIA - Brent Hughes scored his third goal at 14:29 of the third period Tuesday night to lift the Victoria Cougars to a 6-5 win over the New Westminster Bruins in a Western Hockey League game. Hughes scored on a two-on-one break, electing to shoot the puck along the ice to the stick side of New Westminster goaltender Ray Woodley. Wayne Stripp, Clayton Young and Guy Burr added a goal each for the Cougars, who led 4-2 after two periods. The first period ended tied 2-2. Cal Zankowski scored twice for the Bruins. Mario Desjardins, Darren Schwartz and Stuart Sage had a goal each. Woodley faced 39 shots while Victoria netminder Mike Doyle was tested 35 times. Each team was assessed eight minor penalties. Bruins play Thursday at Queens Park Arena against Seattle.

Frank Romeo, who stopped 27 shots, few of them tough, against the team he played for last season. "I think they can play better and we played the best I've ever seen. I still think it will be a long series." Romeo called the whitewash "sweet revenge" for the manner in which he was dropped by the Packers. "They weren't interested in me after we got knocked out of last year's playoffs by Penticton," said the 20-year-old netminder. "They thought they could go with what they had.' "The series will settle down after this," predicted Richmond coach Orland Kurtenbach. "We know it's not going to be this easy." Dave Tomlinson led the Sockeye offence with two goals while Jason Phillips, Bill Hardy, Mike Claringbull, Mike McCormick and Steve Jaques had singles.

By ELLIOTT PAP It wasn't difficult for Kelowna Packer coach Bill Hogaboam to analyze his team's stunning 7-0 loss to Richmond Sockeyes in Tuesday's opener of the B.C. Junior Hockey League championship series. The Packers, who posted a 45-5-2 record during the regular season, were an embarrassment last night, their uniforms about the only thing showing up at Richmond Arena. "They outhustled us all over the ice," said Hogaboam unhappily. "They bumped us and took the body all night. We lacked intensity. We didn't force them in their own end and they moved the puck fairly easily. "It's hard to believe we were that flat for the whole 60 minutes. We played horrible and everybody on a our team knows it." Second game in the best nine final goes tonight, 7:30, at Richmond Arena with Sockeye personnel predicting an entirely different affair. "I think they weren't mentally prepared," said Richmond goalie

-BRIEFLYHayes eulogized by Nixon Sun News Services Former U.S. President Richard Nixon was among those who eulogized Woody Hayes Tuesday at a service in Columbus, Ohio, for, the one-time State football coach. Approximately 1,400 people attended the service, including some of Hayes' former players among them Paul Warfield, Archie Griffin, Art Schlichter and John Brockington - and a former rival from Michigan, coach Bo Schembechler. In 33 years of coaching -28 as the head man at Ohio State - Hayes compiled a remarkable 238-72-10 record. He died of a heart attack last Thursday at the age of ' 74. Soccer great retires Socrates, one of the last great Brazilian soccer stars, has decided to cut short his career by six months to return to his previous profession - physician. His contract with Flamengo of Rio de Janeiro was due to expire in September but the 33-year-old Socrates decided to terminate it when he announced Monday the end of a career that included trips to two World Cup campaigns, in Spain in 1982 and Mexico last year. See You Then again See You Then held off a late drive by American challenger Flatterer to win the Waterford Crystal Champion Hurdles Tuesday at Cheltenham, England for a record-tying third year in a row. It was the fourth time a horse has won the gruelling steeplechase three consecutive years. The other triple-winners were Sir Ken, Hatton's Grace and Persian War. Canadian joins Indy Scott Goodyear became the second Canadian this year, along with Ludwig Heimrath Jr., scheduled to compete on the 1987 Indy car circuit. The 27- year-old Toronto driver, the 1986 East Coast Formula Atlantic champion, has been hired to drive for the Buffalo-based Gohr Racing Team on the 1987 PPG Indy Car World Series circuit. WHL clubs fined The Western Hockey League fined the Spokane Chiefs and Victoria Cougars $2,500 each Tuesday and announced suspensions following a pre-game scuffle last Friday in Victoria. Spokane coach Peter Esdale and Wayne Naka, coach of the Cougars, along with players Tony Horacek of the Chiefs and Kent Lewis of Victoria, were suspended for three games.

Barszczewski is an 18-year-old Powell River product who spent last year with the Clippers and was a victim of the numbers game in Nanaimo this season. He was quickly grabbed by the Isles. "It was either play in Kerry Park or I wasn't going to play hockey this year, said Barszczewski. "I'd like to play Junior A again next year, but not here. It would depend on who's coaching here...The big difference from Junior A and Junior B is the speed and there's a lot more hitting in Junior A. Junior B isn't as serious either." As for the Japanese opposition, Barszczewski has heard firsthand reports. "I talked to a couple guys who have been there. One guy I know from Notre Dame played there for two and a half years and he said they have a lot of big guys." Asked if the Isles were serious about winning the five-game series, Barszczewski said, "We want to kick their butts." In addition to hockey, the Islanders will spend two days at a popular Japanese resort for some rest and relaxation. The Islanders take 17 skaters to Japan March 6 and return March 22. Kerry Park's playoff schedule will open upon their return from Japan.

By The Canadian Press Nanaimo product Bob Foglietta scored once as Moose Jaw Warriors edged Prince Albert Raiders in one of five Western Hockey League games played Wednesday night. In other games, Saskatoon beat Calgary 4-2, Regina got by Brandon 5-4 in overtime, Portland defeated Seattle 6-4, and New Westminster downed Spokane 5-2. VANCOUVER - Bob Nystrom plans court action against the National Hockey League Players' Association to champion the cause of the borderline player. Recent changes in the NHLPA's disability insurance plan and the new collective bargaining agreement with the league have cost the New York Islanders' assistant coach a potential $500,000 in lost benefits. He said this week he isn't out for himself or is trying a squeeze play on the union, but wants to show that all NHLPA members are not equal when it comes to claiming a disability benefit. "I'm not bitter, I'm not crying the blues and I'm not trying to drain the last bit of money out of the NHLPA," said the 34-year-old former right winger who was forced to retire from the Islanders last season because of an injured left eye. Nystrom last played for the Islanders under a termination contract the lowest form of NHL job security which he signed in August, 1985. A month later an insurance company threatened to cancel the disability insurance plan unless the NHLPA drastically revised its payment schedule. Under the new disability agreement, all players under termination contracts, mostly older and fringe players, are excluded from coverage. Nystrom was denied a claim. Previously, any player who suffered a career-ending injury, regardless of age or contract status, would be compensated for $175,000 (U.S.). "I looked into it and found about 13 per cent of all NHL players are playing on a termination," Nystrom said. "These guys are still members of the NHLPA, they pay their dues, they're still playing the same tough, hard game and they're open to the same danger of being injured as anyone else, yet they're not covered." Alan Eagleson, executive director of the NHL.PA, said players on termination contracts became ineligible for disability benefits after Sept. 15. 1985, because too many players took advantage of the system.

Kelowna goaltender Dusty Imoo and a powerful defence helped the Packers trip the Juan de Fuca Whalers 5-2 in B.C. Junior Hockey League action Thursday at Juan de Fuca Recreation Centre. The Whalers outshot the Packers, who hold down first place overall in the league with a 40-3-2 won-lost-tied record, 33-27. But the home club still trailed by period scores of 3-1 and 4-1 before a crowd of 680 fans. Bruce Bianchini, Pat Mangold, Rob Steele, Kerry Russell and Mike Murinko scored for Kelowna. Phil Soukoroff and Sheldon Nohr replied for Juan de Fuca, which has a 16-30 won-lost record. The third period proved chippy with three major fights breaking out. Murinko, Stan Marple and Kevin Tallifer of the Packers and Ron Bhala, Dino Buttizoni and Bruce Courtnall of the Whalers each received five-minute majors for fighting and game misconducts during the final 10 minutes of play. Kelowna's strong defence repeatedly kept the Whalers at bay. Juan de Fuca players who did get through were stopped short by Imoo, although three of the Juan de Fuca shots hit a post. The Whalers face the Richmond Sockeyes at 8 p.m. Saturday in Juan de Fuca.

Times-Colonist news services Keith Crowder scored from close range at 19:07 of the third period and the Boston Bruins edged struggling Pittsburgh 6-5 in a National Hockey League game Thursday night despite three goals and two assists by the Penguins' Mario Lemieux. The game at Boston ended in a bench-clearing brawl as Pittsburgh's Randy Cunneyworth and Crowder traded punches after the buzzer. There were a few side battles before a slugfest between Dan Frawley of the Penguins and Jay Miller of the Bruins touched off a general melee that lasted for several minutes. Crowder poked in his 13th goal just before he tripped over teammate Charlie Simmer, who pushed the puck away from goalie Gilles Meloche while stretched out on the ice.

Times-Colonist news services Yvan Cournoyer remembers Paul Henderson's last-minute, CanadaSoviet Union series-winning goal in Moscow 15 years ago as if it were yesterday. Next month, Cournoyer, Henderson and 15 other members of the 1972 Team Canada squad face 20 members of the same Soviet team in a three-game exhibition series billed as "Relive the Dream.' Cournoyer eminisced about the 1972 series at a Montreal news conference Thursday to promote the upcoming rematch. The teams were tied 5-5 and the eight-game series that had Canadians huddled around every available television set was dead even, each team having won three games, with one game tied. Cournoyer was flanked by Phil Esposito and Henderson and play was in the Soviet end when, with 34 seconds remaining, Henderson banged in his third straight gamewinning goal. Cournoyor jumped into Henderson's arms, creating perhaps the most famous pictorial image of the emotional series. "Phil passed to Paul and he scored," said Cournoyer. "It was just a matter of seconds. "I was nervous, but we did it." Gordie Howe, who was playing for Houston of the t World Hockey Association and wasn't allowed to play in the 1 1972 series, has accepted an invitation play at least one game while former Canadiens great Guy Lafleur will also suit up for Canada.

MONTREAL (CP) - Washington Capitals' right winger Mike Gartner led all scorers with 13 goals and 18 points in 10 games last month to win the National Hockey League's player-of-the-month award for February, the NHL announced Monday. Gartner had a nine-game scoring streak to help his club to a 6-4 wonlost record. He also played his 600th career game, tied a club record for most points in one game with six, set a team-record for his 11th three-goal game and set Capitals' mark for short-handed goals in a season at six.

McMillan, former president of Biltmore Hats Ltd. in Guelph, Ont., who was active in the Ontario Hockey Association, died Friday of cancer at his home in Guelph. He was 79. McMillan started in the stockroom at Biltmore in 1926. He served as president from 1956 until 1974 and was a director and major shareholder. From 1967 u until 1982 he was chairman of the board. Under his leadership company became the largest hat manufacturer in Canada, taking, over Buckley Hat Manufacturing, Brill Hat and Cap and, finally, its largest competitor, John B. Stetson Co. Canada The company also expanded into the United States and its products became known throughout North America. The company is now known as Biltmore-Stetson (Canada) Ltd. McMillan was also a hockey and baseball player and was a member of the Guelph Maple Leafs baseball team when it won the intercounty championship in 1928. Later he coached a hockey team at the Ontario Veterinary College at Guelph and was an 1 executive with Guelph Biltmore Madhatters when the team won the Memorial Cup in 1952. He leaves his wife, Dorothy, a daughter, Norma, a son, Michael, and six grandchildren.

MONTREAL (CP) - Organizers of Rendez-Vous 87 festivities had a sumptuous preview for the 10-course gourmet dinner planned for 1,500 guests in Quebec City this week. The preview event featured the finest culinary achievements of 31 top chefs from Canada, the United States and the Soviet Union. "This is a unique opportunity for Canadian and American chefs to work alongside Soviet chefs," said Jean Soulard, the executive chef of the Quebec City Hilton who led the international team. The gourmet dinner was part of the Rendez-Vous 87 festival, the centrepiece of which was a two-game hockey series between the National Hockey League all-stars and the Soviet national team. While the hockey players squared off on the ice, chefs were hard at work in the kitchens. They were divided into six teams, with each responsible for at least one course. The Soviets, with five chefs, prepared black, white and red caviar on ice sculptures with garnishes of blinis, pumpernickel bread and hard-boiled egg. This was served with Kir and vodka on the rocks flavored with lemon, pepper or aniseed. Next, the Soviets served a cold entree of smoked trout with sour cream. Canada followed with a pheasant terrine with a burgundy sauce, a beaver consomme with pearled manioc, saffron shrimp and an apple sorbet. THE CANADIAN culinary team is headed by Georges Chauvet of Tourism Canada in Ottawa while the Quebec chefs were led by Marcel Kretz of Hotel la Sapiniere of Val David, Que. Finally, the Americans served a meat dish - bison fillet with oignon fondue accompanied by fiddleheads in basil and wild rice with red pepper cubes.

KELOWNA, B.C. (CP) - Richmond Sockeyes jumped to a 4-0 lead in the opening period and relied on intensity and rugged play en route to a 7-5 B.C. Junior Hockey League playoff victory over Kelowna Packers. Richmond holds a commanding 3-0 margin in the best-of-nine league championship series. Dean Rutledge, Dave Tomlinson and Jason Philips each scored twice for Sockeyes while Mike McCormick added a single. D Kelowna was led by Jason Ruff and Pat Mangold, each with two goals, while Jamie Kurki rounded out the scoring. Richmond coach Orland Kurtenbach said "the key to any hockey game is if you've got people who are intense and taking the body. That's what it takes to win.' His counterpart, Kelowna coach Bill Hogaboam, conceded his team was dominated. "They outbumped us, they were all over top of us and bang, it ended up 4-0 (after one period). We only played 40 minutes of hockey tonight. Richmond is too disciplined to fall four goals behind." Richmond goaltender Frank Romeo made 26 saves while Packers Dusty Imoo and Lucien Carignan combined to stop 26 shots. The fourth game in the series goes tonight at Kelowna. •In Kamloops, Nanaimo Bantam B Clippers got four goals from Justin Heathcote in an 8-6 win over Kamloops at the B.6. Bantam B Hockey Championships Friday. Dale Ballance, Steve Sloan, Dan Phillips and Jon McColm added singles for Nanaimo, who meet Kamloops and North Shore games today. Mike Feser was in goal for Nanaimo. •Todd Sceviour scored two goals Friday night as a record Swift Current crowd of 2,872 watched the Broncos defeat the Moose Jaw Warriors 6-4 for their fourth straight win.

Soccer players get just as much press as the Royal Family, and not just for scoring goals either. Take Mo Johnston, the 23- year-old star Celtic and Scotland striker for example. Johnston, 23, made the papers when he was fined 500 pounds (approximately $1,000) for his part in a disco fracas. He was rapped by Sherriff Archie Bell, who said, "Players who are remembered over the years are the ones who have disciplined themselves both on and off the field." And who should you see on a visit to Madame Tussauds Wax Museum in London? Why England star keeper Peter Shilton of course. I had a ball getting caught up in soccer -crazed Britian for two weeks and next week I'll report on British ice hockey, which is growing in popularity all over the country.

NCAA powers triumph By The Canadian Press The defending-champion Michigan State Spartans and the Harvard Crimson, finalists in the U.S. college hockey championship game one year ago, are pining for another confrontation this spring. Both posted easy victories in quarter-final games Friday night - the Spartans getting a 33-save performance from goalie Norm Foster of Vancouver, downing the Maine Black Bears 6-2 at East Lansing, Mich., and the Crimson defeating Bowling Green Falcons 7-1 at Cambridge, Mass. The Minnesota Gophers beat the Boston College Eagles 4-1 in Boston and the No. 1-ranked North Dakota Fighting Sioux defeated the St. Lawrence Saints 3-1 in Grand Forks, N.D., in other quarter-final games. Guy Perron scored his second goal to move Maine within 3-2 early in the third, but goals in the final 12 minutes by Mitch Messier of Regina, Brian McReynolds of Penetanguishene, Ont., and Kevin Miller of Lansing, Mich., ensured victory. While Foster made 33 saves, Maine's Al Loring stopped 26 shots. In Boston, Dave Snuggerud and Tom Chorske each scored a pair of goals and goalie John Blue kicked out 42 shots to lead Minnesota over Boston College, which got its only goal from Ken Hodge of Windsor, Ont. In Grand Forks, forward Tony Hrkac assisted on goals by Lee Davidson, Steve Johnson and Bob Joyce of Saint John, N.B., as North Dakota improved its record to 37-8. St. L Lawrence fell to 24-10. Canada wins semi-final VICTORIA (CP) - Tradition and revenge will be on Douglas Dryburgh's side today in the final of the Uniroyal world junior men's curling championship. The smugly confident Scottish skip will meet Hugh McFadyen's Canadian foursome from Winnipeg. The two, who went 8-1 through the round-robin portion of the 10-country event, won semifinal games Friday to advance to the title game at the Esquimalt Sports Centre. Dryburgh counted one in an extra end to beat Markus Eggler of Switzerland 8-7, while McFadyen eliminated Tore Torvbraten of Norway 11-5. Canada will be shooting for its sixth title in the Uniroyal's 13-year history,

By KEVIN MITCHELL Staff Writer Captain Sean Stotts collected two major trophies at Nanaimo Clippers' awards banquet Friday night at Cavallotti Lodge. Stotts, an 18-year-old Courtenay product in his second season with the Clippers, received the Most Valuable Player and Best Defenceman awards before about 150 guests. A B.C. Junior Hockey League all-star, Stotts was third. in team scoring with 25 goals 67 points, five behind point scoring champion Mike Knapton. Cory Manton, a 19-year-old rookie blueliner, won the Most Improved Player award, while 18-year-old backliner Grant McDonald took the Most Sportsmanlike Player trophy. Centre Todd Finner, who turned 18 three days ago, was named the Clippers' Rookie -of-the-Year. Stotts accepted the award on Finner's behalf as the local minor hockey product is working out with Portland Winterhawks of the Western Hockey League. By The Canadian Press • Prince Albert Raiders showed they mean business Tuesday with a 6-3 victory over the Swift Current Broncos as the Western Hockey League East Division quarterfinals began. "It was a big game for us to establish we were here to play," said Pat Elynuik, who scored three goals to pace the Raiders' scoring attack. "We stuck to dour game and got the goals when we needed them.".

RICHMOND, B.C. (CP) - Jason Phillips snapped a 2-2 tie at 9:31 of the third period Tuesday night to give the Richmond Sockeyes a 3-2 victory over the Kelowna Packers and the B.C. Junior Hockey League title. The Sockeyes went through the playoffs without a loss as they swept the best-of-nine final in five games to set a league record. The 1981-82 Abbotsford. Flyers were also undefeated in the playoffs but needed only 12 games to win their crown. This was the first year the final series was a best-of-nine affair. Bill Hardy and Dean Rutledge gave the Sockeyes period leads of 1-0 and 2-1 while Greg Andrusak and Pat Mangold scored for the Packers who were outshot 35-24. The Sockeyes will next play the winner of the Peace-Cariboo Hockey League in a best-of-three series that begins here April 3. Quesnel leads that best-of-seven series 2-0 over Grande Prairie.

By Dave Senick Times-Colonist Sports Editor It wasn't a goal but a penalty that provided the Victoria Cougars with the thought that a victory was at hand. The Cougars downed the Seattle Thunderbirds 8-5 in a Western Hockey League game Saturday at Memorial Arena. Victoria fell behind 2-1 after the first period but led 6-3 at the end of the second. The Thunderbirds were well

Paddy as in the upper level of Memorial Arena, put on a pair of glasses and began studying a lineup. Here was a man quietly going about his duties, scouting a Western Hockey League game for the National Hockey League's St. Louis Blues. A few heads turned in moment of recognition but Ginnell was left alone to scribble his notes. It wasn't always that way when Ginnell entered a rink. He spent 15 years coaching WHL teams in Flin Flon, Victoria, Lethbridge, Medicine Hat and New Westminster. Entering this season, his 518 regular-schedule career victories were second only to 530 New Westminster's Ernie McLean. In addition to creating winning teams, Ginnell created controversy. He played junior hockey in Flin Flon where a job in a mine went with a spot on the club. It was a tough town that spawned tough teams. The idea stuck with Ginnell he coached, pushing his club grind, fight and intimidate its way to victory. Ginnell handled talented competitors like Bobby Clarke, Reg Leach and d Mel Bridgman but also traded for guys who brawled more than they scored. Victoria, he enlisted Archie Henderson, Kim Clackson, and Brent Gogol who later went on set the all-time WHL season penalty record of 511 minutes. He's been in shouting matches with Peter Pollen, the mayor Victoria at the time, during game when Pollen felt violence had taken precedence over hockey.. Ginnell has watched prospective teammates battle in a benchclearing brawl during an intrasquad game. They were trying make one last impression before a final roster was declared. Asked after another brawl if he sent a player over the boards, Ginnell defiantly stated that, no, he didn't send him out but threw him out. "The controversy didn't bother me," says Ginnell. "In some ways, we had to create excitement. Now you see everybody sitting with their hands on their chins at a hockey game. "It has changed, though. You wouldn't do the same things. You couldn't because it would mean a long suspension. "But I've been to games this year where guys have been hitting each other with sticks and spearing. To me, that's worse than fighting. Geez, if I was coaching one of those teams, the league would be upset and there would be big hubbub." Ginnell invited a 'hubbub'. He saw himself as a promoter trying to fill seats. While players fought during a bench-clearing incident in one game, he posed for a newspaper photographer who arranged discarded gloves and positioned Ginnell for the perfect shot. Another time, Ginnell, under suspension, attempted to slip into an exhibition game. A false mustache, big glasses, floppy hat and over-sized jacket didn't fool the doorman. Later, while waiting outside the rink, he glady donned the disguise again for a television interview. The moment charactarized Ginnell. He had attempted to twist the rules to his favor and was enjoying the furor that ensued. But that was part of the past. Ginnell wears a tie and a neat, wrinkle-freetrench coat. His cowboy hat, complete with a huge feather, and what some called his wild west show have been left behind.

QUEBEC- Former Montreal Canadiens great Bernie Geoffrion suffered a heart attack Saturday while touring a hockey hall of fame exhibit. Geoffrion, 55, is in hospital in stable condition. He collapsed while touring the exhibit with another ex National Hockey League great, Gordie Howe. Nicknamed Boom Boom because of his powerful shot, Geoffrion has had a history of heart problems. Geoffrion played 16 seasons in the NHL, scoring 393 goals and 429 assists, before retiring in 1968. He was the original coach of the Atlanta Flames franchise before the team moved to Calgary and also coached the Montreal Canadiens briefly in 1979.

As expected, Richmond Sockeyes have captured the Coastal Division championship of the B.C. Junior Hockey League. The defending division champions needed overtime, however, and a goal by Paul Rutherford at 4:30 of the first extra period to record a 6-5 decision over Delta Flyers last night at Sungod Arena. Richmond's victory gave it a sweep of the best-of-seven series, which began with a double-overtime game the Sockeyes won 7-6. Mike McCormick, who had the winning goal in the double-overtime game, had what proved to be the winner again Friday. The veteran-loaded Sockeyes now advance to league final against Kelowna Packers, who beat the Vernon Lakers 6-3 Friday to win the Interior Division final 4-2. Play in the best-of-nine BCJHL final opens with 7:30 p.m. games Tuesday and Wednesday at Richmond Arena before shifting to Kelowna.

for Orland Kurtenbach, who takes his Richmond Sockeyes into tonight's opener against Kelowna Packers in the B.C. Junior Hockey League final. "I haven't been involved in one since 1953-54 when I was playing junior for Prince Albert," the Sockeyes' coach was saying Monday. "We met the Flin Flon Bombers and the series went the distance. Flin Flon beat us in overtime in the ninth game. I have no reason to think our series with Kelowna won't go the limit." The Sockeyes and Packers were the class of their respective divisions this past season, but Kelowna did sweep Richmond in their two regular season meetings. "We haven't played them since November," added Kurtenbach, a former Vancouver Canuck captain and coach. Game time tonight at Richmond Arena is 7:30, with game two going Wednesday, same time, same place. The series shifts to Kelowna on Friday.

Halward through The Detroit Red Wings have lost defenceman Doug Halward for the season after he developed phlebitis in his left leg, the National Hockey League team said today. Halward, 31, developed the inflammation and blood clots during the club's road trip, which concluded last weekend. A 12-year veteran, Halward played in six games for the Red Wings after he was acquired from Vancouver in a trade for future considerations on Nov. 21. He missed the next 17 games with a separated shoulder, returned on Jan. 17 and played in five games before suffering a knee injury on Jan. 28.

Sockeyes roll over Kelowna Canadian Press KELOWNA - Richmond Sockeyes jumped to a 4-0 lead in the opening period Friday night and relied on intensity and rugged play en route to a 7-5 B.C. Junior Hockey League playoff victory over Kelowna Packers. Richmond holds a comfortable 3-0 margin in the best-of-nine league championship series. Game four goes here tonight with game five back in Richmond Tuesday. Richmond Orland Kurtenbach said "the key to any hockey game is if you've got people who are intense and taking the body. That's what it takes to win.' His counterpart, Kelowna coach Bill Hogaboam, conceded his team was dominated. "They outbumped us, they were all over top of us ... and bang, it ended up 4-0 (after one period). We only played 40 minutes of hockey tonight. Richmond is disciplined to fall four goals behind.' Dean Rutledge, Dave Tomlinson and Jason Philips each scored twice for the Sockeyes while Mike McCormick added a single. Kelowna was led by Jason Ruff and Pat Mangold, each with two goals, while Jamie Kurki rounded out the scoring. Richmond goaltender Frank Romeo made 26 saves while Packers Dusty Imoo and Lucien Carignan combined to stop 26 shots.

penalty killers. "We count on our power play for a good part of our offence," he said, "but their penalty-killers did a good job of shutting us down." With Doug Smail, Ron Wilson, Mario Marois and Jim 1 Kyte handling most of the load, the Jets snuffed six Vancouver power-play opportunities. The Canucks began this game as if they didn't intend it to be another layer of frustration. Dave Richter, who played his minor hockey in suburban St. Boniface, scored only his second goal of the season early in the first. Just past the midway mark, Tony Tanti made it 2-0 with his 39th and his eighth at the expense of the Jets. Then the momentum abruptly shifted to Winnipeg. Rookie defenceman Fredrik Olausson scored his sixth of the season before the first period ended. Randy Carlyle's 14th goal and Dale Hawerchuk's 45th sent the Jets in front in the second. The third was scoreless.

Kamloops Blazers learned a painful lesson in their pursuit of Western Hockey League records Sunday night. Scoring champion Rob Brown, who had already established four individual scoring this season, suffered an ankle injury in the third period of Kamloops' final regular season game, a 7-3 victory over New Westminster Bruins at Queens Park Arena. "We're certainly hoping the injury isn't any more serious than a bad sprain but even at that, Brownie will still miss the first three, maybe four playoff games against Victoria," said Kamloops coach Ken Hitchcock. "If it is bad, someone else will have to come to the forefront. You don't replace a guy like Rob. He's the best player in the league." The Blazers did manage to break the WHL record for most goals in a season as their total of 496 eclipsed the 1982-83 standard of 495 set by Portland Winter Hawks. The Bruins, meanwhile, concluded their dismal campaign at 18-50-4, going 2-22-1 in their last 25 games. They were 1-14-1 against Kamloops alone. "I don't worry about bad seasons because I've had many more good than bad ones and nobody remembers the bad ones anyway," said New Westminster coach Ernie McLean. The West Division best-of-nine semi-finals open this week with Victoria's Cougars facing Kamloops and Spokane Chiefs meeting Portland.

Sun News Services Richmond Sockeyes are in the driver's seat - and have the motor running - in the B.C. Junior Hockey League championship playoff series. The Sockeyes took a seemingly insurmountable 4-0 lead in the best-of-nine series against Packers with a 5-1 win in the Okanagan city Saturday. A Sockeyes win Tuesday night at Richmond Arena (7:30) will give them a series sweep. "The guys are on a natural high," said Sockeyes coach Orland Kurtenbach. "It was a nice game for us to win. If they're willing to listen and cut down mistakes, we'll do well at home (Tuesday)." The Sockeyes scored three unanswered goals in the last half of the third period en route to the Saturday win. Jason Philips opened the Sockeyes' scoring with a short-handed goal in the first period. Dave Tomlinson extended the e margin to 2-0 early in the second before Brent Mowery scored Kelowna's only goal at 13:23 of the middle frame. Richmond's third period insurance markers came from Philips, Bill Hardy and Mike McCormick.

Special To The Sun WINNIPEG - Vancouver Canucks lost a hockey game they needed desperately to win here Sunday afternoon, a situation they found painfully familiar. To long Canucks fans, to say nothing of long-suffering Canucks coaches, it must seem their star-crossed heroes have spent most of their 17-year National Hockey League history in similar straits coming up just short in - critical situations. Sunday's burden was a 3-2 loss to the Jets, fashioned before an audience of 13,832 at Winnipeg Arena. It was the 74th game of the season for both teams and yielded the Jets' 38th win and the Canucks' 41st loss. In addition to the two points, the Canucks also squandered a brilliant 46-save performance from goaltender Richard Brodeur. Even more painful were the circumstances. Midway through the second period, the out-of-town scoreboard showed the Whalers had defeated Los Angeles Kings 6-3 in Hartford. Victory would have allowed the Canucks to close within four points of the Kings - and the Smythe Division's fourth and final playoff berth. But the Kings' loss proved insufficient motivation to kick-start the Canucks' sputtering offence.
Ned's Place of Salmon Arm won the 18th annual Kiwanis pee wee hockey tournament in Oliver over the weekend.

The Esso swim program, a competitive skills course, is being run by the Sockeye Swim Club and a new session will start on Thursday, March 12. Classes are once a week for eight weeks from 5:15 until 5:45 p.m. Starts, turns, and strokes are the skills that are worked on. It is a wonderful opportunity for children to learn about swim club. You may pre-register for this right away. In early May we will be offering the Esso swim program for instructors. Participants must be at least 16-years-old and have some background in aquatics and leadership. Call us to put your name on the interest list.

Women's hockey The Saskatoon Century 21 Star women's hockey club will be competing in the North Battleford ninth annual Sweetheart Women's tournament this weekend. The Moose Jaw Warriors thrive on overtime. A 7-6 win over Swift Current Broncos on Sunday was the Warriors' fifth win in eight overtime appearances this season. The club's other three overtime outings have resulted i in ties. The Spokane Chiefs (2-0-0), are the only other Western Hockey League club that can boast of an undefeated record in extra time this season. Kamloops Blazers have appeared in the most overtime games, a total of 11. CalWranglers, Medicine Hat Tigers and Swift Current have each played 10 OT games. At the other end of the scale, Spokane's two appearances are the fewest overtime games played by one club. Brandon Wheat Kings (0-3- 3) and the Saskatoon Blades (0-2-2) are still hunting for that elusive first OT victory of the season. Actually, the Blades have not found overtime to be a blessing in recent years. The club is 2-9- 10 in the extra period during Marcel Comeau's tenure as head coach.

Chippers 4, Whalers 11 By MICHAEL RHODE For The Daily Free Press COLWOOD - For the Nanaimo Clippers, the waiting is finally over. Since wrapping up a playoff berth over two weeks ago, the Clippers have been patiently waiting for the playoffs to begin. Tuesday, Nanaimo will open their best League of Coastal Division Junior semi-final Hockey series in Richmond against the first place Sockeyes. Saturday night, the shipmen concluded the regular season schedule in Colwood against Juan de Fuca Whalers in less than spectacular fashion, bowing 11-4.

Cory Kropielinski's goal with less than two minutes to play in regulation time lifted Nanaimo Bantam A Clippers to a 5-4 win over Fuller Lake Flyers Sunday at Kerry Park Arena. The was the Clippers' second games South Island Bantam A Hockey League round-robin playoffs. Saturday at Frank Crane Arena, Nanaimo held Victoria Racquet Club Kings to a 1-1 tie. Heath Dennison notched a pair of goals Sunday to lead the Clippers, while Travis Clark and Graham Aubuchon added singles to set the stage for Kropielinski's winner.

ence. I picked Richmond to finish on top, but who didn't? For some reason I picked Abbotsford Falcons to finish second. Of course, they are lower than the Titanic right now, and have less chance of coming to the surface. Had they held on to goalie Jamie Stewart instead of trading him to Langley, they might have made the playoffs. Stewart is one of the main reasons Langley is in second instead of fourth, where I had them pegged. In third place, I went with the Clippers on the assumption Rich Dutka would be able to find a sniper by season's end. He didn't and the Clippers will finish below .500 and in fourth place facing the powerful Sockeyes in the first round of the playoffs. I picked Juan de Fuca fifth, not because of their players, but because of their name. If they didn't have such a good name, they would have been picked last. That honor was reserved for Delta Flyers, but solid netminding from Stewart and the scoring exploits of Lance Momotani made me look like an idiot. As for my playoff picks? On the Coast, Richmond over Nanaimo five games, Langley over Delta in six games. And finally, Vernon over Richmond in seven.
.. With the B.C. Junior Hockey League season winding down, and playoffs around the corner, it seems this is as good a time as any to see how my preseason picks turned out.

the spirit of enjoyment and competition seldom seen at the national level of other sport activities," added Bartlett. BCSO has programs in a number of sports, including soccer, swimming, fitness, floor hockey, track and field, bowling, softball and skiing. "The emphasis of the programs quality is developing year- round Bartlett. "In this athletes training practices," can improve skills and enjoy the benefits of participation on a weekly basis. It all sounds great, but the program can't run without funds. BCSO is registered as a nonprofit society in B.C. and as a registered charity federally. It is supported by donations and grants, as well as hardworking volunteers. Bartlett says more volunteers are needed in Nanaimo. "We need people with drive and imagination to assist in fundraising, said Bartlett. "We have a strong need for coaches, someone with a good working knowledge of a sport and the willingness to help, and we will teach you the rest.' Bartlett urges anyone with those qualifications to call either himself at 754-1714, or Silvia Miller at 758-0630.

Penticton Knights got four goals from Jackson Penney to whip Summerland Buckaroos 10-5 in B.C. Junior Hockey League play Tuesday night in Penticton. Mike Barzal and Nanaimo product Dave McColm scored two goals apiece, with singles from ex Frantz. Clipper Brett Mayor and Dowell Mike Tremmell scored a pair for the Buckaroos while Shane Varrey, Vince Bajer and Darren McFarland added singles. Peter Holmes scored a pair of goals to pace the Flyers to a 6-3 win over Abbotsford Falcons 6-3 in Delta. Nanaimo's Aaron Nosky, Jeff Kitsch, Jeff Nottingham and Kevin Horne scored the other Delta markers while Rick Rozecki, Scott Gmur and Doug McDonald replied for Abbotsford.

In the other game, the Richmond Sockeyes bombed the Delta Flyers 9-0 to take a 2-0 lead in their bestof Packers 9 semifinal Lakers 4 Kelowna, Pat Mangold and Kerrys Russell scored two goals apiece to pace the Packers. Kevin Taillefer, Bruce Bianchini, Terrance Head, Doug Gardiner and Jay Luknowsky were the other scorers for the Packers who led 6-4 entering the final period. Mark Greyeyes, Ed Tuckwood, Campbell Blair and Shawn Howard scored for the Lakers who were outshot 38-23. Sockeyes 9 Flyers 0 It was back to business in Richmond after the Flyers took the Sockeyes to double overtime before losing 7-6 on Tuesday night. Dean Rutledge had his second three-goal performance in as many games to lead the Richmond attack. Stan Czenczek and Brian Kozak each had two goals while singles went to Mike McCormick and Jason Philips.

By The Canadian Press The Kelowna Packers stepped up the intensity of their checking game in the second and periods Wednesday night and effort paid off with a crucial 9-4 playoff victory in the British Columbia Junior Hockey League. The Packers dominated the Vernon Lakers in the final 40 minutes, outscoring them 4-2 in the second period and thienthe third. "We held just six shots in. the second period and four in the third," said Packers coach Bill Hogaboam after his club took a 3-2 edge in the best-of-seven semifinal series which continues Friday night in Vernon. "We took care of our own end. There were no three-on-twos or two-on-ones." In first period, Kelowna took four minor penalties and the Lakers converted one power-play opportunity to salvage a 2-2 tie. "The big difference was the penalties we took in the first period," said Hogaboam. "In the last two periods, we played with a lot more discipline. "In the e first period we went with three lines. In the second we went with four lines. We had 19 guys contributing. When we did have opportunities, we put them in.'

Kelowna goaltender Dusty Imoo made the good pad save on Rutledge's shot but Hervey was there to flip the puck over him. "No way their man should have been in there for the rebound," admitted Bill Hogoboam, who coaches the Packers with Ed Ruff. Despite the loss, Hogoboam was pleased with the effort that followed Tuesday's 7-0 blowout loss. "Tonight we came to play," said Hogoboam. Hervey, who also collected an assist, started his Canadian journey four years ago with Langley Eagles after having been noticed in some visits to Canada for minor tournaments. He spent a season with Victoria Cougars in the Western Hockey League before being traded to the Lethbridge Broncos. He earned a free agent tryout with the Los Angeles Kings this season but was sent down to Flint in the International League. When players with contracts started getting sent down to Flint, Hervey was cut loose. He wound up with Richmond just before Christmas. It was quite a gift for the Sockeyes. The Packers got a break to tie the game at 7:43 of the third period when a shot deflected in past Richmond goaltender Frank Romeo off one of his own players and was credited to Mike Murinko. Jamie Kurki, Alex Paterson and Kelly Hurd had the other goals for Kelowna, which was outshot 43- 30. Bill Hardy, Rutherford, Dave Tomlinson and Rutledge had the other Richmond scores.
Packers 4 Sockeyes 5 (OT)

Hervey's score ends OT contest By FRANK LUBA Sports Reporter Rebounds and Los Angeles makes you think of basketball and the Lakers. Last night, though, it was hockey and the Richmond Sockeyes that were front and centre as L.A. native Matt Hervey converted a rebound in overtime to give his team a 5-4 decision over the Kelowna Packers at Richmond Arena. The win gave Richmond a 2-0 lead in the best-of-nine B.C. Junior Hockey League championship. Play resumes tomorrow and Saturday in Kelowna's Memorial Arena, a smaller ice surface the hard-hitting Sockeyes might use to their advantage. The experience that Hervey has collected in three seasons of major junior and one of junior A paid off in a big way when he followed Paul Rutherford and Dean Rutledge on their two-on-one breakaway.

The St. Albert Saints and Red Deer Rustlers don't have time to be tired. Both are coming off gruelling seven-game division finals, but they have to be ready to start the best-of-seven Alberta Junior Hockey League championship series tonight at 7:30 at Akinsdale Arena in St. Albert.

It marked quite : a rejuvenation for the Rustlers, who took a year's leave of absence in 1985-86 before being resurrected under the capable direction of general manager Graham Parsons. "The earth's going to move i in this series," Parsons said Monday, indicating he expects a physical series. "It's going to be a dandy. It's going to be a banger." The second game is scheduled Friday in Red Deer, with the third back at Akinsdale at 7:30 p.m. Saturday and fourth in Red Deer next Tuesday. The fifth, sixth and seventh games, if necessary, will be played April 1 in St. Albert, April 3 in Red Deer and April 4 at Akinsdale, with the winner to go on against the British Columbia champion - probably the powerful Richmond Sockeyes, coached by former National Hockey League tough guy Orland Kurtenbach - in a Centennial Cup series to be played in its entirety at the home of the AJHL champion.

Events of the last few days have placed the Oilers under intense pressure. Much is self-imposed. They remain the most talented team in the National Hockey League. Man for man, none of Edmonton's rivals is close. But hockey isn't played mano a mano. It was designed as a team event. At their best, the Oilers understand the concept. They handle the puck unselfishly. They cover up for each other. Win or lose, they spread the praise and share the blame. At their worst, it's a different story. Outsiders are left to search for any real semblance of closeness. A lot of media guys guess that it hasn't. The impression of conflict goes back to last spring, when coaches kept insisting the Oilers refused to follow instructions in the disaster against Calgary. Things don't seem much better this time around. Boss Glen Sather maintains the Oilers are lovers not fighters - although he's convinced they can look after themselves. First he talks about the need for increased team discipline. Then he wonders whether there's any point in counting on officials to call even the most blatant of penalties against Oiler opponents. He should talk more, I submit, about the dismal checking that surfaces sometimes - as it did when Rich Chernomaz punched home a loose puck for an important New Jersey goal in the third period after Andy Moog had turned in a spectacular save. He should talk about the number of times Oiler players have been left free to avoid interviews - especially after they play poorly. He should talk about the fact that the Oilers have a choice. They can follow instructions, work hard and give themselves a chance to win their third Stanley Cup in four years and go down in history as an outstanding team. Or they can pout when things get tough, blame events beyond their control and label themselves for all time as underachievers.

Gilmour, Federko lift Blues Blues 8 North Stars 5 BLOOMINGTON, Minn. (AP) Doug Gilmour had one goal and three assists and Bernie Federko had a pair of goals and one assist as the St. Louis Blues, behind four power-play goals, trounced the Minnesota North Stars 8-5 National Hockey League victory Monday night. Gilmour's 34th goal of the season came during a three-goal spurt in a 3:42 span midway through the opening period to give the Blues a 3-2 lead. Minnesota had enjoyed a brief 2-0 lead on goals by Keith Acton and Brian MacLellan. The Blues scored at 11:06 when Mark Hunter put in his 35th goal of the season on NHL the power play. Gilmour tied the game 2-2 just 28 seconds later when he grabbed Tim Bothwell's wide shot behind the net and circled in front to beat North Stars goalie Don Beaupre. The Blues went ahead 3-2 at 14:48 on Federko's first goal on a rebound shot off the boards by Greg Paslawski. St. Louis got another power-play goal at 9:15 of the second period to take a 4-2 lead. Mark Reeds got the goal after taking a pass from Federko on a 2-on-1 break. The North Stars pulled within 4-3 at 12:29 of the second period on Dirk Graham's goal. But the Blues got four goals in the third period, including Federko's second on the power play. St. Louis's other goals came from Paslawski and Ron Flockhart, and an open-net goal by Rick Meagher. The North Stars got third -period goals from Bob Rouse and Mark Pavelich.

Bruins get blown out Spokane Chiefs scored three sec-period goals in two minutes and 14 seconds to down New Westminster Bruins 7-1 in Western Hockey League action at Queens Park Arena Sunday night. Cal Zankowski scored the lone goal for the Bruins, who lost for the 18th time in their last 19 games. The Bruins were also losers on Saturday, falling 9-4 to the Winter Hawks in Portland. Mario Desjardins, Scott Daniels, Zankowski and Darren Schwartz tallied for the who were outshot 44-33 by Portland. In Kamloops, WHL scoring leader Rob Brown picked up a goal and five assists to lead the Blazers to a 10-2 victory over Victoria Cougars. Brown broke Bruce Eakin's single-season assist record of 125 in the contest. Delta ousts Langley Delta Flyers knocked Langley Eagles out of the B.C. Junior Hockey League playoffs Sunday, winning the deciding game in their best-of-seven Coastal Division semi-final series 5-3. Peter Holmes scored three goals, including the winner, as the Flyers advance to the division final against Orland Kurtenbach's Richmond Sockeyes. In Interior Division final action, Vernon Lakers took a 2-1 lead in their best-of-seven series against Kelowna with a 5-2 victory over the Packers.

Romeo, Romeo, wherefore art thou? In goal for the Richmond Sockeyes, so don't bother him, Juliet, because it's playoff time in the B.C. Junior Hockey League.

**If this game (tonight) doesn't get them going, I don't know what will." Motivation was certainly no problem for Romeo, a 20-year-old native of Revelstoke who compiled a 3.82 average for the Packers last season but was deemed expendable. ** (It was the) best feeling I've ever had in my hockey career," said Romeo of the win. After being let go by Kelowna, he tried out with Victoria Cougars of the Western Hockey League. Two weeks later he headed to Vernon and the Lakers, but before he could be signed the Sockeyes picked him up as a free agent. He's one of nine 20-year-olds on the Sockeyes, who played nearly flawless hockey. Dave Thomlinson paced the winners with two goals while Jason Philips potted the game winner, against Kelowna goalie Lucien Carignan, who was replaced by 16- year-old Dusty Imoo for the third period. The Sockeyes, who held a comfortable 34-27 edge in shots, also got goals from Bill Hardy, Mike Claringbull, Mike McCormick and Steve Jaques. NOTES Attendance at last night's game was estimated at just under 1,000. Tickets for Friday and Saturday's games in Kelowna are at a premium but some might be available through the Sockeyes.

stopped 27 shots to anchor the Sockeyes in a 7-0 victory against his former team, the Kelowna Packers, in the opening game of the best-of-nine championship series. Play between Sockeyes, Coastal Division champions, and the Interior Division-winning Packers resumes tonight at 7:30 p.m. at Richmond Arena. There was only a hint of drama in last night's blowout. Richmond led just 1-0 after one period, but scored 12 seconds into the second period and added four more goals before Kelowna could retreat to the dressing room. Ed Ruff, who coaches Kelowna with Bill Hogaboam, felt it was the Packers' worst game of the year. "They weren't intense and they weren't ready for the game," said Ruff of his squad, which compiled a league best 45-5-2 won-lost-tied record. Motivation won't be a problem again, says Ruff.

"I started in figure skates, a borrowed pair from my sister," he said. "That started me off and then my mom got me a pair of Micro Mascots. I was out watching the Pee Wees and they asked me to start training with them. That's how I got started in hockey." Warnock hasn't scored a goal this season, but he realizes his main job is clearing the opposition from in front of the Stars' net. "4T enjoy hockey, more than football because like the bodily contact and up and down action of hockey,' he said. "I watch taped NHL games on the telly and it's brilliant. I can't get enough of it." Both Warnock and Simpson credit Canadian coach Tom Smith with improving their play over the past two seasons. The Stars stopped Bournemouth Stags 1143 in Heineken First Division play and bowed 9-6 to Cardiff Red Devils in a challenge (exhibition) match in front of 2,500 frenized Wales supporters in other games I caught on my vacation. "I'm having the most fun playing hockey I've ever had," said Yewchin, who joined Oxford 10 games ago after Bournemouth encountered problems paying his weekly salary. "I'd like to play one more year over here before settling down back home," said Yewchin. "The key to a team's success over here is how good your British players are. Most of them are good skaters, but they haven't been taught how to shoot properly."

And the goaltending, is needless to say less than superb. Garry Unger, former NHL ironman, plays for First Division leading Peterborough Pirates and was leading the league with over 200 points when I left last week. Yewchin was nearing the top 10 slot with 120 points. Tony Hand, the Scottish star drafted by Edmonton, was second in Premier Division scoring with 172 points. Al Sims (Boston Bruins) and Steve Moria (exRichmond Sockeye signed by NY Rangers) were also among the Premier top 10 scorers. Oxford, a city with dozens of universities, has had an arena for three years. The Stars attract an average of 500 fans per game, while public skating draws a full house every session. In Cardiff, fans lined up outside 90 minutes before game time. The Red Devils, with star centre John Lawless product) receiving deafening applause everytime he heads up ice in Wayne Gretzky fashion, are a Second Division team expected to move up a division next year. Ken Taggart, vice-chairman of the Stars, has been stationed in Oxford with the U.S. Air Force for 16 years. A Spokane native, Taggart referees a lot of key premier and First Division hockey games. He is always on the lookout for high scoring imports. "An import forward is expected to score between 150 and 200 points and a defenceman 100 points," said Taggart. "And it's easy for a good import to do because you can have lunch in front of the net. The British players don't push the men out from in front." Ex-Finnish First Division player Jari Virta is a big fan favorite in Oxford. The 28-yearold is an awesome stickhandler whose greatest move is one where he flips the puck about eight feet in the air, steps around an opponent and picks up the puck, a move Gretzky wouldn't even try. Heineken has. been a major British Ice Hockey League sponsor for three years and Taggart says the beer company pours 1,200 pounds per club into the First Division (covers cost of referees) and about 3,500 pounds into the Premier Division (covers officials and transportation). Taggart, a hockey chairman . for England, Wales and Northern Ireland, plans to approach computer companies for financial support next year. "They consider ice hockey to be a blood sport," he said. "They don't understand controlled aggression." One major problem in British ice hockey is the lack of referees. Taggart says 50 referees try to keep up with the demands of 200 teams in Great Britain. ICE CHIPS: Ex-Nanaimo Clipper Brian Burley was the centre of controversy after signing with Murrayfield Racers of the Premier Division. Burley, reccomended to the Racers by ex-Clipper Ian Ramsay, had to sit out a few games after Fife Flyers protested his registration. Burley's release from Nelson Maple Leafs had apparently been n verbally approved by a CAHA rep and thus the confusion in Great Britain Former NHLer Glenn Sharpley and exOHA scoring whiz Fred Perlini are also playing in the British League If he doesn't return to Oxford, Yewchin may play and run a new arena complex in Basingstoke, England next year The odd Premier Division game appears live Saturdays on BBC's Grandstand show.

OXFORD, ENGLAND Unlike most, Canadian youngsters, British product Paul Simpson wasn't given a new pair of skates moments after he took his first step as a child. "I bought a pair of Bauer Huggers for two pounds," laughs Simpson. "They lasted about six months and then I stole a pair of Micron Mascots from the rink and they lasted 18 months. Then I bought a pair of Junior Tacks for 60 quid and they were dazzlers.' Simpson so of his Junior Tacks that tucked his pants in his skates to show them off at public skating. That was a few years ago though. Today, the 21-year-old comedian and selfprofessed ladies man is a star non-import with Oxford City Stars ice hockey team. He gets five pounds for every point and recently enjoyed a windfall when he collected 10 points in a 27-8 win over Irvine Magnum Wings. He did so playing on a line with imports Don Yewchin (Nanaimo) and Jim Malner (Calgary). In that same game, Yewchin counted 10 goals and six assists and was naturally named Manof-the-Match, an award every fan anxiously waits for at the end of each game. Simpson only started organized hockey five years ago. "I used to play rugby, soccer, and cricket, but I was never any good at any of them," he said. "I saw a hockey match and said (bleep) soccer. I want to play hockey until I die. I want to play hockey and drink beer. I'm only interested in drinking my face off." Simpson admits that his game needs a little work. "I'm going to learn how to skate one day, he jokes. "And my commitment to backcheck is weak. I've got to work on my figure as well." Carl Warnock, a 22-year-old British defenceman who plays it rough, started skating 11 years ago. His first skates weren't anything special either.

Both coaches were frustrated by the officiating of referee Marty Taylor. "Quote me, what officiating?" said Delta coach Rick Hopper. "Someone is going to get seriously hurt out there," added Hopper. "I think we threw some good bodychecks and got penalized," said Kurtenbach. The defending champion Sockeyes were coming off a 10- day layoff and, according to Kurtenbach, were "just rusty." By FRANK LUBA Sports Reporter Richmond Sockeyes coach Orland Kurtenbach knew his squad was going to be in for a tough time against Delta Flyers last night, he just didn't know HOW tough. It took a goal by Mike McCormick three minutes and 19 seconds into the second overtime period to give the Sockeyes a 7-6 victory against the Flyers in the opening game of the B.C. Junior Hockey League's Coastal Division championship. Sockeyes 7 Flyers 6 The best-of-seven series resumes tonight at 7:30 at Richmond before moving to Delta's Sungod Arena for games at 8 p.m. Friday and Saturday.

rusty as the final outcome couldn't have been guessed from the way things started. Delta really opened like Flyers, soaring to a 3-0 first period lead and going up 4-0 early in the second period before Richmond • narrowed the gap to 4-2. The second period ended with the visitors ahead 5- 3. They went up 6-3 in the third before the veteran Sockeyes fought back for three unanswered goals. Matt Hervey scored the key goal on the power-play at 16:54, sandwiched in between the second and third goals of the evening by Dean Rutledge. Rutledge's tying goal came with 51 seconds to play from a scramble in front of goaltender Jeff Clark. The Sockeyes outshot the

Flyers 12-5 in the first 10-minute sudden-victory overtime but there was no scoring, setting the stage set for the second suddenvictory overtime period. Rutledge led the scoring parade with his three goals and three assists. Linemate Dave Tomlinson added a goal and four assists. Also scoring for the Sockeyes was Steve Jacques. Coastal Division scoring champion Lance Momotani broke out of his playoff scoring slump with three goals for Delta. Peter Holmes added a goal and two assists, as did Jeff Kitsch, one of New Westminster Bruins' players on the team (along with Drew Sawtell). Randy Olson had the Flyers' other goal.

Darren Biggs has quick hands, but since whipping a Vernon Laker last season, hasn't had to drop his gloves much. As for penalty Craig Frankford, he's fearless, but hardly a Mike Tyson on skates. He hasn't been clearly beaten, but hasn't recorded too many decisive wins. NOTES AND QUOTES: ExClipper Tony Feltrin, whose pro hockey career was ended by an eye injury, is now scouting for New York Rangers. Feltrin started this season with New Haven of the AHL, but his vision was impaired to the point where he felt uncomfortable on the ice. He is also attending Malaspina College . Ian Ramsay is back home in Nanaimo after a back injury forced him to leave Murrayfield Racers of the British Hockey League's premier division. The former Clipper star was playing on a forward line with Scottish star Tony Hand and was in the top 10 in scoring and penalty minutes ... Don Yewchin of Nanaimo has switched teams the British league's first division. Yewchin's playing for Oxford after leaving Bournemouth Stags, who are going broke and had stopped paying him... Frankford was rather surprised to receive a letter from Ferris State, who expressed some interest in him. He feels their scouts must have watched him on a good night. He plans to frame the letter ... Larry Quiring of CHUB Radio lived out a boyhood dream when he practiced with the Clippers Wednesday morning. He failed to score a goal, however.

Defending A division champion Esquimalt Thunderbirds are one of 20 teams entered in the sixth annual Nanaimo Old Tubbers Hockey Tournament this weekend. The five division, 20-team tourney opens Friday afternoon with action at both Frank Crane and Civic Arenas. The host Tubbers will play out of the C division, while Nanaimo Bastions will compete in A, Nanaimo Old Seaman in D and Northbrook Restaurant in the E section. Surrey Sidewinders, B division winners last year, have moved up to the A division this year. Cyclone Taylor Sports of Kitsilano round out the A side. What with most of the NHL players getting the week off, what better time to discuss the best fighters in hockey today. I've watched my hockey fight tape about six times now and Bob Nystrom of the Islanders gets my vote for one of the best NHL scrappers. He beats every guy who comes his way in the tape I've got. There are other tapes of course. Take Sandy Vigilante, a 33-year-old New Jersey postal worker. The man has more than 150 hours of hockey fight footage and more than 1,000 fight tapes. His favorite fighter of all time is the late Bob Gassoff, who I had the pleasure of watching as a junior defenceman in Vernon in the early 1970's. Vigilante, in a recent interview with Stan Fischler, said his current favorites are Wendel Clark of the Leafs, Jim Kyte of the Jets and Larry Playfair of the Kings. He also likes Curt Fraser and Al Secord of the Blackhawks, while naming the Rangers' George McPhee as "the fighter with the most guts in the league." Vigilante says a bout between Dave Richter of the North Stars and Steve Dykstra of Sabres was one best last season. Richter landed heavy lefts and almost iced Dykstra, but the Sabre blueliner managed to recover and make it a close one. Mark Topaz, a 28-year-old statistician from Rockaway Park, New York, often confers with Vigilante. Topaz even has an all-time top three fight collection. 1. It was cool and clear in Quebec City, but the top line of the Soviet national hockey team - KrutovLarionov-Makarov - never got going during the long-awaited first game of the Rendez-Vous 87 series. A superb defensive effort by the NHL all-stars resulted in a 4-3 victory over the Soviets, the current world and Olympic champions. Centre Igor Larionov, rightwinger Vladimir Krutov and leftwinger Sergei Makarov, who have combined for goals over the years against the NHL, was virtually shut out. Former Nanaimo Clipper netminder Stacey Nickel was traded Wednesday to Western Division leading Kamloops Blazers in a three-player Western Hockey League deal. Nickel, 20, went from Regina Pats to the Blazers in return for defenceman Chris Tarnowski, 20, and goaltender Rob McKinley, 19. Nickel, a White Rock product, had a 15-15-2 record with one shutout and a 4.58 goals against average with the fifth place Eastern Division Pats.

Matthews gets multi-year pact The Canadian Press VANCOUVER - The British Columbia Lions have signed head coach Don Matthews to a new multi-year contract with the Canadian Football League team, club president Chuck Walter announced Thursday. Walker refused to disclose details of the contract. Matthews has compiled 48 wins, 17 losses and one tie in four years with B.C., leading the club to four Western Conference finals, two Grey Cup finals and one national championship. UVic volleyballers defeated by UBC The UBC Thunderbirds defeated the University of Victoria Vikings 15-7, 15-10, 15-13 in Canada West University Athletic Association men's volleyball league play Thursday at McKinnon Gym. UVic is 3-11 and out of the playoffs. UBC is third at 9-5. Mike McCulloch and Bruce Schmor had 16 spike kills each for UVic. Calgary coach fined CALGARY- Coach John Chapman of the Calgary Wranglers has been fined $200 after r receiving a game misconduct for throwing sticks on the ice during a game Tuesday against the Medicine Hat Tigers, the Western Hockey League announced.

Tonight, millions of Canadians will park themselves in front of their television sets for the second game of the National Hockey League-Soviet Union Rendez-Vouz 87 showdown. This series, to most Canadian youngsters, represents the great athletic dream - to play for your country's pride, in your national game, in front of an adoring public. To 30 young rugby players visiting Canada from New Zealand, RendezVous 87 doesn't mean much. So what is every Kiwi kid's dream? "To play for the All Blacks (New Zealand's national rugby team)," replied five players from the Carlton Rugby Club of Auckland, in unison

Biological Station. He continued his sockeye research, concentrating on how to estimate escapement and to judge stream capacities. Narver, now a Canadian citizen, enjoyed being on the ground floor for the construetion of new spawning channels on the Babine. He is proudest, however, of his work on steelhead enhancement. When he joined the provincial Fish and Wildlife e Department, the graphs on steelhead fishing were dismal. Angling days, the numbers of steelhead retained and total catches all were declining in B.C. It Narver who helped introduce catch-release fishing on steelhead rivers. Only hatchery-reared fish can be kept on Vancouver Island. The province's program has had dramatic results. Fish retention has improved from the 1981 low of about 10,000 to 20,000 in just five years. Angling days hit a low of 120,000 in 1981 and went to 220,000 by 1985. The greatest increase has been in total catch. From a low of 40,000 in 1978 and '79, catch totals (catch-and-release figures for the province) have skyrocketed to 150,000 by 1985. The results could mean steelheaders may soon be allowed to retain some wild stock. "We may have to restrict or two rivers, "We're looking at the possiblity," he confided. and it will probably be done on a tag system." He warns, however, that there is still much work to be done before all steelhead stocks can be declared safe and healthy again. Narver has a way of achieving success. His research on the Chignik River - a system with two major lakes and "homes" for 12 different stocks of sockeye - has resulted in one of the most successful salmon fishery in Alaska. "On a per capita basis, fishermen along the river are the most succesful in the world. Most of them are natives." Narver's early research produced optimum guides for containing limits that are still followed by the state today. There is no denying that Narver has great "inside" respect, the kind that comes from fellow biologists who know and understand technical finery. But there is greater impact when respect is voiced by people "in the field." They i can be enforcment officers, environmentalists or just ordinary game club members. They say David Narver is doing a good job.

on his hands and knees. The trails in Western Alaska were virtual tunnels the great brown bears had to maintain through otherwise impenetrable alder. "I'm not sure I would do that again," he laughed, "especially since discovering how badly bears can smell." His encounters with Alaska's brown bears - the largest in the world - came while he was researching sockeye runs in Alaska, including the Chignik River. During one 13-mile walk along the river, Narver and his partner counted 40 of the magnificent beasts. "We saw that many because he made the walk with the wind always against us," he explained. "On the way out, with the wind behind us, we never saw one." To those bears, he said, man was a complete stranger and Narver often was able to pass safely - while only 20 or 30 feet away. That was in the 1950s, before the advent of fly-in fishing and hunting trips. "They also were pretty well fed. I think every bear in Alaska used to head down to those rivers for the spawning runs of the salmon. They gorged themselves on salmon and weren't interested in anything undelectable as a biologist." He is 52 today. Dave and wife Barbara are the parents of four boys. "If I can get it right," Narver says he is able to introduce sons Doug, 28; Jeff, 26, Stephen, 25, and Chris, 16. Chris has his father's hotshot enthusiasm for fishing and hunting. He bagged his first deer when he was 11 and has shot five bucks in the last four years. A 1956 graduate from Oregon State, Narver worked in three years as a navigation instructor with the U.S. Air Force before returning to Washington to get his masters degree. His research of sockeye salmon runs in Alaska brought him to Nanaimo and the DFO's
Sockeyes one more win away

Kamloops star hurt in finale Kamloops Blazers learned a painful lesson in their pursuit of Western Hockey League records Sunday n night. Scoring champion Rob Brown, who had already established four individual scoring marks this season, suffered an ankle injury in the third period of Kamloops' final regular season game, a 7-3 victory over New Westminster Bruins at Queens Park Arena. "We're certainly hoping the injury isn't any more serious than a bad sprain but even at that, Brownie will still miss the first three, maybe four playoff games against Victoria,' said Kamloops coach Ken Hitchcock. "If it is bad, someone else will have to come to the forefront. You don't replace a guy like Rob. He's the best player in the league." The Blazers did manage to break the WHL record for most goals in a season as their total of 496 eclipsed the 1982-83 standard of 495 set by Portland Winter Hawks. The Bruins, meanwhile, concluded their dismal campaign at 18-50-4, going 2-22-1 in their last 25 games. They were 1-14-1 against Kamloops alone. "I don't worry about bad seasons because I've had many more good than bad ones and nobody remembers the bad ones anyway," said New Westminster coach Ernie McLean. The West Division best-of-nine semi-finals open this week with Victoria's Cougars facing Kamloops and Spokane Chiefs meeting Portland.

penalty killers. "We count on our power play for a good part of our offence," he said, "but their penalty-killers did a good job of shutting us down." With Doug Smail, Ron Wilson, Mario Marois and Jim Kyte handling most of the load, the Jets snuffed six Vancouver power-play opportunities. The Canucks began this game as if they didn't intend it to be another layer of frustration. Dave Richter, who played his minor hockey in suburban St. Boniface, scored only his second goal of the season early in the first. Just past the midway mark, Tony Tanti made it 2-0 with his 39th and his eighth at the expense of the Jets. Then the momentum abruptly shifted to Winnipeg. Rookie defenceman Fredrik Olausson scored his sixth of the season before the first period ended. Randy Carlyle's 14th goal and Dale Hawerchuk's 45th sent the Jets in front in the second. The third was scoreless.

Sun News Services Richmond Sockeyes are in the driver's seat - and have the motor running - in the B.C. Junior Hockey League championship playoff series. The Sockeyes took a seemingly insurmountable 4-0 lead in the best-of-nine series against Kelowna Packers with a 5-1 win in the Okanagan city Saturday, A Sockeyes' win Tuesday night at Richmond Arena (7:30) will give them a series sweep. "The guys are on a natural high," said Sockeyes' coach Orland Kurtenbach. "It was a nice game for us to win. If they're willing to listen and cut down mistakes, we'll do well at home (Tuesday)." The Sockeyes scored three unanswered goals in the last half of the third period en route to the Saturday win. Jason Philips opened the Sockeyes' scoring with a short-handed goal in the first period. Dave Tomlinson extended the margin to 2-0 early in the second before Brent Mowery scored Kelowna's only goal at 13:23 of the middle frame. Richmond's third-period insurance markers came from Philips, Bill Hardy and Mike McCormick.

Special To The Sun WINNIPEG - Vancouver Canucks lost a hockey game they needed desperately to win here Sunday afternoon, a situation they found painfully familiar. To long-suffering Canucks fans, to say nothing of long-suffering Canucks' coaches, it must seem their star-crossed heroes have spent most of their 17-year National Hockey League history in similar straits - coming up just short in critical situations. Sunday's burden was a 3-2 loss to the Jets, fashioned before an audience of 13,832 at Winnipeg Arena. It was the 74th game of the season for both teams and yielded the Jets' 38th win and the Canucks' 41st loss. In addition to the two points, the Canucks also squandered a brilliant 46-save performance from goaltender Richard Brodeur. Even more painful were the circumstances. Midway through the second period, the out-of-town scoreboard showed the Whalers had defeated Los Angeles Kings 6-3 in Hartford. Victory would have allowed the Canucks to close within four points of the Kings - and the Smythe Division's fourth and final playoff berth. But the Kings' loss proved insufficient motivation to kick-start the Canucks' sputtering offence.

Canadian Press CALGARY - Mervyn (Red) Dutton, former president of the National Hockey League and a member of the Hockey Hall of Fame, died here Sunday following a lengthy illness. He was 89. Born Jan. 3, 1898 in Russell, Man., Dutton moved to Calgary in 1922 and played for four years with the Calgary Tigers of the old professional Western Canada Hockey League. When that league was disbanded, Dutton played in the NHL for 10 years, the first four with the Montreal Maroons. He was traded in 1930 to the New York Americans for Lionel Conacher, Canada's athlete of the halfcentury, and spent the next six years there. Dutton scored 29 goals and added 67 assists in 449 regular-season

SEATTLE - Dean Sexsmith and Shawn Chambers each had two goals to help Seattle Thunderbirds gain a 7-7 overtime tie against New Westminster Bruins in a Western Hockey League game Sunday night. Stuart Sage scored three goals to lead the Bruins, who trailed 3-2 after the first period but had a 6-5 lead after 40 minutes. Chris Joseph, Mike Schwengler and Trevor Sim completed the Seattle scoring while Glen Goodall had three assists. Scott Rawson, Sean LeBrun, Cal Zankowski and Mario Desjardins, who fired the equalizing goal on a power play with 7:36 remaining in the game, were the other Bruin snipers. Goalie Drago Adam blocked 34 shots for the Bruins while Danny Lorenz made 35 saves for the Thunderbirds. In Victoria Saturday, Kamloops Blazers drubbed the Cougars 9-4 as Rob Brown collected six points to break Brian Propp's WHL career points mark of 511. Brown now has 514 points, earned in four seasons.

The East Region will have four seeded teams - two of which are likely to be Harvard and St. Lawrence of Canton, N.Y., which finished first and second in the Eastern Collegiate Athletic Conference. The Hockey East finalists are Boston College and Maine. In Sunday's semi Boston College whipped Northeastern 9-3 while Maine edged University of Lowell 5-4. They meet for the league title tonight at Boston Garden. Quarter-final winners will advance to the U.S. national championships to be played March 26-28 in Detroit.

Associated Press North Dakota is seeded first and defending champ Michigan State second among West Region teams playing in the National Collegiate Athletic Association Division I hockey quarter-finals. Ohio's Bowling Green and Minnesota will round out the West's seeded teams. Bowling Green has been seeded third and Minnesota fourth. North Dakota, led by Tony Hrkac of Thunder Bay, Ont., defeated Minnesota 10-6 in a two-game, total-goals championship for the Western Collegiate Hockey Association title. Sockeyes sweep Flyers in BCJHL semi Canadian Press Paul Rutherford scored at 4:37 of overtime to give Richmond Sockeyes a 6-5 win over Delta Flyers Saturday and a four-game sweep of the B.C. Junior Hockey League Coastal Division best-of-seven series. The Sockeyes now meet Kelowna Packers for the league championship. The first two games of the best-of-nine series are Tuesday and Wednesday at Richmond Arena at 7:30. Jason Phillips paced the Sockeyes attack with two power-play goals while singles went to Dave Tomlinson, Brian Kozak and Bill Hawly. Lance Momotani had a pair of goals in the losing cause for Delta while Mike Bafaro, Drew Sawtell and Richard Stantiford had singles.
