Canadian Press CALGARY - Mervyn (Red) Dutton, former president of the National Hockey League and a member of the Hockey Hall of Fame, died here Sunday following a lengthy illness. He was 89. Born Jan. 3, 1898 in Russell, Man., Dutton moved to Calgary in 1922 and played for four years with the Calgary Tigers of the old professional Western Canada Hockey League. When that league was disbanded, Dutton played in the NHL for 10 years, the first four with the Montreal Maroons. He was traded in 1930 to the New York Americans for Lionel Conacher, Canada's athlete of the halfcentury, and spent the six years there. Dutton scored 29 goals and added 67 assists in 449 regular-season

Sockeyes sweep Flyers in BCJHL semi Canadian Press Paul Rutherford scored at 4:37 of overtime to give Richmond Sockeyes a 6-5 win over Delta Flyers Saturday and a four-game sweep of the B.C. Junior Hockey League Coastal Division best-of-seven series. The Sockeyes now meet Kelowna Packers for the league championship. The first two games of the best-of-nine series are Tuesday and Wednesday at Richmond Arena at 7:30. Jason Phillips paced the Sockeyes attack with two power-play goals while singles went to Dave Tomlinson, Brian Kozak and Bill Hawly. Lance Momotani had a pair of goals in the losing cause for Delta while Mike Bafaro, Drew Sawtell and Richard Stantiford had singles.

Associated Press North 1 Dakota is seeded first and defending champ Michigan State second a among West Region teams playing the National Collegiate Athletic Association Division I hockey quarter-finals. Ohio's Bowling Green and Minnesota will round out the West's seeded teams. Bowling Green has been seeded third and Minnesota fourth. North Dakota, led by Tony Hrkac of Thunder Bay, Ont., defeated Minnesota 10-6 in a two-game, total-goals championship for the Western Collegiate Hockey Association title. Saturday, the Sioux repeated Friday's 5-3 win. Bowling Green and Michigan State compete in the Central Collegiate Hockey Association. Quarter pairings won't be
Hockey Hall of Famer Red Dutton dead at 89

NHL games. He coached the Americans for six more years after retiring as a player in 1936. In 1943, Dutton succeeded the ailing Frank Calder as NHL president, serving for three years. Dutton turned the NHL presidency over to Clarence Campbell in 1946 and never entered an NHL arena again until Oct. 9, 1980 = when he dropped the puck at the Calgary Flames' debut game. *One of the reasons I stayed away was because it (hockey) was in my blood," said Dutton. "I couldn't sleep at night.' Although he didn't attend games in person, he followed the NHL closely through television and newspapers. During his coaching tenure, Dutton pioneered the form of travel now commonplace to all professional sports - flying. He was the first to fly a hockey club, the New York Americans, between games in 1938. His last public appearance at a hockey game was last May when he dropped the puck in a ceremonial faceoff to open the Stanley Cup final series between Calgary and the Montreal Canadiens. And despite all his achievements in hockey as a coach, player, president - trustee of the Stanley Cup since March 30, 1952 - and a member of the Hall of Fame selection committee for 15 years, Dutton never realized his ultimate goal. "I had everything else, but I was never a member of a Stanley Cup champion," he said in a 1981 inter"That was one of my regrets of hockey."

Canadian Press SEATTLE - Dean Sexsmith and Shawn Chambers each had two goals to help Seattle Thunderbirds gain a 7-7 overtime tie against New Westminster Bruins in a Western Hockey League game Sunday night. Stuart Sage scored three goals to lead the Bruins, who trailed 3-2 after the first period but had a 6-5 lead after 40 minutes. Chris Joseph, Mike Schwengler and Trevor Sim completed the Seattle scoring while Glen Goodall had three assists. Scott Rawson, Sean LeBrun, Cal Zankowski and Mario Desjardins, who fired the equalizing goal on a power play with 7:36 remaining in the game, were the other Bruin snipers. Goalie Drago Adam blocked 34 shots for the Bruins while Danny Lorenz made 35 saves for the Thunderbirds. In Victoria Saturday, Kamloops Blazers drubbed the Cougars 9-4 as Rob Brown collected six points to break Brian Propp's WHL career points mark of 511. Brown now has 514 points, earned in four seasons. Nelson, Elk Valley brace for showdown

Canadian Press NELSON - The Nelson Maple Leafs will meet the Elk Valley Blazers in the Western International Hockey League championship series, now that both teams have scored swift semi-final victories. The Leafs advanced with a 10-1 win over the Cranbrook Royals Saturday to win their best-of-seven series 4-1. The Blazers crushed the Kimberley Dynamiters 16-1 Saturday to sweep their series in four straight. The final series begins in Fernie next Friday, with game two scheduled for Sunday.

The East Region will have four seeded teams - two of which are likely to be Harvard and St. Lawrence of Canton, N.Y., which finished first and second in the Eastern Collegiate Athletic Conference. The Hockey East finalists are Boston College and Maine. In Sunday's semi-finals, Boston College whipped Northeastern 9-3 while Maine edged the University of Lowell 5-4. They meet for the league title tonight at Boston Garden. Quarter-final winners will advance to the U.S. national championships to be played March 26-28 in Detroit.

Led by two-goal performances of Brad Chin and Robbie James, the Pierrefonds Lions took another step along the comeback trail last Friday when they beat the LaSalle Cyclones 7-3. The victory clinched a berth for the Lions in the Inter-Regional Junior AA Hockey League playoffs. Making the playoffs in the 10-team league isn't much of a reason to brag since only the bottom two squads don't qualify for post-season play. But making the playoffs this season will have more meaning than usual for the Pierrefonds Lions. In December, the season seemed already over for the team and there was talk about "building for next

The John Abbott College Islanders men's hockey team has taken a lot of abuse both on and off the ice this season, but after Tuesday's encounter with Collège Montmorency you have to wonder whether they really deserved all that abuse. The first place Nomades waltzed in to Glenfinnan Arena thinking of nothing more than an easy win to add to their 24-4 record. What they got was a long way from the easy-win category as the Islanders put up quite a battle before the Nomades managed to squeeze out the win.

To do it, the Lions will have to win their last two games of the season - against eighth-place Montreal Athlétiques tomorrow night and topranked St. Hyacinthe on Sunday. If they do finish sixth, the Lions will face third-place St. Antoine in the first playoff round. But a seventh-place finish would pit the Lions against second-place Longueuil. General manager Paul Pellin who also wore the coach's hat for the first half of the season - named Cunningham as his head coach last month. Taking himself out from behind the bench may have turned out to be Pellin's best move of the year. What is the secret of the new coach's success? "We're using each player to his • fullest potential," Cunningham said. "The first thing we did was evaluate every player on the team and identify | his strong and weak points." Cunningham also brought in some new blood in Brad Chin and Stéphane Ishmael, who were playing CEGEP AAA hockey in St. Jérôme. Abbotsford Falcons served noticed Saturday night that they are saving their best for last. Falcons, trailing 3-2 midway through the final period, scored two goals to defeat the visiting Nanaimo Clippers 4-3 in B.C. Junior Hockey League action at MSA Arena. Abbotsford, i in the basement of the six-team Coastal Division, refused to roll over and play dead after the

playoff-bound Clippers moved ahead 3-2 in the second stanza on goals from Lawrence Amy, Mike Knapton and Darren Biggs. John Wilson and Rick Rozecki had scored for the Falcons in the first two periods. Joe Sobotin tied the game at the 11-minute mark of the third period and Troy Kaye notched the game game winner at 16:42. He was ejected from the game a minute later when both teams got involved in a shortlived fracas. It was the Falcons 14th win in 45 outings and they now trail the fifthplace Juan de Fuca Whalers by just four points. Abbotsford outshot Nanaimo 33-27 and received 73 of the game's 137 penalty minutes. Last Wednesday in Richmond, the Sockeyes maintained their dominance over the Falcons by blanking their visitors 5-0. Stan Czenozek, Brian Kozak, Rob Clarke, Bill Hardy and Jason Phillips scored for the division-leading Sockeyes. Falcons play host to Delta Flyers tonight (Wednesday) and Nanaimo Clippers on Saturday. Both games start at 8 p.m.

The loss was the Bruins' fourth consecutive and it kept them in third place in the Adams Division with 61% points, four back of the Canadiens and five behind the division-leading Hartford Whalers. Scoring for the Canadiens was Mats Naslund with a pair of goals and two assists, while Ryan Walter, Claude Lemieux, Bobby Smith, Peter Svoboda and Guy Carbonneau added singles. Steve Kasper, with two goals, and Ray Bourque replied for Boston. Maple Leafs 1 Kings Los Angeles goaltender Rollie Melanson and Toronto goalie Allan Bester turned in strong performances as the Kings and visiting Maple Leafs skated to a tie.

Notes, quotes and leftovers from the wonderful world of sports. . . . Ken Priestlay scored one of the prettiest goals I've ever seen in junior hockey Saturday night in Victoria. Priestlay, who started his junior career in Nanaimo at age 16, got a shot away while being dragged to the ice by Kamloops defenceman Garth Premak. Stacey Nickel, another former Clipper, made the save, but Priestlay managed to one-hand a shot high to the corner past Nickel. When I interviewed Priestlay as a 16-year-old rookie, he told me was intent on playing tier one in hopes of turning pro one day. He scored six goals during a brief stint with Buffalo Sabres earlier this season and his offensive prowess and great breakaway speed could earn him another look before the year's over. "I'd like to further my education through hockey," says. Biggs, who hopes to be a biology instructor. "I'll go as far as I can get with hockey, but I'll always have an education to fall back on." Another Clipper who is a candidate for a hockey scholarship is blueliner Matt Woods. He has. received six offers, including a pair of Canadian universities. "I think it would be a lot better experience than playing tier one," said Woods. North Dakota has been the most persistent, sending Woods information about the school on a weekly basinstead of using hockey to just get in the NHL, which is difficult, I'd rather use it to get an education," said Woods. "It's the best way to go and it makes more sense." Dutka is also approached by scouts with questions about the players they're interested in. "They usually ask a number of questions," said Dutka. ""Is he still eligible? How are his grades? Is he interested in a scholarship? How is his coachability? What's his attitude and work ethic like?' "The coachablity is important," added Dutka. "If they can get 20-22 coachable players into their camp, it makes it a lot easier for the coach." Mike Knapton is in his make or break year for a scholarship. *The playoffs will be the key for me." said Knapton, who has received letters from a couple of schools. "If I have a good playoff, it will really help my chances." Laurence Amy has* visions of scholarships as well, and has received letters from two schools. His reasons for going to school *are not what you might expect.

Staff Writer The last part of the regular season and the playoffs 'are the crunch time. That's when college scouts make their final decisions as to who to offer scholarships to and who to reject. So if it seems as if Nanaimo Clipper players show a little extra jump tonight when they, host Juan de Fuca Whalers p.m., Civic Arena), don't be surprised. A number of Clippers have been approached by colleges in the United States and Canada, and since no offers have been made, hope those offers will come if they have a good playoff. Second-year forward Darren Biggs is so far the leading contender for a scholarship, with letters from 10 universities expressing interest his services despite a shoulder in injury that has affected his performance. would have to say Darren Biggs has the best chance for getting a scholarship," said Clipper coach Rich Dutka. "They (scouts) like his speed, his hustle and his dedication.' the American college route instead' of playing major junior hockey has become increasingly popular over the last few years, and former B.1 Junior Hockey League players like Joe Murphy, Brett Hull, Todd Decker and former Clipper netminder Frank Furlan have been successful in getting an education while playing hockey. Dutka estimates six Clippers have serious shots at gaining. scholarships with in the next couple of years. He says it's important that the players realize scouts always looking for dedicated players. "The guys going for scholarships should have it in the back of their mind that they have to give : a hundred per cent all the time," said Dutka. 6 Biggs, who has been approached by schools like Alaska-Fairbanks, Miami of Ohio and Wisconsin, says his main reason for playing hockey was to some day get a scholarship. "That's my. goal," said Biggs, 18. "That's what I hope to get out of junior hockey." Biggs knows the value of having an education.

Nanaimo Midget A Clippers won their fifth game of the season in dramatic style Saturday in Victoria-Esquimalt, as Mike Biggs scored on a breakway with two seconds left to play to give the Clippers a.4-3 win. The goal came after Darren Kennedy blocked a shot at the blueline, and Biggs picked up the loose puck and raced the length of the ice for the score. Mike Zimmer, Tony Seriani and Wally Bzdel score the. other goals for Nanaimo, who were down 1-0 after one period, and 3-2 after two, Danny Mattice blocked 16 shots in goal for the Clippers, whose record sits at 5-8-2 for third place in the South Island Minor Hockey League. In other action, Nanaimo Bantam A Clippers went 1-1-1 in weekend play. Friday, the Clippers played Victoria Racquet Club to a 0-0 tie in Victoria. Bryan Toffey stopped 38 shots for the shutout.

By The Canadian Press An improving defence and a textbook second period carried the Portland Winter Hawks to a 6-4 victory over the New Westminster Bruins Sunday night in the Western Hockey League. "We looked like a pretty awesome hockey team in the second period," Portland coach Ken Hodge said after his charges fired four goals in the middle frame. "I was very pleased with our penalty killing. Our defence is improving and tonight we got outstanding goaltending from Jim Swan." Bruins coach Ernie McLean, who tossed the team's water bottle holder on to the ice to signify his displeasure with the officiating, also criticized his club's play with the man advantage. "Our power play was non-existent; it was awful," he said after the two-for-11 performance. "Our guys wouldn't shoot the puck." In other games, a near-tragedy was averted in Regina when Pats'

SYDNEY, N.S. (CP) Cape Breton is back to normal following a Canada Winter Games that was a gold mine for Quebec and a reason to boast for organizers. The Games held every two years for developing athletes across Canada ended Saturday with Quebec topping the medal list and officials gushing over packed houses. Quebec, represented by 250 athletes over the two period, went on a tear in the final six days, winning 32 gold to wind up with 47. Ontario finished second with 20 gold from its 252 competitors. The news wasn't all bad for Ontario, however, which edged out Quebec 211-210 for the overall points championship and the Canada Games' Flag. It was the fourth straight time Ontario has captured the title and the eighth time in the 11 Games held. Competition ended Saturday with a schedule heavy on emotion and controversy. In New Waterford, the Nova Scotia men's basketball team delighted a partisan crowd of 1,200 with a 91-76 victory over Quebec. The win appeased a rather poor overall showing by Nova Scotia, which won only five gold and 12 medals in total. The basketball gold capped a week of lopsided wins for Nova Scotia, whose closest margin of victory was 12 points against Ontario. Its widest was a 67-point bulge over P.E.I. Quebec edged Ontario 6-5 in a wild hockey final marred by controversy., 6-4 with a minute to play, Ontario lifted its goaltender for an extra attacker and scored with 34 seconds to play. It appeared to tie the game 14 seconds later on a shot by defenceman Larry Van Hersle - just as referee Charlie Banfield was assessing a penalty. The penalty was against Ontario and the goal was disallowed. The call triggered a wild melee around Banfield, who said afterwards an Ontario player "took a whack" at Quebec goaltender Mar-

"Tretiak was my first idol," Gamble said Wednesday following a 5-2 loss to the New Westminster Bruins in the major junior Western Hockey League. "I'm really happy about this and I hope it goes through. "I think I can learn a lot and I think it would be quite an experience. I think anybody my age would love to work with Tretiak. He has to be one of the greatest goalies ever." Gamble, selected by Vancouver in the second round of the 1985 National Hockey League entry draft while he was with the WHL Medicine Hat Tigers, is so enthused he's ready to travel solo to Moscow, if necessary. By Danny Gallagher L-P Sports Writer The Regina Pats expect defenceman Chris Tarnowski to return to the lineup tonight for a game at the Agridome against the Saskatoon Blades. However, Pats head coach and general manager Doug Sauter said he isn't 100 per cent certain Tarnowski will make an appearance. Tarnowski quit the Western Hockey League team Monday and returned to his home in Edmonton to ponder his future. "It's 50/50 that he'll show up," said Sauter. "I'm not speculating." SWIFT CURRENT (CP) - The Swift Current Broncos had their highest one-game goal output of the year as they crushed the Brandon Wheat Kings 10-2 in Western Hockey League action Thursday. The Broncos were led by Joe Sakic and Peter Soberlak who both had four-point nights. Sakic scored his 48th, 49th and 50th goals of the year to become the first rookie to reach the 50-goal plateau this season. Soberlak had four assists. Others came from Tim Tisdale with two, Erin Ginnell, Clark Polglase, Ian Herbers, Todd Sceviour and Tracy Egelund. It was the first goal of the year for Poglase who has been with the team all season. Dale Marquette and Curtis Bateman replied for the Wheat Kings. The Broncos fired 48 shots at Brandon goalies George Manelukand Derek Babe, while Swift Current's Trevor Kruger stopped 22 of 24 drives. The game was originally scheduled for Jan. 2 but had to be delayed because of the Swift Current bus accident. It also marked the first time the Broncs have won back-to-back games since the accident. They defeated Saskatoon Blades 4-3 in overtime Tuesday.

The B.C. Hockey Association won't let former Pat Bryan Wells play with the Richmond Sockeyes of the B.C.. Junior Hockey League. Wells joined the Sockeyes Jan. 29 but didn't play. He was back home in Moosomin Wednesday after realizing that repeated attempts to allow him to play have failed. Wells was suspended Jan. 21 for the remainder of the WHL season because of a match penalty he received last Nov. 29 for cross-checking Moose Jaw Warriors defenceman Lyle Odelein in the mouth. Sockeyes head coach Orland Kurtenbach said yesterday that even though the WHL had permitted Wells to play in any other league, the BCHA has decided to abide by the suspension and so has the Canadian Amateur Hockey Association.

"I think it will go the limit," he added. The Packers beat the Sockeyes 4-3 in Richmond and 7-4 in Kelowna but that was before Richmond made extensive changes to its roster, loading up with players like goaltender Frank Romeo and defencemen Matt Hervey and Mike Claringbull (both 20 and with major junior experience). The Packers will counter with a roster that includes four players already committed to U.S. schools and at least two others entertaining offers. "I would say we're whatever you want us to be," said Swail when asked about his team. That ability to do it all resulted in a league-best 45-5-2 regular season record, a momentum that could be severely tested by the Sockeyes.

Staff Reporter More than the B.C. Junior Hockey League championship is at stake when the Richmond Sockeyes and the Kelowna Packers take to the ice tomorrow night at 7:30. The best-of-nine series between the Coastal Division champion Sockeyes and the Interior Division title-holding Packers also provides the teams with the chance theoretically to make some money. Play in the series, which continues Wednesday at Richmond Arena before heading off to Kelowna for games Friday and Saturday, really means something and as such attracts far more fans than regular season contests. That consideration also means the number of games played in each community is of key importance and will determine the series schedule. If it's tied after four games, two more games will be played in Richmond, the next two in Kelowna Memorial Arena and then the last game back in Richmond. Victories were scarce in Heart Lake Oldtimers Hockey League action on the weekend. Only the Habs managed a win, a 5-4 verdict over the Stars in the six-team league. The Kings and Leafs tied 4-4 and the Hawks knotted the Bruins 3-3 in other games. Bob Eby's three-goals powered the Habs. Art Cowan added one goal and one assist and Jinian Irvine one goal. Bill Kennedy, Farquarson, Gary Hamilton and Bill Connors drew one assist each. Jim Reid led the Stars with one goal and two assists and John Bianchi had one and one. Keith Dunn and John Roberts were the other scorers. Mike Topping claimed two assists and Robert Richard, John Koop and Barry Dimaline one each. Guy Bradley's third goal boosted the Kings to their deadlock with the Leafs. John Sudak added two goals and Bob Spafford one. John McKnight figured for three assists and Larry Bradley and Roger Thiffault one each. John Matwijszyn, Neil MacLean and Jim LeSarge had one goal and one assist each for the Leafs. Wayne Martin was the other scorer. Bob Cox gained two assists and Wayne Young and Bryon Arnott one each. The Hawks' Alan Parker fired the equalizer against the Bruins. Bruce Murray assisted. Bill Hannah and Scott Gray were the other scorers and Eric Deleeuw and Ernie Dalcourt had one assist each. Gene Gibbs, George Richards and Karry shared the Bruins' scoring. Ken Bickerton, Ron Young and Bill Hales had one assist each.

Canadian Press Nanaimo Clippers, Merritt Warriors and Penticton Knights all had something in common Saturday night. All three teams know what it is like to be drubbed 4-0 in their B.C. Junior Hockey League best-of-seven division semifinal series. In Nanaimo, the pain was that much sharper for the Clippers. The Richmond Sockeyes won the last game in the Coastal Division series by a 7-0 romp. Mike McCormick led Richmond with four goals while goalie Frank Romeo earned the shutout with 31 saves. The Sockeyes now wait for the winner of the Delta-Langley series, which continues tonight.

SPORTS SHORTS Huskies on way to final News Services WINNIPEG The Saskatchewan Huskies earned a spot in the CWUAA hockey final with a 5-1 victory yesterday over the Manitoba Bisons. Five players scored for Saskatchewan, which captured the best-of-three semifinal series 2-1, and goaltender Ross McKay kicked out 28 shots to preserve the win. Saskatchewan will face the University of Calgary Dinosaurs in the best-of-three conference final in Calgary next weekend. Mason perseveres NAIROBI, Kenya Carl Mason of England won the Kenya Open golf tournament in a three-hole, sudden-death playoff against countryman Roger Chapman. Mason and Chapman, along with Englishmen Gordon Brand and Martin Poxon, were tied at 275 after four rounds and remained tied after the first extra hole. Poxon and Brand were eliminated on the second playoff hole before Mason clinched the title when Chapman's tee shot went into long grass. The victory was worth $15,500 from a total purse of $100,000. Marathon record set NAGOYA, Japan - Carla Beurskens of The Netherlands, running into head winds and sleet, won the Nagoya International women's marathon in record time, beating Japan's Kazue Kojima by six minutes and 32 seconds. The 35-year-old Beurskens, winner of the 1986 Honolulu Marathon and -up in the 1986 Boston Marathon, took the lead after 16 kilometres and finished in 2:28.27, breaking the meet record of East German Katrin Doerre by 1:06. Rain dogs Pirates BRADENTON, Fla. Rain chased the Pittsburgh Pirates indoors for the second half of = practice at the Pirate City complex. Rain and heavy winds hit the Pirates midway through practice. The team battled the elements for almost an hour, then went under cover to finish the workout using two indoor batting cages.

Sockeyes roll over Kelowna Canadian Press KELOWNA - Richmond Sockeyes jumped to a 4-0 lead in the opening period Friday night and relied on intensity and rugged play en route to a 7-5 B.C. Junior Hockey League playoff victory over Kelowna Packers. Richmond holds a comfortable 3-0 margin in the best-of-nine league championship series. Game four goes here tonight with game five back in Richmond Tuesday. Richmond coach Orland Kurtenbach said "the key to any hockey game is if you've got people who are intense and taking the body, That's what it takes to win." His counterpart, Kelowna coach Bill Hogaboam, conceded his team was dominated. "They outbumped us, they were all over top of ... and bang, it ended up 4-0 (after one period). We only played 40 minutes of hockey tonight. Richmond is too disciplined to fall four goals behind." Dean Rutledge, Dave Tomlinson and Jason Philips each scored twice for the Sockeyes while Mike McCormick added a single. Kelowna was led by Jason Ruff and Pat Mangold, each with two goals, while Jamie Kurki rounded out the scoring. Richmond goaltender Frank Romeo made 26 saves while Packers Dusty Imoo and Lucien Carignan combined to stop 26 shots.

Knights feel the pressure Canadian Press It seems the Vernon Lakers are for real. The Lakers took a 2-0 lead in their B.C. Junior Hockey League quarter-final series Wednesday night by dumping the Penticton Knights, the defending Centennial Cup champions, 6-3. In other games, Langley. Vernon captain Doug Spooner scored two goals to lead the Lakers past the Knights in Penticton. Brad Woitas scored his second goal at 6:44 of sudden-death overtime to give the Eagles the first game of their series with the Flyers; Kevin Taillefer scored with 14 seconds remaining in regulation time to give the Packers a 2-0 lead in their series against the Warriors; and Mike McCormick scored twice to lead the Sockeyes to a 2-0 series lead over the visiting Clippers.

SPECIAL TEAMS BOOST LAKERS Canadian Press Special teams and an early goal helped the Vernon Lakers get the jump on the Penticton Knights with a 6-2 victory as the B.C. Junior Hockey League playoffs got under way on three fronts Tuesday night. Vernon scored four power goals and added a shorthanded marker to take a 1-0 lead in their best-of-seven series. Al Young, who scored once on the power play and got the shorthanded goal, and Mark Greyeyes counted twice for Vernon. In the other quarter-final openers, Kevin Taillefer scored four times as the Kelowna Packers drubbed the Merritt Warriors 11-1 and Brian Kozak scored twice in an overtime period as the Richmond Sockeyes registered a 7-4 win over Nanaimo Clippers. The playoffs continue tonight with Vernon at Penticton, Kelowna at Merritt, Nanaimo at Richmond and the Delta Flyers opening their series with the Eagles in Langley.

• It's no wonder Soviet coach Anatoli Tarasov is eager to get back to Canuckland for an operation to repair his wonky hip. Where else do they give you room service and a free lunch to criticize their hockey team? Crazy people, these capitalists. When he left Vancouver a couple of weeks ago, Tarasov was loaded down with mementos from the Canucks, including a desk top totem pole and a Haida Indian talking stick usually reserved for visiting heads of state. One of Tarasov's most prized souvenirs is Steve Tambellini's hockey stick. Tarasov is an admirer of Tambellini's father, Addie, who played with the 1961 world champion Trail Smoke Eaters. Besides feeling wronged and his character impugned, Pat Quinn's insistence on fighting his suspension has a practical side. "I don't think it will inhibit my ability to work with other clubs," he said. "But I don't want anybody I work with here to ever doubt that I'm not being straightforward with them."

Special to The Sun CALGARY - "I'm cheering for the Jets," Tom Watt said while forcing a smile and nursing a beer. Cheering for Vancouver Canucks at the Saddledome Tuesday night wasn't much fun for Watt as the NHL's poorest excuse for a hockey team was blanked 2-0 by Calgary Flames. So, coach Watt jumped on the Jets' bandwagon, praying Winnipeg would do him a favor by beating Los Angeles Kings, the team Vancouver has to catch to avoid an early tee -time on the golf course. Yet, just as Watt was noting that the Jets might beat L.A., Flames' executive Al Coates strode by and piped: "Kings 8-3". Nothing like bad news to make a man's brew go flat: "Why don't you keep walking, rabbit ears?" quipped Watt, struggling to maintain his sense of humor after watching a red-hot Reggie Lemelin hang an old-fashioned goose egg on the Canucks. "I don't think we were a screwup defensively," Watt had noted earlier in grasping for a silver lining. "Our penalty killing improved." Although the Canucks kept Lemelin hopping with 33 shots, their powerless play continued, going 0- 3. They've failed to pot a powerplay goal in their last 17 chances. Furthermore, the drought continued for captain Stan Smyl and Petri Skriko, neither of whom has scored in the last 11 games. Smyl, who is also pointless in his last nine games, didn't even get a shot on goal. "We have a lot of guys having dry spells," understated Watt, whose Canucks trail the Kings by 11 points with 17 games remaining. "I've been pleased with our efforts all year, but, when you don't get points, you can't keep saying that." The last thing Vancouver needed last night was to run into a hot goaltender. But that they did in Lemelin, who hasn't lost since Dec. 11.

While the New Westminster Bruins are doing all the things a team does when the regular season is over and it hasn't made the playoffs, the Western Hockey League club isn't finished yet. Preparations including fund-raising continue as the Queen's Park Arena crew prepares for its departure to Europe April 10 for the Coupe Beard competition April 14-21 in Switzerland. Tuesday night, though, the club honored some of its players at an awards banquet at Centennial Lodge that was attended by about 150 people. In awards decided by the coaching staff, Burnaby native Stuart Sage was named the most improved player while linemate Scott Rawson, who's from North Vancouver, was named the rookie of the year and top scorer (edging Sage by one point). Gerry Johannson and Jayson More shared most valuable defenceman honors

The award will be presented to Mathers, and posthumously to Baker, the great Princeton star and a legend in U.S. hockey history, at the Stanley Cup luncheon during the championship series. Shoulder aching ST. LOUIS - A strained shoulder will keep. 35-goal scorer Mark Hunter out of the St. Louis Blues lineup for a minimum of five days. Hunter, who plays right wing, was examined by orthopedic surgeon Dr. Jerome Gilden who said the injury was a strain of the joint between the shoulder and breast bone. A counter punch BOSTON - A lawyer for Marvin Hagler is expected to seek a temporary restraining order tomorrow to block a World Boxing Association decision to strip him of his middleweight title. Hagler's lawyer, Morris Goldings, has a hearing scheduled in the federal Appeals Court before Judge Robert Keeton and is expected to ask for a preliminary injunction.
HOCKEY NOTEBOOK

News Services GAINESVILLE, Fla. - Mike Smith of Kenora, Ont., led the decathlon at the Florida Relays track and field invitational with 4,238 points after the first day of competition yesterday. Smith, competing for the University of Toronto Track Club, set a meet record in winning the 400 metres in 47.63 seconds. He also ran 10.97 seconds in the 100 metres, went 7.25 metres in the long jump, 2.06 metres in the high jump, and threw the shot put 13.72 metres. Knut Gundersen of Mount St. Mary's College in Emmitsburg, Md., stood second with 3,929 points. The competitors complete the final five events today. Stamps ink pair CALGARY - The CFL's Calgary Stampeders have signed Canadian university all-stars Rob Ros and David Pappin to contracts for the 1987 season. Ros, 23, was a leading receiver for UBC Thunderbirds. He earned all-star honors last year, making two touchdown catches in the Vanier Cup including the game winner against Western Ontario Mustangs. Pappin, 23, played linebacker for McMaster Marauders. Big puck awards NEW YORK - Frank Mathers, president and general manager of the Hershey Bears of the American Hockey League, and the late Hobey Baker have been awarded the Lester Patrick Trophy for service to hockey. The trophy is awarded annually by the NHL and the New York Rangers "for outstanding service to hockey in the U.S. in fond memory of a great man in the history of a great sport.'

the Bruins have high hopes for are a pair of 16-year-olds, forward Stu Barnes of the Alberta Junior Hockey League's St. Albert Saints and goaltender Dusty Imoo of the Kelowna Packers. Barnes led the Saints in goal scoring as a rookie while Imoo, who hails from Surrey, was the leading regular goalie in the BCJHL's Interior Division with a 3.103 goals-against average. Imoo, whose Packers lost the league final to Richmond Sockeyes Tuesday, is contemplating his future. He has the options of continuing in junior A, jumping into major junior with the Bruins (who lose Adam) or continuing with his accelerated schooling and head off to an American scholarship. Joe Sakic, a high-scoring centre with the Swift Current Broncos, has become the first rookie since the WHL's first season (1966-67) to be named the East Division player of the year. The 17-year-old native of Burnaby had 60 goals and 133 points in 72 games.

"I played 18 years of professional hockey and I'm remembered for one 10-second shift." The player, who collected 760 points in the NHL in 1,067 games was - you guessed - Paul Henderson. Ashe said, the superstar of Super Series has the mixed blessing ofhaving his entire career overshadowed by the events of September, 1972. Most Canadians can remember where they were when watching, or listening to Henderson score the dramatic goal. Playing in Super Series, the first meeting of NHL allstars and the Soviet Nationals, Team Canada trailed 3-1- with three games remaining in Moscow. And i it was Henderson who scoredtie-breaking goals in each game, including the series winner with 34 seconds remaining in Game 8. Henderson started his NHL career with Detroit in 1962 and played for Toronto from 1968-74. He then shifted to the World Hockey Association Toronto Toros before completing his career with the Birmingham Bulls. Henderson will be among those in the lineup for the Legends of Hockey east- -west challenge March 27 at the Pacific Coliseum. Proceeds go to Rick Hansen's Manin-Motion world tour.

By The Canadian Press The availability of two key players could affect the playoff chances of the Kamloops Blazers and the Victoria Cougars, the clubs' coaches said Sunday night as the teams finished the regular Western Hockey League schedule. The Blazers will likely be missing all-star centre Rob Brown when they meet the Cougars Thursday night in the first game of their best-of-nine West Division semifinal. And the Cougars are hoping Buffalo Sabres of the NHL will soon be eliminated from playoff contention so they can release Ken Priestlay. Brown suffered an ankle injury as the Blazers set a goal-scoring record they downed the New Westminster Bruins 7-3 while the Cougars bowed 6-4 to the Portland Winter Hawks. The Spokane Chiefs thumped the Seattle Thunderbirds 7-3 in the other game. "We need a few days to get healthy and hope that Buffalo gets eliminated from the NHL playoffs," said Victoria coach Wayne Naka, referring to Priestlay, who had 82 points in 33 games with Victoria, is on emergency recall with the Sabres. 'A lot of people looking at the playoffs don't expect Kamloops to lose to anybody but we didn't get into the playoffs to die in five. There have been times that the Victoria Cougars have played well and have beaten the Kamloops Blazers. The key is winning one of the first three games." During the regular season, Victoria beat the Blazers five out of 14 games, winning three in a row against Kamloops which finished the WHL season at 55-14-3, the best record in Canadian major junior hockey. WHL playoffs begin Tuesday in the East Division with the Swift Current Broncos visiting the Prince Albert Raiders for the first game of their best-of-five' first-round matchup. On Wednesday, Prince Albert is at Swift Current and Spokane opens the other best-of-nine West Division semifinal in Portland. Blazers 7 Bruins 3 Two third-period goals by former Bruin Glenn Mulvenna secured the Kamloops victory and established a WHL record for most goals in a season for the Blazers. Kamloops finished with 496 goals in 72 games, eclipsing the 1982-83 record of 495 set by Portland. Sockeyes in control Richmond Sockeyes have taken a stranglehold in their best-of-nine B.C. Junior Hockey League final series with Kelowna Packers. The Coastal Division champs beat Kelowna 5-1 Saturday before 1,600 fans in Kelowna to take a 4-0 lead in the series. The Sockeyes held a 2-1 lead late in the game and salted the game away with three goals in the closing minutes. Jason Phillips led the winners with a pair of goals, Dave Thomlinson, Mike McCormick and ex-Nanaimo Clipper Bill Hardy added singles for Richmond, who held period leads of 1-0 and 2-1. Brent Mowery scored the lone goal for Kelowna, who were outshot 27-24. The fifth and possibly final game will take place Tuesday in Rich-

in the net. Vancouver coach Tom Watt credited Jets rookie goaltender Eldon (Pokey) Reddick with playing a strong game. "Even though Winnipeg had a wide range in the shots, I thought Reddick ma made some big stops when we could have got back in the D game, said Watt. •Peter Zezel will be lost to the Philadelphia Flyers for three weeks, team officials said after the centre underwent arthroscopic surgery Saturday. After Thurday night's National Hockey League loss to the Los Angeles Kings, Zezel's sore left knee was examined by team surgeon John Gregg, who suspected a torn cartilage. Further examinations confirmed the diagnosis and Zezel underwent surgery Saturday, Gregg said. Gregg said Zezel should be able to return in about three weeks for the second game of the playoffs.

Clippers capture B.C. Bantam title Nanaimo Bantam B Clippers bounced back from a 10-6 loss Saturday to North Shore Winter Club to beat the same team 9-3 Sunday and win the provincial Bantam B hockey championship in Kamloops. The win caps a great year for the Clippers, who ran away with the Island title to advance to the provincial tourney. In the title game, Paul Scorer fired a hat-trick, while tourney Most Valuable Player Justin Heathcote scored twice. Jim Carpenter, Brad Dunn, Troy Miller and Steve Sloan added singles, while Mike Feser played an outstanding game in goal for the Clippers. In the Saturday loss, Dunn scored a pair of goals, while Lonnie Sullivan, Scorer, Jon McColm and Sloan scored singles in a losing cause. Earlier Saturday, Nanaimo beat defending champions Kamloops 7-2, with Scorer notching the hat-trick. McColm with two, Sullivan and Dunn also scored for the winners. "I thought our boys played super,' said team manager Larry Smith. "The title game was an all-out team effort." Cougars, Blazers set for playoffs

snapped Dionne. At one time they had formed a mutual admiration society. In his first year coaching L.A., Quinn noted, "You know he is a great athlete but when you are coaching against him you like to think he can't play defensively. But he does play defence, he does care very much about the team and he is proud. I guess playing in Los Angeles is part of the problem. There is a feeling among hockey people that L.A. is sort of a different world." Toiling in Los Angeles, Dionne compromised fame. The second leading scorer of all time never played in a Stanley Cup final, or even a semi-final. But that lack of exposure and the absence of fan pressure probably prolonged his career. Guy Lafleur, drafted No. 1 to Dionne's No. 2 in 1971, burned out a while ago. Injuries claimed

. Johnson put together a line with two rookies and veteran checker Tim Hunter Tuesday night, and it paid unexpected dividends. Unexpected to everyone but Johnson. "I expect great things out of them," said Johnson, after rookies Joe Nieuwendyk and Gary Roberts scored second-period goals to rally the Flames to a 5-3 National Hockey League victory over the Washington Capitals. Nieuwendyk, a 21-year old centre who signed with the Flames last Thursday, was appearing in his first NHL game after playing three seasons at Cornell University. He scored at 10:03 of the second period to tie the score at 2-2. Roberts, playing in his 23rd NHL game, put the Flames ahead at 13:54. "Timmy took out both defencemen, and I just went in and picked the puck up," said Nieuwendyk. "I couldn't ask for a better way to start. After a disappointing season at Cornell, where we didn't make the playoffs, this is a great opportunity.' Goaltender Mike Vernon, still a rookie despite playing in 21 of the Flames' 22 playoff games last spring, posted his 28th victory of the season for Calgary. The Flames equalled a club record with their 18th road victory of the season. The Flames and Edmonton Oilers are the only clubs in the league to have winning road records this season. • New York Islanders received second-period goals from Mikko Makela, Brent Sutter and Duane Sutter to lead the Isles to a 6-3 win over Pittsburgh Penguins. Mike Bossy had a goal, his 37th, and an assist as the Islanders ended Pittsburgh's two-game winning streak. The Penguins have not won more than two consecutive games since opening the season with a team-record seven straight victories. •Randy Moller put Quebec ahead for good in the second period and Michel Goulet added two goals in the third as the Nordiques ended a five-game winless streak, beating Hartford Whalers 6-4. The win moved Quebec back into fourth place and the final playoff spot in the Adams Division with 59 points, two more than the idle Buffalo Sabres.

Sockeyes take opener in OT Canadian Press They worked overtime in the B.C. Junior Hockey League Tuesday night and both winners came up with 7-6 victories. In Richmond, Mike McCormick scored at 3:19 of the second overtime period to give the Sockeyes the nod over Delta Flyers. Game two of their best-of-seven series is tonight at 7:30 at Richmond Arena. In Vernon, Jamie Kurki scored at 4:26 of the first overtime period to lead Kelowna Packers past the Lakers, tying that series 2-2.

Special to The Sun CALGARY - "I'm cheering for the Jets," Y Tom Watt said while forcing a smile and nursing a beer. Cheering for Vancouver Canucks at the Saddledome Tuesday night wasn't much fun for Watt as the NHL's poorest excuse for a hockey team was blanked 2-0 by Calgary Flames. So, coach Watt jumped on the Jets' bandwagon, praying Winnipeg would do him a favor by beating Los Angeles Kings, the team Vancouver has to catch to avoid an early tee-time on the golf course. Yet, just as Watt was noting that the Jets might beat L.A., Flames' executive Al Coates strode by and piped: "Kings 8-3". Nothing like bad news to make a man's brew go flat: "Why don't you keep walking, rabbit ears?" quipped Watt, struggling to maintain his sense of humor after watching a red-hot Reggie Lemelin hang an old-fashioned goose egg on the Canucks. "I don't think we were a screwup defensively," Watt had noted earlier in grasping for a silver lining. "Our penalty killing improved." Although the Canucks kept Lemelin hopping with 33 shots, they're powerless play continued, going 0- 3. They've failed to pot a powerplay goal in their last 17 chances. Furthermore, the drought continued for captain Stan Smyl and Petri 10 Skriko, neither of whom has scored in the last 11 games. Smyl, who is also pointless in his last nine games, didn't even get a shot on goal. "We have a lot of guys having dry spells," Canucks understated Watt, whose trail the Kings by 11 points with 17 games remaining. "I've been pleased with our efforts all year, but, when you don't get points, you can't keep saying that." The last thing Vancouver needed last night was to run into a hot goaltender. But that they did in Lemelin, who hasn't lost since Dec. 11.

SPECIAL TEAMS BOOST LAKERS Canadian Press Special teams and an early goal helped the Vernon Lakers get the jump on the Penticton Knights with a 6-2 victory as the B.C. Junior Hockey League playoffs got under way on three fronts Tuesday night. Vernon scored four power-play goals and added a shorthanded marker to take a 1-0 lead in their best-of-seven series. Al Young, who scored once on the power play and got the shorthanded goal, and Mark Greyeyes counted twice for Vernon. In the other quarter-final openers, Kevin Taillefer scored four times as the Kelowna Packers drubbed the Merritt Warriors 11-1 and Brian Kozak scored twice in an overtime period as the Richmond Sockeyes registered a 7-4 win over Nanaimo Clippers. The playoffs continue tonight with Vernon at Penticton, Kelowna at Merritt, Nanaimo at Richmond and the Delta Flyers opening their series with the Eagles in Langley.

I wish them all the success. hope people don't take it the wrong way, but it was something I had to do." • It's no wonder Soviet coach Anatoli Tarasov is eager to get back to Canuckland for an operation to repair his wonky hip. Where else do they give you room service and a free lunch to criticize their hockey team? Crazy people, these capitalists. When he left Vancouver a couple of weeks ago, Tarasov was loaded down with mementos from the Canucks, including a desk top totem pole and a Haida Indian talking stick usually reserved for visiting heads of state. One of Tarasov's most prized souvenirs is Steve Tambellini's hockey stick. Tarasov is an admirer of Tambellini's father, Addie, who played with the 1961 world champion Trail Smoke Eaters. Besides feeling wronged and his character impugned, Pat Quinn's insistence on fighting his suspension has a practical side. "I don't think it will inhibit my ability to work with other clubs," he said. "But I don't want anybody I work with here to ever doubt that I'm not being straightforward with them.'

The Packers beat the Sockeyes 4-3 in Richmond and 7-4 in Kelowna but that was before Richmond made extensive changes to its roster, loading up with players like goaltender Frank Romeo and defencemen Matt Hervey and Mike Claringbull (both 20 and with major junior experience). The Packers will counter with a roster that includes four players already committed to U.S. schools and at least two others entertaining offers. "I would say we're whatever you want us to be,' said Swail when asked about his team. That ability to do it all resulted in a league-best 45-5-2 regular season record, a momentum that could be severely tested by the Sockeyes.

More than the B.C. Junior Hockey League championship is at stake when the Richmond Sockeyes and the Kelowna Packers take to the ice tomorrow night at 7:30. The best-of-nine series between the Coastal Division champion Sockeyes and the Interior Division title-holding Packers also provides the teams with the chance theoretically to make some money. Play in the series, which continues Wednesday at Richmond Arena before heading off to Kelowna for games Friday and Saturday, really means something and as such attracts far more fans than regular season contests. That consideration also means the number of games played in each community is of key importance and will determine the series schedule. If it's tied after four games, two more games will be played in Richmond, the next two in Kelowna Memorial Arena and then the last game back in Richmond. SPORTS RICHMOND SOCKEYES won the B.C. Junior Hockey League title for the first time in eight years, sweeping Kelowna Packers in five games and running their post-season record to 13 consecutive victories. F1
He's one of nine 20-year-olds on the Sockeyes, who played nearly flawless hockey.
"(It was the) best feeling I've ever had in my hockey career," said Romeo of the win.
Packers 0 Sockeyes 7

Romeo is Frank Romeo, who stopped 27 shots to anchor the Sockeyes in a 7-0 victory against his former team, the Kelowna Packers, in the opening game of the best-of-nine championship series Tuesday. Play between Sockeyes, Coastal Division champions, and the Interior Division-winning Packers continued last night. There was only a hint of drama in the blowout. Richmond led just 1-0 after one period, but scored 12 seconds into the second period and added four more goals before Kelowna could retreat to the dressing room. Ed Ruff, who coaches Kelowna with Bill Hogaboam, felt it was the Packers' worst game of the year. "They weren't intense and they weren't ready for the game," said Ruff of his squad, which compiled a league best 45-5-2 record.

After being let go by Kelowna, he tried out with Victoria of the Western Hockey League. Two weeks later he headed to Vernon and the Lakers, but before he could be signed the Sockeyes picked him up as a free agent.

Little drama in this victory By FRANK LUBA Sports Reporter Romeo, Romeo, wherefore art thou? In goal for the Richmond Sockeyes, so don't bother him, Juliet, because it's playoff time in the B.C. Junior Hockey League.

Kelowna goaltender Dusty Imoo and a powerful defence helped the Packers trip the Juan de Fuca Whalers 5-2 in B.C. Junior Hockey League action Thursday at Juan de Fuca Recreation Centre. The Whalers outshot the Packers, who hold down first place overall in the league with a 40-3-2 won-lost-tied record, 33-27. But the home club still trailed by period scores of 3-1 and 4-1 before a crowd of 680 fans. Bruce Bianchini, Pat Mangold, Rob Steele, Kerry Russell and Mike Murinko scored for Kelowna. Phil Soukoroff and Sheldon Nohr replied for Juan de Fuca, which has a 16-30 won-lost record. The third period proved chippy with three major fights breaking out. Murinko, Stan Marple and Kevin Tallifer of the Packers and Ron Bhala, Dino Buttizoni and Bruce Courtnall of the Whalers each received five-minute majors for fighting and game misconducts during the final 10 minutes of play. Kelowna's strong defence repeatedly kept the Whalers at bay. Juan de Fuca players who did get through were stopped short by Imoo, although three of the Juan de Fuca shots hit a post. The Whalers face the Richmond Sockeyes at 8 p.m. Saturday in Juan de Fuca.
Heroes of '72 hockey series to skate again against Soviets

VANCOUVER - Bob Nystrom plans court action against the National Hockey League Players' Association to champion the cause of the borderline player. Recent changes in the NHLPA's disability insurance plan and the new collective bargaining agreement with the league have cost the New York Islanders' assistant coach a potential $500,000 in lost benefits. He said this week he isn't out for himself or is trying g a squeeze play on the union, but wants to show that all NHLPA members are not equal when it comes to claiming a disability benefit. "I'm not bitter, I'm not crying the blues and I'm not trying to drain the last bit of money out of the NHLPA," said the 34-year-old former right winger who was forced to retire from the Islanders last season because of an injured left eye. Nystrom last played for the Islanders under a termination contract - the lowest form of NHL job security - which he signed in August, 1985. A month later an insurance company threatened to cancel the disability insurance plan unless the NHLPA drastically revised its payment schedule. Under the new disability agreement, all players 1 under termination contracts, mostly older and fringe players, are excluded from coverage. Nystrom was denied a claim. Previously, any player who suffered a career-ending injury, regardless of age or contract status, would be compensated for $175,000 (U.S.). "I looked into it and found about 13 per cent of all NHL players are playing on a termination," Nystrom said. "These guys are still members of the NHLPA, they pay their dues, they're still playing the same tough, hard game and they're open to the same danger of being injured as anyone else, yet they're not covered." Alan Eagleson, executive director of the NHLPA, said players on termination contracts became ineligible for disability benefits after Sept. 15, 1985, because too many players took advantage of the system.

Harry Sinden, coach of Team Canada '72 and now general manager of the Boston Bruins, and John Ferguson, the Winnipeg Jets general manager who was an assistant coach of the 1972 team, will share the coaching duties. The Soviet team includes goaltender Vladislav Tretiak, defenceman Aleksander Gusev and snipers Aleksander Yakushev and Boris Mikhailov. The games are set for the Copps Coliseum in Hamilton, Ont., on March 13; the Montreal Forum March 15; and the Ottawa Civic Centre on March 17. Cournoyer, who played his first hockey game in five years a week ago, said this time around, the Canadians know what to expect. "They were in such good shape," said Cournoyer. "But the Russians

won't change. So if we lose, we can't say won't know what to expect. "There will be pressure on us but it will be more for fun. We have pride, too. I'm going to get ready the best I can for that game. We don't want to look stupid. But they have pride and they will do their best to win." Rod Gilbert, who toiled for the New York Rangers, said the lowest point of his hockey career followed the Soviet's 7-3 victory at the Forum in the series-opener, and then there was the high point. "After trailing 5-3 with nine minutes to go, we came back and scored two quick goals to make it 5-5 and then of course Henderson scored the winner." In Pittsburgh, meanwhile, Penguins centre Mario Lemieux received medical clearance to play for the NHL all-stars against the Soviet Union in the Rendez-Vous 87

Times-Colonist news services Yvan Cournoyer remembers Paul Henderson's last-minute, CanadaSoviet Union series-winning goal in Moscow 15 years ago as if it were yesterday. Next month, Cournoyer, Henderson : and 15 other members of the 1972 Team Canada squad face 20 members of the same Soviet team in a three-game exhibition series billed as "Relive the Dream.' Cournoyer eminisced about the 1972 series at a Montreal news conference Thursday to promote the upcoming rematch. The teams were tied 5-5 and the eight series that had Canadians huddled around every available television set was dead even, each team having won three games, with one game tied. Cournoyer was flanked by Phil Esposito and Henderson and play was in the Soviet end when, with 34 seconds remaining, Henderson banged in his third straight g gamewinning goal. Cournoyor jumped into Henderson's arms, creating perhaps the most famous pictorial image of the emotional series. "Phil passed to Paul and he scored," said Cournoyer. "It was just a matter of seconds. "I was nervous, but we did it." Gordie Howe, who was playing for Houston of the now-defunct World Hockey Association and wasn't allowed to play in the 1972 series, has accepted an invitation to play at least one game while former Canadiens great Guy Lafleur will also

Times-Colonist news services Keith Crowder scored from close range at 19:07 of the third period and the Boston Bruins edged struggling Pittsburgh 6-5 in a National Hockey League game Thursday night despite three goals and two assists by the Penguins' Mario Lemieux. The game at Boston ended in a bench-clearing brawl as Pittsburgh's Randy Cunneyworth and Crowder traded punches after the buzzer. There were a few side battles before a slugfest between Dan Frawley of the Penguins and Jay Miller of the Bruins touched off a general melee that lasted for several minutes. Crowder poked in his 13th goal just before tripped over teammate Charlie Simmer, who pushed the puck away from goalie Gilles Meloche while stretched out on the ice.

The Canadian Press QUEBEC - Injuries have ravaged three members of the starting lineup of the National Hockey League all-star team, but coach Jean Perron is still optimistic his squad can defeat the Soviets in the Rendez-Vous 87 series this week. At a news conference Sunday night, Perron, who as coach of the Stanley Cup champion Montreal Canadiens will be in charge of the NHL all-star team, said he has been told that starting defencemen Paul Coffey and Mark Howe and right winger Mike Bossy won't be able to play in the games Wednesday and Friday. Coffey of the Edmonton Oilers, Howe of the Philadelphia Flyers and Bossy of the New York Islanders are all suffering from back injuries. Starting centre Mario Lemieux of the Pittsburgh Penguins has been nursing an injured right knee, but is expected to play. While Perron said it was "sad" that the fans who voted for the starting lineup wouldn't be able to see their picks play, he indicated there was enough talent on the 27- man NHL roster to compensate for the injuries. Twenty-two players are expected to dress for the games. Perron will meet with his assistants - Bob Johnson of the Calgary Flames and Michel Bergeron of the Quebec Nordiques - before the NHL's first practice tonight to discuss the revamped starting lineup and to determine the specialty teams. Another thing the coaches will decide is the starting goalie. Goalie Clint Malarchuk of the Nordiques was picked in the fan voting, but the coaches are free to choose whomever they want. PRO HOCKEY SPOTLIGHT SOUVENIRS: When Wayne Gretaky'e parents bought their modest, ranch-style house 25 years ago in Brantford, Ontario, they had no idea what an attraction it would become. Certainly, they never thought people would go through their trash cans looking for souvenirs, but that's what's happening. Gretzky says the curious visitors apparently y hope to find stray skate or something. The Gretzkys now have a security system installed throughout the house. ARCH RIVAL: Hartford defenseman UM on the Whalers' rivalry with the Boston Bruins: "There's a lot of fans here who've either rooted for Boston or the Rangers in the past. Now we're pulling in a lot of those fans." SELL OUT: The Pittsburgh Penguins have sold out (16,033) 14 of their first 20 home games this season and have an average attendance increase of 4,311 per game. Detroit is up roughly 2,000 fans a game, with St. Louis and Hartford also show jumps in excess of 1,500. New Jersey Devils attendance, meanwhile, is down by nearly 1,500 per game, with eight crowds under 8,000, including the record-low 334 who showed up at the Meadowlands during a Jan. 22 snowstorm. The weather was so bad that night many players parked their cars on the nearby New Jersey Turnpike because they couldn't get in the parking lot.

DAVE EY/The seasons are planned. Oregon Fish and Wildlife Commission. Each has a vote. If they do not agree, fishing seasons revert to the previous year's regulations. The Compact met in January to set spring chinook, shad and sturgeon seasons, and will meet in June for sockeye seasons, and in August and September for fall chinook and coho seasons. Public testimony is accepted at Compact sessions. Quotas are not used in Columbia River to divvy the salmon between sportsmen and commercial fishermen. In a nutshell, the biologists have a predicted salmon return to the river. They know how many fish are needed by the hatcheries and can use previous years' data to estimate the sports catch. Commercial seasons are then set to "mop up" much of the balance available. Each state sets its own salmon sport-fishing regulations for the tributaries. In Washington, proposed changes are made available by the Department of Fisheries in February. A public hearing is scheduled Feb. 28 in Olympia and the changes are adopted in midMarch, effective beginning April 1.

Flames score with new line Canadian Press Calgary Flames' coach Bob Johnson 1 put together a line with two rookies and veteran checker Tim Hunter Tuesday night, and it paid unexpected dividends. Unexpected to everyone but Johnson. "I expect great things out of them," said Johnson, after rookies Joe Nieuwendyk and Gary Roberts scored second-period goals to rally the Flames to a 5-3 National Hockey League victory over the Washington Capitals. Nieuwendyk, a 21-year old centre who signed with the Flames last Thursday, was appearing in his first NHL game after playing three seasons at Cornell University. He scored at 10:03 of the second period to tie the score at 2-2. Roberts, playing in his 23rd NHL game, put the Flames ahead at 13:54. "Timmy took out both defencemen, and I just went in and picked the puck up," said Nieuwendyk. "I couldn't ask for a better way to start. After a disappointing season at Cornell, where we didn't make the playoffs, this is a great opportunity.", Goaltender Mike Vernon, still a rookie despite playing in 21 of the Flames' 22 playoff games last spring, posted his 28th victory of the season for Calgary. The Flames equalled a club record with their 18th road victory of the season. The Flames and Edmonton Oilers are the only clubs in the league to have winning road records this season. •New York Islanders received second-period goals from Mikko Makela, Brent Sutter and Duane Sutter to lead the Isles to a 6-3 win over Pittsburgh Penguins. Mike Bossy had a goal, his 37th, and an assist as the Islanders ended Pittsburgh's two-game winning streak. The Penguins have not won more than two consecutive games since opening the season with a team-record seven straight vietories. •Randy Moller put Quebec ahead for good in the second period and Michel Goulet added two goals in the third as the Nordiques ended a five-game winless streak, beating Hartford Whalers 6-4. The win moved Quebec back into fourth place and the final playoff spot in the Adams Division with 59 points, two more than the idle Buffalo Sabres.

Sockeyes take opener in OT Canadian Press They worked overtime in the B.C. Junior Hockey League Tuesday night and both winners came up with 7-6 victories. In Richmond, Mike McCormick scored at 3:19 of the second overtime period to give the Sockeyes the nod over Delta Flyers in the first game of their best-of-seven semi-final series. Game two goes tonight at 7:30, also at Richmond Arena. Goalie Jeff Clark was outstanding for the Flyers with 51 saves.

It has been a long season for Ryan Stewart - for all the wrong reasons. A first-round draft choice of the Winnipeg Jets in 1985, Stewart nearly disappeared off the hockey map this year when a series of illnesses sent him home to bed after just 10 games. He was suffering from mononucleosis, a bowel disease and bronchitis and lost 20 pounds in the process. He began contemplating life without hockey. "'The doctors thought I might have colitis, which would have ended my career," said Stewart. "They would have had to take part of my colon out. I was thinking about going to school and joining the RCMP like my dad. But then things started to get better." Portland Winter Hawks' general manager Brian Shaw, looking to bolster his team for the Western Hockey League playoffs, acquired Stewart from Brandon on the Feb. 10 trading deadline. "I just started skating 10 days ago," said Stewart, who scored his first goal in his second game back Sunday as Portland beat the hapless New Westminster Bruins 6-4 at Queens Park Arena. "I have a lot of work ahead of me but fortunately I'm with a good hockey team. I also have to thank Brandon, which won't make the playoffs, for trading me to a contender so I could try and salvage my season.'

Vernon puts early end to season for Knights Canadian Press Nanaimo Clippers, Merritt Warriors and the Penticton Knights had something in common Saturday night. They were erased in four straight games from B.C. Junior Hockey League playoffs. Richmond got four goals from Mike McCormick and a 31-save performance by Frank Romeo while drubbing the Clippers 7-0 in Nanaimo. The Sockeyes now wait for the winner of the best-of-seven DeltaLangley coastal semi-final, which continues tonight with the Flyers leading 2-1 after a stirring rally for a 10-8 victory Saturday. Merritt Warriors put up a struggle to avoid elimination, before losing 5-4 at home. Kelowna advances to the Interior final against the Vernon Lakers, who knocked out the defending Centennial Cup champions on goals by Alan Young, Shawn Howard, Craig Bedard and Campbell Blair.

The Canadian Press OTTAWA - Canada will be represented in hockey and figure skating at the World University Winter Games later this month. The Canadian Interuniversity Athletic Union confirmed the University of Alberta Golden Bears, defending CIAU hockey champions, as Canada's representatives at the Games in Czechoslovakia from Feb. 21 to 28. In figure skating, Martin Marceau, 21, of Laval des Rapides, Que., will compete in the men's event, Julie Brault, 21, of Bellefeuille, Que., will compete in the women's, and Pamela Watson, 22, of Hudson, Que., and Aime LeBlanc, 22, of St. Leonard, Que., will compete in the ice dance. The CIAU said there would be no representation in Alpine or Nordic skiing due to financial constraints and eligibility requirements. "We are extremely gratified that the University of Alberta has accepted the CIAU invitation to represent our country at the Games," CIAU president Gib Chapman said Monday. By Dave Senick Times-Colonist Sports Editor Orland Kurtenbach has spent time in the rink of a small prairie town, wondering if hockey would be his future. He has also watched others, in places like Seattle and Tulsa, struggle with the same question. association with hockey has spanned more than three decades through which there have been changes in what he remembers as a player "The and sees personalities as a coach., much different.' says Kurtenbach. "I look back and I see guys in the dressing room who might remind me of someone I remember playing with. But there are other changes, there always is going to be with time." Kurtenbach played junior hockey with the Prince Albert Mintos in the mid-1950s and continued a nineyear National Hockey League career with the New York Rangers, Boston Bruins, Toronto Maple Leafs and Vancouver Canucks. He has also coached the Canucks, in the organization's farm system, and is now with the Richmond Sockeyes of the B.C. Junior Hockey League. "There weren't the options available,' says Kurtenbach. NHL- SOVIET MATCHUPS Capsule looks at the prior matchups between National Hockey League players and various Soviet Union teams: 1985-86: The Soviet Red Army and Moscow Dynamo combined to post a 7-2-1 record on a tour of 10 NHL cities. Quebec (5-1 over Red Army) and Calgary (4-3 over Dynamo) were the only NHL winners and Pittsburgh tied Dynamo 3-3. 1984: Team Canada beat the Soviets 3-2 in overtime of the Canada Cup semifinals en route to a championship victory over Sweden. The Soviets had beaten Team Canada (6-3) and Team USA (2-1) in earlier round-robin play. 1982-83: The Soviet all-stars earned a 42 record against six NHL teams, with Edmonton (4-3) and Calgary (3-2) posting the only NHL victories. 1981: The Soviet team routed Team Canada 8-1 in the championship of the Canada Cup to avenge a 7-3 loss during round-robin play. The Soviets also beat Team USA 41 in round-robin competition. 1979-80: Moscow Dynamo and the Soviet Red Army combined for 5-3-1 record in a tour of nine NHL cities. Vancouver (6-2 over Dynamo), Montreal (4-2 over Red Army) and Buffalo (6-1 over Red Army) posted the NHL victories and Washington tied Dynamo 5-5. 1979: The Soviets dropped the first game of a best-of-three Challenge Cup series at Madison Square Garden 4-2, but rebounded with 5-4 and 6-0 victories. 1978-79: The Soviet Wings posted a 2-1-1 record in a tour of four NHL cities, losing 6-5 in Detroit and tying Philadelphia 4-4. 1977-78: Soviet Spartak won three of five in a tour of five NHL cities, losing 2-0 at Vancouver and 5-2 at Montreal. 1976: In first Canada Cup tournament, the Soviets posted a 2-2-1 record - including a 3-1 loss to Team Canada but failed to reach the championship, which Team Canada captured with a triumph over Czechoslovakia. The Soviets beat Team USA 5-0 in round-robin play. 1975-76: The Soviet Red Army and Wings combined to post a 5-2-1 record against NHL clubs. Philadelphia (4-1 over Red Army) and Buffalo (12-6 over Wings) were the NHL winners while Montreal tied Red Army 3-3. 1972: After dropping two of the four games in Canada and tying a third, Team Canada travelled to Moscow and shocked the Soviet team by winning the final three games to take the Summit Series 4-3-1, SCORES LIST Scores of the previous games between National Hockey League players and various Soviet Union teams: 1985-86 Tour Red Army 5 Los Angeles 2 Red Army 6 Edmonton 3 Quebec 5 Red Army 1 Red Army 6 Montreal 1 Red Army 4 St. Louis 2 Red Army 4 Minnesota 3 Calgary 4 Moscow Dynamo 3 Pittsburgh 3 Moscow Dynamo 3 Moscow Dynamo 06 Boston 4 Moscow Dynamo 7 Buffalo 4 1984 Canada Cup Round Robin Soviet Union 6 Team Canada 3 Soviet Union 2 USA 1 Championship Team Canada 3 Soviet Union 2 (OT) 1982-83 Tour Edmonton 4 Soviet all-stars 3 Soviet all-stars 3 Quebec 0 Soviet stars 5 Montreal 0 Calgary 3 Soviet all-stars 2 Soviet all-stars 6 Minnesota 3 Soviet all-stars 5 Philadelphia 1 1981 Canada Cup Round Robin Team Canada 7 Soviet Union 3 Soviet Union 4 Team USA 1 Championship Soviet Union 8 Team Canada 1 1979-80 Tour Vancouver 6 Moscow Dynamo 2 Moscow Dynamo 7 Winnipeg 0 Washington 5 Moscow Dynamo 5 Moscow Dynamo 4 Edmonton 1 Red Army 5 NY Rangers 2 Red Army 3 NY Islanders 2 Montreal 4 Red Army 2 1 Buffalo 6 Red Army Red Army 6 Quebec 4 1979 Challenge Cup NHL all-stars 4 Soviet Union 2 Union 5 NHL all-stars 4 Soviet Union 0

Approachable and kind. hearted, particularly towards the lower ranks of his staff, he succeeded in humanising the museum a did have an impatient side, however, and could take extremely tough line if his educational, projects and innovations were not put into action. was for several years a member of the Museums and Galleries Commission and served on the Area Museums From 1938 to 1959 genCouncil for the South eral secretary of the Mineralogical Society and in 1967 he was elected a Fellow of Queen Mary College. An authority on gems and jade, he was president of the Mineralogical Society in 1965, and president of the Gemmological Association in 1973. His publications included Crystal Structure of Minerals (with W L Bragg). Claringbull was knighted in 1975. He married first, in 1938, Grace Mortimer (who died in 1953); they had a son and a daughter. He married secondly, in 1953, Enid Lambert.

SIR FRANK CLARINGBULL, the geologist and mineralogist who has died aged 79, was an innovative director of the Natural History Museum where he greatly improved the style of presentation of exhibits during his tenure from 1968 to 1976. The hugely popular Hall of Human Biology, which opened in 1977, came into being largely on his initiative. Earlier, in 1953, as keeper of mineralogy and head of the X-ray section, Claringbull had worked on the jawbones of a number of prehistoric animals - and an unmarked specimen from the skull of "Piltdown Man". Discovered in 1908 by Charles Dawson, a Hastings solicitor, the Piltdown Man was at first thought to be the "missing link" between and homo sapiens. The Piltdown bone was found to contain mainly gypsum, which absent specimens of soil from the site - had been produced when the various bones were "cooked". The infamous hoax was confirmed when Claringbull brought X-ray powder photography to bear in making his tests on the skull. Gordon Frank Claringbull was born on Aug 21, 1911, and educated at Finchley Grammar School and Queen Mary College, London University, where he took a First in Geology and wrote a doctoral thesis on rocks in Aberdeenshire. He joined the mineralogical department of the Natural History Museum in 1935 as an assistant keeper. 1939 he took time out from then Museum University to go and to study Birming- X- ray crystallography with the chemist, Prof Gordon Cox; and outbreak of war he was invited by Cox to join research team working at the University for the Ministry of Supply. In 1943 he followed Cox to the Special Operations Executive laboratory at Welwyn Garden City, where he spent the rest of the war years working on the development of sabo-

tage explosives. After the war Claringbull visited. the Nazi concentration at Belsen to seek out imprisoned German scientists for Military Intelligence, before returning to the Natural History Museum. During his keepership of the mineralogical department, he was over-run with visitors bringing many mineral pieces for his expert opinion. He was frequently forced to disillusion people who had brought back specimens from India, believing them be rubies. Occasionally he would also adjudge on fragments of meteorites, notably on the one that fell at Barwell, Leics, in 1965. According to Claringbull, this specimen, with its pieces weighing 88lb, was the biggest ever to fall on Britain the record was previously held by the 56lb-meteorite that fell in Yorkshire in 1795. As the director of the Natural History Museum, Claringbull controlled a staff of about 900 in five principal departments, in which he did much to break down the older conservative tradition. He recruited staff for the task of presentation and systematised a department of public services;

Rangers 11, Braves 10: Paul Skilling and Josh Norwitt had two hits each and the Rangers came up with five runs in the bottom of the sixth inning to stymie the Braves on Wednesday, May 20. Nick McGoldrick went 2-for-3 with a home run and two runs scored for the Braves while Ricky Khoury and Adam Pearson each had two hits. Mets 20, Orioles 15: Mark Smith homered while John Cardinale and Josh Fowler combined to score seven runs and record six hits as the Mets dumped the O's on Thursday. Matt Rogina had two doubles and a triple to go along with his two runs scored for the Orioles while Matt O'Leal and Johnny Bellotti each had three hits and crossed the plate twice. Phillies 18, Cardinals 15: Marc Rizio had two hits and scored four times while Nick Claringbull had two hits and crossed the plate three times as the Phils shot down the Cards on Thursday. Andrew Hawker and Denis Claringbull surf around the moral issues surrounding the digital revolution and the need for regulation

However, anyone with a modestly powered PC and some suitable software, can offer a service on the Internet. Add a certain amount of ingenuity in the use of forwarding addresses and the operator can become virtually untraceable. As readers consider these issues (no doubt mainly through the old-fashioned medium of newsprint stuffed through the letterbox) it may be tempting to assume that the cyber-punks and the net-surfers are merely getting their comeuppance. It is fair enough to argue that, since most subscribers have volunteered to join the Internet, they have only themselves to blame if they find it a bit anarchic. However, technology has a habit of moving from the optional to the essential. The Internet is still, relatively speaking, comparable to the early days of radio when people strained to hear what was coming through on their -made sets. Nowadays we regard radio as indispensable, if only to allow us to hear the traffic news o on BRMB or to make calls on portable phones. Once the cables start to hum with new interactive service, businesses may find they need to take part to compete. Subscribers may then find they have to pay a premium price if they want to stay with more traditional methods of trading. When this happens, it will be important that everyone can turn to the new networked services with a high degree of trust. Van we depend on self by the service providers? Would an "information services police" be a threat to freedom of expression? Will companies selling goods to people in their homes be able to gain much too detailed a picture of their tastes and preferences? Will it ever be possible to prevent countries from providing safe havens for the "information pirates?" In the Birmingham Centre for Business Ethics, we regard this as something which should be debated well in advance of the day when the doors open on the new electronic marketplace. Since Birmingham is well placed to exploit the new communications technology, it would be good to see us leading the way in both the business and d the ethics. Andrew Hawker is a Lecturer in Information Technology, Department of Accounting in Finance, University of Birmingham. Denis Claringbull is Senior Industrial Chaplain, Birmingham Diocese. The authors are founder members of the Birmingham Centre for Business Ethics.
