SRI International has promoted Ruth Claringbull to western regional director, in charge of marketing, client contacts and business development in 11 western states and Canada. A 15-year SRI employee, Claringbull, 39, joined the SRI research and consulting team in her native England. She has been at the Menlo Park HQ for 11 years, most recently as Western marketing manager for SRI's Business Intelligence Program. James Weil, 42, has moved up to e.v.p. of marketing and sales at Britton Lee in Los Gatos ... MicroPro International, San Rafael, has promoted John Speller, 52, to v.p. of international sales.

chanic. His "real war" had been spent as a child growing up. in a London where air raid shelters were second homes and doodlebugs a regular occurence. "Night after night in a shelter good place to nurture faith," he says crisply. Later, the family moved to Kent where he came under the "amazing" influence of a club leader called Alan Nelson. But when he faced the selection board for his theological training, "I thought I didn't stand a chance." n fact, he went to King's College, Cambridge, needed six months off to get over an attack of rheumatic fever "It actu- ally gave me time to think, I discovered God had a job for me,' and was eventually ordained priest in Canterbury Cathedral by the then Archbishop, Dr Geoffrey Fisher. Today, after "almost a lifetime in Birmingham," he says the developments in the city over the past few years the NEC, the ICC, the NIA. the decline of the metal-bashing industry, change of function of the Jewellery Quarter itself have brought extraordinary logistical changes to his ministry. "But not the coring, the heart if you like," he insists. "It is still Cross and Resurrection, the Crucified and the Risen. The eternal cycle. It seems to have happened so often in my life." Not that the industrial chaplain, who these days heads a team of priests who minister "at the workplace,' the sort of man to insist on prayers along the car assembly line. "In fact," he says, glasses twinkling, "people seem to think I am unpredictable. "My wife says am extrovert, not at all like the usual occupier of a pulpit." Which probably accounts for "Steam Sunday", the annual summer shindig at St Paul's when the church of Matthew Boulton and James Watt becomes a minor arm of the Great Western Railway. he Rev Canon Denis T tiful abashed Claringbull, church that was his totally beau- really unbuilt for worship, gathers the country's steam enthusiasts around him, lays narrow gauge tracks in the aisles, and hisses and clanks his way through a day of make-believe. "Of course, we clear it all away in time for Evensong,' he says expansively. "Otherwise the Boss might not be pleased." O Front Line Mission. Ministry in the Market Place. By Denis Claringbull. (The Canterbury Press, £7.95).

I discovered I had made a friend." The same happened when Canon Claringbull met a rather bolshy, shop steward at the height the great union versus management skirmishes. "We don't want your bloody sort round here," was the greeting to the man in dog collar. "But by the time I had convinced him I had no management axe to grind, we had become good friends," he says. The secret, the head of the Diocesan Industrial Chaplaincy explains, is not to take sides. "You have to remember you are a guest, you cannot be too closely affiliated to either the workforce or management." There is, he implies, a very delicate line to be drawn, a tightrope to be walked, and a great deal of stress, distress, and sheer bad feeling to be dissipated. You wonder how he copes. Interestingly, the answer lies outside the Anglican Church that has dominated his adult life. "I am part of the co-coun-

Co-counselling is a unique form of off-loading problems in which two people trained and chosen for their sympathy and compatibility alternately act as counsellor and client. "We tend to meet at fairly regular intervals, sit on the floor, hold hands and simply let the problems rip," he says. "For the session one of us is counsellor, the other the counselled." At the end of the session the "client" is "wiped clean" of his - or her problems. On the other hand the counsellor is usually "wiped out". Taking on someone else's problems at this heightened level is exhausting work. In the New Testament Jesus Christ described the energy drain of performing miracles as "Virtue is gone out of me." Canon Claringbull knows exactly what he meant. But, he says, and this is the priest speaking, the pool of Christian love is inexhaustible; faith often does move mountains, and his faith in a benevolent Father is unshakeable. Old-fashioned perhaps he still uses the great King James Bible and the Book of Common Prayer for services at St Paul's but his faith is as strong as the steel core of his newly-built church spire, and as far-reaching as the power of prayer itself. He was attracted to the priesthood after RAF service he was a wireless me..

He still does, of course. Colour, creed and faith simply did not matter. The welfare of the workforce did. "Perhaps it was an easy time to begin," he says. "There was a feeling of great confidence. I got to know people at their place of work, enjoyed their trust at levels where were relatively happy. "That helped a great deal when I discovered situations where they were very unhappy. the Daniel Pidgeon Fund by the Geological Society of London. In 1939, Claringbull spent several months in the Chemistry Department of Birmingham University for the determination of crystal structure under Dr E.G. Cox, now Sir Gordon Cox. After the outbreak of war, he returned to Birmingham, having been seconded to the Ministry of Supply to work with Professor Cox on work which involved the application of the methods of mineralogy, chemistry and X-ray crystallography to the study of certain explosives. When Professor Cox was called to another appointment, Claringbull took over the leadership of the team until 1943, when he rejoined Cox to undertake special scientific duties for

the War Office connected with Combined Operations. He returned to the Natural History Museum in 1945 and was appointed Keeper of Mineralogy. The post -war period was one of significant developments in the fields of analytical and other techniques used in the characterisation of minerals and Claringbull was responsible for ensuring that the Department of Mineralogy was equipped with instrumental means of analysis, including the electron microprobe. Claringbull was appointed Director of the Museum in 1968 at a time of awakening interest in Britain in the educational and cultural role of museums. From the beginning of his time as Director, he took personal control of exhi-
OBITUARIES Sir Frank Claringbull bull

ogical Society and Gemmological Association in both of which he held several important offices and served as President. He was knighted in 1975 and retired as Director in 1976. Following his retirement he served as a member of the Standing Commission on Museums and Galleries. Neil Chalmers Gordon Frank Claringbull, geologist and museum director, born 21 August 1911, Assistant Keeper British Museum (Natural History) 1935-48, Principal Scientific Officer 1948-53, Keeper of Mineralogy, 1953-68, President Mineralogical Society 1965-67, Director British Museum (Natural History) 1968- 76, President Gemmological Association 1972-90, Kr 1975, Member Commission on Museums and Galleries 1976-83, married 1938 Grace Mortimer (died 1953; one son, one daughter), 1953 Enid Lambert, died 23 November 1990.

facilities for the public. He also made sure that the exhibitions incorporated the best educational practices of the day so that they were accessible to the general public. The immensely popular "Hall of Human Biology", which opened in 1977, was the first fruit of this initiative and his educational philosophy is central to the Museum's public work to this day. Frank Claringbull was lively and enthusiastic in all that he did, and was ready to concern himself with the problems of his colleagues' day-by-day work. He had a fund of anecdotes and those who came into contact with him rapily found themselves engaged in lively debate. Throughout his working life he kept close links with the Mineral-

FRANK CLARINGBULL will be remembered as a fine geologist who made distinguished contributions in the field of X-ray crystallography and as an innovative Director of the British Museum (National History). Claringbull was born in 1911, and was educated at Finchley Grammar School and then at Queen Mary College, London, where he was awarded Ph D for his study of igneous rocks in the Balmoral area. He joined the staff of the Museum in 1935, and continued to work there, except for the war years, until his retirement in 1976. first duties were the care of the rock collection and, with Dr W. Campbell Smith, the systematic study of rocks. In 1936, he continued his research work in Inverness and Aberdeenshire and, in 1937, undertook a study, of the Ross of Mull granite, a project for which he was awarded a moiety of

AN uncertain outlook is forecast for a wind-blown weathercock which took flight from his perch on the spire of a Birmingham church. Canon Denis Claringbull, vicar of St Paul's in the Jewellery Quarter, is finding out whether it would be better to repair the wounded but historic old bird or replace him with a new rooster. The vicar, churchwarden Robert Ball and Beadle Alan Sandilands mounted a search of St Paul's Square after a passer-by

"The old cock, made of hollow copper, is in bits and is pretty badly dented and beaten up. Most of the gilt has gone from when it was taken down and refurbished only ten years ago." The weathercock has flown over St Paul's Square since the spire was added to the church 170 years ago and this long service could swing a decision on the future in his favour, ex• plained Canon Claringbull. "I do hope the experts think he is worth saving," he said.
• NEW TWIST: Churchwarden Robert Ball and Canon Denis Claringbull with the flyaway weathercock Vane hope for rooster

called in and asked: "Where's your weathercock gone?" "We went out and looked in vain for nearly two hours before we finally located him - embedded in the church roof," said Canon Claringbull. "He had been there for quite a while without anyone realising that he he had been blown off his perch by high winds."

A Birmingham church leader today called for radical changes in the economy to help the region's jobless. "A massive programme of investment in such things as drains, railways, houses, education and other essential services is required as an immediate first step," said the Rev. Dennis Claringbull. Mr. Claringbull, the Bishop of Birmingham's industrial

shorter working week, a shorter working year and a shorter working life. "If new technology can create wealth employing fewer people, we need to find ways of sharing out this created wealth more equitably.' But to achieve that, traditional institutions of society would have to be questioned and re-structured, Mr. Claringbull said.

Sir Frank Claringbull

FRANK CLARINGBULL was, as Neil Chalmers has written [obituary, 26 November] , lively and enthusiastic in all he did, sometimes to the point of impatience, writes Sir Gordon Cox. He was also highly adaptable and very quick in the uptake. When he came to study X-ray crystallography with me in 1939 he assimilated that subject, still almost as much an art as a science,

with astonishing rapidity. Then, in the early part of the war, he was back in Birmingham, working with such understanding and vigour on problems arising in the manufacture of explosives that before he left the work in 1943 he had produced a dozen reports on topics ranging from the ignition of explosives by electric sparks to the properties of conducting bakelite. Claringbull's versatility made him an obvious choice for recruitment to the small team that provided scientific support for the great range of activities of Special Operations Executive, and he re-

Claringbull was a realist who knew that the Director should be seen to lead the museum from the front, but he was a modest man whose pride was not so much in his own achievements as in those of the scholars and highly-skilled specialists on whom the museum's reputation rested. He also cared about details of their working conditions; it was typical that almost his first act on being appointed Director was to have all the fire'bells rung, thereby revealing, as no doubt he suspected, that they were inaudible to about a third of the staff.

The door will be open to anyone worried about their drinking or about someone else who might be hitting the bottle. The pilot scheme will be launched on March 17. Outreach worker Norman Claringbull says the "Time Out" centre will offer a casual, drop in service with a sociable and friendly atmosphere. If the response is good the service

will be extended to the day time and Tuesdays between 12.30pm and 3.30pm are already being ringed on the Outreach Service's diary. Mr Claringbull says that for many sectors of the alcohol misusing population spending some time living in a hostel is either impossible or inappropriate. "The Outreach Service is now targetting those sectors of the population where an alternative means of help to that provided by a residential programme is more suitable," he said.

Pirates 11, Royals 3: Joe McMackin went 2-for-2 and Joe Denning : scored twice as the Pirates toppled the Royals on Saturday. Darrel Brant cracked a two-run home run for the Royals while Zack Millar had two hits. * * * In Rancho Cotati Minor Little League action from last week: Cubs 11, Orioles 4: David Vondrak went 3-for-3 with a double and three runs scored as the National League-leading Cubs downed the Orioles on Monday, May 11. Craig Harner added three runs scored and two hits to the Cubs' (8-2) attack while Nick Honzik went 2-for-3 and crossed the plate two times and Jeff Thornhill scored twice. Matt Rogina Jr. went 2-for-3 with a double for the Orioles (2-9). Blue Jays 19, Phillies 5: Kenny Metcalf, Keegan Billings, B.J. Billings and Shamus Stafford each scored three times as the Blue Jays (6-3-1) topped the Phillies on Monday, May 11, despite managing only five hits. Daniel Claringbull went 2-for-2 for the Phillies (4-7) while Joe Uhr and Rachel Denning each scored twice. Braves 17, Cardinals 2: Nick McGoldrick and Ricky Khoury each went 2-for-3 and scored twice as the Braves routed the Cards on Wednesday, May 13. Steven Scott, Kevin Perry, Colin Urbanski and Adam Pearson each crossed the plate twice for the Braves (9-3-1). Erick Lindsey managed Cardinals (2-7-1) only hit on evethe ning. Twins 9, Angels 8: Ricky Price went 2-for-2 and scored three times and the Twins came up with two runs in the bottom of the sixth to stop the Angels on Thursday. Alfred Pereira went 3-for-3 with a double for the Twins (7-3-1) while Joel Hanley and Justin Brewer had two hits each and Bruno Bianchi and Brendon Hanley each scored twice. Tage Wert went 3-for-3 with a double, triple and two runs scored for the Angels (2-11) while Jarrett Battenberg added two hits. Reds 10, Athletics 6: Righthander Dustin Moffitt fanned 11 in five innings of work as the Reds quieted the Athletics on Thursday. Paul Alabona and Jonathan Conte each had two hits and scored twice for the Reds (4-7-1). Dan Brechner scored twice for the Athletics (4-9) while Matt Groom struck out nine in four innings of work. Twins 17, Pirates 17: Nick Villanveva went 2-for-3 with two runs scored for the Twins and Tim Brown went 2-for-2 and scored

Justin Miranda went 4-for-4 for the Athletics while Drew Battenberg scored three times. Additionally, Adam Romero and Melody Rocha each had two hits and scored twice. Phillies 15, Orioles 14: Marc Rizio went 2-for-2 with two runs scored and three RBIs as the Phillies came up with one run on the bottom of the fifth inning to outlast the Orioles on Saturday. Dan Claringbull had two hits and scored twice for the Phils while Brent Stockham went 2-for-3 and drove in two runs. Additionally, Nick Langlois, Dan Mazzoni had two hits and Nick Claringbull scored twice. Matt Rogina Jr. crossed the plate three times for the Orioles while Nino Calabro went 2-for-2 and drove in two runs. Additionally, Kevin Shaw scored twice and Stephen Torres-Greene had two RBIs. Indians 12, Reds 5: Nolan Pearson went 2-for-2 as the Indians blasted the Reds on Saturday. Chase Townsend, Brandon Scatto and Cody Larson each scored twice for the Tribe (9-3-1).

However, the Pats picked up three more injuries in Medicine Hat. Terry Lloyd and Mark Janssens sustained left-shoulder injuries and Ken McIntyre's right eye was swollen almost shut after he was the victim of an attack by Tigers' Mike Claringbull. McIntyre and d Claringbull were jostling near the Pats bench midway through the third when Claringbull started going at McIntyre with a cast that is supporting his broken right wrist. Claringbull received a match penalty and was suspended for last night's game against Prince Albert. But while they were shorthanded for five minutes because of Claringbull's antics, the Tigers still managed two goals. Lloyd and McIntyre didn't dress against the Broncos. Janssens did but played sparingly. Brent Fedyk also played in spots for the third consecutive game. He is still bothered by a bruised back. More WHL on Page B4.

Hanley went 3-for-3 and crossed the plate four times as the Twins outlasted the Athletics on Saturday. Justin Brewer and Nick Villanveva each went 2-for-2 and scored three times for the Twins while Mike Passer had two hits including a double and scored twice. Dan Brechner went 2-for-3 with a triple and two runs scored for the Athletics (4-6) while Mike Van Midde, Tom Ortega, Scott Van Buskirk and Heath Blackwell each crossed the plate twice. Indians 10, Phillies 9: Chase Townsend and Nolan Pearson each had two hits and the Indians scored twice in the bottom of the sixth to stop the Phillies on Saturday. Brent Hermsmeyer scored three times for the Tribe. Can Claringbull went 2-for-2 with a double and two runs scored for the Phillies while Rachel Denning and Matt Anglin each chipped

Nick Claringbull went 2-for-4 for the Phillies (3-6) while Dan Claringbull and Dan Mazzoni each scored twice. Cubs 21, Cardinals 1: David Vondrak and Craig Harner both had two hits and scored three times as the Cubbies annihilated the Cardinals on Saturday. Sean Cavanaugh and Brad Vondrak both crossed the plate three times for the Cubs while Nick Honzik, Jeff Thornhill and Geoff Harner each scored twice. William Carrica and Judith Estrin have joined Network Computing Devices Inc., a Palo Alto startup firm, as president and executive vice president. Palo Alto's Hewlett-Packard Co. has announced that Michel A. Naggiar, currently director of Corporate Marketing Services in Palo Alto, has been named general manager of the Direct Marketing Division based in Sunnyvale. He will have the worldwide responsibility for sales and marketing of HP's computer and instrument supplies. Watkins-Johnson Co. of Palo Alto announced that Carol H. Rooson, a Saratoga resident, has been appointed secretary of the company. SRI International has appointed Ruth Claringbull as regional director of western region. In addition to handling the 11-state western marketing region, Claringbull will be responsible for client contacts and business development in Canada. Douglas L. Hamilton, a Menlo Park resident, has been promoted to assistant vice president in the

meanz new car Chertsey housewife Elizabeth Claringbull is full of beans after winning a car in a competition she entered 350 times. Mrs Claringbull, of Little Green Lane, won the Metro City in a competition organised by Heinz to promote its centenary year. She received one of 100 cars being given away in prize draws, entered by filling in labels on the company's products. Alastair Dimmock was installed : as Chipping Campden's new Rotary president when the outgoing president, David Feasey handed over the chain of office. On June 30 Birmingham's senior industrial chaplain and current president of Birmingham Rotary Club, the Rev Canon Denis Claringbull preached at St James Church, Chipping Campden to mark the opening of Chipping Campden Rotary club's second year. Canon Claringbull brought greetings from one of England's oldest Rotary clubs - founded in 1914 - to Campden, one of the newest. • Campden's Women's Institute summer fayre and coffce morning this Saturday will be in aid of three charities the fund for the repair and restoration of Chipping Campden's famous 15th century embroideries; a Cobalt scanner, and WI funds. The event will be held in the Town Hall and entrance is free. The final total of £2,771.77p from this year's house-to-house collection for Christian Aid was over fifty per cent up on last year's total. £2,091.40p came from Chipping Campden and Broad Campden, £572.43p from Mickleton and £107.94p from Ebrington, Hidcotes and Charingworth. Chipping Campden Chamber of Trade held a skittles evening at the Lygon Arms Hotel which was organised by the committee under the chairmanship of Ben Yates. Liz Spittle was the best skittler and Ian Spittle was second.

Helen Claringbull refuses to allow bogus caller, Aron McMahon into her house. A bogus conman! FIFTEEN-year-und Helen Claringbull knew exactly what to do when a bogus caller came to her door. She kept the door securely on the chain and demanded to see the caller's identification before letting him into her home. If she had not, who knows what night have happened... In reality, Helen and the bogus caller, Aron McMahon, aged 14, are both pupils at Abbeylands School in Addlestone. Last Thursday, they were showing elderly visitors at the Eileen Tozer Day Centre, in Station Road, Addlestone, what to look for and what to do when a stranger knocks on the door. Pupils peformed series of little plays, which they had written themselves, designed to show old people how not to fall prey to door-to-door conmen. Supt. Ian Pedrick, of Addlestone police, said: "It is a sad fact of life that one of the most vulnerable sections of society is the elderly, who are so trusting of people who come to their door." He added: "People do take advantage of this very trusting section of society.'
